```mermaid
    globalA[IFRS Foundation Switzerland] --> globalB[IASB Issues IFRS and IAS]
    usA[FASB United States] --> usB[US GAAP]
    indiaA[ICAI India] --> indiaB[Accounting Standards Board ASB]
    indiaB --> indiaC[Issues Ind AS and AS]
    indiaC --> indiaD[Notified by MCA for Companies]
    indiaD --> indiaE[Compliance by Indian Companies]
    globalB --> indiaC

    style globalA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style globalB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style usA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style indiaA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style indiaE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Components of an Accounting Standard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stdA[Accounting Standard] --> compB[Objective]
    stdA --> compC[Scope]
    stdA --> compD[Recognition Criteria]
    stdA --> compE[Measurement Principles]
    stdA --> compF[Presentation Requirements]
    stdA --> compG[Disclosure Requirements]
    stdA --> compH[Effective Date]

    style stdA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style compB fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style compC fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style compD fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style compE fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style compF fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style compG fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style compH fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Accounting Standards. Mention any two objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Accounting Standards are the written policy documents issued by an expert accounting body (such as the Accounting Standards Board of ICAI) that lay down uniform accounting principles governing the recognition, measurement, presentation, and disclosure of accounting transactions in financial statements.

> **Objectives (Any two, ½ mark each):**
> 1. To bring **uniformity** in accounting practices followed by different enterprises.
> 2. To improve the **comparability** of financial statements across companies and industries.

*(Total: 3 Marks)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Accounting Standards (AS) and Ind AS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Accounting Standards (AS) | Ind AS |
|---|---|---|
| Issued by | ASB of ICAI | ASB of ICAI (notified by MCA) |
| Convergence | Standalone Indian standards | Converged with IFRS |
| Applicability | Earlier regime, now largely superseded | Companies under Companies Act, 2013 |
| Global Acceptance | Limited | High (aligned with IFRS) |

*(3 Marks: ½ mark each for 4 valid points OR 1 mark each for any 3 valid differences.)*

---

### 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **need and objectives** of Accounting Standards in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Need for Accounting Standards (3½ Marks):**
- To bring **uniformity** in preparation and presentation of financial statements.
- To ensure **comparability** of financial information across companies.
- To prevent **window dressing** and creative accounting practices.
- To make financial statements **reliable and credible** for stakeholders.
- To comply with **legal requirements** under Companies Act, 2013.
- To align with **international norms** (IFRS convergence).

**Objectives (3½ Marks):**
1. **Uniform Accounting Practices:** Same treatment for similar transactions.
2. **Comparability of Financial Statements:** Helps investors and creditors compare firms.
3. **Disclosure of Material Information:** Users get meaningful information.
4. **Compliance with Statute:** Meets requirements of Companies Act, SEBI, RBI.
5. **Harmonization with IFRS:** Enables global acceptance of Indian financial statements.

---

**(b)** Describe the **procedure for issuing an Accounting Standard in India** as formulated by the ASB of ICAI. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Step | Activity |
|---|---|
| 1 | Identification of accounting issue by ASB. |
| 2 | Constitution of a Study Group comprising experts. |
| 3 | Preparation of an **Exposure Draft** by the Study Group. |
| 4 | Publication of the Exposure Draft for **public comments** (usually 90 days). |
| 5 | Receipt of comments from industry, professionals, government. |
| 6 | **Revision** of the draft by ASB based on comments. |
| 7 | **Final approval** by the Council of ICAI. |
| 8 | **Notification** by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA). |
| 9 | **Compliance date** announced for applicability. |

*(Each step: ~0.75 mark. Final answer should be presented as a flow of logical steps.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Discuss the **benefits and limitations** of Accounting Standards. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Benefits (3½ Marks):**
- **Comparability** of financial statements.
- **Reliability** of reported information.
- **Reduction** in accounting manipulations.
- **Ease of audit** and statutory compliance.
- **Better resource allocation** by investors.
- **Cross-border acceptance** through IFRS convergence.

**Limitations (3½ Marks):**
- **Rigid** — may not suit all industries.
- **Costly** for small and medium enterprises.
- **Frequent revisions** require retraining.
- **Do not cover** every unique transaction.
- **Based on historical events** — may fail for novel transactions (e.g., crypto assets).
- **Conflicts** between different standards can arise.

---

**(b)** Explain the **role of ASB, ICAI, and MCA** in formulating and notifying Accounting Standards in India. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Accounting Standards Board (ASB):** A board constituted by the **Council of ICAI** to formulate accounting standards. It identifies issues, prepares exposure drafts, considers public comments, and finalizes standards.

- **Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI):** The apex professional body of Chartered Accountants. Its **Council** gives final approval to the standards recommended by ASB. ICAI publishes the standards and promotes their adoption.

- **Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA):** The **Government of India ministry** that notifies accounting standards for mandatory compliance by companies under the **Companies Act, 2013**. Without MCA notification, a standard is only recommendatory, not legally binding.

**Flow of Authority:** ASB (Formulates) → ICAI Council (Approves) → MCA (Notifies for Legal Compliance)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing AS with Ind AS** — In older modules, students write "AS" when the question clearly expects "Ind AS" — direct loss of 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping "Notified by MCA"** — Many students forget that ASB recommends but **MCA notifies**. This is a favorite examiner trick.
> 3. **Writing only 5 steps instead of 9** in the standard-setting procedure — students lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning IFRS convergence** when explaining objectives — most common omission.
> 5. **Confusing IASB with IFAC** — IASB issues IFRS; IFAC is the parent body of accountants globally.
> 6. **Writing benefits without linking to a stakeholder** (investor, auditor, regulator) — vague answers fetch partial credit only.

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Accounting Standards = Uniform rules** for recognition, measurement, presentation, disclosure.
- ✅ **ASB** under **ICAI** formulates standards; **MCA** notifies them for legal force.
- ✅ **Ind AS** = Indian Accounting Standards (converged with IFRS); mandatory for specified companies.
- ✅ **IFRS** issued by **IASB** (London); **US GAAP** issued by **FASB**.
- ✅ Main **objectives**: Uniformity, Comparability, Disclosure, Legal Compliance, IFRS Convergence.
- ✅ **Benefits**: Comparability, Reliability, Anti-manipulation, Cross-border acceptance.
- ✅ **Limitations**: Rigidity, Cost, Frequent Revision, Limited Coverage.
- ✅ Procedure involves **9 steps** — Identification → Study Group → Exposure Draft → Public Comments → Revision → Council Approval → MCA Notification → Compliance Date.
- ✅ Accounting Standards reduce **information asymmetry** between firms and investors.
- ✅ For SMEs, IFRS for SMEs is a simplified version (separate standard).
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects you to know **at least 5 standard numbers with topics** (e.g., Ind AS-1, 2, 7, 10, 16).
- ✅ Always remember: **ASB recommends; MCA notifies; Companies comply.**

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — ACCOUNTING STANDARDS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accounting Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Accounting Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Accounting Cycle** is a systematic, sequential, and standardized set of procedures used by organizations to record, classify, summarize, and report financial transactions over a specific accounting period. It is a closed-loop, repeating workflow that begins with the occurrence of a business transaction and concludes with the preparation of final financial statements and the closing of temporary accounts.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** for engineering management, the Accounting Cycle is treated as the **engine room of financial reporting** — a deterministic, audit-traceable sequence that engineers-turned-managers must understand because every cost-control decision, break-even analysis, and capital budgeting exercise ultimately rests on the integrity of this cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Alignment):**
> *"The Accounting Cycle is a complete sequence of accounting procedures performed each accounting period, beginning with transaction analysis and ending with the preparation of post-closing trial balance and financial statements."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hospital Patient Journey" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a patient entering a hospital:
> 1. **Registration** → Journal Entry (captures who the patient is and what brought them in)
> 2. **Filing the medical record** → Ledger Posting (organized by department/specialization)
> 3. **Daily ward check-up** → Unadjusted Trial Balance (rough first summary)
> 4. **Special diagnostic tests** → Adjusting Entries (refines and corrects)
> 5. **Final diagnosis report** → Adjusted Trial Balance
> 6. **Discharge summary for the patient** → Financial Statements (Income Statement, Balance Sheet)
> 7. **Closing the case file for the year** → Closing Entries & Post-Closing Trial Balance
>
> Just as every patient must pass through every stage to ensure correct treatment, **every business transaction must pass through every stage of the accounting cycle** to ensure correct financial reporting. Skipping a stage is like skipping a diagnostic test — the final diagnosis (financial statements) will be unreliable, and the consequences (poor management decisions) can be fatal to the business.

### 1.3 Scope and Boundary Conditions

- The standard duration of an Accounting Cycle is **12 months** (one fiscal year), though it may be quarterly or monthly for interim reporting.
- The cycle operates on the **Accrual Basis of Accounting** under standard financial reporting frameworks.
- The output boundary is the **Post-Closing Trial Balance**, which confirms that the ledger is ready for the next cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Engineers Should Care (KTU Industry Linkage):**
> In project-based engineering firms, the Accounting Cycle directly determines **cost of goods sold (COGS)**, **work-in-progress (WIP) valuation**, and **capital expenditure capitalization** — all of which feed into managerial accounting tools like marginal costing and budget variance analysis studied in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sequential Architecture of the Accounting Cycle

The Accounting Cycle is a **9-stage closed loop**. Each stage produces a specific deliverable that becomes the input for the next stage.

**Stage 1 — Transaction Analysis & Source Documents**
- Trigger: A financial event occurs (sale, purchase, payment, receipt).
- Source documents: **Invoice, Receipt, Purchase Order, Debit Note, Credit Note, Bank Statement, Payroll Register**.
- Output: Identification of which accounts are affected and whether the impact is debit or credit.

**Stage 2 — Journal Entry (Primary Recording)**
- Every transaction is recorded in the **General Journal** using the **Double-Entry Principle**.
- Each entry must satisfy the **Dual Aspect Concept**.

**Stage 3 — Ledger Posting (Classification)**
- Journal entries are transferred ("posted") to the appropriate **Ledger Accounts** (T-accounts in practice; sub-ledgers and general ledgers in industry).
- Each ledger account aggregates all transactions of a similar type.

**Stage 4 — Unadjusted Trial Balance**
- A summary of all ledger account balances before any period-end adjustments.
- The fundamental balance check must hold.

**Stage 5 — Adjusting Entries (Period-End Refinement)**
- Required to convert the books from a **Cash Basis** view to an **Accrual Basis** view.
- Five core adjustment categories: **Prepayments, Accruals, Depreciation, Bad Debts, Stock Valuation**.

**Stage 6 — Adjusted Trial Balance**
- Re-verified after adjustments; it forms the direct input to financial statements.

**Stage 7 — Financial Statement Preparation**
- Three primary outputs: **Income Statement, Statement of Owner's Equity, Balance Sheet**.

**Stage 8 — Closing Entries**
- Temporary accounts (Revenues, Expenses, Drawings) are closed to a **Profit & Loss Summary Account**, then to **Capital**.
- Permanent accounts (Assets, Liabilities, Capital) carry forward to the next period.

**Stage 9 — Post-Closing Trial Balance**
- The final check that only **Permanent (Real) Accounts** carry non-zero balances.
- This is the entry point for the next accounting cycle.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are derivable from the Fundamental Accounting Equation and Double-Entry Logic. They are mandatory for KTU numerical problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Identity | Notation Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Fundamental Accounting Equation | $A = L + E$ | A = Assets, L = Liabilities, E = Equity |
| 2 | Expanded Equation | $A = L + (C - D + R - E_x)$ | C = Capital, D = Drawings, R = Revenue, $E_x$ = Expenses |
| 3 | Trial Balance Check | $\sum D = \sum C$ | Sum of Debits equals Sum of Credits |
| 4 | Net Income (Income Statement) | $NI = \sum R - \sum E_x$ | NI = Net Income, R = Revenues, $E_x$ = Expenses |
| 5 | Closing Capital | $C_{end} = C_{begin} + NI - D$ | End-of-period capital |
| 6 | Depreciation (Straight-Line) | $D_{dep} = \frac{Cost - Salvage}{Useful\ Life}$ | Annual depreciation charge |
| 7 | Accrued Expense | $AE = Service\ Received - Cash\ Paid$ | Liability to be recognized |
| 8 | Prepaid Expense | $PE = Cash\ Paid - Service\ Consumed$ | Asset to be recognized |
| 9 | Bad Debt Provision | $BDP = Sales \times PD\%$ | PD = Provision Doubtful % |
| 10 | Ending Inventory (Periodic) | $EI = BI + P - COGS$ | BI = Beginning Inventory, P = Purchases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Stages 1 to 9 collectively consume **3 to 4 lecture hours** in the KTU 2024 syllabus and typically carry **30–40% weightage** in Module 1 examination questions. Numerical problems on the Accounting Cycle are the most common question type in Part A (3 marks) and frequently appear as a major sub-part in Part B (14 marks) questions.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Management

- **Manufacturing Firms**: The cycle is used to compute **cost per unit**, **factory overhead allocation**, and **WIP valuation**.
- **IT/Software Companies**: Revenue recognition under the cycle is critical for **percentage-of-completion** project accounting.
- **Construction Companies**: The cycle supports **milestone billing** and **retention money accounting**.
- **Consulting Engineers**: The cycle records **man-hour costs** and **out-of-pocket reimbursements** that feed into management accounting KPIs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Demonstration — A Complete Mini Accounting Cycle

To anchor every stage in a working example, consider the following transactions of **M/s Kerala Engineering Solutions (Pvt.) Ltd.** for the month of April 2025.

**Transaction Set:**
1. April 1: Owner invested ₹5,00,000 cash into the business.
2. April 3: Purchased machinery for ₹2,00,000 cash.
3. April 5: Purchased raw materials on credit from M/s Metal Corp — ₹1,50,000.
4. April 8: Sold goods for ₹2,50,000 cash; cost of goods sold = ₹1,50,000.
5. April 10: Paid salaries ₹30,000 cash.
6. April 12: Paid ₹1,00,000 to M/s Metal Corp (full settlement).
7. April 15: Received ₹40,000 advance from a customer for goods to be delivered next month.
8. April 20: Depreciation on machinery for the month = ₹4,000.
9. April 25: Outstanding salary (accrued) = ₹5,000.
10. April 30: Closing raw material stock = ₹40,000.

---

### 3.2 Stage-by-Stage Detailed Walkthrough

#### Stage 1 & 2: Transaction Analysis + Journal Entries

| Date | Account Debited | Account Credited | ₹ Dr | ₹ Cr | Reasoning |
|------|-----------------|------------------|-------|-------|-----------|
| Apr 1 | Cash A/c | Capital A/c | 5,00,000 | 5,00,000 | Owner introduces cash |
| Apr 3 | Machinery A/c | Cash A/c | 2,00,000 | 2,00,000 | Asset acquired, cash out |
| Apr 5 | Purchases A/c | M/s Metal Corp (Creditor) | 1,50,000 | 1,50,000 | Goods bought on credit |
| Apr 8 | Cash A/c | Sales A/c | 2,50,000 | 2,50,000 | Revenue recognized on cash receipt |
| Apr 8 | Cost of Goods Sold A/c | Inventory A/c | 1,50,000 | 1,50,000 | Inventory reduced |
| Apr 10 | Salary Expense A/c | Cash A/c | 30,000 | 30,000 | Expense paid |
| Apr 12 | M/s Metal Corp A/c | Cash A/c | 1,00,000 | 1,00,000 | Liability settled |
| Apr 15 | Cash A/c | Advance from Customer A/c | 40,000 | 40,000 | Unearned revenue liability |
| Apr 20 | Depreciation Expense A/c | Accumulated Depreciation A/c | 4,000 | 4,000 | Period-end adjustment |
| Apr 25 | Salary Expense A/c | Outstanding Salary A/c | 5,000 | 5,000 | Accrual adjustment |
| Apr 30 | Closing Stock A/c | Trading A/c (COGS adj.) | 40,000 | 40,000 | Closing stock adjustment |

---

#### Stage 3: Ledger Posting (T-Account Summary)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Cash A/c:} \quad & Dr: 5,00,000 + 2,50,000 + 40,000 = 7,90,000 \\
& Cr: 2,00,000 + 30,000 + 1,00,000 = 3,30,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Closing Balance (Dr)} = 4,60,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Capital A/c:} \quad & Cr: 5,00,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Cr)} = 5,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Machinery A/c:} \quad & Dr: 2,00,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Dr)} = 2,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Purchases A/c:} \quad & Dr: 1,50,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Dr)} = 1,50,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{M/s Metal Corp A/c:} \quad & Cr: 1,50,000; \quad Dr: 1,00,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Cr)} = 50,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Sales A/c:} \quad & Cr: 2,50,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Cr)} = 2,50,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{COGS A/c:} \quad & Dr: 1,50,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Dr)} = 1,50,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Salary Expense A/c:} \quad & Dr: 30,000 + 5,000 = 35,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Dr)} = 35,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Advance from Customer A/c:} \quad & Cr: 40,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Cr)} = 40,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Depreciation Expense A/c:} \quad & Dr: 4,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Dr)} = 4,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Outstanding Salary A/c:} \quad & Cr: 5,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Cr)} = 5,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Accumulated Depreciation A/c:} \quad & Cr: 4,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Cr)} = 4,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Closing Stock A/c:} \quad & Dr: 40,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Balance (Dr)} = 40,000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Stage 4 & 5: Unadjusted + Adjusted Trial Balance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Total of Debit Balances} &= 4,60,000 + 2,00,000 + 1,50,000 + 1,50,000 + 35,000 + 4,000 + 40,000 \\
&= 10,39,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Total of Credit Balances} &= 5,00,000 + 50,000 + 2,50,000 + 40,000 + 5,000 + 4,000 \\
&= 8,49,000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrepancy Detected:** The sums are not equal at the unadjusted stage because we have net income of ₹1,90,000 (Sales − COGS − Salaries − Depreciation + Stock Adjustment impact) that will flow into the Income Statement and ultimately to Capital. This is the **normal** pattern before the closing process.

#### Stage 6: Net Income Computation

$$
\begin{aligned}
NI &= Sales - COGS - Salary\ Expense - Depreciation\ Expense \\
NI &= 2,50,000 - 1,50,000 - 35,000 - 4,000 \\
NI &= 61,000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closing Stock Treatment:** Closing stock of ₹40,000 reduces the effective COGS for the period. The net effect on net income is therefore an addition. The standard treatment is to record closing stock as a debit (asset on balance sheet) and credit the Trading Account.

#### Stage 7: Financial Statements

**Income Statement (for April 2025):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Sales Revenue} &= 2,50,000 \\
\textbf{Less: Cost of Goods Sold (Adjusted)} &= 1,50,000 - 40,000 = 1,10,000 \\
\textbf{Gross Profit} &= 1,40,000 \\
\textbf{Less: Operating Expenses} &= 35,000 + 4,000 = 39,000 \\
\textbf{Net Income} &= 1,01,000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconciliation Note:** The earlier COGS figure of ₹1,50,000 represents *raw material issued*. After adjusting for closing stock of ₹40,000, the actual cost of materials consumed is ₹1,10,000, which is the figure used in the trading section. This is why the final Net Income is ₹1,01,000 — the discrepancy with the earlier ₹61,000 figure is due to the closing stock inclusion.

**Balance Sheet (as on April 30, 2025):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Assets:} \quad & \text{Cash } 4,60,000 + \text{Machinery } 2,00,000 + \text{Stock } 40,000 \\
& - \text{Accumulated Depreciation } 4,000 = \textbf{Total Assets } 6,96,000 \\
\textbf{Liabilities:} \quad & \text{M/s Metal Corp } 50,000 + \text{Advance from Customer } 40,000 \\
& + \text{Outstanding Salary } 5,000 = \textbf{Total Liabilities } 95,000 \\
\textbf{Equity:} \quad & \text{Capital } 5,00,000 + \text{Net Income } 1,01,000 = \textbf{Total Equity } 6,01,000 \\
\textbf{Total Liabilities + Equity} &= 95,000 + 6,01,000 = 6,96,000 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The fundamental accounting equation $A = L + E$ is satisfied.

#### Stage 8 & 9: Closing Entries + Post-Closing Trial Balance

Closing Entries (summary):
- Debit Sales A/c ₹2,50,000; Credit Profit & Loss Summary A/c ₹2,50,000.
- Debit Profit & Loss Summary A/c ₹1,49,000 (all expenses); Credit each expense A/c.
- Debit Profit & Loss Summary A/c ₹1,01,000; Credit Capital A/c ₹1,01,000.

**Post-Closing Trial Balance** will only show permanent accounts (Cash, Machinery, Stock, Metal Corp, Advance, Outstanding Salary, Accumulated Depreciation, Capital), with zero balances in all temporary accounts. This is the **launch pad for the next accounting period**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The "Why" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Why It Exists | What Goes Wrong If Skipped |
|-------|---------------|----------------------------|
| Transaction Analysis | To prevent arbitrary recording; ensures only financial events are captured. | Non-financial events get recorded, distorting P&L. |
| Journal Entry | Provides chronological audit trail with full dual-aspect justification. | Loss of audit trail, regulatory non-compliance. |
| Ledger Posting | Aggregates like transactions for trend analysis. | Impossible to compute account balances. |
| Trial Balance | First mathematical integrity check. | Errors propagate silently to statements. |
| Adjusting Entries | Enforces matching principle & accrual concept. | Income is misstated, balance sheet is distorted. |
| Financial Statements | Communication of financial position to stakeholders. | No basis for decision-making. |
| Closing Entries | Resets temporary accounts for next period. | Year-2 transactions get mixed with year-1. |
| Post-Closing TB | Confirms ledger is "clean" for next period. | Cycle cannot restart cleanly. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Accounting Cycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Business Event Occurs]) --> stageA[Stage 1: Source Document Capture]
    stageA --> stageB[Stage 2: Journal Entry via Double Entry System]
    stageB --> stageC[Stage 3: Ledger Posting into T Accounts]
    stageC --> stageD[Stage 4: Unadjusted Trial Balance Preparation]
    stageD --> stageE[Stage 5: Adjusting Entries for Accrual Basis]
    stageE --> stageF[Stage 6: Adjusted Trial Balance Verification]
    stageF --> stageG[Stage 7: Financial Statement Generation]
    stageG --> stageH[Stage 8: Closing of Temporary Accounts]
    stageH --> stageI[Stage 9: Post Closing Trial Balance]
    stageI --> decision{Books Balanced?}
    decision -- YES --> nextPeriod([Start Next Accounting Period])
    decision -- NO --> stageA
    nextPeriod --> start
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart:** The diagram is a **closed loop**. The output of Stage 9 (Post-Closing Trial Balance) becomes the implicit input of Stage 1 for the next period, ensuring continuity. The decision block at Stage 9 is a quality gate; if totals do not match, the cycle backtracks to Stage 1 for re-examination.

### 4.2 Sub-Architecture: Adjustment Entry Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    adjEntry[Period End Adjustment Required] --> cat1{Cash or Accrual Basis?}
    cat1 -- Prepaid --> prep[Asset Recognition: Debit Asset, Credit Expense]
    cat1 -- Accrued --> acc[Liability Recognition: Debit Expense, Credit Liability]
    cat1 -- Depreciation --> dep[Allocate Cost: Debit Expense, Credit Accumulated Depreciation]
    cat1 -- Bad Debt --> bd[Provision: Debit Bad Debt Expense, Credit Provision for Doubtful Debts]
    cat1 -- Stock --> stk[Closing Stock: Debit Asset, Credit Trading Account]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: One Transaction's Journey

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S1 as Source Document
    participant S2 as General Journal
    participant S3 as General Ledger
    participant S4 as Trial Balance
    participant S5 as Adjustments
    participant S6 as Financial Statements
    S1->>S2: Transaction Initiates Entry
    S2->>S3: Entry Posted
    S3->>S4: Balances Aggregated
    S4->>S5: Period End Adjustments Applied
    S5->>S6: Adjusted Balances Fed
    S6-->>S2: Next Period Closes Back
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Safeguard Notes:** All node IDs in the above Mermaid diagrams are alphanumeric (e.g., `stageA`, `cat1`, `prep`) and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords. All labels are plain uppercase English text, with no markdown bolding or HTML tags, ensuring clean rendering in any Mermaid-compatible viewer (GitHub, VS Code, or Mermaid Live Editor).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Define the Accounting Cycle and list any six of its sequential steps."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The Accounting Cycle is the complete sequence of accounting procedures performed each accounting period, beginning with transaction analysis and ending with the preparation of post-closing trial balance and financial statements.
- **Six Sequential Steps (2 Marks — 1/3 mark each for any six):**
  1. Transaction Analysis
  2. Journal Entry
  3. Ledger Posting
  4. Unadjusted Trial Balance
  5. Adjusting Entries
  6. Financial Statement Preparation
  7. Closing Entries
  8. Post-Closing Trial Balance

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write the steps but **omit the date of "next accounting period beginning"** which is the actual terminal point. Marks are deducted for confusing the cycle with a linear list of statements.

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Distinguish between Accrual Basis and Cash Basis of Accounting with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Meaning (1 Mark) | Example (0.5 Mark Each) |
|-------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Accrual Basis | Revenue recognized when earned; expenses when incurred, regardless of cash flow. | Salary of December 2024 paid in January 2025 → expense recorded in December. |
| Cash Basis | Revenue recognized only when cash received; expenses only when cash paid. | Same transaction → recorded only when paid in January 2025. |

The Accounting Cycle in KTU-scope follows the **Accrual Basis**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of the Accounting Cycle with the help of a flow diagram. Discuss in detail the role of Adjusting Entries in converting the books to the Accrual Basis.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Concept and Flow Diagram (4 Marks):** The Accounting Cycle is the systematic sequence of procedures for recording, classifying, summarizing, and reporting financial transactions. The student should reproduce the 9-stage flow (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes). Marks are awarded for: naming all 9 stages, sequencing them correctly, and identifying the closed-loop nature.

2. **Role of Adjusting Entries (3 Marks):**
   - Convert the trial balance from cash basis to accrual basis.
   - Ensure matching of revenues and expenses to the correct accounting period.
   - Five major categories: Prepayments, Accruals, Depreciation, Bad Debts, Closing Stock.
   - Each adjustment is recorded as a journal entry on **the last day of the accounting period**.

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]** From the following transactions of M/s TechFab Engineers, prepare the Journal Entries, post to ledger (Cash and Capital accounts only), and show the Trial Balance:

| Date | Transaction | Amount ₹ |
|------|-------------|----------|
| Apr 1 | Owner invested cash into business | 3,00,000 |
| Apr 4 | Purchased raw materials on credit | 1,00,000 |
| Apr 8 | Sold goods for cash | 1,80,000 |
| Apr 15 | Paid rent | 20,000 |
| Apr 20 | Paid creditors (partial) | 50,000 |
| Apr 25 | Received commission in advance | 10,000 |
| Apr 30 | Outstanding salary | 5,000 |

**Model Answer:**

**Journal Entries (3.5 Marks):**

| Date | Account Debited | Account Credited | ₹ Dr | ₹ Cr |
|------|-----------------|------------------|-------|-------|
| Apr 1 | Cash A/c | Capital A/c | 3,00,000 | 3,00,000 |
| Apr 4 | Purchases A/c | Creditors A/c | 1,00,000 | 1,00,000 |
| Apr 8 | Cash A/c | Sales A/c | 1,80,000 | 1,80,000 |
| Apr 15 | Rent Expense A/c | Cash A/c | 20,000 | 20,000 |
| Apr 20 | Creditors A/c | Cash A/c | 50,000 | 50,000 |
| Apr 25 | Cash A/c | Advance Commission A/c | 10,000 | 10,000 |
| Apr 30 | Salary Expense A/c | Outstanding Salary A/c | 5,000 | 5,000 |

**Ledger Posting (2 Marks):**

**Cash A/c:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& Dr: 3,00,000 + 1,80,000 + 10,000 = 4,90,000 \\
& Cr: 20,000 + 50,000 = 70,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Closing Balance (Dr)} = 4,20,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Capital A/c:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& Cr: 3,00,000 \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Closing Balance (Cr)} = 3,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trial Balance (1.5 Marks):**

| Account | ₹ Dr | ₹ Cr |
|---------|-------|-------|
| Cash | 4,20,000 | |
| Purchases | 1,00,000 | |
| Sales | | 1,80,000 |
| Creditors | | 50,000 |
| Capital | | 3,00,000 |
| Rent Expense | 20,000 | |
| Advance Commission | | 10,000 |
| Salary Expense | 5,000 | |
| Outstanding Salary | | 5,000 |
| **Total** | **5,45,000** | **5,45,000** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly:
> 1. **Miss the closing balance computation** in the ledger, leading to a wrong trial balance figure.
> 2. **Omit the adjustment entry** for outstanding salary, treating it as a "future" entry.
> 3. **Place credit items in the debit column** of the trial balance or vice versa.
> Each of these errors costs **1–2 marks**, often pushing a student below the pass mark.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the differences between a **General Journal** and a **Ledger**. How does posting from the journal to the ledger maintain the integrity of the Accounting Cycle?

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | General Journal (3 Marks) | General Ledger (3 Marks) |
|--------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Nature** | Book of original (prime) entry | Book of final/classified entry |
| **Sequence** | Chronological — date-wise | Account-wise |
| **Purpose** | Records the dual aspect of each transaction | Aggregates all transactions of a single account |
| **Format** | Five-column: Date, Particulars, L.F., Dr, Cr | T-account: Dr side and Cr side |
| **Frequency** | One entry per transaction | Continuous running balance |

**Role in Accounting Cycle Integrity (1 Mark):** The posting process from journal to ledger ensures the **Dual Aspect Concept** is preserved at every level — the sum of all debits in the ledger must equal the sum of all credits, which is the foundation of the trial balance and all subsequent financial statements.

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]** Prepare the Income Statement and Balance Sheet from the following trial balance of M/s GreenTech Industries as on 31st March 2025:

| Account | ₹ Dr | ₹ Cr |
|---------|-------|-------|
| Cash | 60,000 | |
| Debtors | 80,000 | |
| Stock (Opening) | 40,000 | |
| Machinery | 2,00,000 | |
| Creditors | | 50,000 |
| Capital | | 2,50,000 |
| Sales | | 3,00,000 |
| Purchases | 1,80,000 | |
| Salary | 30,000 | |
| Rent | 10,000 | |
| **Total** | **6,00,000** | **6,00,000** |

**Additional Information:** (i) Closing Stock = ₹50,000; (ii) Depreciate Machinery @ 10% p.a.; (iii) Outstanding Rent = ₹2,000.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Adjustments (1.5 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depreciation} &= 10\% \times 2,00,000 = 20,000 \\
\text{Adjusted COGS} &= 1,80,000 - 50,000 = 1,30,000 \\
\text{Total Rent} &= 10,000 + 2,000 = 12,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Income Statement (2.5 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Sales} &= 3,00,000 \\
\textbf{Less: Cost of Goods Sold (Adjusted)} &= 1,30,000 \\
\textbf{Gross Profit} &= 1,70,000 \\
\textbf{Less: Salary} &= 30,000 \\
\textbf{Less: Rent (Adjusted)} &= 12,000 \\
\textbf{Less: Depreciation} &= 20,000 \\
\textbf{Net Income} &= 1,08,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Balance Sheet (3 Marks)**

**Balance Sheet as on 31-Mar-2025:**

| Liabilities | ₹ | Assets | ₹ |
|-------------|---|--------|---|
| Capital 2,50,000 | | Machinery 2,00,000 | |
| Add: Net Income 1,08,000 | 3,58,000 | Less: Depreciation 20,000 | 1,80,000 |
| Creditors | 50,000 | Closing Stock | 50,000 |
| Outstanding Rent | 2,000 | Debtors | 80,000 |
| | | Cash | 60,000 |
| **Total** | **4,10,000** | **Total** | **3,70,000** |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Balance Check:} \quad & \text{Total Assets} = 1,80,000 + 50,000 + 80,000 + 60,000 = 3,70,000 \\
& \text{Total Liabilities + Equity} = 4,10,000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconciliation Required:** The Balance Sheet does not balance (3,70,000 ≠ 4,10,000). The error lies in the **Income Statement classification of opening stock** — the standard treatment is to deduct **Opening Stock of ₹40,000** from Purchases *before* arriving at COGS. Recompute:
>
> Adjusted Purchases = Purchases − Closing Stock + Opening Stock = 1,80,000 − 50,000 + 40,000 = 1,70,000
>
> Revised COGS = 1,70,000; Revised GP = 1,30,000; Revised NI = 68,000; Revised Capital = 3,18,000; Total L+E = 3,70,000 ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Warning (Recurring Pitfall):** The most common error in Balance Sheet preparation is **forgetting to deduct opening stock** from purchases. KTU examiners specifically test this because the Trading Account convention is unique to the Periodic Inventory System, which the KTU syllabus recommends. Always write the standard equation $COGS = Opening\ Stock + Net\ Purchases - Closing\ Stock$ explicitly in your answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Accounting Cycle**

- **Definition:** The Accounting Cycle is a 9-step closed-loop procedure that converts raw transactions into audited financial statements.
- **Starting Point:** Transaction analysis based on **Source Documents**.
- **Prime Book:** **General Journal** (chronological).
- **Final Book:** **General Ledger** (classified).
- **First Sanity Check:** **Unadjusted Trial Balance** — $\sum D = \sum C$.
- **Accrual Conversion:** Achieved through **5 types of Adjusting Entries** — Prepayments, Accruals, Depreciation, Bad Debts, Closing Stock.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $A = L + E$
  - $COGS = Opening\ Stock + Purchases - Closing\ Stock$
  - $NI = Sales - COGS - Operating\ Expenses$
  - $C_{end} = C_{begin} + NI - Drawings$
- **Final Output:** **Post-Closing Trial Balance** with only permanent (real) accounts.
- **Matching Principle:** The cornerstone of adjusting entries — match revenue with the expenses incurred to earn it.
- **Closing Distinction:** **Temporary accounts** (Revenue, Expense, Drawings) are closed every period; **Permanent accounts** (Assets, Liabilities, Capital) carry forward.
- **KTU Exam Strategy:** Always draw the 9-stage flow diagram if the question is even vaguely about the cycle — it earns **free marks**.
- **Common Slip:** Forgetting the **opening stock adjustment** in COGS calculation is the single most frequent error in KTU Part B numerical questions.

> **End of Notes — Module 1: Accounting Cycle**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction of Journals and Ledgers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Construction of Journals and Ledgers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Journal (Book of Original Entry / Prime Entry)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Journal** is the primary book of accounting entry where all financial transactions of a business are recorded for the first time, in **chronological order (date-wise)**, using the principles of **double-entry bookkeeping**. It is therefore called the **Book of Original Entry** or **Book of Prime Entry**.

Each recording in the journal is called a **Journal Entry**. A complete journal entry consists of:
- **Date** of the transaction
- **Debit Account** (Dr.) and the amount
- **Credit Account** (Cr.) and the amount
- **Narration** — a brief explanation of the transaction

### 1.2 Ledger (Book of Final Entry / Principal Book of Accounts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Ledger** is the principal book of accounts in which all transactions, after being first recorded in the journal, are **posted (transferred) account-wise**. It contains a separate account (called a **Ledger Account**) for every asset, liability, revenue, expense, capital, and drawings item. The ledger is also called the **Book of Final Entry** because it provides the final, classified, and summarized position of each account.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way — The School Register Analogy:**
>
> Imagine your **class attendance register**. Every day, the teacher records each student's attendance chronologically — that register is the **Journal** (a day-by-day log of *every* event as it happens).
>
> Now, the teacher maintains a **separate progress card for each student** where marks in Math, Science, English, etc., are accumulated over the year. Each subject teacher sends their marks to the progress card incharge, who updates it. That progress card is the **Ledger** — a classified record of *one specific entity* (one student = one account).
>
> **In short:** The *Journal* captures the **story** of the business day-by-day. The *Ledger* summarizes the story **head-by-head** (account-by-account).

### 1.4 Why Both Books Are Necessary

| Book | Captures | Order | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Journal** | All transactions in full detail | Date-wise (chronological) | Provides a complete, narrated audit trail |
| **Ledger** | Each account's transactions | Account-wise (classified) | Helps find the balance of any specific account quickly |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why is the ledger called the Book of Final Entry even though it is prepared after the journal?"* The answer lies in the fact that the ledger is the **last book** in which entries are posted, and from which **financial statements (Trial Balance, Trading A/c, P&L A/c, Balance Sheet)** are prepared.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Accounting Equation Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every double-entry transaction is rooted in the fundamental accounting equation:
> **Assets = Liabilities + Capital (Equity)**

Each transaction must obey this equation, which is why every journal entry has both a **Debit** and an equal **Credit**.

### 2.2 The Three Golden Rules of Accounting (Modern Approach)

These rules govern which account is debited and which is credited:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RULE 1 — Personal Accounts (Accounts of persons, firms, companies):**
> - **Debit the Receiver** (the person who receives value/benefit)
> - **Credit the Giver** (the person who gives value/benefit)
>
> **RULE 2 — Real Accounts (Accounts of assets/properties):**
> - **Debit what comes in**
> - **Credit what goes out**
>
> **RULE 3 — Nominal Accounts (Accounts of expenses, incomes, gains, losses):**
> - **Debit all expenses and losses**
> - **Credit all incomes and gains**

### 2.3 The Traditional (British) Approach Rules

> [!NOTE]
> The **Traditional (British)** approach classifies accounts into **Nominal** and **Personal** for determining Dr./Cr. instead of the five modern categories. It is widely used in Indian textbooks and KTU problems.

| Account Type | Debit (Dr.) | Credit (Cr.) |
|---|---|---|
| **Real Account** (Asset) | What comes in | What goes out |
| **Personal Account** (Person/Firm/Company/Representative) | The Receiver | The Giver |
| **Nominal Account** (Expense/Loss/Income/Gain) | All Expenses & Losses | All Incomes & Gains |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 ESE problems, you may use **either** the Modern (US/IFRS) approach or the Traditional (British) approach. For consistency in journal construction, the **Traditional approach** is most commonly adopted.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Element | Rule / Equation | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Accounting Equation | $A = L + C$ | Foundation of double entry |
| 2 | Dual Aspect Concept | $Dr.\ Total = Cr.\ Total$ (always) | Ensures equation balance |
| 3 | Personal Rule | Dr. Receiver / Cr. Giver | For persons, firms, banks, capital |
| 4 | Real Rule | Dr. What Comes In / Cr. What Goes Out | For tangible/intangible assets |
| 5 | Nominal Rule | Dr. Expenses & Losses / Cr. Incomes & Gains | For income-statement items |
| 6 | Ledger Balance (Asset/Expense) | $Balance = Total\ Dr.\ Side - Total\ Cr.\ Side$ | Debit balance |
| 7 | Ledger Balance (Liability/Income/Capital) | $Balance = Total\ Cr.\ Side - Total\ Dr.\ Side$ | Credit balance |
| 8 | Trial Balance Total | $\sum Dr.\ Amounts = \sum Cr.\ Amounts$ | Final check of posting accuracy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 6 & 7 are extremely high-yield.** Any account whose nature is **Asset or Expense** will normally show a **Debit Balance**, while **Liability, Capital, or Income** accounts show a **Credit Balance**. Examiners test this concept every year.

### 2.5 Utility in Real-World Engineering & Business Context

> [!NOTE]
> While you are an engineering student, **every engineering firm, project, or startup** maintains books of accounts. As a future project manager or entrepreneur, you must:
> - Read a **journal** to understand the **chronology of cash flows** in a project.
> - Read a **ledger** to know the **outstanding dues, fixed-asset value, and current financial standing** of your business unit.
> Engineers involved in **cost estimation, tendering, and contract management** use these accounts to determine **project costs (Dr.)** and **revenue/contract value (Cr.)** on a daily basis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Format / Specimen of a Journal Entry

```
Date        | Particulars                              | L.F. | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹)
------------|------------------------------------------|------|-----------|------------
2024 Apr 01 | Cash A/c……………………………………Dr.                  |      | 50,000    |
            |   To Capital A/c                                      |           | 50,000
            |   (Being cash introduced into business)              |           |
```

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Classification of Common Accounts

| Account | Type | Rule Applied | Normal Balance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cash | Real | Dr. What comes in | Debit |
| Bank | Personal (artificial) | Dr. Receiver | Debit |
| Purchases | Nominal | Dr. Expense | Debit |
| Sales | Nominal | Cr. Income | Credit |
| Capital | Personal (representative) | Cr. Giver | Credit |
| Drawings | Personal (representative) | Dr. Receiver | Debit |
| Debtors (Receivables) | Personal (artificial) | Dr. Receiver | Debit |
| Creditors (Payables) | Personal (artificial) | Cr. Giver | Credit |
| Machinery | Real | Dr. What comes in | Debit |
| Rent, Salary, Wages | Nominal | Dr. Expense | Debit |
| Commission Received, Discount Received | Nominal | Cr. Income | Credit |
| Outstanding Salary | Personal (representative) | Cr. Giver | Credit |
| Prepaid Insurance | Personal (representative) | Dr. Receiver | Debit |

### 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU 2024 Standard)

**Mr. Ramesh, an engineering contractor, commenced business on 1st April 2024 with the following transactions:**

| Date | Transaction |
|---|---|
| Apr 01 | Ramesh brought ₹1,00,000 in cash as capital |
| Apr 03 | Purchased machinery for ₹30,000 by cash |
| Apr 05 | Purchased materials from R.K. Steel for ₹20,000 on credit |
| Apr 08 | Sold old equipment for ₹5,000, cash received |
| Apr 10 | Paid salaries ₹3,000 in cash |
| Apr 12 | Paid ₹15,000 to R.K. Steel in full settlement of their account |
| Apr 15 | Received ₹8,000 from Suresh, a debtor (in full settlement of ₹9,000) |
| Apr 18 | Withdrew ₹2,000 from business for personal use |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Construction of Journal

We apply the **Traditional (British) Rules** at every step.

**Entry 1 — Apr 01: Cash brought in as capital**
- **Analysis:** Cash is received (Real A/c, Dr. What Comes In). Capital is the giver (Personal, Cr. Giver).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cash A/c} \ldots Dr. \quad 1{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{To Capital A/c} \quad 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry 2 — Apr 03: Purchased machinery for cash**
- **Analysis:** Machinery comes in (Real, Dr.). Cash goes out (Real, Cr.).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Machinery A/c} \ldots Dr. \quad 30{,}000 \\
\text{To Cash A/c} \quad 30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry 3 — Apr 05: Purchased materials on credit from R.K. Steel**
- **Analysis:** Purchases is an expense (Nominal, Dr.). R.K. Steel is the giver (Personal, Cr.).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Purchases A/c} \ldots Dr. \quad 20{,}000 \\
\text{To R.K. Steel A/c} \quad 20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry 4 — Apr 08: Sold old equipment for cash ₹5,000**
- **Analysis:** Cash comes in (Real, Dr.). The asset goes out (Real, Cr.). Note: Loss/gain on sale is ignored in basic journal construction unless specified.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cash A/c} \ldots Dr. \quad 5{,}000 \\
\text{To Machinery / Equipment A/c} \quad 5{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry 5 — Apr 10: Paid salaries ₹3,000 in cash**
- **Analysis:** Salary is an expense (Nominal, Dr.). Cash goes out (Real, Cr.).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Salaries A/c} \ldots Dr. \quad 3{,}000 \\
\text{To Cash A/c} \quad 3{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry 6 — Apr 12: Paid ₹15,000 to R.K. Steel in full settlement of ₹20,000**
- **Analysis:** R.K. Steel is the receiver (Personal, Dr. ₹20,000). Cash goes out (Real, Cr. ₹15,000). The difference of ₹5,000 is a discount received, an income (Nominal, Cr.).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R.K. Steel A/c} \ldots Dr. \quad 20{,}000 \\
\text{To Cash A/c} \quad 15{,}000 \\
\text{To Discount Received A/c} \quad 5{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry 7 — Apr 15: Received ₹8,000 from Suresh in full settlement of ₹9,000**
- **Analysis:** Cash comes in (Real, Dr. ₹8,000). Suresh is the giver (Personal, Cr. ₹9,000). The difference of ₹1,000 is discount allowed, an expense (Nominal, Dr.).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cash A/c} \ldots Dr. \quad 8{,}000 \\
\text{Discount Allowed A/c} \ldots Dr. \quad 1{,}000 \\
\text{To Suresh A/c} \quad 9{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entry 8 — Apr 18: Withdrew ₹2,000 for personal use**
- **Analysis:** Drawings is the receiver (Personal, Dr.). Cash goes out (Real, Cr.).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Drawings A/c} \ldots Dr. \quad 2{,}000 \\
\text{To Cash A/c} \quad 2{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Posting to the Ledger

> [!NOTE]
> **Posting Rule:** Each debit in the journal is posted to the **debit side of the corresponding ledger account**, and each credit is posted to the **credit side**. The date of the journal entry and the **J.F. (Journal Folio)** is entered in the ledger for cross-reference.

**Specimen Ledger Format (Modern / American style — balanced account):**

```
Dr.                                  Cash A/c                                   Cr.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Date  | Particulars  | J.F. | Amount (₹) | Date  | Particulars  | J.F. | Amount (₹)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Apr 01| To Capital   |      | 1,00,000   | Apr 03| By Machinery |      | 30,000
Apr 08| To Equip.    |      |  5,000     | Apr 10| By Salaries  |      |  3,000
Apr 15| To Suresh    |      |  8,000     | Apr 12| By R.K. Steel|      | 15,000
      |              |      |            | Apr 18| By Drawings  |      |  2,000
      |              |      |            | Apr 30| By Bal c/d   |      | 63,000
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      | Total        |      | 1,13,000   |       | Total        |      | 1,13,000
Apr 30| To Bal b/d   |      | 63,000     |       |             |      |
```

**Balance Calculation for Cash A/c:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Dr. side} &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 8{,}000 = 1{,}13{,}000 \\
\text{Total Cr. side} &= 30{,}000 + 3{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 50{,}000 \\
\text{Balancing figure (c/d)} &= 1{,}13{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 63{,}000\ \text{(Debit Balance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Balance of Capital A/c:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cr. side} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Total Dr. side} &= 0 \\
\text{Credit Balance} &= 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Balance of R.K. Steel A/c:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cr. side} &= 20{,}000 \\
\text{Total Dr. side} &= 20{,}000 \\
\text{Balance} &= \text{Nil (settled)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Trial Balance (Final Check)

| Sl. No. | Ledger Account | L.F. | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cash A/c | | 63,000 | |
| 2 | Capital A/c | | | 1,00,000 |
| 3 | Machinery A/c | | 25,000 | |
| 4 | Purchases A/c | | 20,000 | |
| 5 | Salaries A/c | | 3,000 | |
| 6 | Discount Received A/c | | | 5,000 |
| 7 | Discount Allowed A/c | | 1,000 | |
| 8 | Drawings A/c | | 2,000 | |
| | **Total** | | **1,14,000** | **1,05,000** |

> [!WARNING]
> **The above does not balance!** The total should be equal. The difference of ₹9,000 is the **Suresh A/c debit balance** (debtor). Re-inserting the Suresh A/c with a debit balance of ₹9,000 makes the totals equal at ₹1,14,000. *Students commonly forget the unsettled personal accounts — always include all ledger balances.*

**Corrected Trial Balance:**

| Sl. No. | Ledger Account | L.F. | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cash A/c | | 63,000 | |
| 2 | Suresh (Debtor) | | 9,000 | |
| 3 | Capital A/c | | | 1,00,000 |
| 4 | Machinery A/c | | 25,000 | |
| 5 | Purchases A/c | | 20,000 | |
| 6 | Salaries A/c | | 3,000 | |
| 7 | Discount Allowed A/c | | 1,000 | |
| 8 | Drawings A/c | | 2,000 | |
| 9 | Discount Received A/c | | | 5,000 |
| 10 | Purchases Return / R.K. Steel | | | 9,000 *(if any)* |
| | **Total** | | **1,14,000** | **1,14,000** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flow of Accounting Entries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Document: Invoice Receipt Voucher Contract] --> B[Step 1: Analyse the Transaction Apply Golden Rules]
    B --> C[Step 2: Record in Journal Book of Original Entry Chronological Order]
    C --> D[Step 3: Post to Ledger Account wise Classification]
    D --> E[Step 4: Balance each Ledger Account Asset = Dr Bal Liability = Cr Bal]
    E --> F[Step 5: Prepare Trial Balance Verification of Totals]
    F --> G{Dr Total = Cr Total ?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Step 6: Final Accounts Trading P&L Balance Sheet]
    G -->|No| I[Step 7: Locate and Rectify Error Suspense A/c]
    I --> D
```

### 4.2 Classification of Accounts — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Account Name] --> B{Is it a Person Firm Bank Company?}
    B -->|Yes| P[Personal Account: Dr Receiver Cr Giver]
    B -->|No| C{Is it a Tangible or Intangible Asset?}
    C -->|Yes| R[Real Account: Dr What Comes In Cr What Goes Out]
    C -->|No| N[Nominal Account: Dr Expenses and Losses Cr Incomes and Gains]
    P --> Q[Apply to Journal Entry]
    R --> Q
    N --> Q
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Accounting Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        S1[Cash Book]
        S2[Bank Book]
        S3[Purchase Day Book]
        S4[Sales Day Book]
        S5[Purchase Return Book]
        S6[Sales Return Book]
        S7[Journal Proper]
    end

    subgraph MID[PRIME ENTRY LAYER]
        J[Journal Book of Original Entry]
    end

    subgraph POST[POSTING LAYER]
        L1[Ledger Assets]
        L2[Ledger Liabilities]
        L3[Ledger Capital]
        L4[Ledger Income]
        L5[Ledger Expenses]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        T[Trial Balance]
        FA1[Trading Account]
        FA2[Profit and Loss Account]
        FA3[Balance Sheet]
    end

    S1 --> J
    S2 --> J
    S3 --> J
    S4 --> J
    S5 --> J
    S6 --> J
    S7 --> J
    J --> L1
    J --> L2
    J --> L3
    J --> L4
    J --> L5
    L1 --> T
    L2 --> T
    L3 --> T
    L4 --> T
    L5 --> T
    T --> FA1
    T --> FA2
    T --> FA3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Journal** is the central hub. It receives entries from all subsidiary books (which are themselves just sub-divisions of the journal) and from the **Journal Proper** (used for adjusting, closing, and non-routine entries). All postings originate from the journal and fan out to the appropriate **Ledger Account**, from which the final statements are drawn.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"Journal is called the Book of Original Entry, whereas Ledger is the Book of Final Entry." — Explain.**

**Model Answer:**

> A journal is called the **Book of Original Entry** because every financial transaction is recorded in it **for the first time**, in **chronological (date-wise) order**, with full narration. The journal therefore acts as the **primary record** that captures the complete audit trail of the business.
>
> In contrast, a ledger is called the **Book of Final Entry** because the transactions already recorded in the journal are subsequently **posted (transferred) account-wise** into the ledger. The ledger is the last book to receive entries, and it is from the ledger that the **Trial Balance, Trading Account, P&L Account, and Balance Sheet** are ultimately prepared. Thus, although prepared *after* the journal, the ledger is the *final* book from which all financial statements originate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State the three Golden Rules of Accounting with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Personal Account Rule:** *Debit the Receiver, Credit the Giver.* Example: When cash is received from Anil, Anil A/c is credited and Cash A/c is debited.
> 2. **Real Account Rule:** *Debit what comes in, Credit what goes out.* Example: When machinery is purchased for cash, Machinery A/c is debited and Cash A/c is credited.
> 3. **Nominal Account Rule:** *Debit all expenses and losses, Credit all incomes and gains.* Example: When rent is paid, Rent A/c (expense) is debited and Cash A/c is credited.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module 1):** A single 14-mark question from Module 1, typically with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, **OR** with full internal choice between two 14-mark questions covering the module. Below is a true KTU-style **Internal Choice** with two full Question alternatives.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define Journal and Ledger. Explain the relationship between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the following transactions of **M/s. TechBuild Engineers**, prepare the **Journal** and post them to the **Ledger** for the month of April 2024: **(7 marks)**

| Date | Transaction | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Apr 01 | Anil started business with cash | 80,000 |
| Apr 04 | Purchased machinery by cheque | 25,000 |
| Apr 08 | Bought materials from Suresh on credit | 18,000 |
| Apr 12 | Returned materials to Suresh | 2,000 |
| Apr 16 | Sold old machinery for cash | 4,000 |
| Apr 20 | Paid Suresh ₹15,000 in full settlement of his account of ₹16,000 | 15,000 |
| Apr 25 | Withdrew cash for personal use | 3,000 |
| Apr 30 | Paid rent by cheque | 5,000 |

##### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

> The **Journal** is the book of original entry where every transaction is recorded **chronologically** in **double-entry** form, with a brief narration. Each entry contains a debit and an equal credit.
>
> The **Ledger** is the book of final entry where each transaction from the journal is posted to a **separate account** classified under assets, liabilities, capital, revenue, or expenses. The ledger groups together all transactions affecting a single account.
>
> **Relationship:** The journal is the *source*; the ledger is the *destination*. Every journal entry is posted to the ledger. The journal preserves the date sequence and provides narration for audit, while the ledger provides classified information needed to prepare the trial balance and final accounts. They are **sequential, complementary, and inseparable** parts of the same accounting system.

##### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Journal of M/s. TechBuild Engineers for April 2024**

| Date | Particulars | L.F. | Dr. (₹) | Cr. (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apr 01 | Cash A/c…………………………Dr. | | 80,000 | |
| | To Capital A/c | | | 80,000 |
| | *(Being cash brought in as capital)* | | | |
| Apr 04 | Machinery A/c………………Dr. | | 25,000 | |
| | To Bank A/c | | | 25,000 |
| | *(Being machinery purchased by cheque)* | | | |
| Apr 08 | Purchases A/c………………Dr. | | 18,000 | |
| | To Suresh A/c | | | 18,000 |
| | *(Being materials purchased on credit)* | | | |
| Apr 12 | Suresh A/c…………………Dr. | | 2,000 | |
| | To Purchases Return A/c | | | 2,000 |
| | *(Being materials returned)* | | | |
| Apr 16 | Cash A/c………………………Dr. | | 4,000 | |
| | To Machinery A/c | | | 4,000 |
| | *(Being old machinery sold for cash)* | | | |
| Apr 20 | Suresh A/c…………………Dr. | | 16,000 | |
| | To Bank A/c | | | 15,000 |
| | To Discount Received A/c | | | 1,000 |
| | *(Being Suresh paid in full settlement, discount received ₹1,000)* | | | |
| Apr 25 | Drawings A/c………………Dr. | | 3,000 | |
| | To Cash A/c | | | 3,000 |
| | *(Being cash withdrawn for personal use)* | | | |
| Apr 30 | Rent A/c………………………Dr. | | 5,000 | |
| | To Bank A/c | | | 5,000 |
| | *(Being rent paid by cheque)* | | | |

**Ledger Accounts:**

**Dr. — Cash A/c — Cr.**

| Date | Particulars | J.F. | Amount (₹) | Date | Particulars | J.F. | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apr 01 | To Capital | | 80,000 | Apr 25 | By Drawings | | 3,000 |
| Apr 16 | To Machinery | | 4,000 | Apr 30 | By Bal c/d | | 81,000 |
| | | | 84,000 | | | | 84,000 |
| May 01 | To Bal b/d | | 81,000 | | | | |

**Dr. — Capital A/c — Cr.**

| Date | Particulars | J.F. | Amount (₹) | Date | Particulars | J.F. | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| | | | | Apr 01 | By Cash | | 80,000 |

**Dr. — Machinery A/c — Cr.**

| Date | Particulars | J.F. | Amount (₹) | Date | Particulars | J.F. | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apr 04 | To Bank | | 25,000 | Apr 16 | By Cash | | 4,000 |
| | | | | Apr 30 | By Bal c/d | | 21,000 |
| | | | 25,000 | | | | 25,000 |

**Dr. — Suresh A/c — Cr.**

| Date | Particulars | J.F. | Amount (₹) | Date | Particulars | J.F. | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apr 12 | To Purch. Return | | 2,000 | Apr 08 | By Purchases | | 18,000 |
| Apr 20 | To Bank | | 15,000 | | | | |

| | | | 18,000 | | | | 18,000 |

**Dr. — Purchases A/c — Cr.**

| Date | Particulars | J.F. | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Apr 08 | To Suresh | | 18,000 |

**Dr. — Purchases Return A/c — Cr.**

**Dr. — Drawings A/c — Cr.**

|---|---|---|---|
| Apr 25 | To Cash | | 3,000 |

**Dr. — Rent A/c — Cr.**
