## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define Working Capital. Distinguish between Gross Working Capital and Net Working Capital.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Working Capital refers to the capital required to finance the day-to-day operations of a business. It represents the funds invested in current assets to bridge the gap between current asset acquisitions and current liability settlements.

**Distinction (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Gross Working Capital | Net Working Capital |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Total current assets of the firm | Excess of current assets over current liabilities |
| Perspective | Quantitative (deployment) | Qualitative (financing) |
| Formula | $\text{GWC} = \text{Current Assets}$ | $\text{NWC} = \text{CA} - \text{CL}$ |
| Interpretation | Focus on asset deployment | Focus on long-term financing adequacy |

---

**Question A2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Repeated Pattern]**
*What is the Operating Cycle? Write its formula.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Operating Cycle is the average time period elapsing between the acquisition of raw materials and the final realization of cash from the sale of finished goods. It represents the duration for which capital remains locked in the operating cycle of the firm.

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$\text{Operating Cycle} = \text{RM Storage Period} + \text{WIP Period} + \text{FG Storage Period} + \text{Receivables Period}$$

---

### Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Part B – Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper based on Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the various **factors determining the working capital requirement** of a business. (7 marks)

**(b)** From the following information, **estimate the working capital required** by the firm: (7 marks)

| Item | ₹ |
|---|---|
| Annual Sales | 24,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold (75% of sales) | 18,00,000 |
| Raw Material Consumption (60% of COGS) | 10,80,000 |
| Wages & Manufacturing Overheads | 4,50,000 |
| Administrative & Selling Expenses | 2,70,000 |

**Assumed periods:**
- Raw Material Storage: 2 months
- WIP Holding: 1 month (assume 100% RM and 50% wages locked in WIP)
- Finished Goods Storage: 1 month
- Receivables Collection: 2 months
- Payables Deferral: 1 month (on raw material only)

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The working capital requirement of a firm is influenced by a complex interplay of internal and external factors. Each industry and firm has unique working capital needs based on its operational characteristics.

**Detailed Factors (6 marks — allocate ~0.5 to 1 mark per factor):**

1. **Nature of Business:** Manufacturing firms require higher WC due to the conversion of raw materials through multiple stages. Service firms (e.g., IT, consulting) require minimal WC. Trading firms fall in between.

2. **Size of the Business:** Larger firms typically have larger absolute WC but proportionately smaller WC due to economies of scale and better bargaining power with suppliers.

3. **Production Cycle Length:** Firms with longer production cycles (e.g., shipbuilding, heavy machinery) lock up capital in WIP for extended periods, requiring higher WC.

4. **Credit Policy:** Firms offering lenient credit terms to customers build higher receivables, increasing WC. Conversely, firms obtaining generous supplier credit reduce WC.

5. **Seasonal Variations:** Firms facing seasonal demand (e.g., AC manufacturers, apparel) experience fluctuating WC needs and must arrange temporary financing during peak seasons.

6. **Growth and Expansion:** Rapidly growing firms require progressively higher WC to support larger inventory and receivable bases.

7. **Operating Efficiency:** Better inventory turnover ratios (e.g., through EOQ, JIT) and faster receivables collection reduce WC.

8. **Price Level Changes:** Inflation increases nominal WC requirements as the rupee value of current assets rises.

9. **Profit and Dividend Policy:** Higher profitability generates internal accruals, reducing dependence on external WC financing. Higher dividend payouts reduce internal sources.

10. **Other Factors:** Taxation policies, market competition, technological changes, and government regulations also influence WC needs.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks (Full Numerical Solution)

**Step 1: Compute Annual Operational Values (1 mark)**

```
Raw Material Consumption           = ₹10,80,000
Wages + Manufacturing Overheads   = ₹4,50,000
Cost of Goods Sold                 = ₹18,00,000
Sales                              = ₹24,00,000
```

**Step 2: Compute Raw Material Inventory (1 mark)**

$$\text{RM Inventory} = \frac{10{,}80{,}000 \times 2}{12} = ₹1{,}80{,}000$$

**Step 3: Compute WIP Inventory (2 marks)**

RM embedded in WIP for 1 month:

$$\text{WIP}_{\text{RM}} = \frac{10{,}80{,}000 \times 1}{12} = ₹90{,}000$$

Conversion cost (50% of wages considered locked in WIP):

$$\text{WIP}_{\text{Conversion}} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.50 \times 1}{12} = ₹18{,}750$$

$$\text{Total WIP} = 90{,}000 + 18{,}750 = ₹1{,}08{,}750$$

**Step 4: Compute Finished Goods Inventory (1 mark)**

$$\text{FG Inventory} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000 \times 1}{12} = ₹1{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 5: Compute Receivables (1 mark)**

$$\text{Receivables} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000 \times 2}{12} = ₹4{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6: Compute Payables Deduction (0.5 marks)**

$$\text{Payables} = \frac{10{,}80{,}000 \times 1}{12} = ₹90{,}000$$

**Step 7: Net Working Capital Aggregation (0.5 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net WC} &= 1{,}80{,}000 + 1{,}08{,}750 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 - 90{,}000 \\
&= ₹7{,}48{,}750
\end{aligned}$$

**Estimated Working Capital Required = ₹7,48,750**

---

### Part B – Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Alternative Question Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **sources of working capital financing** with suitable examples. Differentiate between **spontaneous, negotiated, and internal sources**. (7 marks)

**(b)** The following information relates to **X Ltd.**: (7 marks)

| Item | ₹ |
|---|---|
| Current Assets | 8,00,000 |
| Current Liabilities | 4,00,000 |
| Inventory | 2,50,000 |
| Cash and Bank Balance | 80,000 |

**Compute and interpret:**
1. Current Ratio
2. Quick Ratio
3. Cash Ratio
4. Working Capital Turnover (assume Net Credit Sales = ₹20,00,000)
5. Net Working Capital

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Working capital can be financed through multiple sources, classified into three categories based on their origin and negotiation requirements.

**Three Categories of Working Capital Sources (6 marks):**

**1. Spontaneous Sources (2 marks):**
These arise **automatically** from the firm's ordinary business operations and do not require explicit negotiation. The firm obtains financing simply by transacting.
- **Trade Credit (Accounts Payable):** Credit extended by suppliers for raw materials purchased. For example, purchasing raw material on 30-day credit.
- **Accrued Expenses:** Wages and salaries accrued but not yet paid (e.g., monthly salary payable on the 1st of the next month).
- **Outstanding Expenses:** Expenses incurred but unpaid, such as electricity bills, taxes.

**Advantage:** Free, automatic, and increases with sales volume.

**2. Negotiated Sources (2 marks):**
These require **explicit negotiation** with banks, financial institutions, or capital markets.
- **Bank Loans:** Short-term unsecured loans repayable within one year.
- **Cash Credit / Overdraft:** A flexible arrangement allowing the firm to withdraw up to a pre-approved limit.
- **Commercial Paper (CP):** Unsecured short-term promissory notes issued by large, creditworthy firms in the money market.
- **Factoring:** Selling accounts receivable to a factor at a discount to obtain immediate cash.
- **Banker's Acceptance:** A short-term credit instrument guaranteed by a bank.

**3. Internal Sources (2 marks):**
These are generated **internally** from the firm's operations without external borrowing.
- **Retained Earnings:** Profits not distributed as dividends, reinvested in the business.
- **Depreciation Provision:** A non-cash expense that retains cash within the firm.
- **Tax Provisions:** Tax amounts set aside but not yet paid.
- **Sale of Unproductive Assets:** Disposing of idle machinery or investments.

**Conclusion:**
A balanced working capital strategy combines all three sources — relying primarily on spontaneous and internal sources, and using negotiated sources for temporary needs.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks (Full Numerical Solution)

**Given Data:**

```
Current Assets (CA)        = ₹8,00,000
Current Liabilities (CL)   = ₹4,00,000
Inventory                  = ₹2,50,000
Cash and Bank Balance      = ₹80,000
Net Credit Sales           = ₹20,00,000
```

**Computation 1: Current Ratio (1 mark)**

$$\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{\text{CA}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 2.00 : 1$$

**Computation 2: Quick Ratio (1.5 marks)**

Quick Assets = Current Assets − Inventory = 8,00,000 − 2,50,000 = ₹5,50,000

$$\text{Quick Ratio} = \frac{5{,}50{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 1.375 : 1$$

**Computation 3: Cash Ratio (1 mark)**

$$\text{Cash Ratio} = \frac{\text{Cash and Bank Balance}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{80{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 0.20 : 1$$

**Computation 4: Working Capital Turnover (1.5 marks)**

Net Working Capital = CA − CL = 8,00,000 − 4,00,000 = ₹4,00,000

$$\text{WC Turnover} = \frac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{NWC}} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 5.0 \text{ times}$$

**Computation 5: Net Working Capital (1 mark)**

$$\text{NWC} = \text{CA} - \text{CL} = ₹4{,}00{,}000$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
- The Current Ratio of **2:1** matches the conventional benchmark — the firm has adequate short-term solvency.
- The Quick Ratio of **1.375:1** exceeds the benchmark of 1:1 — good liquidity even without inventory liquidation.
- The Cash Ratio of **0.20:1** indicates modest cash reserves relative to short-term obligations.
- The WC Turnover of **5 times** suggests efficient utilization of working capital — every ₹1 of NWC supports ₹5 of sales.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to deduct Payables** in WC estimation problems — this is the **single most common error**. Always remember: Payables REDUCE net WC, they don't add to it.
>
> 2. **Confusing Operating Cycle with Cash Conversion Cycle** — these are *not* the same. OC is gross time; CCC is OC minus the payables deferral period.
>
> 3. **Misapplying Quick Ratio formula** — students often forget to exclude **prepaid expenses** along with inventory from current assets. Strict Quick Assets = Cash + Marketable Securities + Receivables only.
>
> 4. **Confusing Gross vs. Net Working Capital terminology** — KTU examiners expect precise language. "Working capital" without qualifier in KTU textbooks usually means **Net Working Capital (NWC)**.
>
> 5. **Missing the WIP conversion component** — in WC estimation, students often compute WIP using only raw material value, forgetting that wages and overheads are also partially locked in WIP.
>
> 6. **Ignoring units and time periods** — converting annual figures to monthly holding requires division by 12; to daily, division by 365. Mixing units causes answer mismatches.
>
> 7. **EOQ miscalculation trap** — using total carrying cost instead of per-unit carrying cost inflates EOQ erroneously.
>
> 8. **No final interpretation** — in ratio problems, simply computing the ratio without managerial commentary costs at least 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Working Capital Management**

- [ ] **Working Capital** = Current Assets − Current Liabilities (Net WC view, KTU-preferred)
- [ ] **Gross WC** = Total Current Assets (deployment view)
- [ ] **Operating Cycle** = RM + WIP + FG + Receivables (all in days)
- [ ] **Cash Conversion Cycle** = Operating Cycle − Payables Deferral Period
- [ ] **Permanent WC** = Minimum always-required current assets; **Temporary WC** = Seasonal excess
- [ ] **Key Liquidity Ratios:** Current Ratio (ideal 2:1), Quick Ratio (ideal 1:1), Cash Ratio (most conservative)
- [ ] **WC Turnover** = Net Sales / Net Working Capital (efficiency metric)
- [ ] **EOQ Formula:** $\sqrt{2AO / C}$ (where A=annual demand, O=order cost, C=carrying cost/unit)
- [ ] **Safety Stock** = (Max usage − Avg usage) × Lead Time
- [ ] **Reorder Level** = Avg usage × Lead Time + Safety Stock
- [ ] **Baumol Optimal Cash** = $\sqrt{2bT / i}$ (cash management model)
- [ ] **Sources of WC:** Spontaneous (trade credit), Negotiated (bank loans, CP, factoring), Internal (retained earnings, depreciation)
- [ ] **Factors affecting WC:** Nature of business, size, production cycle, credit policy, seasonality, growth, inflation, dividend policy, operating efficiency
- [ ] **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Estimate WC by aggregating phase holdings and deducting payables
- [ ] **Excess WC** → idle assets, low ROI; **Inadequate WC** → illiquidity, operational disruption
- [ ] **Kingfisher Airlines** is a classic KTU case study on WC collapse despite profitability
- [ ] **Always deduct Payables** in WC estimation — common trap
- [ ] **Always interpret ratios** — computation without commentary is incomplete

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ratio Analysis** is a systematic process of evaluating a business entity's financial statements by computing meaningful mathematical relationships between selected financial variables drawn from the **Balance Sheet** and the **Profit & Loss Account**. It is the most widely used tool of **Financial Statement Analysis** for interpreting accounting data in both absolute and relative terms.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, ratio analysis is classified under the broader domain of **Management Accounting techniques for financial decision-making**, serving as a quantitative backbone for liquidity assessment, solvency testing, operating efficiency measurement, and profitability evaluation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Orientation):**
> "Ratio Analysis is the process of determining and interpreting numerical relationships based on financial statements. It is a tool of financial analysis used for inter-firm and intra-firm comparison, and for assessing the overall financial health of an enterprise."

### 1.2 Types of Ratios — Broad Classification

For KTU examination purposes, ratios are systematically classified into **four master categories**, each addressing a distinct dimension of business performance.

| S. No. | Category of Ratio | Primary Question Answered |
|:------:|:------------------|:--------------------------|
| 1 | **Liquidity Ratios** | Can the firm pay its short-term obligations? |
| 2 | **Solvency / Leverage Ratios** | Can the firm survive in the long run with its debt structure? |
| 3 | **Activity / Turnover Ratios** | How efficiently are assets being utilized? |
| 4 | **Profitability Ratios** | How well is the firm generating returns? |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Doctor's Checkup" Analogy for Ratio Analysis**
>
> Imagine you go to a doctor for a health check-up. The doctor does not just measure one thing; she measures:
> - **Blood pressure & sugar level** (short-term stability) → **Liquidity Ratios**
> - **Cholesterol & long-term cardiac health** (long-term survival) → **Solvency Ratios**
> - **Energy & stamina during exercise** (efficiency of organs) → **Activity Ratios**
> - **Overall fitness score & weight management** (how well the body produces results) → **Profitability Ratios**
>
> Just like these four tests together give the doctor a complete health picture, the **four types of ratios** together give the financial manager a complete view of the firm's "financial fitness."

### 1.4 Why Ratios Are Computed — The "Why" Behind the Tool

Ratios are never computed in isolation; they are **comparative by nature**. The four pillars of comparison for KTU-level answers are:

1. **Intra-firm Comparison** — Comparing ratios of the current year with previous years (Trend Analysis).
2. **Inter-firm Comparison** — Comparing ratios of the firm with industry averages or competitors.
3. **Comparison with Standards** — Comparing against pre-set industrial or ideal benchmarks.
4. **Comparison with Projected Figures** — Comparing actual performance with budgeted targets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single ratio by itself is **meaningless**. A Current Ratio of 2:1 in 2020 and 2:1 in 2024 may tell very different stories depending on industry norms, inflation, and business growth.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Liquidity Ratios — The Short-Term Survival Toolkit

**Purpose:** Measure the firm's ability to meet its **short-term obligations** (current liabilities) using its **short-term assets** (current assets).

#### 2.1.1 Current Ratio (Working Capital Ratio)

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. It is the most fundamental liquidity measure.
2. It expresses the relationship between **Current Assets (CA)** and **Current Liabilities (CL)**.
3. Numerator includes all assets convertible into cash within **12 months**.
4. Denominator includes all obligations due within **12 months**.

**Standard Benchmark:** **2 : 1** is conventionally considered ideal. A ratio below 1 indicates potential insolvency risk; a ratio too high indicates idle current assets.

#### 2.1.2 Quick Ratio (Acid Test Ratio)

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. It is a **stricter** test of liquidity than the Current Ratio.
2. It **excludes inventory and prepaid expenses** because they are the least liquid current assets.
3. Sometimes also excludes store and spares.
4. It measures the ability to pay current liabilities using **truly liquid assets**.

**Standard Benchmark:** **1 : 1** is considered ideal.

#### 2.1.3 Absolute Liquid Ratio (Cash Ratio)

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. The most conservative liquidity measure.
2. Considers only **Cash + Bank + Marketable Securities** (near-cash assets) in the numerator.
3. Used to evaluate the firm's ability to pay current liabilities *immediately*.

**Standard Benchmark:** **0.5 : 1** or **1 : 2** is generally accepted.

#### 2.1.4 Basic Defense Interval (Operating Cash Flow Ratio)

**Conceptual Logic:**
Measures how many days the firm can operate using its available liquid assets without additional cash inflow.

### 2.2 Solvency / Leverage Ratios — The Long-Term Survival Toolkit

**Purpose:** Measure the firm's ability to survive in the long run and assess the proportion of debt in the capital structure.

#### 2.2.1 Debt-Equity Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. Measures the relationship between **Long-term Debt (External Equities)** and **Shareholders' Funds (Internal Equities)**.
2. Indicates the relative claims of outsiders vs. owners against the firm's assets.
3. Higher ratio → higher financial risk; Lower ratio → more financial stability.

#### 2.2.2 Debt-Total Assets Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. Shows what proportion of total assets is financed by debt.
2. Useful for assessing overall leverage.

#### 2.2.3 Proprietary Ratio (Equity Ratio)

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. The inverse perspective of the Debt-Equity Ratio.
2. Shows what proportion of total assets is financed by shareholders' funds.

#### 2.2.4 Interest Coverage Ratio (Times Interest Earned)

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. Measures the firm's ability to pay interest on long-term debt from operating profits.
2. **EBIT** is Earnings Before Interest and Taxes.

#### 2.2.5 Fixed Assets to Net Worth Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
Measures the extent to which shareholders' funds are invested in fixed (non-current) assets.

### 2.3 Activity / Turnover Ratios — The Efficiency Toolkit

**Purpose:** Measure how efficiently the firm utilizes its various assets.

#### 2.3.1 Inventory / Stock Turnover Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. Indicates how many times inventory is replaced during a period.
2. **Average Inventory** = (Opening Stock + Closing Stock) / 2.

#### 2.3.2 Debtors / Receivables Turnover Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. Indicates how quickly the firm collects cash from credit sales.
2. **Average Collection Period** = (365 / Debtors Turnover Ratio) or (12 / Monthly Turnover).

#### 2.3.3 Creditors / Payables Turnover Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. Indicates how quickly the firm pays its suppliers.
2. **Average Payment Period** = (365 / Creditors Turnover Ratio).

#### 2.3.4 Fixed Assets Turnover Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
Measures the efficiency of fixed asset utilization in generating sales.

#### 2.3.5 Working Capital Turnover Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
Measures the efficiency of working capital utilization in generating sales.

#### 2.3.6 Total Assets Turnover Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
Measures the overall efficiency of asset utilization.

### 2.4 Profitability Ratios — The Bottom-Line Toolkit

**Purpose:** Measure the firm's overall efficiency in generating profits from its operations and investments.

#### 2.4.1 Gross Profit Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
Indicates the margin left after covering **Cost of Goods Sold**. Reflects production/purchasing efficiency and pricing policy.

#### 2.4.2 Net Profit Ratio (Net Profit Margin)

**Conceptual Logic:**
The ultimate measure of operational efficiency — profit left from every ₹100 of sales after all expenses.

#### 2.4.3 Operating Profit Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
Profit from **core operations** before interest and taxes. Indicates pure operational efficiency.

#### 2.4.4 Return on Capital Employed (ROCE)

**Conceptual Logic:**
The single most important profitability ratio. It shows the return earned on **total capital employed** (Equity + Long-term Debt).

#### 2.4.5 Return on Equity (ROE) / Return on Shareholders' Investment

**Conceptual Logic:**
Measures the return earned on shareholders' funds only.

#### 2.4.6 Earnings Per Share (EPS)

**Conceptual Logic:**
Profit attributable to each equity share. Critical for stock market valuation.

#### 2.4.7 Dividend Per Share (DPS) and Dividend Payout Ratio

**Conceptual Logic:**
DPS = Total Dividend ÷ Number of Equity Shares. Payout Ratio = DPS / EPS.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 Examinations**
> Use `\vert` (not `|`) for all absolute value notations in exam scripts.

| **Category** | **Ratio Name** | **Formula** | **Ideal Benchmark** |
|:-------------|:---------------|:------------|:-------------------:|
| Liquidity | Current Ratio | $\text{Current Assets} \div \text{Current Liabilities}$ | **2 : 1** |
| Liquidity | Quick Ratio | $(\text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Exp.}) \div \text{CL}$ | **1 : 1** |
| Liquidity | Absolute Liquid Ratio | $(\text{Cash} + \text{Bank} + \text{Marketable Sec.}) \div \text{CL}$ | **0.5 : 1** |
| Solvency | Debt-Equity Ratio | $\text{Long-term Debt} \div \text{Shareholders' Funds}$ | $\le$ **1 : 1** |
| Solvency | Proprietary Ratio | $\text{Shareholders' Funds} \div \text{Total Assets}$ | Higher is better |
| Solvency | Interest Coverage Ratio | $\text{EBIT} \div \text{Interest}$ | $\ge$ **3 times** |
| Activity | Inventory Turnover | $\text{COGS} \div \text{Average Inventory}$ | Industry specific |
| Activity | Debtors Turnover | $\text{Net Credit Sales} \div \text{Avg. Debtors}$ | Industry specific |
| Activity | Creditors Turnover | $\text{Net Credit Purchases} \div \text{Avg. Creditors}$ | Industry specific |
| Activity | Fixed Assets Turnover | $\text{Net Sales} \div \text{Net Fixed Assets}$ | Higher is better |
| Activity | Working Capital Turnover | $\text{Net Sales} \div \text{Working Capital}$ | Higher is better |
| Profitability | Gross Profit Ratio | $(\text{Gross Profit} \div \text{Net Sales}) \times 100$ | Industry specific |
| Profitability | Net Profit Ratio | $(\text{Net Profit} \div \text{Net Sales}) \times 100$ | Industry specific |
| Profitability | Operating Profit Ratio | $(\text{Operating Profit} \div \text{Net Sales}) \times 100$ | Industry specific |
| Profitability | ROCE | $(\text{EBIT} \div \text{Capital Employed}) \times 100$ | Higher is better |
| Profitability | ROE | $(\text{Net Profit} \div \text{Shareholders' Equity}) \times 100$ | Higher is better |
| Profitability | EPS | $(\text{Net Profit} - \text{Preference Div.}) \div \text{No. of Equity Shares}$ | Higher is better |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

### 3.1 The Fundamental Derivation Logic of Every Ratio

All financial ratios follow a **unified mathematical template** rooted in the **DuPont Identity** and the **Matching Principle** of accounting.

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{Relevant Financial Variable from P\&L}}{\text{Relevant Financial Variable from Balance Sheet (or P\&L)}}
$$

The **denominator** is always the **base or driver**, while the **numerator** is the **outcome or result** generated per unit of the base.

#### 3.1.1 Derivation of Current Ratio from Working Capital Concept

**Step 1 — Definition of Working Capital:**

$$
\text{Working Capital (WC)} = \text{Current Assets (CA)} - \text{Current Liabilities (CL)}
$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by Current Liabilities to obtain a relative measure:**

$$
\frac{\text{WC}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{\text{CA} - \text{CL}}{\text{CL}}
$$

**Step 3 — Split the right-hand side into two terms:**

$$
\frac{\text{WC}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{\text{CA}}{\text{CL}} - \frac{\text{CL}}{\text{CL}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the second term:**

$$
\frac{\text{WC}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{\text{CA}}{\text{CL}} - 1
$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging to isolate the Current Ratio:**

$$
\frac{\text{CA}}{\text{CL}} = 1 + \frac{\text{WC}}{\text{CL}}
$$

This derivation proves that the Current Ratio is a **direct mathematical extension of working capital**, scaled to unity. Hence, Current Ratio $\ge 1$ implies **positive working capital**, and Current Ratio $< 1$ implies **negative working capital**.

#### 3.1.2 Derivation of Debt-Equity Ratio's Link to Capital Structure

**Step 1 — Total Capital Employed:**

$$
\text{Capital Employed} = \text{Equity (E)} + \text{Long-term Debt (D)}
$$

**Step 2 — The Debt-Equity Ratio (D/E) expresses debt's share of capital structure:**

$$
\text{D/E} = \frac{\text{D}}{\text{E}}
$$

**Step 3 — Proportion of Debt in Capital Employed:**

$$
\text{Proportion of Debt} = \frac{\text{D}}{\text{D} + \text{E}}
$$

**Step 4 — Expressing the debt proportion in terms of D/E:**

$$
\frac{\text{D}}{\text{D} + \text{E}} = \frac{\text{D/E}}{1 + \text{D/E}}
$$

**Step 5 — For example, if D/E = 1, then:**

$$
\frac{1}{1+1} = 0.5 = 50\%
$$

Therefore, a D/E ratio of 1:1 means debt forms **50%** of total capital employed.

### 3.2 Comprehensive Solved Example — KTU 2024 Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **PROBLEM [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**
>
> From the following information, compute: (a) Current Ratio, (b) Quick Ratio, (c) Absolute Liquid Ratio, (d) Debt-Equity Ratio, (e) Inventory Turnover Ratio, (f) ROCE.
>
> - **Equity Share Capital:** ₹ 5,00,000
> - **12% Preference Share Capital:** ₹ 2,00,000
> - **12% Debentures:** ₹ 3,00,000
> - **Trade Payables (Creditors):** ₹ 1,50,000
> - **Outstanding Expenses:** ₹ 50,000
> - **Inventory:** ₹ 2,00,000
> - **Trade Receivables (Debtors):** ₹ 1,80,000
> - **Cash at Bank:** ₹ 90,000
> - **Cash in Hand:** ₹ 10,000
> - **Prepaid Expenses:** ₹ 20,000
> - **Land & Building:** ₹ 4,00,000
> - **Plant & Machinery:** ₹ 3,00,000
> - **Net Sales:** ₹ 12,00,000
> - **Cost of Goods Sold:** ₹ 8,00,000
> - **Net Profit (after tax):** ₹ 1,50,000
> - **Opening Inventory:** ₹ 1,50,000
> - **Interest on Debentures:** ₹ 36,000
> - **Income Tax Rate:** 30%

---

#### Step 1 — Compute Current Assets (CA)

$$
\text{CA} = \text{Inventory} + \text{Debtors} + \text{Cash at Bank} + \text{Cash in Hand} + \text{Prepaid Expenses}
$$

$$
\text{CA} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Current Liabilities (CL)

$$
\text{CL} = \text{Trade Payables} + \text{Outstanding Expenses}
$$

$$
\text{CL} = 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute (a) Current Ratio

$$
\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{\text{CA}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 2.5 : 1
$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹1 of short-term liability, the firm has ₹2.50 of short-term assets. The ideal benchmark is 2:1, so the firm has **adequate short-term liquidity**.

#### Step 4 — Compute (b) Quick Ratio

$$
\text{Quick Assets} = \text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Expenses}
$$

$$
\text{Quick Assets} = 5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000 = 2{,}80{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Quick Ratio} = \frac{2{,}80{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 1.4 : 1
$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹1 of current liability, the firm has ₹1.40 of *truly liquid* assets. The ideal benchmark is 1:1, so the firm has **strong immediate liquidity**.

#### Step 5 — Compute (c) Absolute Liquid Ratio

$$
\text{Absolute Liquid Assets} = \text{Cash at Bank} + \text{Cash in Hand}
$$

$$
\text{Absolute Liquid Assets} = 90{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Absolute Liquid Ratio} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 0.5 : 1
$$

**Interpretation:** This matches the standard benchmark of 0.5:1. The firm can pay **50% of current liabilities immediately** using cash.

#### Step 6 — Compute (d) Debt-Equity Ratio

$$
\text{Long-term Debt} = \text{12\% Debentures} = 3{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Shareholders' Funds} = \text{Equity Share Capital} + \text{Preference Share Capital}
$$

$$
\text{Shareholders' Funds} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Debt-Equity Ratio} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} = 0.43 : 1
$$

**Interpretation:** The firm's debt is **less than half of shareholders' funds**, indicating a conservative and safe capital structure.

#### Step 7 — Compute (e) Inventory Turnover Ratio

$$
\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{\text{Opening} + \text{Closing Inventory}}{2} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000}{2} = 1{,}75{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Inventory Turnover Ratio} = \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg. Inventory}} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1{,}75{,}000} = 4.57 \text{ times}
$$

**Interpretation:** Inventory is replaced approximately **4.57 times per year**, meaning the average inventory holding period is about **80 days** (365 ÷ 4.57).

#### Step 8 — Compute (f) Return on Capital Employed (ROCE)

**Step 8a — Compute EBIT:**

$$
\text{Net Profit after Tax (NPAT)} = 1{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\text{NPAT} = \text{EBIT} - \text{Interest} - \text{Tax}
$$

$$
\text{EBIT} = \text{NPAT} + \text{Interest} + \text{Tax}
$$

First, compute tax:

$$
\text{Profit before Tax (PBT)} = \frac{\text{NPAT}}{1 - t} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{0.70} = 2{,}14{,}285.71
$$

Then EBIT:

$$
\text{EBIT} = 2{,}14{,}285.71 + 36{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}285.71
$$

**Step 8b — Compute Capital Employed:**

$$
\text{Capital Employed} = \text{Equity} + \text{Pref. Capital} + \text{Debentures} + \text{Trade Payables} + \text{Outstanding Exp.}
$$

$$
\text{Capital Employed} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 8c — Compute ROCE:**

$$
\text{ROCE} = \left(\frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Capital Employed}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{ROCE} = \left(\frac{2{,}50{,}285.71}{12{,}00{,}000}\right) \times 100 = 20.86\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The firm earns approximately **20.86% on every ₹100 of capital employed**, which is a strong return.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — The Ratio Computation Pipeline

This block diagram shows **how raw financial data flows** through the analysis pipeline to produce actionable ratio outputs for management decision-making.

```mermaid
graph LR
    raw1["Balance Sheet Data"] --> pro1["Identify CA and CL"]
    raw2["Profit and Loss Data"] --> pro2["Identify Sales COGS NP"]
    raw1 --> pro3["Identify Debt and Equity"]
    raw2 --> pro4["Identify EBIT and Interest"]

    pro1 --> out1["Liquidity Ratios"]
    pro2 --> out1
    pro3 --> out2["Solvency Ratios"]
    pro4 --> out2
    pro2 --> out3["Profitability Ratios"]
    pro1 --> out4["Activity Ratios"]
    pro2 --> out4

    out1 --> final["Comparative Analysis"]
    out2 --> final
    out3 --> final
    out4 --> final

    final --> dec["Management Decision Making"]
```

### 4.3 The DuPont Triangle — Visualizing Inter-Relationship of Ratios

The following diagram illustrates how **ROCE** is the apex ratio, derived from three sub-components: **Net Profit Margin**, **Asset Turnover**, and **Capital Structure (Leverage)**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    apex["ROCE Apex Ratio"]
    npm["Net Profit Margin NPM"]
    ato["Asset Turnover ATO"]
    lev["Capital Structure Leverage"]

    apex --> npm
    apex --> ato
    apex --> lev

    npm --> def1["NP divided by Sales"]
    ato --> def2["Sales divided by Assets"]
    lev --> def3["Total Assets divided by Equity"]
```

**DuPont Identity (Mathematical Form):**

$$
\text{ROCE} = \text{Net Profit Margin} \times \text{Asset Turnover} \times \text{Leverage}
$$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between Current Ratio and Quick Ratio. State the ideal benchmarks for each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Current Ratio | Quick Ratio |
|:------|:--------------|:------------|
| Formula | $\text{CA} \div \text{CL}$ | $(\text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Exp.}) \div \text{CL}$ |
| Strictness | Less strict | Stricter test of liquidity |
| Ideal | **2 : 1** | **1 : 1** |
| Purpose | Broad short-term solvency | Immediate liquidity |

**(1 mark** for formula of each, **0.5 marks** for strictness difference, **0.5 marks** each for benchmarks — total 3 marks.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"What is the significance of the Debt-Equity Ratio? What does a ratio of 0.5:1 indicate?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Debt-Equity Ratio measures the proportion of long-term debt relative to shareholders' funds. It indicates the **extent of financial leverage** and **long-term solvency risk** of the firm.

A ratio of **0.5:1** means that for every ₹1 of shareholders' funds, the firm uses **₹0.50 of long-term debt**. This is a **conservative capital structure**, indicating lower financial risk and higher creditworthiness.

**(1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for significance, **1 mark** for interpretation of 0.5:1.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Ratio Computation**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the four major categories of ratios used in financial statement analysis. List **any two ratios** under each category along with their formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** From the following data of M/s Techtronics Pvt. Ltd., compute: **(i)** Current Ratio, **(ii)** Quick Ratio, **(iii)** Inventory Turnover Ratio, **(iv)** Debt-Equity Ratio, **(v)** Gross Profit Ratio, and **(vi)** Return on Capital Employed. **(7 Marks)**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|:-----|------------:|
| Equity Share Capital | 10,00,000 |
| 10% Preference Share Capital | 5,00,000 |
| 11% Debentures | 5,00,000 |
| Trade Payables | 3,00,000 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 1,00,000 |
| Inventory (Closing) | 4,00,000 |
| Trade Receivables | 3,60,000 |
| Cash at Bank | 1,80,000 |
| Cash in Hand | 20,000 |
| Prepaid Expenses | 40,000 |
| Land & Building | 8,00,000 |
| Plant & Machinery | 6,00,000 |
| Net Sales | 24,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 16,00,000 |
| Net Profit (after 30% tax) | 2,10,000 |
| Opening Inventory | 3,00,000 |
| Interest on Debentures | 55,000 |

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Explanation of Four Categories (7 Marks)**

**(1) Liquidity Ratios (1.75 Marks):** Measure the ability to meet short-term obligations.
- Current Ratio: $\text{CA} \div \text{CL}$
- Quick Ratio: $(\text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid}) \div \text{CL}$

**(2) Solvency Ratios (1.75 Marks):** Measure long-term financial stability and leverage.
- Debt-Equity Ratio: $\text{Long-term Debt} \div \text{Shareholders' Funds}$
- Interest Coverage Ratio: $\text{EBIT} \div \text{Interest}$

**(3) Activity Ratios (1.75 Marks):** Measure efficiency of asset utilization.
- Inventory Turnover: $\text{COGS} \div \text{Average Inventory}$
- Debtors Turnover: $\text{Credit Sales} \div \text{Average Debtors}$

**(4) Profitability Ratios (1.75 Marks):** Measure profit-generating capacity.
- Net Profit Ratio: $(\text{NP} \div \text{Sales}) \times 100$
- ROCE: $(\text{EBIT} \div \text{Capital Employed}) \times 100$

---

**Part (b) — Computation of Ratios (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute Current Assets (CA):**

$$
\text{CA} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Current Liabilities (CL):**

$$
\text{CL} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000
$$

**(i) Current Ratio:**

$$
\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 2.5 : 1
$$

**(ii) Quick Ratio:**

$$
\text{Quick Assets} = 10{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 5{,}60{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Quick Ratio} = \frac{5{,}60{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 1.4 : 1
$$

**(iii) Inventory Turnover Ratio:**

$$
\text{Avg. Inventory} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000}{2} = 3{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Inventory Turnover} = \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} = 4.57 \text{ times}
$$

**(iv) Debt-Equity Ratio:**

$$
\text{Long-term Debt} = 5{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Shareholders' Funds} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{D/E Ratio} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} = 0.33 : 1
$$

**(v) Gross Profit Ratio:**

$$
\text{Gross Profit} = \text{Sales} - \text{COGS} = 24{,}00{,}000 - 16{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Gross Profit Ratio} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 33.33\%
$$

**(vi) Return on Capital Employed (ROCE):**

First compute EBIT:

$$
\text{PBT} = \frac{\text{NPAT}}{0.70} = \frac{2{,}10{,}000}{0.70} = 3{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{EBIT} = \text{PBT} + \text{Interest} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 55{,}000 = 3{,}55{,}000
$$

Compute Capital Employed:

$$
\text{Capital Employed} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ROCE} = \frac{3{,}55{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 14.79\%
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **limitations of Ratio Analysis** in financial decision-making. How can these limitations be overcome? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following are the ratios extracted from the books of M/s FutureTech Industries:
- Current Ratio = 2.5
- Quick Ratio = 1.2
- Debt-Equity Ratio = 0.6
- Inventory Turnover = 6 times
- Debtors Turnover = 8 times
- Net Profit Ratio = 10%
- Return on Capital Employed = 18%

If Net Sales = ₹ 20,00,000 and Cost of Goods Sold = ₹ 14,00,000, compute: **(i)** Current Assets, **(ii)** Closing Inventory, **(iii)** Long-term Debt, **(iv)** Trade Receivables, **(v)** Net Profit, **(vi)** Capital Employed. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Limitations of Ratio Analysis (7 Marks)**

1. **No Standard Benchmarks (1 Mark):** Different industries have different ideal ratios; a single benchmark cannot apply universally.

2. **Window Dressing (1 Mark):** Companies can manipulate financial statements (e.g., showing fake sales) to inflate ratios.

3. **Ignores Price Level Changes (1 Mark):** Ratios are based on historical cost accounting and ignore inflation.

4. **Qualitative Factors Ignored (1 Mark):** Ratios capture only quantitative aspects; factors like management quality, brand value, and labour morale are excluded.

5. **Limited Comparability (1 Mark):** Different firms may use different accounting policies (e.g., FIFO vs. LIFO), making inter-firm comparison invalid.

6. **Based on Past Data (1 Mark):** Ratios reflect historical performance and may not predict future conditions.

7. **Overcoming Limitations (1 Mark):** Use ratios alongside **trend analysis, common-size statements, fund flow analysis, cash flow analysis, and qualitative factors** for holistic decision-making.

---

**Part (b) — Backward Computation (7 Marks)**

**(i) Current Assets:**
Working Capital formula:

$$
\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{\text{CA}}{\text{CL}} = 2.5
$$

Since Quick Ratio = 1.2, we have:

$$
\frac{\text{Quick Assets}}{\text{CL}} = 1.2
$$

Subtract Quick from Current:

$$
\frac{\text{CA} - \text{Quick Assets}}{\text{CL}} = 2.5 - 1.2 = 1.3
$$

This implies (CA − Quick Assets) = 1.3 × CL. The difference between Current Assets and Quick Assets equals Inventory + Prepaid Expenses.

To compute, we use Sales and COGS:

**First, find Current Liabilities using the Current Ratio constraint along with other data:**

Inventory Turnover = 6 times, COGS = ₹14,00,000:

$$
\text{Avg. Inventory} = \frac{14{,}00{,}000}{6} = 2{,}33{,}333
$$

Debtors Turnover = 8 times, Sales = ₹20,00,000 (assuming all credit):

$$
\text{Avg. Debtors} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{8} = 2{,}50{,}000
$$

Net Profit = 10% × 20,00,000 = ₹2,00,000.

ROCE = 18%:

$$
\text{Capital Employed} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{0.18}
$$

Assuming for simplicity EBIT ≈ Net Profit + Tax + Interest, and using simplified assumption: Capital Employed = (2,00,000 / 0.10) × ... Let us use direct approach.

Given ROCE = 18%, NP = ₹2,00,000 (assumed pre-interest):

$$
\text{If EBIT} = 2{,}00{,}000 \Rightarrow \text{Capital Employed} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{0.18} = 11{,}11{,}111
$$

Long-term Debt from D/E = 0.6:

$$
\frac{\text{Debt}}{15} = 0.6 \Rightarrow \text{Debt} = \text{Equity} \times 0.6
$$

Cap Employed = Equity + Debt = Equity × 1.6, so:

$$
\text{Equity} = \frac{11{,}11{,}111}{1.6} = 6{,}94{,}444
$$

$$
\text{Long-term Debt} = 0.6 \times 6{,}94{,}444 = 4{,}16{,}667
$$

**(v) Net Profit:**

$$
\text{Net Profit} = 10\% \times 20{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students commonly lose marks in three specific areas on this topic:
> 1. **Confusing Average Inventory with Closing Inventory** — The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests whether students use the *average* (not closing) inventory in the turnover formula. Always compute: $(\text{Opening} + \text{Closing}) / 2$.
> 2. **Forgetting to include Outstanding Expenses in CL** — Many students treat only Trade Payables as current liabilities, ignoring accrued expenses. This understates CL and inflates the Current Ratio artificially.
> 3. **Including Preference Share Capital in Current Liabilities or Long-term Debt** — Preference shares are *shareholders' funds*, NOT debt. They go into the equity side for D/E ratio computation. Mistakenly putting them in debt inflates leverage and costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not converting NPAT to EBIT for ROCE** — ROCE uses EBIT, not Net Profit. Students must "gross up" the profit figure by adding back tax and interest.
> 5. **Ignoring the relationship between ratios** — KTU 2024 questions often ask for *comment* on the computed ratios. Merely writing numbers without an interpretive sentence (e.g., "This is higher than the ideal 2:1, indicating...") results in loss of 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 Module 2, Topic: Ratio Analysis - Types**

- **Liquidity Ratios** test **short-term survival**. Current Ratio = **2:1**, Quick Ratio = **1:1**, Absolute Liquid = **0.5:1**.
- **Solvency Ratios** test **long-term survival**. Debt-Equity is the primary metric.
- **Activity Ratios** test **efficiency** of asset use. Always use **average** values (not closing) for inventory, debtors, and creditors.
- **Profitability Ratios** test **bottom-line success**. ROCE uses **EBIT**; ROE uses **Net Profit**.
- **Current Assets** = Inventory + Debtors + Cash + Bank + Marketable Securities + Prepaid Expenses + Stores & Spares.
- **Current Liabilities** = Trade Payables + Outstanding Expenses + Short-term Loans + Bank Overdraft + Proposed Dividend.
- **Capital Employed** = Total Assets − Current Liabilities = Equity + Long-term Debt (Preference + Debentures).
- **EBIT** = Net Profit + Tax + Interest. **EBITDA** = EBIT + Depreciation + Amortization.
- **Shareholders' Funds** = Equity Share Capital + Preference Share Capital + Reserves & Surplus (NOT fictitious assets).
- A single ratio is **meaningless without comparison** — always compare with prior years, industry average, or projected figures.
- **EPS** is reported in ₹ per share; **ROE** and **ROCE** are reported as percentages.
- The **DuPont Identity** links ROCE to Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Leverage.
- The **Ideal Quick Ratio = 1:1** is a *rule of thumb*, not a legal or absolute standard.
- **Interest Coverage Ratio $\geq$ 3 times** is conventionally considered safe for debt servicing.
- **Higher turnover ratios** generally indicate better efficiency, but excessively high values may signal stock-outs or overly restrictive credit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computation and Interpretation of Financial Ratios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computation and Interpretation of Financial Ratios

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Financial Ratios** are quantitative metrics derived from financial statements (Balance Sheet, Profit & Loss Account, and Cash Flow Statement) used to evaluate the overall financial health, performance, and operational efficiency of a business enterprise. They express the mathematical relationship between two accounting variables, providing a standardized basis for **inter-firm comparison (benchmarking)**, **intra-firm trend analysis**, and **stakeholder decision-making**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET741) syllabus, ratio analysis is classified as a fundamental tool of **Management Accounting** that aids engineering managers in making informed financial decisions concerning working capital, capital budgeting, and cost control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** A financial ratio is meaningless in isolation. The **real value** of any ratio emerges only when it is compared against (a) prior years' figures of the same firm (Trend Analysis), (b) industry averages (Cross-Sectional Analysis), or (c) competitors' ratios (Benchmarking).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a doctor examining a patient. A single reading like "blood pressure = 120/80" is informative but not conclusive. The doctor compares it against **normal ranges**, **previous readings**, and **patient history** before diagnosing.

Financial ratios work the same way:
- The **Balance Sheet** is the skeleton (structural health → Solvency ratios).
- The **Profit & Loss Account** is the heartbeat (operational health → Profitability ratios).
- The **Cash Flow** is the bloodstream (liquidity health → Liquidity ratios).

Just as a doctor uses a **stethoscope** to interpret vital signs, a finance manager uses ratio analysis to interpret the **"vital signs" of a business**. An engineering manager can use these ratios to decide whether to launch a new product line, invest in machinery, or extend credit to a customer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standards / Conventions Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - All ratios are expressed as **pure numbers** (decimals) or **percentages (%)**.
> - Standard accounting figures: Sales, Net Profit, Assets are taken **after depreciation** unless otherwise stated.
> - **Average** = (Opening Balance + Closing Balance) / 2 — used for Assets, Inventory, and Debtors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Financial Ratios
Financial ratios are systematically classified into **five major categories** based on the aspect of business performance they measure:

1. **Liquidity Ratios** — Short-term solvency / ability to pay current obligations.
2. **Solvency / Leverage Ratios** — Long-term financial stability / debt structure.
3. **Activity / Efficiency / Turnover Ratios** — Effectiveness in utilizing assets.
4. **Profitability Ratios** — Ability to generate earnings.
5. **Market Value / Valuation Ratios** — Investor perception and market performance.

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### A. Liquidity Ratios
- **Why?** To measure the firm's ability to meet short-term liabilities (due within 12 months) using short-term assets.
- **How?** By comparing **Current Assets** (CA) and **Quick Assets** (QA) against **Current Liabilities** (CL).

#### B. Solvency Ratios
- **Why?** To assess long-term financial risk by examining the proportion of debt in the capital structure.
- **How?** By computing the ratio of **Total Debt** to **Total Equity/Assets**, and **Interest Coverage**.

#### C. Activity Ratios
- **Why?** To evaluate how efficiently a firm uses its assets (inventory, debtors, fixed assets) to generate sales.
- **How?** By comparing **Sales (or COGS)** with the corresponding **asset base** — higher turnover = better efficiency.

#### D. Profitability Ratios
- **Why?** To determine the firm's capacity to generate profits from sales, assets, or equity.
- **How?** By computing profit margins and **Return on Investment (ROI)** metrics.

#### E. Market Value Ratios
- **Why?** To capture the equity market's perception of the firm's value and growth prospects.
- **How?** By relating market price per share with earnings and book value.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Category** | **Ratio Name** | **Formula** | **Ideal / Standard Benchmark** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Liquidity** | Current Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | $\mathbf{2 : 1}$ | Ability to pay short-term debt |
| **Liquidity** | Quick / Acid Test Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Exp.}}{\text{CL}}$ | $\mathbf{1 : 1}$ | Strict liquidity test |
| **Liquidity** | Cash Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities}}{\text{CL}}$ | $\mathbf{0.5 : 1}$ | Most conservative liquidity measure |
| **Solvency** | Debt-Equity Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}$ | $\mathbf{\leq 1 : 1}$ (or 2:1) | Capital structure balance |
| **Solvency** | Debt-Total Assets Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | $\mathbf{\leq 0.5}$ | % of assets financed by debt |
| **Solvency** | Interest Coverage Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest Expense}}$ | $\mathbf{\geq 3 \text{ times}}$ | Capacity to pay interest |
| **Activity** | Inventory Turnover | $\dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Industry-specific (e.g., 6-8x) | Stock movement speed |
| **Activity** | Debtors Turnover | $\dfrac{\text{Credit Sales}}{\text{Average Debtors}}$ | Industry-specific | Collection efficiency |
| **Activity** | Average Collection Period | $\dfrac{365 \text{ (or 12 months)}}{\text{Debtors Turnover}}$ | Lower is better | Days to collect receivables |
| **Activity** | Asset Turnover | $\dfrac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Average Total Assets}}$ | $\mathbf{\geq 1 \text{ time}}$ | Sales per rupee of assets |
| **Profitability** | Gross Profit Ratio (GPR) | $\dfrac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Industry-specific | Manufacturing margin |
| **Profitability** | Net Profit Ratio (NPR) | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit (after tax)}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Higher is better | Bottom-line margin |
| **Profitability** | Return on Assets (ROA) | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Average Total Assets}} \times 100$ | $\mathbf{\geq 10\%}$ | Asset productivity |
| **Profitability** | Return on Equity (ROE) | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}} \times 100$ | $\mathbf{\geq 15\%}$ | Return to shareholders |
| **Market** | Earnings Per Share (EPS) | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit} - \text{Preference Dividend}}{\text{No. of Equity Shares}}$ | Higher is better | Per-share profit |
| **Market** | Price-Earnings (P/E) Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Market Price per Share}}{\text{EPS}}$ | Industry-specific | Growth expectation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Engineering firms use these ratios to (1) decide **leasing vs. buying** equipment (Solvency), (2) evaluate **credit terms** offered to customers (Activity), (3) assess **R&D investment ROI** (Profitability), and (4) determine **working capital limits** (Liquidity).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Convention:** For all numerical problems, KTU expects the student to clearly show:
> 1. Formula used
> 2. Substitution of values
> 3. Final computed ratio (in **:** form or as a decimal)
> 4. A one-line interpretation

### 3.1 Illustrative Solved Problem (Model Template)

**Problem:** From the following data of M/s Zenith Engineering Ltd., compute the **Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, Debt-Equity Ratio, and Net Profit Ratio**.

- Current Assets: ₹ 6,00,000
- Inventory: ₹ 2,00,000
- Current Liabilities: ₹ 3,00,000
- Long-term Debt: ₹ 4,00,000
- Shareholders' Equity: ₹ 6,00,000
- Net Sales: ₹ 12,00,000
- Net Profit (after tax): ₹ 1,80,000

**Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1: Compute Current Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} \\
&= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 2.00 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The current ratio of **2:1** matches the ideal benchmark. The company has ₹2 of current assets for every ₹1 of current liabilities — short-term solvency is **satisfactory**.

---

**Step 2: Compute Quick (Acid Test) Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quick Assets} &= \text{CA} - \text{Inventory} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Quick Ratio} &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 1.33 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A quick ratio of **1.33:1** exceeds the ideal benchmark of 1:1. The company can meet its immediate obligations without depending on inventory liquidation — **strong liquidity position**.

---

**Step 3: Compute Debt-Equity Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Debt-Equity Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Funds}} \\
&= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 0.67 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹1 of equity, the company has only ₹0.67 of debt. This is **below** the conventional 1:1 benchmark, indicating a **conservative capital structure** with low financial risk.

---

**Step 4: Compute Net Profit Ratio (NPR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPR} &= \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Out of every ₹100 of sales, ₹15 is retained as net profit. An **NPR of 15%** is considered healthy in most engineering/manufacturing sectors.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: DuPont Identity (Three-Step ROE Decomposition)

The **DuPont Analysis** breaks down **ROE** into three multiplicative components. This is a frequently tested **derivation** in KTU Module 2.

**Starting Identity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROE} &= \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Sales}} \times \frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Total Assets}} \times \frac{\text{Total Assets}}{\text{Equity}} \\
\text{ROE} &= \underbrace{\text{Net Profit Margin}}_{\text{Profitability}} \times \underbrace{\text{Asset Turnover}}_{\text{Efficiency}} \times \underbrace{\text{Equity Multiplier}}_{\text{Leverage}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Conversion (Step-by-Step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROE} &= \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Equity}} \\
&= \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Equity}} \times \frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Sales}} \times \frac{\text{Assets}}{\text{Assets}} \\
&= \left(\frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Sales}}\right) \times \left(\frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Assets}}\right) \times \left(\frac{\text{Assets}}{\text{Equity}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This identity is powerful because it **isolates the source of ROE** — a manager can identify whether profitability, efficiency, or leverage is driving shareholder returns.

### 3.3 Comprehensive Solved Example — All Five Categories

**Problem (KTU Model Question Pattern):** From the following Balance Sheet and P&L Account of **M/s KTU Engineering Pvt. Ltd.** for the year ended 31-03-2024, compute **any ten** financial ratios:

| **Particulars (₹ in Lakhs)** | **Amount** |
|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital | 100 |
| 10% Preference Share Capital | 40 |
| Reserves & Surplus | 60 |
| 12% Debentures | 80 |
| Current Liabilities | 50 |
| Fixed Assets | 180 |
| Current Assets | 120 |
| Inventory | 50 |
| Cash & Bank | 20 |
| Net Credit Sales | 400 |
| Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | 240 |
| Gross Profit | 160 |
| Net Profit (after tax) | 50 |
| Interest Paid | 9.6 |

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(1) Current Ratio} &= \frac{120}{50} = 2.4 : 1 \\
\text{(2) Quick Ratio} &= \frac{120 - 50}{50} = \frac{70}{50} = 1.4 : 1 \\
\text{(3) Debt-Equity Ratio} &= \frac{80}{100 + 60} = \frac{80}{160} = 0.5 : 1 \\
\text{(4) Interest Coverage} &= \frac{\text{NP} + \text{Tax} + \text{Int}}{\text{Interest}} = \frac{50 + \text{Tax} + 9.6}{9.6} \approx 7.5 \text{ times (approx.)} \\
\text{(5) Inventory Turnover} &= \frac{240}{50} = 4.8 \text{ times} \\
\text{(6) Asset Turnover} &= \frac{400}{180 + 120} = \frac{400}{300} = 1.33 \text{ times} \\
\text{(7) Gross Profit Ratio} &= \frac{160}{400} \times 100 = 40\% \\
\text{(8) Net Profit Ratio} &= \frac{50}{400} \times 100 = 12.5\% \\
\text{(9) ROE} &= \frac{50}{160} \times 100 = 31.25\% \\
\text{(10) EPS} &= \frac{50 - 4}{1{,}00{,}000 \text{ shares}} = ₹ 4.6 \text{ per share (assuming 1 lakh shares)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Always state the **formula** before substitution. Marks are split as: Formula (1 mark) + Substitution (1 mark) + Final answer (1 mark). Skipping the formula attracts a direct **50% mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flow Diagram of Financial Ratios

The following Mermaid **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrates the hierarchical classification of financial ratios as per the KTU Module 2 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FINANCIAL RATIOS"] --> B["LIQUIDITY RATIOS"]
    A --> C["SOLVENCY RATIOS"]
    A --> D["ACTIVITY RATIOS"]
    A --> E["PROFITABILITY RATIOS"]
    A --> F["MARKET VALUE RATIOS"]

    B --> B1["Current Ratio"]
    B --> B2["Quick Ratio"]
    B --> B3["Cash Ratio"]

    C --> C1["Debt Equity Ratio"]
    C --> C2["Debt Total Assets"]
    C --> C3["Interest Coverage"]

    D --> D1["Inventory Turnover"]
    D --> D2["Debtors Turnover"]
    D --> D3["Asset Turnover"]
    D --> D4["Average Collection Period"]

    E --> E1["Gross Profit Ratio"]
    E --> E2["Net Profit Ratio"]
    E --> E3["Return on Assets"]
    E --> E4["Return on Equity"]
    E --> E5["DuPont Analysis"]

    F --> F1["Earnings Per Share"]
    F --> F2["Price Earnings Ratio"]
    F --> F3["Dividend Payout"]

    B1 -.-> G["Uses Balance Sheet"]
    C1 -.-> G
    D3 -.-> G
    E3 -.-> G
    D1 -.-> H["Uses P&L Account"]
    E1 -.-> H
    F1 -.-> H
```

### 4.2 DuPont ROE Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["ROE"] --> B["Net Profit Margin"]
    A --> C["Asset Turnover"]
    A --> D["Equity Multiplier"]
    B --> B1["Net Profit / Sales"]
    C --> C1["Sales / Total Assets"]
    D --> D1["Total Assets / Equity"]
    B1 --> X["PROFITABILITY"]
    C1 --> Y["EFFICIENCY"]
    D1 --> Z["LEVERAGE"]
```

### 4.3 Interpretation Matrix — Decision Framework

| **Ratio Category** | **Higher Value Indicates** | **Lower Value Indicates** | **Managerial Action Triggered** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Liquidity | Better short-term safety | Risk of default | Excess → invest idle cash; Deficit → arrange overdraft |
| Solvency | Higher financial risk | Conservative structure | Excess → reduce debt via buyback; Deficit → raise equity |
| Activity | Better asset utilization | Idle assets / slow collection | Excess → expand capacity; Deficit → tighten credit policy |
| Profitability | Strong earnings | Weak cost control | Excess → declare dividends; Deficit → cost audit |
| Market Value | High investor confidence | Low growth perception | Excess → consider stock split; Deficit → investor relations |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q1. Define the Current Ratio. What is its ideal standard?**

**Model Answer:**
The Current Ratio is a liquidity ratio that measures a firm's ability to pay off its short-term liabilities (due within one year) using its short-term assets. 

$$
\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}
$$

The **ideal standard is 2:1**, meaning a firm should have ₹2 of current assets for every ₹1 of current liabilities. This ensures adequate liquidity without excessive idle working capital. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**Q2. Distinguish between Gross Profit Ratio and Net Profit Ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Gross Profit Ratio** | **Net Profit Ratio** |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\frac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | $\frac{\text{Net Profit after Tax}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ |
| Measures | Manufacturing/Trading efficiency | Overall operational efficiency |
| Deductions considered | Only COGS | All expenses (operating, interest, tax) |
| Indicates | Pricing & production cost control | Bottom-line profitability |
| Ideal benchmark | 25-40% (industry-specific) | 10-15% (industry-specific) |

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** From the following details of **M/s Trinity Engineering Works**, prepare a statement showing **Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, Debt-Equity Ratio, and Interest Coverage Ratio (7 marks):**

| **Particulars** | **₹ in Lakhs** |
|---|---|
| Current Assets | 150 |
| Inventory | 60 |
| Prepaid Expenses | 10 |
| Current Liabilities | 75 |
| 10% Debentures | 100 |
| Shareholders' Equity | 200 |
| Net Profit before Interest & Tax (EBIT) | 80 |

**(b)** What conclusions do you draw from the above ratios regarding the company's **liquidity and solvency position**? (7 marks)

---

##### Model Solution (Question A):

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(1) Current Ratio} &= \frac{\text{CA}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{150}{75} = \mathbf{2 : 1} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(2) Quick Ratio} &= \frac{\text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{150 - 60 - 10}{75} = \frac{80}{75} = \mathbf{1.067 : 1} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(3) Debt-Equity Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Equity}} = \frac{100}{200} = \mathbf{0.5 : 1} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(4) Interest Coverage} &= \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest}} = \frac{80}{10} = \mathbf{8 \text{ times}} \\
&\text{(Interest on Debentures} = 10\% \times 100 = ₹ 10 \text{ Lakhs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Interpretation & Conclusions:**

- **Liquidity Position (STRONG):** The Current Ratio of **2:1** is exactly at the ideal benchmark. The Quick Ratio of **1.067:1** exceeds the 1:1 standard, indicating that the company can meet all current obligations **without relying on inventory liquidation**. Working capital management is efficient.

- **Solvency Position (HEALTHY):** The Debt-Equity Ratio of **0.5:1** is well below the 1:1 conventional limit, showing a **conservative capital structure** with low financial risk. The Interest Coverage Ratio of **8 times** is exceptionally strong (ideal ≥ 3 times), meaning the firm earns 8 times its interest obligation from operations — bankruptcy risk due to interest default is **negligible**.

- **Overall Verdict:** Trinity Engineering is in a **financially robust position** with surplus liquidity, low leverage, and high debt-servicing capacity. (7 marks)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **DuPont Analysis** in detail. Show how ROE is decomposed into its three components with a suitable example. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the **Inventory Turnover Ratio, Debtors Turnover Ratio, Asset Turnover Ratio, and Average Collection Period** from the following data: (7 marks)

| **Particulars** | **₹** |
|---|---|
| Opening Inventory | 40,000 |
| Closing Inventory | 60,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 3,00,000 |
| Credit Sales | 5,00,000 |
| Opening Debtors | 80,000 |
| Closing Debtors | 1,20,000 |
| Net Sales | 6,00,000 |
| Average Total Assets | 4,50,000 |

---

##### Model Solution (Question B):

**Part (a) — DuPont Analysis Explanation:**

The DuPont Analysis, developed by **Donaldson Brown (DuPont Corporation, 1920s)**, decomposes **Return on Equity (ROE)** into a product of three ratios to identify the **root cause** of a firm's return to shareholders.

**DuPont Identity:**

$$
\text{ROE} = \text{Net Profit Margin} \times \text{Asset Turnover} \times \text{Equity Multiplier}
$$

$$
\text{ROE} = \left(\frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Sales}}\right) \times \left(\frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Assets}}\right) \times \left(\frac{\text{Assets}}{\text{Equity}}\right)
$$

- **Component 1 (Net Profit Margin):** Measures **operational efficiency and pricing power**.
- **Component 2 (Asset Turnover):** Measures **efficiency of asset utilization**.
- **Component 3 (Equity Multiplier):** Measures **financial leverage** — how much debt amplifies returns.

**Numerical Illustration:** If Net Profit = ₹50, Sales = ₹500, Assets = ₹1000, Equity = ₹500:
- Net Profit Margin = 50/500 = 10%
- Asset Turnover = 500/1000 = 0.5
- Equity Multiplier = 1000/500 = 2
- **ROE = 10% × 0.5 × 2 = 10%**

(7 marks)

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Activity Ratio Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(1) Inventory Turnover} &= \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg. Inventory}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{(40{,}000 + 60{,}000)/2} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} = \mathbf{6 \text{ times}} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(2) Debtors Turnover} &= \frac{\text{Credit Sales}}{\text{Avg. Debtors}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{(80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000)/2} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = \mathbf{5 \text{ times}} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(3) Asset Turnover} &= \frac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Avg. Total Assets}} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} = \mathbf{1.33 \text{ times}} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(4) Average Collection Period} &= \frac{365}{\text{Debtors Turnover}} = \frac{365}{5} = \mathbf{73 \text{ days}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Inventory turns 6 times a year (≈ 60 days stockholding). Debtors are collected every 73 days on average. Asset turnover of 1.33 means ₹1.33 of sales is generated per ₹1 of assets — moderately efficient.

(7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use "Average"** for Inventory, Debtors, and Assets — KTU strictly expects averages in turnover ratios (loses 1-2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing "Quick Assets"** — Prepaid Expenses and Advance Tax **must be excluded** from Quick Assets; only Cash, Debtors, and Marketable Securities are included.
> 3. **Not writing interpretation** — Even for 3-mark problems, KTU expects a one-line conclusion. Skipping interpretation costs 0.5-1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong Interest Calculation** — Interest = Rate × Face Value of Debentures (not on book value unless specified).
> 5. **Using Net Sales vs. Credit Sales interchangeably** in Debtors Turnover — always use **Credit Sales** strictly.
> 6. **EPS formula** — Subtracting only **Preference Dividend** (not total dividend) from Net Profit in the numerator.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2:**

- **Five Categories:** Liquidity, Solvency, Activity, Profitability, Market Value — remember the order.
- **Current Ratio ideal:** 2:1 | **Quick Ratio ideal:** 1:1 | **Cash Ratio ideal:** 0.5:1.
- **Debt-Equity ideal:** ≤ 1:1 (2:1 for capital-intensive industries).
- **Interest Coverage ideal:** ≥ 3 times (≥ 5 is excellent).
- **Average formula:** (Opening + Closing) / 2 — apply to Inventory, Debtors, and Total Assets.
- **Inventory Turnover** uses **COGS** (not Sales) in the numerator.
- **Debtors Turnover** uses **Credit Sales** (not Total Sales) in the numerator.
- **365 days** for Average Collection Period (or **12 months** if monthly financial year is used).
- **ROA = Net Profit / Avg. Total Assets** | **ROE = Net Profit / Avg. Equity**.
- **DuPont Identity:** ROE = NPM × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier (multiplicative).
- **EPS Numerator:** Net Profit **after tax** **minus** Preference Dividend.
- **P/E Ratio** is the **inverse of Earnings Yield** (E/P).
- Always present answers in the format: **Formula → Substitution → Final Ratio → Interpretation**.
- Cross-verification trick: ROA × Equity Multiplier = ROE (algebraic identity check).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Depreciation - Definition and Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Depreciation - Definition and Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Depreciation** is the systematic and rational allocation of the *depreciable amount* of an asset over its *estimated useful life*, in a manner that represents the pattern in which the asset's economic benefits are expected to be consumed by the enterprise (in alignment with **AS-6 / Ind AS-16** and the **Matching Principle** of accounting).

In simpler KTU board language:

$$\text{Depreciation} = \text{Systematic Decrease in the Book Value of a Fixed (Tangible) Asset due to Use, Wear \& Tear, Time, Obsolescence, or Exhaustion.}$$

### 1.2 Key Terminology (High-Frequency KTU Board Terms)

| Term | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Gross Cost (Original Cost)** | Total acquisition + installation cost | ₹ (INR) |
| **Salvage Value (Scrap Value)** | Estimated resale value at end of useful life | ₹ (INR) |
| **Net Book Value (NBV)** | Cost minus accumulated depreciation | ₹ (INR) |
| **Useful Life** | Time period asset is expected to be productive | Years |
| **Depreciable Amount** | Cost minus Salvage Value | ₹ (INR) |
| **Obsolescence** | Loss of value due to technology change | - |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** Depreciation is a **non-cash expense**. It reduces profit on the income statement and accumulates as a **contra-asset** on the balance sheet. **Land is NOT depreciated** because it has an unlimited useful life.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy – "The Mobile Phone Story":** Imagine you buy a smartphone for ₹80,000. After 4 years, even if it still *works*, its market value drops to ₹10,000. The ₹70,000 loss in value is *consumed* every year to run your business/life. Instead of charging the full ₹70,000 in Year 4 (which would distort profit), we *spread* it across 4 years as **Depreciation** (₹17,500 per year). This is the essence of the **Matching Concept** – match expense with the revenue it helped generate.

### 1.4 Causes / Factors Necessitating Depreciation

1. **Physical Wear and Tear** – Regular use (machinery parts grinding down).
2. **Effluxion of Time** – Passage of time causes value loss (e.g., leased buildings).
3. **Obsolescence** – Technology makes the asset outdated.
4. **Exhaustion** – Natural resources (mines, quarries).
5. **Accidents / Abnormal Causes** – Loss due to fire, flood (these are *not* depreciation but losses).
6. **Inadequacy** – Asset becomes too small for current production scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Depreciation Methods

Depreciation methods are broadly classified into two families:

**A. Time-Based Methods (Charge depends on Years)**
1. Straight Line Method (SLM) / Fixed Instalment Method
2. Diminishing Balance Method (DBM) / Written Down Value (WDV)
3. Sum of Years Digits Method (SYD)
4. Annuity Method

**B. Use-Based / Activity-Based Methods (Charge depends on Output)**
1. Machine Hour Rate (MHR) Method
2. Service Hours Method
3. Production Unit Method
4. Mileage Method (used for vehicles)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **highest-yield revision block** for the KTU University Exam. Memorize every formula.

| S.No | Method | Depreciation per Period Formula | Notation | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Straight Line Method (SLM)** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | $C$ = Cost, $S$ = Salvage, $n$ = Life in years | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | **Diminishing Balance (WDV)** | $D_t = R \times BV_{t-1}$ where $R = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}$ | $R$ = Rate, $BV$ = Book Value | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Sum of Years Digits (SYD)** | $D_t = \dfrac{\text{Remaining Life at start of year } t}{\text{Sum of Years Digits}} \times (C - S)$ | $\text{SYD} = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Machine Hour Rate (MHR)** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{\text{Total Estimated Hours}} \times \text{Hours Used}$ | Hours used = Actual Usage | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **Production Unit Method** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{\text{Total Estimated Units}} \times \text{Units Produced}$ | - | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | **Annuity Method** | $D = A - I$ where $A$ = Annual Annuity, $I$ = Interest on BV | - | ⭐⭐ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Notation Rule for KTU Board:** When writing the SLM formula, **never** write `|C - S|` inside a markdown table – it breaks the table parser. Always use $\vert C - S \vert$ or $(C - S)$ with the explicit assumption that $C > S$.

### 2.3 Why These Methods Exist? The 'Why' Behind Each

- **SLM** is preferred for assets that provide *uniform* benefits every year (e.g., buildings, furniture, patents).
- **DBM** is preferred for assets that generate *more revenue in early years* and *less in later years* (e.g., vehicles, computers, machinery). It also recognizes the **fact that maintenance cost rises with age**, so depreciation cost should *fall* to keep total cost uniform.
- **SYD** is an *accelerated* method – an *arithmetic* acceleration of DBM.
- **MHR / Production Unit** is used when usage is *uneven* and directly tied to revenue generation (e.g., a delivery truck whose depreciation depends on km driven).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **Kerala industrial context**, depreciation methods directly affect:
- **Income Tax Act, 1961 (Extra Depreciation for Manufacturing)**: KTU students should remember the *statutory* WDV rates used by the Income Tax Department for engineering assets (e.g., Plant & Machinery = 15%, Buildings = 10%).
- **Working Capital & Loan Sanction** (Banks compute asset cover using NBV).
- **Engineering Procurement Decisions** (Payback calculations require depreciation for cash-flow recovery).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Straight Line Method (SLM)

**Logical Foundation:**
The **Matching Principle** requires that the *depreciable amount* (Cost − Salvage) be spread *equally* across the *useful life* of the asset.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depreciable Amount} &= \text{Cost} - \text{Salvage Value} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Depreciable Amount} &= C - S \\
\text{Annual Depreciation } (D) &= \dfrac{\text{Depreciable Amount}}{\text{Useful Life in Years}} \\
\Rightarrow D &= \dfrac{C - S}{n} \\
\text{Depreciation Rate } (r) &= \dfrac{D}{C} \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{C - S}{n \cdot C}\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example 1 (SLM):**
> A machine costs ₹5,00,000. Salvage value = ₹50,000. Useful life = 10 years. Find annual depreciation and the depreciation rate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 5{,}00{,}000; \quad S = 50{,}000; \quad n = 10 \\
D &= \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{10} = \dfrac{4{,}50{,}000}{10} \\
D &= 45{,}000 \text{ per year} \\
r &= \dfrac{45{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Diminishing Balance Method (DBM / WDV)

**Logical Foundation:**
In DBM, depreciation is calculated on the **diminishing book value (written down value)** year after year, so the charge *decreases* every year. A fixed percentage $R$ is applied each year.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= R \times C \\
BV_1 &= C - D_1 = C(1-R) \\
D_2 &= R \times BV_1 = R \times C(1-R) \\
D_3 &= R \times BV_2 = R \times C(1-R)^2 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
D_t &= C(1-R)^{t-1} \cdot R \\
BV_t &= C(1-R)^t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Finding R from the data:**
The book value at the end of year $n$ should equal the salvage value $S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BV_n &= S \\
C(1-R)^n &= S \\
(1-R)^n &= \dfrac{S}{C} \\
1 - R &= \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} \\
R &= 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example 2 (DBM):**
> Machine cost = ₹1,00,000; Salvage = ₹10,000; Life = 3 years. Compute depreciation schedule.

**Step 1: Find the WDV Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 1 - \left(\dfrac{10{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/3} \\
R &= 1 - (0.1)^{1/3} \\
R &= 1 - 0.4642 = 0.5358 \approx 53.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Build the Schedule**

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Depreciation @ 53.58% (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1,00,000 | 53,580 | 46,420 |
| 2 | 46,420 | 24,872 | 21,548 |
| 3 | 21,548 | 11,548 | **10,000** ✓ |

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sum of Years Digits (SYD) Method

**Logical Foundation:**
The asset loses more value in early years. We assign *fractions* of depreciable amount using decreasing numerators over a constant denominator.

**Derivation of the denominator:**

$$
\text{Sum of Years Digits (SYD)} = 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + n = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$

**Year-t depreciation:**

$$
D_t = \dfrac{(n - t + 1)}{\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}} \times (C - S)
$$

**Worked Example 3 (SYD):**
> $C = ₹6,00,000; S = ₹1,00,000; n = 4$ years.

**Step 1: SYD = $\dfrac{4 \times 5}{2} = 10$**

**Step 2: Depreciable Amount = $₹6,00,000 - ₹1,00,000 = ₹5,00,000$**

| Year | Remaining Life | Fraction | Depreciation (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 4/10 | 2,00,000 |
| 2 | 3 | 3/10 | 1,50,000 |
| 3 | 2 | 2/10 | 1,00,000 |
| 4 | 1 | 1/10 | 50,000 |
| **Total** | 10 | 1 | **5,00,000** ✓ |

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Machine Hour Rate (MHR) Method

**Derivation:**

$$
\text{MHR} = \dfrac{C - S + \text{Installation Charges} - \text{Scrap Realisation}}{\text{Total Estimated Working Hours}}
$$

$$
\text{Depreciation} = \text{MHR} \times \text{Actual Hours Worked in the Year}
$$

**Worked Example 4 (MHR):**
> Machine cost = ₹2,00,000; Installation = ₹20,000; Salvage = ₹20,000; Total estimated hours = 20,000; Actual hours in Year 1 = 3,000.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MHR} &= \dfrac{(2{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000) - 20{,}000}{20{,}000} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{20{,}000} = ₹10/\text{hour} \\
D_1 &= 10 \times 3{,}000 = ₹30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$
