### 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Comparative Snapshot

| Criterion | SLM | DBM / WDV | SYD | MHR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Charge Pattern** | Constant | Declining | Declining (faster) | Variable |
| **Basis** | Time (Years) | Time (Years) | Time (Years) | Usage (Hours/Units) |
| **Total Charge** | $(C - S)$ | Approaches $S$ but rarely exact | $(C - S)$ | $(C - S)$ |
| **Book Value at End** | $S$ | $S$ (theoretically) | $S$ | $S$ (over time) |
| **Income Tax Allowed?** | Yes (Companies Act, 2013) | Yes (preferred by IT Act) | Not allowed by IT Act | Allowed |
| **Best For** | Buildings, Furniture | Vehicles, Machinery | Manufacturing Plants | Buses, Cranes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Taxonomy of Depreciation Methods

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Depreciation Methods]:::root
    A --> B[Time-Based Methods]:::branch
    A --> C[Use-Based Methods]:::branch
    B --> D1[Straight Line Method SLM]:::leaf
    B --> D2[Diminishing Balance WDV]:::leaf
    B --> D3[Sum of Years Digits SYD]:::leaf
    B --> D4[Annuity Method]:::leaf
    C --> E1[Machine Hour Rate MHR]:::leaf
    C --> E2[Production Unit Method]:::leaf
    C --> E3[Service Hours Method]:::leaf
    C --> E4[Mileage Method]:::leaf
    D1 --> F1[Constant Annual Charge]:::note
    D2 --> F2[Declining Charge on Book Value]:::note
    D3 --> F3[Arithmetic Acceleration]:::note
    E1 --> F4[Variable per Usage]:::note
    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d33,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#a47148,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef note fill:#f4a261,stroke:#e76f51,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture – Depreciation Calculation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Asset Cost C]:::raw
    INPUT2[Salvage Value S]:::raw
    INPUT3[Useful Life n]:::raw
    INPUT4[Hours or Units Used]:::raw
    INPUT5[Method Selector]:::ctrl
    CORE[Depreciation Engine]:::core
    MOD1[SLM Module]:::mod
    MOD2[WDV Module]:::mod
    MOD3[SYD Module]:::mod
    MOD4[MHR Module]:::mod
    OUT1[Annual Depreciation]:::out
    OUT2[Accumulated Depreciation]:::out
    OUT3[Net Book Value NBV]:::out
    REPORT[P and L Statement Entry]:::report
    BS[Balance Sheet Contra-Asset]:::report
    INPUT1 --> CORE
    INPUT2 --> CORE
    INPUT3 --> CORE
    INPUT4 --> CORE
    INPUT5 --> CORE
    CORE --> MOD1
    CORE --> MOD2
    CORE --> MOD3
    CORE --> MOD4
    MOD1 --> OUT1
    MOD2 --> OUT1
    MOD3 --> OUT1
    MOD4 --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> OUT2
    OUT1 --> OUT3
    OUT2 --> REPORT
    OUT3 --> BS
    classDef raw fill:#e63946,stroke:#9d0208,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#457b9d,stroke:#1d3557,color:#ffffff
    classDef core fill:#1d3557,stroke:#0b1d33,color:#ffffff
    classDef mod fill:#f1faee,stroke:#a8dadc,color:#1d3557
    classDef out fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#048a6f,color:#000000
    classDef report fill:#ffd166,stroke:#cca43b,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Decision Flow – Which Method to Apply?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is asset usage uniform per year?}:::q
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Does the asset generate equal revenue each year?}:::q
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use Machine Hour / Production Unit Method]:::rec
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Use Straight Line Method]:::rec
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Use Diminishing Balance or SYD]:::rec
    Q4 --> END1[SLM recommended]:::end
    Q3 --> END2[Use-based method]:::end
    Q5 --> END3[Accelerated method]:::end
    classDef q fill:#fefae0,stroke:#dda15e,color:#283618
    classDef rec fill:#84a98c,stroke:#52796f,color:#ffffff
    classDef end fill:#283618,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"Define depreciation. State any four causes of depreciation."*

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
Depreciation is the systematic allocation of the depreciable amount of a tangible fixed asset over its useful life, in accordance with the matching principle.
**Four causes:**
1. Physical wear and tear due to usage.
2. Effluxion of time (e.g., lease, patents).
3. Technological obsolescence.
4. Exhaustion of natural resources.
*(Acceptable additions: Inadequacy, Accidents – but clarify accidents are losses, not depreciation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*"Distinguish between Straight Line Method and Written Down Value Method of depreciation."*

**Model Answer (Tabular Form – KTU Preferred Layout):**

| Basis | SLM | WDV |
|---|---|---|
| Basis of Calculation | Original Cost | Written Down Value |
| Annual Charge | Equal/Constant | Decreasing |
| Salvage Value | Considered in formula | Ignored in formula |
| Suitability | Buildings, Furniture | Machinery, Vehicles |
| Books of Companies Act | Permitted | Permitted (block assets) |
| Income Tax | Permitted | Default method |

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – **Internal Choice**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation, every Part B question has an **internal choice** (either/or). Solve the question paper pattern. Below, **Q3 OR Q4** is provided.

---

#### **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Pattern] – 14 Marks**

*(a) A company purchased a machine on 1st April 2021 for ₹4,00,000. Installation and transportation charges amounted to ₹50,000. The estimated useful life is 5 years, and the salvage value is ₹50,000. Compute the depreciation for each year using:*
*(i) Straight Line Method, and (ii) Sum of Years Digits Method.* **(7 marks)**

*(b) On 1st January 2023, the company acquired a vehicle for ₹6,00,000. Estimated total running is 1,00,000 km and salvage value is ₹1,00,000. The vehicle ran 15,000 km, 20,000 km, 25,000 km and 30,000 km in the four years respectively. Calculate depreciation using the Mileage Method and show the asset account for all four years.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Step 1: Total Cost**
$C = 4{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 4{,}50{,}000; S = 50{,}000; n = 5; C - S = 4{,}00{,}000$

**Part (a) – Step 2: SLM Calculation**

$$
D = \dfrac{C - S}{n} = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{5} = ₹80{,}000 \text{ per year (constant)}
$$

**Part (a) – Step 3: SYD Schedule**

$$
\text{SYD} = \dfrac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15
$$

| Year | Remaining Life | Fraction | Depreciation (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 5/15 | 1,33,333 |
| 2 | 4 | 4/15 | 1,06,667 |
| 3 | 3 | 3/15 | 80,000 |
| 4 | 2 | 2/15 | 53,333 |
| 5 | 1 | 1/15 | 26,667 |
| **Total** | 15 | 1 | **4,00,000** ✓ |

---

**Part (b) – Mileage Method**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depreciation per km} &= \dfrac{C - S}{\text{Total Estimated km}} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = ₹5/\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Year | km Run | Depreciation (₹) | Acc. Dep. (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2023 | 15,000 | 75,000 | 75,000 | 5,25,000 |
| 2024 | 20,000 | 1,00,000 | 1,75,000 | 4,25,000 |
| 2025 | 25,000 | 1,25,000 | 3,00,000 | 3,00,000 |
| 2026 | 30,000 | 1,50,000 | 4,50,000 | 1,50,000 |
| 2027 (write-off to 1,00,000) | - | 50,000 (balancing) | 5,00,000 | 1,00,000 ✓ |

---

#### **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam – Internal Choice] – 14 Marks**

*(a) Explain the **Diminishing Balance Method** of depreciation. Derive the formula for finding the WDV rate, given Cost, Salvage Value and Useful Life.* **(7 marks)**

*(b) A plant was purchased for ₹2,00,000 on 1st July 2022. It has an estimated life of 4 years and a residual value of ₹25,920. Compute the depreciation for each year using the WDV method.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Conceptual Explanation (4 marks)**
In the Diminishing Balance Method, depreciation is charged on the *written down book value* of the asset at the beginning of each year, so the charge is heavy in the early years and progressively reduces. The salvage value is **not directly deducted** in the formula; instead, the rate is chosen such that the book value at the end of useful life approximately equals the salvage value.

**Part (a) – Formula Derivation (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BV_n &= C(1 - R)^n = S \\
(1 - R)^n &= \dfrac{S}{C} \\
R &= 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2{,}00{,}000; \quad S = 25{,}920; \quad n = 4 \\
R &= 1 - \left(\dfrac{25{,}920}{2{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/4} = 1 - (0.1296)^{0.25} \\
R &= 1 - 0.6 = 0.4 = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Dep @ 40% (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Jul-Dec, half year) | 2,00,000 | 40,000 | 1,60,000 |
| 2 | 1,60,000 | 64,000 | 96,000 |
| 3 | 96,000 | 38,400 | 57,600 |
| 4 | 57,600 | 23,040 | 34,560 |
| 5 (6 months) | 34,560 | 8,640 | **25,920** ✓ |

*(If the question assumes full first year, ignore the half-year adjustment and present the standard 4-year table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout – Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to add installation/transportation charges** to the cost (often missed – 1 mark lost).
> 2. **Mixing up SLM salvage treatment vs. WDV salvage treatment** – SLM *subtracts* salvage, WDV uses it *only in rate derivation*.
> 3. **Wrong SYD denominator**: Students write `n(n-1)/2` instead of `n(n+1)/2`. Cost: 1 mark per error.
> 4. **Decimal/rounding errors in WDV rate** – the rate must produce the *exact* salvage value; round consistently.
> 5. **In Mileage Method**, students often forget the final-year *balancing charge* to reach exact salvage value.
> 6. **P&L entry not mentioned** – KTU asks for "Show the Asset Account"; a *narrative* solution without ledger format loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 📌 **Definition**: Depreciation is a *systematic, non-cash* allocation of cost, NOT a valuation exercise.
- 📌 **Matching Principle** is the underlying *cause* of depreciation.
- 📌 **Land is never depreciated.** Inventory is never depreciated.
- 📌 **SLM** = Constant charge; **WDV** = Declining charge.
- 📌 **SYD** denominator = $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$.
- 📌 **WDV Rate Formula**: $R = 1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}$.
- 📌 **MHR / Production Unit / Mileage** are *use-based* methods – choose when usage is uneven.
- 📌 **Total depreciation under SLM, SYD, MHR = (Cost − Salvage)** exactly.
- 📌 **Under WDV**, total depreciation = Cost − Salvage (only at the *theoretical* end-of-life).
- 📌 For **Income Tax purposes**, WDV (block-wise) is the *default* method.
- 📌 **Companies Act, 2013 (Schedule II)** specifies useful life – KTU questions may test this.
- 📌 **Methods compared in the exam**: Always present in a *table*; KTU examiners reward tabular answers.
- 📌 **When in doubt, mention Ind AS-16 / AS-6** compliance – it shows conceptual depth.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Concepts Relating to Income Measurement and Profit Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cost Concepts Relating to Income Measurement and Profit Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Cost" in the Context of Income Measurement?

In **Management Accounting**, the term **Cost** refers to the *resource sacrificed* (measured in monetary terms) to achieve a specific business objective. For income measurement, costs are not historical artefacts — they are **behavioural classifications** that determine how revenue is matched against expenditure to arrive at **periodic profit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *"Cost is the amount of expenditure (actual or notional) incurred on, or attributable to, a specified thing or activity."* — CIMA UK Terminology, adopted in KTU syllabus.

## 1.2 Income Measurement vs. Profit Planning

| Aspect | Income Measurement | Profit Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Backward-looking (Ex-post) | Forward-looking (Ex-ante) |
| **Question Answered** | "What profit did we earn?" | "What profit should we target?" |
| **Tools Used** | Cost classification, Matching Principle | CVP Analysis, Budgeting, Target Costing |
| **Time Horizon** | Single accounting period | Short, medium & long range |
| **Output** | Income Statement / P&L | Pro-forma statements, BEP charts |

> [!NOTE]
> Both functions depend on a *single foundational concept* — the **behavioural classification of cost** (Fixed vs. Variable vs. Semi-Variable). Without this classification, neither profit calculation nor planning is possible.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tea Stall Model"

Imagine a small tea stall near a Kerala engineering college:

- **Fixed Cost (FC):** The monthly rent of the stall (₹15,000) — paid whether you sell 1 cup or 1,000 cups.
- **Variable Cost (VC):** Tea leaves, milk, sugar, electricity per cup — ₹10/cup.
- **Selling Price (SP):** ₹20/cup.
- **Contribution per cup:** ₹20 − ₹10 = **₹10** (this is what each cup "contributes" toward rent and profit).

> **The Insight:** Until cumulative contribution equals the rent, the stall owner is in **loss**. After crossing that magic number, every additional cup becomes **pure profit**. This "magic number" is called the **Break-Even Point (BEP)** — the cornerstone of profit planning.

This is exactly how engineers-turned-managers analyse multi-crore manufacturing plants — only the numbers are bigger and the costs are more layered.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Cost-for-Profit Analysis

1. **Cost Behaviour** — How does cost react to volume?
2. **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Relationship** — How do these three variables interact?
3. **Profit Volume (P/V) Ratio** — What is the rate at which profit grows with sales?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Three — They Are the Most Repeated KTU Board Concept!**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Behavioural Classification of Costs (The Foundation)

### A. Fixed Costs (FC)
- Do **NOT** vary with output volume within a relevant range.
- Also called **Period Costs** or **Capacity Costs**.
- Examples: Rent, Salaries, Depreciation (straight-line), Insurance.
- **Per unit behaviour:** Falls as volume rises (apportionment effect).

### B. Variable Costs (VC)
- Vary **proportionally and directly** with output.
- Total VC = Variable cost per unit × Units produced.
- Examples: Direct materials, Direct labour (piece-rate), Sales commission.
- **Per unit behaviour:** Remains **constant**.

### C. Semi-Variable Costs (SVC) / Mixed Costs
- Contain **both fixed and variable elements**.
- Examples: Electricity bill (fixed minimum charge + variable consumption charge), telephone bill.
- **Separation methods** (KTU-favourite theory question):
  1. **High-Low Method**
  2. **Least Squares Method (Regression Analysis)**
  3. **Scatter Graph Method**
  4. **Account Analysis Method**

### D. Step-Fixed Costs
- Remain constant over a range, then jump to a new level.
- Example: Supervisor salary — one supervisor for 1–50 workers; two for 51–100.

## 2.2 Other Critical Cost Concepts (KTU Theory Hot-Spots)

- **Sunk Cost:** Cost already incurred and unrecoverable (irrelevant for decision-making).
- **Opportunity Cost:** Benefit sacrificed from the next-best alternative.
- **Relevant Cost:** Future cost that differs between alternatives.
- **Differential Cost:** Difference in total cost between two alternatives.
- **Marginal Cost:** Additional cost of producing **one extra unit**.
- **Absorption Cost:** Full cost (Direct Material + Direct Labour + Variable OH + Fixed OH absorbed).
- **Out-of-Pocket Cost:** Current cash expenditure.
- **Imputed Cost:** Notional cost (e.g., owner's own capital interest).

## 2.3 Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis — The Core Engine

**Assumptions (Board-favourite theory question):**
1. Costs are linear — fixed cost is constant; variable cost per unit is constant.
2. Selling price per unit is constant.
3. Sales mix is constant (in multi-product cases).
4. Production = Sales (no inventory change).
5. Only one product or constant product mix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When KTU gives a problem where inventory changes, they will explicitly state "production ≠ sales" — and you must adjust opening/closing inventory in the income statement.**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contribution (C)} &= \text{Sales (S)} - \text{Variable Cost (VC)} \\
\text{Contribution per unit} &= \text{Selling Price per unit} - \text{Variable Cost per unit} \\
\text{Profit (P)} &= \text{Contribution} - \text{Fixed Cost (F)} \\
\text{BEP (in units)} &= \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Contribution per unit}} \\
\text{BEP (in ₹)} &= \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{P/V Ratio}} \\
\text{P/V Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 \\
\text{Margin of Safety (MOS)} &= \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales} \\
\text{MOS Ratio} &= \frac{\text{MOS}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100 \\
\text{Required Sales (units)} &= \frac{\text{F} + \text{Desired Profit}}{\text{Contribution per unit}} \\
\text{Required Sales (₹)} &= \frac{\text{F} + \text{Desired Profit}}{\text{P/V Ratio}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### High-Yield Mixed & Advanced Formulas

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales for Zero Profit} &= \frac{\text{F}}{\text{P/V Ratio}} \quad (\text{i.e., BEP in ₹}) \\
\text{Variable Cost Ratio} &= 1 - \text{P/V Ratio} \\
\text{Profit at a Given Sales Level} &= \text{Sales} \times \text{P/V Ratio} - \text{F} \\
\text{Change in Profit} &= \text{Change in Sales} \times \text{P/V Ratio} \\
\text{Shut-down Point} &= \text{Point where contribution just equals avoidable fixed cost}
\end{aligned}
$$

### High-Low Method Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Variable Cost per unit} &= \frac{\text{Highest Activity Cost} - \text{Lowest Activity Cost}}{\text{Highest Activity Units} - \text{Lowest Activity Units}} \\
\text{Fixed Cost} &= \text{Total Cost at high point} - (\text{VC per unit} \times \text{High point units})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Alert:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` in all LaTeX inside markdown tables to avoid breaking table rendering. This formula sheet obeys that rule.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In **manufacturing engineering firms**, CVP is used for:
- Pricing industrial orders (make-to-order vs. catalogue).
- Deciding whether to **automate** (lump-sum fixed cost ↑ vs. variable cost ↓).
- Selecting the **most profitable product mix** when capacity is constrained.
- **Bid preparation** for engineering tenders — quoted price must clear contribution threshold.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Formula

We start from the **fundamental accounting identity** of CVP:

**Profit = Sales − Variable Cost − Fixed Cost**

Let:
- $S$ = Sales revenue
- $V$ = Total variable cost
- $F$ = Total fixed cost
- $P$ = Profit
- $x$ = Number of units sold
- $p$ = Selling price per unit
- $v$ = Variable cost per unit

### Step 1: Express Sales and VC in unit form

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= p \cdot x \\
V &= v \cdot x \\
\text{Contribution } C &= S - V = (p - v) \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Contribution is the "available pool" left after variable costs are recovered. This pool must first absorb all fixed costs (₹15,000 rent) before any profit emerges.

### Step 2: Equate Profit to Zero (the BEP condition)

At BEP, $P = 0$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= C - F = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad C &= F \\
\Rightarrow \quad (p - v) \cdot x_{\text{BEP}} &= F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The exact volume at which contribution equals fixed cost is the break-even volume.

### Step 3: Solve for BEP units

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{BEP}} &= \frac{F}{p - v} \\
&= \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Contribution per unit}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Derive BEP in ₹

Multiply BEP units by selling price $p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP (₹)} &= x_{\text{BEP}} \times p \\
&= \frac{F}{p - v} \times p \\
&= \frac{F}{(p - v)/p} \\
&= \frac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This completes the derivation. **This is the most asked derivation in KTU Module 3.**

## 3.2 Fully Worked-Out Solved Problem (Comprehensive Model)

> **Problem:** A small engineering firm produces a specialised valve.
> - Selling price per unit: **₹200**
> - Variable cost per unit: **₹120**
> - Total Fixed Cost: **₹4,00,000**
> - Expected Sales: **8,000 units**
>
> **Calculate:** (a) P/V Ratio, (b) BEP in units and ₹, (c) Margin of Safety (amount & ratio), (d) Profit at expected sales, (e) Sales required to earn a target profit of ₹6,00,000.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

#### (a) P/V Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contribution per unit} &= p - v = 200 - 120 = ₹80 \\
\text{P/V Ratio} &= \frac{80}{200} \times 100 = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) BEP in units and ₹

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP (units)} &= \frac{F}{\text{Contribution per unit}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{80} = 5{,}000 \text{ units} \\
\text{BEP (₹)} &= 5{,}000 \times 200 = ₹10{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Verification using P/V ratio: } \quad \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{0.40} &= ₹10{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (c) Margin of Safety

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual Sales (₹)} &= 8{,}000 \times 200 = ₹16{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{MOS (₹)} &= 16{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹6{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{MOS Ratio} &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{16{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 37.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (d) Profit at expected sales

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Contribution} &= 8{,}000 \times 80 = ₹6{,}40{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= \text{Contribution} - F = 6{,}40{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (e) Sales for Target Profit ₹6,00,000

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Required Contribution} &= F + \text{Target Profit} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Required Sales (units)} &= \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{80} = 12{,}500 \text{ units} \\
\text{Required Sales (₹)} &= \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = ₹25{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table

| Scenario | Formula Used | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Find BEP | $x = F / (p - v)$ | No profit, no loss point |
| Find Profit | $P = \text{Sales} \times \text{P/V Ratio} - F$ | Net surplus after FC recovery |
| Find required volume | $x = (F + P) / (p - v)$ | Volume to hit a target |
| Effect of price change | New P/V = $(p_{new} - v) / p_{new}$ | Recalculate P/V from scratch |
| Multi-product BEP | Weighted avg P/V across sales mix | Use sales-mix proportions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always show units in every step.** KTU examiners award partial credit for dimensional consistency. A bare number without "units" or "₹" loses 0.5 mark per instance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost Classification Tree (Mermaid Concept Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["TOTAL COST OF PRODUCTION"]
    nodeB["BY ELEMENT"]
    nodeC["BY BEHAVIOUR"]
    nodeD["BY FUNCTION"]
    nodeE["BY RELEVANCE"]
    nodeF["Material Labour Expenses"]
    nodeG["Fixed Variable SemiVariable StepFixed"]
    nodeH["Production Selling Admin R and D"]
    nodeI["Relevant Sunk Opportunity Differential"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI
```

## 4.2 CVP Analytical Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn1["FIXED COST F"]
    nodeIn2["VARIABLE COST PER UNIT v"]
    nodeIn3["SELLING PRICE p"]
    nodeCalc1["CONTRIBUTION PER UNIT equals p minus v"]
    nodeCalc2["P OVER V RATIO equals Contribution divided by Sales times 100"]
    nodeCalc3["BEP UNITS equals F divided by Contribution per unit"]
    nodeCalc4["MARGIN OF SAFETY equals Actual Sales minus BEP Sales"]
    nodeOut1["PROFIT equals Contribution minus Fixed Cost"]
    nodeOut2["REQUIRED SALES for target profit"]
    nodeIn1 --> nodeCalc3
    nodeIn2 --> nodeCalc1
    nodeIn3 --> nodeCalc1
    nodeCalc1 --> nodeCalc2
    nodeCalc1 --> nodeCalc3
    nodeCalc2 --> nodeCalc2
    nodeCalc3 --> nodeOut1
    nodeIn1 --> nodeOut1
    nodeCalc1 --> nodeOut2
    nodeIn1 --> nodeOut2
    nodeCalc3 --> nodeCalc4
```

## 4.3 Profit Planning Decision Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart["Start: Set Target Profit P target"]
    nodeStep1["Step 1: Compute Contribution per unit"]
    nodeStep2["Step 2: Compute Required Contribution equals F plus P target"]
    nodeStep3["Step 3: Compute Required Volume equals Required Contribution divided by Contribution per unit"]
    nodeStep4["Step 4: Compare with Capacity"]
    nodeYes["Capacity Sufficient: Plan Confirmed"]
    nodeNo["Capacity Insufficient: Reduce FC or Increase SP"]
    nodeStart --> nodeStep1
    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4
    nodeStep4 --> nodeYes
    nodeStep4 --> nodeNo
```

## 4.4 CVP Graph — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeY["Rupees"]
    nodeX["Volume in Units"]
    nodeSP["Total Sales Line STEEP SLOPE"]
    nodeVC["Total Variable Cost Line STEEP SLOPE"]
    nodeTC["Total Cost Line PARALLEL TO VC ABOVE BY F"]
    nodeBEP["BREAK EVEN POINT INTERSECTION OF SALES AND TC"]
    nodeLoss["LOSS AREA BELOW BEP"]
    nodeProfit["PROFIT AREA ABOVE BEP"]
    nodeX --> nodeSP
    nodeX --> nodeVC
    nodeSP --> nodeBEP
    nodeVC --> nodeTC
    nodeBEP --> nodeLoss
    nodeBEP --> nodeProfit
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the CVP Graph:** To the **left** of BEP → Loss zone. At BEP → No profit, no loss. To the **right** of BEP → Profit zone. The vertical gap between Sales line and Total Cost line at any volume equals **Total Profit (or Loss)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Marginal Costing and Absorption Costing. Why does marginal costing give a different profit figure when inventory levels change?**

**Model Answer:**

**Marginal Costing** treats only **variable costs** as product costs. Fixed manufacturing overhead is charged **entirely to the period** in which it is incurred.

**Absorption Costing** treats both variable AND fixed manufacturing costs as product costs. Fixed overhead is absorbed into units via a recovery rate.

**Why profit differs:**
- When **production > sales** (inventory builds up): Absorption costing **defers** some fixed cost into closing inventory, inflating current profit.
- When **production < sales** (inventory drawn down): Previously deferred fixed cost is released, reducing current profit.
- Under marginal costing, fixed cost is **fully written off** every period, so profit is independent of inventory movement.

> This is a 3-mark conceptual question; award 1 mark for each distinction + 1 mark for the inventory effect explanation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of "Margin of Safety". How does it indicate the strength of a business?**

**Model Answer:**

**Margin of Safety (MOS)** is the difference between **actual/anticipated sales** and **break-even sales**. It represents the amount by which sales can drop before the firm begins to incur losses.

$$
\text{MOS} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}
$$

**Strength Interpretation:**
- A **large MOS** → strong cushion against adverse market conditions; firm can absorb sales decline.
- A **small MOS** → risky position; even minor sales drop pushes the firm into loss.
- **MOS Ratio** above **25–30%** is generally considered healthy for engineering/manufacturing firms.

**3-Mark Distribution:** Definition (1), Formula (1), Interpretation of business strength (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** From the following data of an engineering component manufacturing unit, calculate:
1. P/V Ratio
2. Break-even point (in units and in ₹)
3. Margin of Safety in ₹ and as a percentage
4. Profit when sales are ₹12,00,000

| Item | Amount |
|---|---|
| Selling price per unit | ₹100 |
| Variable cost per unit | ₹60 |
| Total Fixed Cost | ₹4,00,000 |
| Actual Sales (units) | 10,000 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the management wishes to earn a profit of ₹5,00,000, how many units must be sold? If the selling price is reduced by 10% due to market competition, recalculate the new BEP. State whether the firm should accept the price cut, assuming volume can be increased to 14,000 units.

### Model Solution

#### (a) Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Contribution per unit**
$$
C_{\text{unit}} = 100 - 60 = ₹40
$$

**Step 2: P/V Ratio**
$$
\text{P/V} = \frac{40}{100} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

**Step 3: BEP in units and ₹**
$$
x_{\text{BEP}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{40} = 10{,}000 \text{ units (but wait, this equals sales!)}
$$

> Correction: Let's recompute with corrected data. BEP = ₹4,00,000 / 0.40 = ₹10,00,000 — this means **BEP (₹) = ₹10,00,000**.

**Step 4: Actual Sales**
$$
\text{Actual Sales (₹)} = 10{,}000 \times 100 = ₹10{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Margin of Safety**
$$
\text{MOS (₹)} = 10{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹0
$$
$$
\text{MOS Ratio} = 0\%
$$

> **Note to students:** This problem reveals that at the given sales level, the firm is exactly at BEP — a critical business insight often tested.

**Step 6: Profit at sales of ₹12,00,000**
$$
\text{Contribution} = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = ₹4{,}80{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} = 4{,}80{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹80{,}000
$$

**7-Mark Distribution:** Step 1 (1) + Step 2 (1) + Step 3 (2) + Step 5 (1) + Step 6 (2).

#### (b) Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Required volume for ₹5,00,000 profit**
$$
x = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000}{40} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{40} = 22{,}500 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: New selling price after 10% reduction**
$$
p_{\text{new}} = 100 - 10 = ₹90
$$

**Step 3: New contribution per unit**
$$
C_{\text{new}} = 90 - 60 = ₹30
$$

**Step 4: New BEP**
$$
x_{\text{BEP,new}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{30} = 13{,}333.33 \text{ units} \approx 13{,}334 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5: Profit at 14,000 units (after price cut)**
$$
\text{Profit} = (14{,}000 \times 30) - 4{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}20{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹20{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Decision**
- New profit (₹20,000) < Old achievable profit at higher volume.
- However, **break-even volume rises from 10,000 to 13,334 units** — riskier.
- **Recommendation:** Accept the price cut **only if** the company is confident of sustained 14,000+ unit sales AND has sufficient capacity. Otherwise **reject**.

**7-Mark Distribution:** Step 1 (1) + Step 2–4 (2) + Step 5 (2) + Step 6 decision (2).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to **recalculate P/V ratio** after a price change. Always recompute P/V with the **new price**. Also, when stating a decision, **justify with a number** — don't just say "accept" or "reject" without computing the resulting profit.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A company manufacturing electric motors has the following cost structure:
- Fixed Overheads: ₹6,00,000 per annum
- Variable cost per motor: ₹800
- Selling price: ₹1,200
- Present annual sales: 1,500 motors

**Required:**
1. Present profit
2. New profit if commission of ₹100 per motor is introduced (sales expected to rise by 25%)
3. New profit if fixed cost rises by ₹2,00,000 but variable cost falls by ₹100 per unit (sales constant)
4. Which alternative is best?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **High-Low Method** for separating semi-variable costs. The following data is available:

| Month | Production (units) | Total Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| January | 2,000 | 30,000 |
| February | 3,000 | 35,000 |
| March | 4,000 | 40,000 |
| April | 5,000 | 45,000 |

Using High-Low, find the Fixed Cost and Variable Cost per unit. Also state one limitation of the method.

### Model Solution

#### (a) Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Present Profit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contribution per unit} &= 1{,}200 - 800 = ₹400 \\
\text{Total Contribution} &= 1{,}500 \times 400 = ₹6{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= 6{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{Nil (At BEP)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: With Commission Introduced**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New variable cost} &= 800 + 100 = ₹900 \\
\text{New contribution} &= 1{,}200 - 900 = ₹300 \\
\text{New sales volume} &= 1{,}500 \times 1.25 = 1{,}875 \text{ motors} \\
\text{Total Contribution} &= 1{,}875 \times 300 = ₹5{,}62{,}500 \\
\text{Profit} &= 5{,}62{,}500 - 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{Loss of ₹37,500}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: With Fixed Cost Increase + Variable Cost Reduction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New Fixed Cost} &= 6{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹8{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{New Variable Cost per unit} &= 800 - 100 = ₹700 \\
\text{New Contribution per unit} &= 1{,}200 - 700 = ₹500 \\
\text{Total Contribution (1,500 units)} &= 1{,}500 \times 500 = ₹7{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= 7{,}50{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = \text{Loss of ₹50,000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Best Alternative**

The original plan yields **zero profit** (best among the three, because alternatives 2 and 3 give losses). Therefore, the company should **retain the existing cost structure** until it can either:
- Increase selling price, OR
- Increase sales volume beyond 1,500 units.

**7-Mark Distribution:** Step 1 (1), Step 2 (2), Step 3 (2), Step 4 with justification (2).

#### (b) Solution — 7 Marks

**Concept (2 marks):** The High-Low Method uses the **highest and lowest activity levels** to estimate the variable cost per unit. The variable cost per unit equals the difference in cost divided by the difference in activity. Fixed cost is then derived as a residual.

**Step 1: Identify High and Low Points**

- Highest activity: April — 5,000 units, Cost = ₹45,000
- Lowest activity: January — 2,000 units, Cost = ₹30,000

**Step 2: Compute Variable Cost per unit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VC per unit} &= \frac{45{,}000 - 30{,}000}{5{,}000 - 2{,}000} \\
&= \frac{15{,}000}{3{,}000} = ₹5 \text{ per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Fixed Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fixed Cost} &= \text{Total Cost at high point} - (\text{VC per unit} \times \text{units}) \\
&= 45{,}000 - (5 \times 5{,}000) \\
&= 45{,}000 - 25{,}000 = ₹20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification at Low Point:**
$$
\text{Total Cost} = 20{,}000 + (5 \times 2{,}000) = 20{,}000 + 10{,}000 = ₹30{,}000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Limitation (1 mark)**

> The High-Low method **ignores intermediate data points** and is therefore **vulnerable to outliers**. If the high or low point is abnormal (e.g., a one-time strike month), the resulting cost equation is misleading.

**7-Mark Distribution:** Concept (2) + Step 1 (1) + Step 2 (1) + Step 3 (1) + Verification (1) + Limitation (1).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Cost Behaviour** is the bedrock — classify every cost as Fixed, Variable, Semi-Variable, or Step before doing CVP.
- **Contribution = Sales − Variable Cost.** Memorise this in your sleep.
- **BEP is where Contribution = Fixed Cost.** Profit begins only after that.
- **P/V Ratio = Contribution / Sales × 100.** A **higher P/V ratio** = more profit sensitivity to sales changes.
- **Margin of Safety** = Cushion. **MOS Ratio > 25%** is healthy.
- **Change in Profit = Change in Sales × P/V Ratio** — a lightning-fast formula for KTU.
- **Absorption Costing** defers fixed cost into inventory → higher profit when stock builds; **Marginal Costing** expels all fixed cost in the period.
- **High-Low Method** = (High cost − Low cost) / (High units − Low units) for VC/unit; residual = FC.
- **Always recompute** P/V and BEP after any price or cost change.
- **Assumptions of CVP** are favourite theory questions — state all 5 cleanly.
- **Shut-down point** < BEP < Profit zone — understand the distinction.
- For **multi-product BEP**, use **weighted average P/V ratio** based on sales mix.
- **Show units (₹, units, %)** in every step of your answer.
- **Avoid Sunk Costs** in decision-making — they are irrecoverable and irrelevant.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Note:** Students who *write definitions in their own words* + *show all formula substitutions clearly* + *state assumptions* + *end with a one-line business interpretation* consistently score 13–14/14 on 14-mark CVP questions. Adopt this four-part answer architecture.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Concepts for Control and Decision Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cost Concepts for Control and Decision Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Cost Concepts for Control and Decision Making

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Management Accounting Definition (KTU 2024):**
> Management accounting is the process of identification, measurement, accumulation, analysis, preparation, interpretation, and communication of financial information used by management to plan, evaluate, and control within an organization. The two pillars of management accounting applied in engineering enterprises are **Cost Control** and **Cost for Decision Making**.

### 1.1.1 Cost Control
**Cost control** is the process of monitoring and regulating the expenditure of an organization to ensure that costs remain within the **predetermined limits** set by management. It is a *forward-looking* and *preventive* function, primarily achieved through:
- **Standard Costing**
- **Budgetary Control**
- **Variance Analysis**
- **Responsibility Accounting**

### 1.1.2 Cost for Decision Making
**Cost for decision making** refers to the classification and use of cost data to choose the best alternative among several available options. It is a *selective* function based on **relevant costs**, which differ from one alternative to another.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board Favourite):**
> - **Cost Control** → Past + Present (comparing actuals with standards/budgets)
> - **Cost for Decision Making** → Future (predicting costs for alternatives)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Family Budget"**
> Imagine a household where the father (Finance Manager) sets a monthly budget of ₹50,000. The mother (Cost Controller) tracks daily expenses against this budget. If grocery bills exceed the limit, she pulls the family to a cheaper store — this is **Cost Control**.
>
> Now the family debates: *"Should we take a vacation or save for a new car?"* They don't consider the **sunk cost** of an old car (already paid for) but DO consider the **opportunity cost** of losing interest on the savings. This is **Cost for Decision Making**.

In an engineering firm, the same logic applies:
- A project manager controls costs by comparing **actual expenses** with **budgeted expenses** (Variance Analysis).
- A production manager decides whether to **make a component in-house or buy from a vendor** using **relevant costs**.

## 1.3 Key Cost Concepts — Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost (KTU Definition):**
> Cost is the amount of resources, usually measured in monetary terms, sacrificed to achieve a specific objective (production of goods, rendering of services, etc.).

### Fundamental Cost Categories for Control & Decisions

| Concept | Definition | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Variable Cost (VC)** | Cost that changes **directly and proportionately** with output | Total VC ↑ with output, per-unit VC constant |
| **Fixed Cost (FC)** | Cost that remains **constant** in total regardless of output level | Total FC constant, per-unit FC ↓ with output |
| **Semi-Variable Cost** | Cost having both fixed and variable components | Partial change with output |
| **Step Cost** | Fixed cost that increases in **discrete jumps** at certain activity levels | Constant within range, then jumps |
| **Sunk Cost** | Cost already **incurred and irrecoverable** | Irrelevant for future decisions |
| **Opportunity Cost** | Benefit **forgone** by choosing one alternative over the next best | Implicit, not recorded in books |
| **Relevant Cost** | Future cost that **differs** between alternatives | Used in decision making |
| **Differential Cost** | Difference in **total cost** between two alternatives | Used for short-term decisions |
| **Marginal Cost** | Additional cost of producing **one extra unit** | MC = ΔTC / ΔQ |
| **Avoidable Cost** | Cost that can be **eliminated** by not choosing an option | Relevant in shut-down decisions |
| **Unavoidable Cost** | Cost that **continues** even if the option is rejected | Often irrelevant |

### 1.4 Standard Costing & Budgetary Control — Quick Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Costing:**
> A technique of cost control that compares **pre-determined standard costs** of production with **actual costs** to ascertain variances. Standards are set for materials, labour, and overheads.
>
> **Budgetary Control:**
> A system of cost control where actual results are compared with **budgeted figures** to identify deviations and take corrective action.

Both are **cost-control tools**, not decision-making tools.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Costs (Engineering Perspective)

### 2.1.1 By Behaviour
- **Variable Cost (VC):** $VC = \text{Rate per unit} \times Q$
- **Fixed Cost (FC):** $FC = \text{Constant}$
- **Semi-Variable:** $Y = a + bX$ (where `a` = fixed part, `b` = variable rate, `X` = activity)

### 2.1.2 By Relevance to Decision
- **Relevant Costs:** Future + Differential
- **Irrelevant Costs:** Sunk, Fixed (in short term), Notional

### 2.1.3 By Controllability
- **Controllable Cost:** Can be regulated by a manager at a given level
- **Uncontrollable Cost:** Cannot be influenced at that level

### 2.1.4 By Function
- Production / Manufacturing Cost
- Administration Cost
- Selling & Distribution Cost
- R&D Cost
- Finance Cost

---

## 2.2 Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis — The Core

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CVP Analysis is the heart of "Cost for Decision Making" in KTU 2024 Module 3.** It examines the interrelationship of **Cost**, **Volume**, and **Profit** to aid short-term managerial decisions.

### 2.2.1 Core Assumptions of CVP
1. All costs can be classified into **Fixed** and **Variable** (semi-variable split using High-Low method).
2. **Selling price per unit, Variable cost per unit, and Total fixed cost remain constant.**
3. **Sales mix** is constant (in multi-product cases).
4. Production = Sales (no inventory change).
5. Behaviour pattern is **linear** within the relevant range.

### 2.2.2 Break-Even Point (BEP) — The Decision Anchor

> [!NOTE]
> **BEP is the sales volume at which Total Revenue = Total Cost, i.e., Profit = 0.** It is the **no-profit-no-loss point**.

### 2.2.3 Margin of Safety (MoS)
**MoS** is the difference between **actual/budgeted sales** and **break-even sales**. It indicates how much sales can drop before the firm incurs a loss.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — it covers ~70% of Module 3 numerical problems in KTU exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Cost (TC)** | $TC = FC + (VC \times Q)$ | $Q$ = Units produced/sold |
| 2 | **Total Revenue (TR)** | $TR = SP \times Q$ | $SP$ = Selling Price/unit |
| 3 | **Profit (P)** | $P = TR - TC = (SP - VC)Q - FC$ | |
| 4 | **Contribution (C)** | $C = SP - VC$ (per unit) | $C = TR - TVC$ (total) |
| 5 | **Total Contribution** | $TCt = SP \cdot Q - VC \cdot Q = (SP - VC) \cdot Q$ | |
| 6 | **BEP (Units)** | $BEP_Q = \dfrac{FC}{SP - VC} = \dfrac{FC}{C}$ | |
| 7 | **BEP (Rupees)** | $BEP_{\text{₹}} = \dfrac{FC \times SP}{SP - VC} = \dfrac{FC}{P/V \text{ ratio}}$ | |
| 8 | **P/V Ratio** | $\dfrac{SP - VC}{SP} = \dfrac{C}{SP} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%** |
| 9 | **Margin of Safety (Units)** | $MoS_Q = \text{Actual Sales (units)} - BEP_Q$ | |
| 10 | **MoS (₹)** | $MoS_{\text{₹}} = \text{Actual Sales (₹)} - BEP_{\text{₹}}$ | |
| 11 | **MoS Ratio** | $\dfrac{MoS}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100\%$ | |
| 12 | **Profit using MoS** | $P = MoS \times P/V \text{ ratio}$ | |
| 13 | **Desired Profit Sales (units)** | $Q = \dfrac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{SP - VC}$ | |
| 14 | **Desired Profit Sales (₹)** | $₹ = \dfrac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{P/V \text{ ratio}}$ | |
| 15 | **Margin of Safety %** | $\dfrac{\text{Profit}}{P/V \text{ ratio}}$ | |
| 16 | **High-Low Method (Variable Cost/unit)** | $\dfrac{HC - LC}{HU - LU}$ | $HC$ = High cost, $LC$ = Low cost, $HU/LU$ = activity levels |
| 17 | **High-Low Fixed Cost** | $FC = HC - (VC/\text{unit} \times HU)$ | |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Always use **Selling Price** as the denominator of P/V ratio (not Cost). Many students wrongly compute (Contribution/Cost) and lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of CVP

| Decision | CVP Application |
|---|---|
| **Make or Buy** | Compare relevant (variable) costs; ignore sunk & allocated fixed costs |
| **Special Order** | Accept if special price ≥ Variable cost (and covers avoidable fixed cost) |
| **Shut Down** | Shut if contribution < Avoidable fixed cost |
| **Product Mix** | Rank products by **Contribution per limiting factor** |
| **Sales Mix Change** | Re-compute weighted P/V ratio |
| **Price Change** | Impact on BEP, MoS, and profit |

---

## 2.5 Standard Costing & Variances (Control Tool)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variance = Standard Cost − Actual Cost** (Favourable when actual is less than standard for costs)

### 2.5.1 Material Variances
- **Material Cost Variance (MCV)** = $(SP \times SQ) - (AP \times AQ)$
- **Material Price Variance (MPV)** = $AQ \times (SP - AP)$
- **Material Usage/Quantity Variance (MUV/MQV)** = $SP \times (SQ - AQ)$

### 2.5.2 Labour Variances
- **Labour Cost Variance (LCV)** = $(SR \times SH) - (AR \times AH)$
- **Labour Rate Variance (LRV)** = $AH \times (SR - AR)$
- **Labour Efficiency Variance (LEV)** = $SR \times (SH - AH)$

### 2.5.3 Overhead Variances
- **Overhead Cost Variance (OCV)** = Absorbed OH − Actual OH
- **Volume Variance** = Absorbed OH − Budgeted OH
- **Expenditure Variance** = Budgeted OH − Actual OH

> [!NOTE]
> **F (Favourable):** When actual cost is **less** than standard.
> **A (Adverse/Unfavourable):** When actual cost is **more** than standard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of BEP Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** At Break-Even Point, Total Revenue (TR) = Total Cost (TC). Profit = 0.

**Step 1:** Write the basic equation.

$$\text{Profit} = TR - TC$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the components.

$$P = (SP \times Q) - [FC + (VC \times Q)]$$

**Step 3:** At BEP, P = 0, so:

$$0 = (SP \times Q) - FC - (VC \times Q)$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate Q.

$$(SP \times Q) - (VC \times Q) = FC$$

**Step 5:** Factor out Q.

$$Q \times (SP - VC) = FC$$

**Step 6:** Solve for Q (Break-Even Units).

$$BEP_Q = \frac{FC}{SP - VC} = \frac{FC}{C}$$

**Step 7:** Multiply both sides by SP to get BEP in Rupees.

$$BEP_{\text{₹}} = \frac{FC \times SP}{SP - VC}$$

**Step 8:** Divide numerator and denominator by SP.

$$BEP_{\text{₹}} = \frac{FC}{\frac{SP - VC}{SP}} = \frac{FC}{P/V \text{ ratio}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** $BEP_{\text{₹}}$ does NOT require the actual number of units. It only needs FC, SP, and VC/unit. This is the most asked KTU formula.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of P/V Ratio

**Step 1:** Start with contribution per unit.

$$C = SP - VC$$

**Step 2:** Divide by Selling Price to get ratio.

$$P/V = \frac{SP - VC}{SP} = \frac{C}{SP}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by 100 to express in percentage.

$$P/V \text{ ratio} \% = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Algebraically, the contribution per rupee of sales is:

$$P/V = 1 - \frac{VC}{SP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows that P/V ratio increases when VC/SP ratio falls — i.e., higher margin products (engineering goods) have higher P/V ratio than commodity products.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Desired Profit Sales

**Step 1:** From the profit equation.

$$P = (SP - VC)Q - FC$$

**Step 2:** Set P = Desired Profit (DP) and solve for Q.

$$DP + FC = (SP - VC)Q$$

$$Q = \frac{FC + DP}{SP - VC} = \frac{FC + DP}{C}$$

**Step 3:** In Rupees, divide by P/V.

$$\text{Sales (₹)} = \frac{FC + DP}{P/V \text{ ratio}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Problem — Master Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU Module 3 standard question pattern. Master this template.**

**Problem:** A company manufactures an engineering product with the following data:
- Selling Price = ₹100 per unit
- Variable Cost = ₹60 per unit
- Fixed Cost = ₹4,00,000 per year

**Required:**
1. Calculate BEP in units and rupees
2. Find P/V ratio
3. Sales required to earn a profit of ₹2,00,000
4. Margin of Safety if actual sales are 12,000 units
5. New BEP if FC increases by ₹1,00,000

### Solution

**Step 1 (a):** BEP in units.

$$BEP_Q = \frac{FC}{SP - VC} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{100 - 60} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{40} = 10{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 1 (b):** BEP in rupees.

$$BEP_{\text{₹}} = 10{,}000 \times 100 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2:** P/V ratio.

$$P/V = \frac{100 - 60}{100} = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40 \text{ or } 40\%$$

**Step 3:** Required sales for ₹2,00,000 profit.

$$Q = \frac{FC + DP}{SP - VC} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000}{40} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{40} = 15{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Margin of Safety.

$$MoS_Q = 12{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ units}$$

$$MoS_{\text{₹}} = 2{,}000 \times 100 = ₹2{,}00{,}000$$

$$MoS \text{ ratio} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 16.67\%$$

**Profit Check:** $P = MoS \times P/V = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = ₹80{,}000$ ✓ (matches $P = (40 \times 12{,}000) - 4{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}80{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 80{,}000$)

**Step 5:** New BEP with additional FC of ₹1,00,000.

$$\text{New FC} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹5{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{New } BEP_Q = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{40} = 12{,}500 \text{ units}$$

---

## 3.5 High-Low Method — Worked Example

**Problem:** Given the following data, separate semi-variable cost into fixed and variable using the High-Low method.

| Month | Activity (Units) | Total Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Jan | 5,000 | 45,000 |
| Feb | 7,000 | 57,000 |
| Mar | 4,000 | 39,000 |
| Apr | 8,000 | 63,000 |

**Step 1:** Identify highest and lowest activity levels.

- Highest: April → 8,000 units @ ₹63,000
- Lowest: March → 4,000 units @ ₹39,000

**Step 2:** Calculate variable cost per unit (slope).

$$VC/\text{unit} = \frac{63{,}000 - 39{,}000}{8{,}000 - 4{,}000} = \frac{24{,}000}{4{,}000} = ₹6 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate fixed cost using high point.

$$FC = 63{,}000 - (6 \times 8{,}000) = 63{,}000 - 48{,}000 = ₹15{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Verify with low point.

$$FC = 39{,}000 - (6 \times 4{,}000) = 39{,}000 - 24{,}000 = ₹15{,}000 \text{ ✓}$$

**Step 5:** Cost equation.

$$Y = 15{,}000 + 6X$$

---

## 3.6 Make or Buy Decision — Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule:** Buy if **Buyer's price < Marginal cost to make**. Include only **relevant costs** (variable + avoidable fixed).

**Comparison Table (7 marks standard):**

| Item | Make (₹) | Buy (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Material | XX | – |
| Direct Labour | XX | – |
| Variable Overhead | XX | – |
| Fixed OH (avoidable only) | XX | – |
| Purchase Price | – | XX |
| **Total Relevant Cost** | **XX** | **XX** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Ignore in Make-vs-Buy:**
> - Sunk cost (past R&D)
> - Allocated fixed cost (depreciation, rent of building — if unavoidable)
> - Notional costs

---

## 3.7 Special Order Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accept a special order if:** Special price ≥ Variable cost **AND** there is **spare capacity**.
> If capacity must be created, accept only if Special price covers VC + Additional FC.

---

## 3.8 Shut-Down Decision

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule:** A product/department should be shut down if its **Contribution < Avoidable Fixed Cost** (i.e., unavoidable cost is the only residual loss).

**Shut-Down Loss Test:** If shut-down → Lose avoidable FC + Lose contribution
- If lost contribution > saved avoidable FC → Continue
- If lost contribution < saved avoidable FC → Shut down

---

## 3.9 Product Mix / Limiting Factor Decision

**Step 1:** Identify the **limiting factor** (key resource, e.g., raw material, machine hours, labour hours).

**Step 2:** Compute **Contribution per unit of limiting factor** for each product.

$$\text{Rank} = \frac{\text{Contribution per unit of product}}{\text{Units of limiting factor per product}}$$

**Step 3:** Allocate the limiting factor in the order of **highest rank** to maximise profit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Costs — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Cost] --> B[By Element]
    A --> C[By Behaviour]
    A --> D[By Function]
    A --> E[By Decision Relevance]
    A --> F[By Controllability]
    B --> B1[Material]
    B --> B2[Labour]
    B --> B3[Expenses]
    C --> C1[Variable Cost]
    C --> C2[Fixed Cost]
    C --> C3[Semi-Variable]
    C --> C4[Step Cost]
    D --> D1[Production]
    D --> D2[Administration]
    D --> D3[Selling and Distribution]
    E --> E1[Relevant Cost]
    E --> E2[Irrelevant Cost]
    E1 --> E1A[Differential Cost]
    E1 --> E1B[Opportunity Cost]
    E1 --> E1C[Avoidable Cost]
    E2 --> E2A[Sunk Cost]
    E2 --> E2B[Committed Fixed Cost]
    E2 --> E2C[Notional Cost]
    F --> F1[Controllable]
    F --> F2[Uncontrollable]
```

---

## 4.2 Cost Control vs Cost for Decision Making — Comparative Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cost Concepts in Management Accounting] --> B[Cost Control]
    A --> C[Cost for Decision Making]
    B --> B1[Standard Costing]
    B --> B2[Budgetary Control]
    B --> B3[Variance Analysis]
    B --> B4[Responsibility Accounting]
    C --> C1[Marginal Costing]
    C --> C2[CVP and BEP]
    C --> C3[Relevant Costing]
    C --> C4[Differential Costing]
    B1 --> D1[Set Standards]
    B1 --> D2[Compare Actuals]
    B1 --> D3[Compute Variances]
    B1 --> D4[Take Corrective Action]
    C1 --> E1[Separate Fixed and Variable]
    C1 --> E2[Decision Focus on Variable Cost]
    C2 --> E3[Find BEP]
    C2 --> E4[Compute MoS and P V Ratio]
    C2 --> E5[Target Profit Planning]
```

---

## 4.3 CVP / BEP Graphical Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sales Volume on X axis in units] --> B[Cost and Revenue in Rupees on Y axis]
    B --> C[Fixed Cost Line - Horizontal at FC]
    B --> D[Total Cost Line - Starts at FC and Slopes Upward]
    B --> E[Total Revenue Line - Starts at Origin and Slopes Upward]
    F[BEP Point] --> G[Intersection of TR and TC Lines]
    F --> H[At BEP - Profit is Zero]
    I[Loss Zone] --> J[Left of BEP - TR less than TC]
    K[Profit Zone] --> L[Right of BEP - TR greater than TC]
    M[Contribution] --> N[Vertical Distance between TR and TVC at any Volume]
    O[Margin of Safety] --> P[Horizontal Distance between Actual Sales and BEP]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision-Making Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify the Decision Problem] --> B[Step 2: List All Alternatives]
    B --> C[Step 3: Identify Relevant Costs and Revenues]
    C --> D[Step 3a: Eliminate Sunk Costs]
    C --> E[Step 3b: Eliminate Unavoidable Fixed Costs]
    C --> F[Step 3c: Include Opportunity Costs]
    D --> G[Step 4: Compute Relevant Cost per Alternative]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Step 5: Compare Alternatives]
    H --> I[Step 6: Select Lowest Cost or Highest Profit Option]
    I --> J[Step 7: Implement and Monitor]
```

---

## 4.5 Variance Analysis Tree (Cost Control)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Cost Variance] --> B[Material Variance]
    A --> C[Labour Variance]
    A --> D[Overhead Variance]
    B --> B1[Material Price Variance]
    B --> B2[Material Usage Variance]
    C --> C1[Labour Rate Variance]
    C --> C2[Labour Efficiency Variance]
    D --> D1[Overhead Expenditure Variance]
    D --> D2[Overhead Volume Variance]
    D2 --> D2a[Calendar Variance]
    D2 --> D2b[Capacity Variance]
    D2 --> D2c[Efficiency Variance]
```

---

## 4.6 Make-or-Buy Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Make or Buy Decision] --> B{Is Capacity Available?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Compare Marginal Cost vs Purchase Price]
    B -- No --> D[Include Additional Fixed Cost too]
    C --> E{Marginal Cost less than Purchase Price?}
    E -- Yes --> F[MAKE in house]
    E -- No --> G[BUY from outside]
    D --> H{Marginal Cost plus Additional FC less than Purchase Price?}
    H -- Yes --> F
    H -- No --> G
    F --> I[Consider Qualitative Factors - Quality, Reliability, Confidentiality]
    G --> I
```

> [!TIP]
> **Block-Level Architecture Insight:** Notice that all decision flows converge to a "Qualitative Check" node. KTU examiners love deducting marks from students who consider **only quantitative** factors.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Contribution". Why is fixed cost considered irrelevant in short-term decision making?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Contribution** is the difference between **Selling Price** and **Variable Cost** per unit (or between Sales and Total Variable Cost in totality). It is the amount that contributes towards the recovery of fixed costs and generation of profit.

> **Why Fixed Cost is irrelevant in short-term decisions:** In the short run, fixed costs are **committed and unavoidable** — they will be incurred regardless of the decision (e.g., continue, shut down, accept special order, etc.). Since they do not **differ between alternatives**, they cannot influence the choice. Only **variable costs** and **avoidable fixed costs** that vary across options are relevant.

$$\text{Contribution per unit} = SP - VC$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between "Sunk Cost" and "Opportunity Cost" with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Sunk Cost | Opportunity Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Cost that has been **incurred in the past** and cannot be recovered | Benefit **foregone** by not choosing the next best alternative |
| **Nature** | Historical, actual | Hypothetical, implied |
| **Recorded in books?** | Yes, already accounted for | No, not recorded in accounting records |
| **Relevance** | **Irrelevant** for future decisions | **Relevant** for decision making |
| **Example** | ₹5,00,000 spent last year on R&D of a failed product | Interest of 8% foregone on ₹10 lakh used to buy machinery |

---

---

## 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Model Question]**

A manufacturing company produces a single product. The following data is given:
- Selling Price per unit: ₹200
- Variable Cost per unit: ₹140
- Fixed Costs: ₹6,00,000 per annum
- Actual Sales last year: 12,000 units

**Required:**
- **(a) [7 marks]** Calculate:
  1. Break-Even Point in units and rupees
  2. P/V ratio
  3. Profit earned at actual sales
  4. Margin of Safety in units, rupees, and percentage
- **(b) [7 marks]** If the management wants to earn a target profit of ₹3,00,000 next year, how many units must be sold? Also, advise how many extra units must be sold if fixed costs increase by ₹1,20,000 due to a new lease.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — BEP, P/V Ratio, Profit, MoS

**Step 1: Contribution per unit.**

$$C = SP - VC = 200 - 140 = ₹60 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 2: BEP in units.**

$$BEP_Q = \frac{FC}{C} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{60} = 10{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: BEP in rupees.**

$$BEP_{\text{₹}} = 10{,}000 \times 200 = ₹20{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4: P/V ratio.**

$$P/V = \frac{C}{SP} = \frac{60}{200} = 0.30 \text{ or } 30\%$$

**Step 5: Profit at 12,000 units.**

$$P = (C \times Q) - FC = (60 \times 12{,}000) - 6{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}20{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 6: Margin of Safety (units).**

$$MoS_Q = 12{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 7: Margin of Safety (₹).**

$$MoS_{\text{₹}} = 2{,}000 \times 200 = ₹4{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 8: Margin of Safety (%).**

$$MoS\% = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{12{,}000 \times 200} \times 100 = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 16.67\%$$

#### Part (b) — Target Profit Sales & Impact of FC Increase

**Step 1: Target sales (units).**

$$Q = \frac{FC + DP}{C} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000}{60} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{60} = 15{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2: New fixed cost.**

$$\text{New FC} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = ₹7{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 3: New target sales with new FC.**

$$Q_{\text{new}} = \frac{7{,}20{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000}{60} = \frac{10{,}20{,}000}{60} = 17{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4: Extra units required.**

$$\Delta Q = 17{,}000 - 15{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ additional units}$$

**Managerial Insight:**
> The increase in fixed cost of ₹1,20,000 requires 2,000 extra units (since each unit contributes ₹60) to maintain the target profit. The **Operating Leverage** of this firm is high — small changes in cost/price cause large changes in profit.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Model Question]**

An engineering firm receives a **special order** for 2,000 units at ₹160 per unit. The firm's normal data is:
- Normal Selling Price: ₹200 per unit
- Variable Cost per unit: ₹140
- Fixed Cost: ₹6,00,000
- Normal annual sales: 12,000 units
- The firm has **spare capacity** of 3,000 units
- A **special machine** must be rented at ₹40,000 for the order

**Required:**
- **(a) [7 marks]** Should the company accept the special order? Show calculations.
- **(b) [7 marks]** If the firm had **no spare capacity** and accepting the order means losing 1,000 units of normal sales, recompute the decision.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — With Spare Capacity

**Step 1: Relevant cost per unit.**

$$VC = ₹140 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 2: Additional fixed cost (special machine).**

$$\text{Additional FC} = ₹40{,}000$$

**Step 3: Total relevant cost of special order.**

$$\text{Total Cost} = (140 \times 2{,}000) + 40{,}000 = 2{,}80{,}000 + 40{,}000 = ₹3{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 4: Revenue from special order.**

$$\text{Revenue} = 160 \times 2{,}000 = ₹3{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 5: Incremental profit/(loss).**

$$\Delta P = 3{,}20{,}000 - 3{,}20{,}000 = ₹0$$

**Decision:** The order yields **neither profit nor loss** — **borderline case**. The company may negotiate a higher price or consider **qualitative factors** (customer goodwill, future repeat orders). On strict quantitative grounds, the company is **indifferent**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Better Decision Insight:** If the company has a long-term strategic relationship with this buyer, accepting at break-even may still be justified. KTU expects students to mention this.

#### Part (b) — Without Spare Capacity (Opportunity Cost Applies)

**Step 1: Contribution lost from displaced normal sales.**

$$\text{Lost Contribution} = (200 - 140) \times 1{,}000 = 60 \times 1{,}000 = ₹60{,}000$$

**Step 2: Contribution gained from special order.**

$$\text{Contribution from Special Order} = (160 - 140) \times 2{,}000 = 20 \times 2{,}000 = ₹40{,}000$$

**Step 3: Additional fixed cost (special machine).**

$$\text{Additional FC} = ₹40{,}000$$

**Step 4: Net incremental profit/(loss).**

$$\Delta P = 40{,}000 - 60{,}000 - 40{,}000 = ₹(-60{,}000)$$

**Decision:** The order should be **REJECTED** because it results in a loss of ₹60,000.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Area:**
> 1. **Forgetting Opportunity Cost:** Many students compute only the special order's contribution without considering lost contribution from displaced normal sales. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Ignoring Spare Capacity:** Students assume the order displaces normal sales even when spare capacity exists. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Allocating Common Fixed Cost:** Some students absorb full annual fixed cost into the special order — **this is WRONG**. Only **avoidable/incremental** fixed cost is relevant. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Confusing BEP formula with Desired Profit formula:** BEP has profit = 0, while Desired Profit has profit = target. Mixing them up leads to wrong answers. **Loss: 3 marks.**
> 5. **Wrong denominator in P/V ratio:** Using cost instead of selling price. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section 30 minutes before the exam.**

### 🎯 Core Cost Concepts
- ✅ **Variable Cost (VC):** Changes with output, constant per unit.
- ✅ **Fixed Cost (FC):** Constant in total, falls per unit.
- ✅ **Sunk Cost:** Past, irrecoverable → **irrelevant** for future decisions.
- ✅ **Opportunity Cost:** Benefit of next-best alternative foregone → **always include**.
- ✅ **Relevant Cost:** Future + Differential.
- ✅ **Differential Cost:** Difference in total cost between two alternatives.
- ✅ **Avoidable vs Unavoidable Cost:** Avoidable = can be eliminated by choosing an option.

### 🎯 CVP / BEP Must-Knows
- ✅ $BEP_Q = \dfrac{FC}{SP - VC}$
- ✅ $BEP_{\text{₹}} = \dfrac{FC}{P/V \text{ ratio}}$
- ✅ $P/V \text{ ratio} = \dfrac{C}{SP} = 1 - \dfrac{VC}{SP}$
- ✅ $P = MoS \times P/V$
- ✅ $Q = \dfrac{FC + DP}{C}$
- ✅ MoS = Actual Sales − BEP Sales
- ✅ Higher P/V ratio → **Higher Operating Leverage** → Higher risk, higher reward.

### 🎯 Standard Costing & Variance (Control)
- ✅ **Variance = Standard − Actual** (F if Standard > Actual for costs).
- ✅ MCV = MPV + MUV; LCV = LRV + LEV.
- ✅ MPV = AQ(SP − AP); MUV = SP(SQ − AQ).
- ✅ LRV = AH(SR − AR); LEV = SR(SH − AH).

### 🎯 Decision Rules (Cheat Sheet)
| Decision | Rule |
|---|---|
| **Make or Buy** | Make if Marginal Cost < Buy Price |
| **Special Order** | Accept if SP ≥ VC (and covers additional FC) |
| **Shut Down** | Shut if Contribution < Avoidable FC |
| **Product Mix** | Rank by Contribution per unit of Limiting Factor |
| **Replace Asset** | Replace if Net savings > Required return on investment |
| **Sales Mix** | Re-compute weighted average P/V ratio |

### 🎯 Linear Algebra & Graph Essentials
- ✅ Slope of TC line = Variable Cost per unit.
- ✅ Y-intercept of TC line = Fixed Cost.
- ✅ TR line passes through origin with slope = Selling Price.
- ✅ BEP = Intersection of TR and TC lines.
- ✅ Loss zone = left of BEP; Profit zone = right of BEP.

### 🎯 High-Low Method
- ✅ Use **highest and lowest activity levels** (not costs).
- ✅ $VC/\text{unit} = \dfrac{HC - LC}{HU - LU}$
- ✅ $FC = HC - (VC/\text{unit} \times HU)$

### 🎯 Key Mnemonics
- 🧠 **"CVP = Control, Volume, Profit"** — three pillars of decision making.
- 🧠 **"SARA"** for cost control: **S**tandards → **A**ctuals → **R**eport variances → **A**ction.
- 🧠 **"FC + DP = (SP − VC) × Q"** — the most-written equation in KTU exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** In KTU 14-mark questions, **always structure the answer in two sub-parts** (a) and (b), show **all formulas before substitution**, **label every value**, and **end with a one-line managerial conclusion**. This fetches 2–3 grace marks even in partially correct answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Job Costing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Course Code:** PEIET741
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Job Costing
> - **Scheme:** 2024 (NEP 2020 Aligned, Outcome-Based Education)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Job Costing** (also known as **Job Order Costing** or **Specific Order Costing**) is a fundamental method of cost accounting under the **Cost Accounting Standards (CAS)** framework, where manufacturing costs are accumulated and recorded **separately for each individual job, work order, or batch**. Every job is treated as a distinct cost unit, and a unique **Job Cost Sheet** is maintained to capture all direct and attributable indirect expenses incurred in its completion.

In the KTU 2024 management accounting framework, Job Costing is positioned as the foundational **specific order costing technique** (as distinct from Process Costing, which is covered in subsequent modules). It is the prescribed methodology for industries where production is **non-repetitive, customer-specific, and heterogeneous** in nature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Job Costing is classified under **"Cost Accumulation Methods - Specific Order Costing"** in Module 3. Students must clearly distinguish it from **Process Costing** (continuous flow) and **Batch Costing** (group of identical units).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bespoke Tailor

Imagine you walk into a **bespoke tailor** in Kerala to get a custom wedding suit stitched. The tailor does **not** produce 500 identical suits. He:

1. Takes **your** specific measurements (your "job specifications").
2. Allocates **specific fabric** to your order only (Direct Material).
3. Records the **exact hours** the master craftsman spends on your suit (Direct Labour).
4. Notes any **special buttons, embroidery thread, or designer lining** you requested (Direct Expenses).
5. Apportions a fair share of his **shop rent, electricity, and sewing machine depreciation** to your order (Overheads).

At the end, he hands you a single invoice breaking down *your* cost. That invoice is essentially a **Job Cost Sheet**. If two friends order suits the same week, each gets a **separate** cost sheet — even though both are "suits."

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world industries using Job Costing:** Custom furniture workshops, ship-building yards, civil construction contracts, printing presses (custom wedding cards), automobile garages, interior design projects, machine tool fabrications, and aircraft maintenance.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Exam Vocabulary)

| Term | KTU Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Job** | A specific work order or customer order treated as a distinct costing unit. |
| **Job Cost Sheet** | The primary document recording all prime cost + overheads for one job. |
| **Prime Cost** | Direct Material + Direct Labour + Direct/Chargeable Expenses. |
| **Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)** | The rate at which indirect costs are charged to jobs (e.g., per labour hour). |
| **Cost Unit** | The unit of product/service for which cost is ascertained (e.g., one custom desk). |
| **Cost Centre** | The location/function where costs are incurred (e.g., the carpentry shop). |

## 1.4 Key Features of Job Costing

- **Separate cost identity** for each job (no averaging).
- **High precision** but **administratively expensive**.
- Requires a **Job Order Number** system for traceability.
- Suitable only for **low-volume, high-variety** production environments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — The 5-Stage Logic Chain

The implementation of Job Costing in an engineering/manufacturing enterprise follows a deterministic five-stage pipeline:

1. **Job Authorisation & Numbering** — A unique alphanumeric Job Number (e.g., `JOB-2025-101`) is issued from a Job Register upon receipt of a customer order. The Job Number travels with all documents.
2. **Material Requisition (Stores Issue Note)** — Direct materials are issued *only* against the specific job's requisition slip. Materials are valued using FIFO/LIFO/Weighted Average.
3. **Labour Time-Ticket Allocation** — Workers submit daily time cards. Hours are apportioned to jobs using a **job-wise time sheet** signed by the foreman.
4. **Overhead Absorption (Predetermined Rate)** — At month-end, factory overheads are charged to jobs using a **predetermined OAR** (calculated at the start of the year).
5. **Job Cost Sheet Finalisation** — The job is closed: Prime Cost + Absorbed Overheads = **Total Job Cost**. Profit margin is added to obtain **Quoted/Invoice Price**.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following six formulas — they account for **~80% of marks** in Module 3 problems.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Prime Cost (PC)** | $PC = DM + DL + DE$ | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 2 | **Works/Factory Cost (WC)** | $WC = PC + \text{Factory Overheads}$ | ₹ |
| 3 | **Cost of Production (COP)** | $COP = WC + \text{Admin Overheads}$ | ₹ |
| 4 | **Total Cost (TC)** | $TC = COP + \text{Selling \& Dist. OH}$ | ₹ |
| 5 | **Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)** | $\displaystyle OAR = \frac{\text{Estimated Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Base}}$ | ₹/hour or % of Wages |
| 6 | **Selling Price (SP)** | $\displaystyle SP = TC \times \left(1 + \frac{\text{Profit\%}}{100}\right)$ | ₹ |

Where:
- $DM$ = Direct Material Cost
- $DL$ = Direct Labour Cost (Hours × Rate)
- $DE$ = Direct/Chargeable Expenses
- **Base** for OAR may be Direct Labour Hours, Machine Hours, or Direct Wages.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Formula

- **Prime Cost** captures everything *directly traceable* to a single job — anything you can "point at" and say "this went into THAT job."
- **Overheads** are *indirect* (rent, supervisor salary, depreciation of shared machines) — they cannot be traced directly, so we **absorb** them using a pre-decided rate.
- The OAR is **predetermined** (not actual) to ensure timely job completion reports and stable pricing quotations.
- **Why predetermined?** Because waiting for year-end actuals would delay quotes by 12 months — commercially impossible.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

In an **engineering services firm** (e.g., a custom robotics integrator or a fabrication workshop), Job Costing enables:
- Accurate **bidding** for tenders.
- Identification of **non-profitable** jobs (post-completion variance analysis).
- **Cost control** via comparison of estimated vs. actual job cost.
- Compliance with **CAS-1 (Cost Accounting Standard on Classification of Costs)** and the **Companies (Cost Records and Audit) Rules, 2014** (where applicable).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern (14-Mark Style):**
>
> *M/s Kerala Engineering Works commenced Job No. 107 on 1st March 2025. The following data was extracted from the books:*
>
> *Direct Materials issued: ₹ 45,000 (Material A) + ₹ 18,000 (Material B) = ₹ 63,000.*
> *Direct Labour: 800 hours @ ₹ 75/hour.*
> *Chargeable Expenses (specialised subcontract work): ₹ 12,500.*
> *Estimated Annual Factory Overheads: ₹ 6,00,000.*
> *Estimated Annual Direct Labour Hours for the firm: 50,000 hours.*
> *The firm adds 25% profit on total cost.*
>
> *Required: (a) Calculate the Overhead Absorption Rate. (b) Prepare the Job Cost Sheet. (c) Determine the Invoice Price.*

---

## 3.1 Part (a) — Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR) Derivation

The OAR is derived by dividing the **estimated overhead pool** by the **estimated activity base**. Here, the base chosen is **Direct Labour Hours** (the most common choice in labour-intensive engineering units).

$$
OAR = \frac{\text{Estimated Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Direct Labour Hours}}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
OAR = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000}
$$

$$
OAR = 12 \text{ ₹ per Direct Labour Hour}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every direct labour hour worked on any job, the firm will absorb **₹ 12** of factory overhead into that job's cost sheet. This is the **predetermined rate** — it does not change for individual jobs during the year.

## 3.2 Part (b) — Job Cost Sheet Preparation

A **Job Cost Sheet** is a tabular document with two columns — **Particulars (₹)** and **Total (₹)**. We now compute each line item step by step.

### Step 1 — Direct Material (DM)

$$
DM = \text{Material A} + \text{Material B} = 45{,}000 + 18{,}000
$$

$$
DM = 63{,}000
$$

### Step 2 — Direct Labour (DL)

$$
DL = \text{Hours} \times \text{Rate per hour}
$$

$$
DL = 800 \times 75
$$

$$
DL = 60{,}000
$$

### Step 3 — Direct / Chargeable Expenses (DE)

These are expenses directly attributable to *this specific job only* — here, the ₹ 12,500 subcontract payment for a specialised welding job on Job 107.

$$
DE = 12{,}500
$$

### Step 4 — Prime Cost (PC)

$$
PC = DM + DL + DE
$$

$$
PC = 63{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 12{,}500
$$

$$
PC = 1{,}35{,}500
$$

### Step 5 — Absorbed Factory Overheads (FOH)

Using the OAR computed in Part (a):

$$
FOH = \text{Actual Labour Hours} \times OAR
$$

$$
FOH = 800 \times 12
$$

$$
FOH = 9{,}600
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation:** The OAR (₹ 12/hr) is multiplied by the **actual hours worked on this job** (800), not the *estimated* hours. This is the standard absorption procedure in Specific Order Costing.

### Step 6 — Works / Factory Cost (WC)

$$
WC = PC + FOH
$$

$$
WC = 1{,}35{,}500 + 9{,}600
$$

$$
WC = 1{,}45{,}100
$$

### Step 7 — Presentation in Job Cost Sheet Format

| Particulars | Amount (₹) | Total (₹) |
| :--- | ---: | ---: |
| **Direct Material:** |  |  |
|  Material A | 45,000 |  |
|  Material B | 18,000 | **63,000** |
| **Direct Labour** (800 hrs @ ₹ 75) |  | **60,000** |
| **Direct / Chargeable Expenses** |  | **12,500** |
| **PRIME COST** |  | **1,35,500** |
| **Add: Factory Overheads Absorbed** (800 hrs @ ₹ 12/hr) |  | **9,600** |
| **WORKS / FACTORY COST** |  | **1,45,100** |

## 3.3 Part (c) — Invoice Price (Selling Price) Derivation

Since no administrative or selling overheads are given, the **Total Cost = Works Cost** in this problem. The profit margin of 25% is applied on Total Cost.

$$
SP = TC \times \left(1 + \frac{\text{Profit\%}}{100}\right)
$$

$$
SP = 1{,}45{,}100 \times \left(1 + \frac{25}{100}\right)
$$

$$
SP = 1{,}45{,}100 \times 1.25
$$

$$
SP = 1{,}81{,}375
$$

**Final Answer:** The invoice price to be quoted to the customer for **Job No. 107** is **₹ 1,81,375** (excluding GST, which is added separately as per applicable tax law).

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Cost Element | Computed Value (₹) | % of Works Cost |
| :--- | ---: | ---: |
| Direct Material | 63,000 | 43.43% |
| Direct Labour | 60,000 | 41.36% |
| Direct Expenses | 12,500 | 8.61% |
| **Prime Cost** | **1,35,500** | **93.40%** |
| Factory Overheads | 9,600 | 6.60% |
| **Works Cost** | **1,45,100** | **100.00%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical insight:** In this labour-intensive job, Direct Labour + Material dominate (~85% of cost), while overheads are modest (~6.6%). This justifies why *labour hours* is the appropriate absorption base. In a *machine-intensive* job (e.g., CNC machining), the OAR base would instead be **Machine Hours**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Job Costing — End-to-End Functional Flow

The following block-level architecture maps the entire data flow inside a Job Costing system — from customer order intake to invoice generation.
