| Inspection Pool | 1,00,000 | Number of inspections |
| Material Handling Pool | 1,00,000 | Number of material orders |
| **Total Overhead** | **4,00,000** | — |

---

### 3.2 Stage 1: Compute Cost Driver Rates

**Step A — Setup Cost Driver Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup Driver Rate} &= \frac{\text{Total Setup Cost}}{\text{Total Setups}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{100 \text{ setups}} \\[6pt]
&= \text{₹ } 2{,}000 \text{ per setup}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Inspection Cost Driver Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inspection Driver Rate} &= \frac{\text{Total Inspection Cost}}{\text{Total Inspections}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{200 \text{ inspections}} \\[6pt]
&= \text{₹ } 500 \text{ per inspection}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Material Handling Driver Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Material Handling Driver Rate} &= \frac{\text{Total Material Handling Cost}}{\text{Total Material Orders}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{100 \text{ orders}} \\[6pt]
&= \text{₹ } 1{,}000 \text{ per order}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Stage 2: Assign Overheads to Each Product

**Product X — Overhead Assignment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup OH} &= 20 \text{ setups} \times 2{,}000 = 40{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Inspection OH} &= 50 \text{ inspections} \times 500 = 25{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Material Handling OH} &= 30 \text{ orders} \times 1{,}000 = 30{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Total OH Assigned to X} &= 40{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 95{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Product Y — Overhead Assignment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup OH} &= 80 \text{ setups} \times 2{,}000 = 1{,}60{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Inspection OH} &= 150 \text{ inspections} \times 500 = 75{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Material Handling OH} &= 70 \text{ orders} \times 1{,}000 = 70{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Total OH Assigned to Y} &= 1{,}60{,}000 + 75{,}000 + 70{,}000 = 3{,}05{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification Check:**

$$
95{,}000 + 3{,}05{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \;\; \checkmark \text{ (matches total OH pool)}
$$

---

### 3.4 Stage 3: Compute Per-Unit Cost Under ABC

**Product X — Per Unit Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Material} &= 200 \\[4pt]
\text{Direct Labour} &= 2 \text{ hrs} \times 50 = 100 \\[4pt]
\text{Overhead per unit} &= \frac{95{,}000}{10{,}000} = 9.5 \\[4pt]
\text{Total Cost per unit (X)} &= 200 + 100 + 9.5 = \text{₹ } 309.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Product Y — Per Unit Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Material} &= 350 \\[4pt]
\text{Direct Labour} &= 3 \text{ hrs} \times 50 = 150 \\[4pt]
\text{Overhead per unit} &= \frac{3{,}05{,}000}{2{,}000} = 152.5 \\[4pt]
\text{Total Cost per unit (Y)} &= 350 + 150 + 152.5 = \text{₹ } 652.5
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Stage 4: Compare with Traditional Costing

Traditional method uses **Direct Labour Hours** as the single allocation base.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total DLH} &= (10{,}000 \times 2) + (2{,}000 \times 3) = 20{,}000 + 6{,}000 = 26{,}000 \text{ hrs} \\[6pt]
\text{Traditional OH Rate} &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{26{,}000 \text{ hrs}} = \text{₹ } 15.3846 \text{ per DLH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Overhead Per Unit (Traditional):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Product X} &= 2 \text{ hrs} \times 15.3846 = \text{₹ } 30.77 \\[4pt]
\text{Product Y} &= 3 \text{ hrs} \times 15.3846 = \text{₹ } 46.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Per-Unit Cost (Traditional):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Product X} &= 200 + 100 + 30.77 = \text{₹ } 330.77 \\[4pt]
\text{Product Y} &= 350 + 150 + 46.15 = \text{₹ } 546.15
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.6 Stage 5: Distortion Analysis Matrix

| Metric | Product X | Product Y |
|---|---|---|
| **ABC Cost per unit** | ₹309.50 | ₹652.50 |
| **Traditional Cost per unit** | ₹330.77 | ₹546.15 |
| **Distortion (Trad − ABC)** | +₹21.27 (over-costed) | −₹106.35 (under-costed) |
| **% Distortion** | +6.87% | −16.30% |
| **KTU Inference** | Simple product penalised | Complex product subsidised |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation (Board-Worthy Sentence):**
> *Product X is **over-costed** by ₹21.27 per unit and Product Y is **under-costed** by ₹106.35 per unit under the traditional method, demonstrating how a single volume-based allocation base distorts product cost information in a multi-product environment with diverse batch and product-level activities.*

---

### 3.7 Profit Impact Analysis (Bonus 2-Mark Insight)

Assume selling prices: **X = ₹400, Y = ₹700**

| Profitability | Product X | Product Y |
|---|---|---|
| Selling Price | 400.00 | 700.00 |
| ABC Cost | 309.50 | 652.50 |
| **Profit (ABC view)** | **+₹90.50** | **+₹47.50** |
| Profit (Traditional view) | +₹69.23 | +₹153.85 |
| Strategic Risk | Under-pricing risk | Over-pricing risk |

This proves ABC reveals the **true star product** of the company.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — ABC Two-Stage Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Resources: Salaries, Depreciation, Utilities, Rent] --> B[Identify Major Activities in the Value Chain]
    B --> C1[Activity 1: Machine Setup]
    B --> C2[Activity 2: Quality Inspection]
    B --> C3[Activity 3: Material Handling]
    B --> C4[Activity 4: Order Processing]
    C1 --> D1[Setup Cost Pool - Cost Driver: No. of Setups]
    C2 --> D2[Inspection Cost Pool - Cost Driver: No. of Inspections]
    C3 --> D3[Handling Cost Pool - Cost Driver: No. of Orders]
    C4 --> D4[Processing Cost Pool - Cost Driver: No. of POs]
    D1 --> E[Stage 1: Compute Cost Driver Rate per Pool]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F1[Product X - Driver Consumption]
    E --> F2[Product Y - Driver Consumption]
    F1 --> G1[OH Assigned to X = Rate x X Consumption]
    F2 --> G2[OH Assigned to Y = Rate x Y Consumption]
    G1 --> H1[Total ABC Cost of X]
    G2 --> H2[Total ABC Cost of Y]
    H1 --> I[Distortion vs Traditional: Revealed]
    H2 --> I
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — ABC vs Traditional Costing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TR["Traditional Costing - Single Base"]
        T1[All Overheads Pooled] --> T2[Single Allocation Base: DLH or MH]
        T2 --> T3[Overhead Rate = Total OH / Total Base]
        T3 --> T4[Product A Cost]
        T3 --> T5[Product B Cost]
    end
    subgraph AB["Activity-Based Costing - Multi-Driver"]
        A1[Overheads Traced to Activity Pools] --> A2[Pool 1 - Setup]
        A1 --> A3[Pool 2 - Inspection]
        A1 --> A4[Pool 3 - Order Processing]
        A2 --> A5[Driver Rate 1]
        A3 --> A6[Driver Rate 2]
        A4 --> A7[Driver Rate 3]
        A5 --> B1[Product A - Causal Share]
        A6 --> B1
        A7 --> B1
        A5 --> B2[Product B - Causal Share]
        A6 --> B2
        A7 --> B2
    end
    T4 --> CMP[Cost Comparison and Distortion Analysis]
    T5 --> CMP
    B1 --> CMP
    B2 --> CMP
```

### 4.3 Mermaid 4-Level Activity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Resource Costs in the Organisation] --> U[Unit-Level Activities - one per unit produced]
    R --> B[Batch-Level Activities - one per batch]
    R --> P[Product-Level Activities - per product line]
    R --> F[Facility-Level Activities - per plant]
    U --> U1[Driver: Machine Hours, DLH, Units Produced]
    B --> B1[Driver: Number of Setups, Inspections, Orders]
    P --> P1[Driver: Engineering Change Orders, Design Specs]
    F --> F1[Driver: Plant Area, Factory Rent, Admin Cost]
    B1 --> W[WARNING: Non-volume drivers cause distortion under DLH-based costing]
    P1 --> W
    F1 --> W
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Read the Diagram in the Exam:** Always start with **Resources → Activities → Pools → Drivers → Products**. This is the exact chain KTU's textbook diagrams follow. Memorise this flow — it appears in almost every KTU Module 3 question paper.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Activity-Based Costing. Mention any two limitations of traditional volume-based costing.*

**Model Answer:**

Activity-Based Costing (ABC) is a costing methodology that identifies the activities performed in an organisation, assigns the cost of each activity to products and services based on the actual consumption of cost drivers, rather than using a single volume-based allocation base such as direct labour hours.

Two limitations of traditional volume-based costing:

1. **Cost Distortion:** It uses a single base (DLH/MH) to allocate all overheads, which is inappropriate for batch-level, product-level, and facility-level costs that do not vary with volume. This leads to over-costing of high-volume products and under-costing of low-volume complex products.
2. **Inappropriate for Modern Automation:** In automated factories, direct labour is a small fraction of total cost, making DLH a poor proxy for overhead consumption. ABC resolves this by using multiple activity-based cost drivers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the four levels of activity hierarchy in ABC with one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit-Level Activity** | Performed each time a unit is produced | Direct labour hours, machine hours per unit |
| **Batch-Level Activity** | Performed each time a batch is produced | Machine setup, quality inspection per batch |
| **Product-Level Activity** | Performed to support a specific product line | Engineering design changes, product specifications |
| **Facility-Level Activity** | Performed to sustain the entire facility | Factory rent, plant manager salary, building depreciation |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> *(1) Students frequently forget to verify that the sum of assigned overheads equals the total overhead pool — this loses 1 mark instantly. (2) Many confuse the **cost driver rate** with the **total cost assigned**; the rate is per-unit-of-driver, the assigned cost is the rate multiplied by the product's driver consumption. (3) Students often skip the "Distortion Analysis" table — KTU's 2024 scheme specifically tests this in 2-mark sub-parts.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q from Module 3]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *A company manufactures two products P and Q using the same plant. The following data is extracted:*

| Particulars | Product P | Product Q |
|---|---|---|
| Production volume (units) | 5,000 | 1,000 |
| Direct Material per unit (₹) | 150 | 200 |
| Direct Labour hours per unit | 4 | 6 |
| Machine setups per year | 10 | 40 |
| Purchase orders per year | 20 | 80 |
| Quality inspections per year | 30 | 70 |

*Activity cost pools and drivers:*

| Activity Pool | Total Cost (₹) | Cost Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Setup | 1,00,000 | No. of setups |
| Purchasing | 1,00,000 | No. of purchase orders |
| Inspection | 80,000 | No. of inspections |

*Compute the cost per unit of each product using ABC.*

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Cost Driver Rates**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup Rate} &= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{10 + 40} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{50} = \text{₹ } 2{,}000 \text{ per setup} \\[6pt]
\text{Purchasing Rate} &= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{20 + 80} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{100} = \text{₹ } 1{,}000 \text{ per order} \\[6pt]
\text{Inspection Rate} &= \frac{80{,}000}{30 + 70} = \frac{80{,}000}{100} = \text{₹ } 800 \text{ per inspection}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Overhead Assigned to Product P**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup OH} &= 10 \times 2{,}000 = 20{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Purchasing OH} &= 20 \times 1{,}000 = 20{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Inspection OH} &= 30 \times 800 = 24{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Total OH (P)} &= 64{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Overhead Assigned to Product Q**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup OH} &= 40 \times 2{,}000 = 80{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Purchasing OH} &= 80 \times 1{,}000 = 80{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Inspection OH} &= 70 \times 800 = 56{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Total OH (Q)} &= 2{,}16{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $64{,}000 + 2{,}16{,}000 = 2{,}80{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000$ ✓

**Step 3: Per-Unit Cost Under ABC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Product P per unit} &= 150 + (4 \times 50) + \frac{64{,}000}{5{,}000} = 150 + 200 + 12.8 = \text{₹ } 362.80 \\[6pt]
\text{Product Q per unit} &= 200 + (6 \times 50) + \frac{2{,}16{,}000}{1{,}000} = 200 + 300 + 216 = \text{₹ } 716.00
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare the per-unit cost of each product under the traditional method (using direct labour hours as the single base) with the ABC cost. Comment on cost distortion.*

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Traditional Method:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total DLH} &= (5{,}000 \times 4) + (1{,}000 \times 6) = 20{,}000 + 6{,}000 = 26{,}000 \text{ hrs} \\[6pt]
\text{Total Overhead} &= 2{,}80{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{OH Rate per DLH} &= \frac{2{,}80{,}000}{26{,}000} = \text{₹ } 10.7692 \text{ per DLH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Per-Unit Cost (Traditional):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Product P} &= 150 + 200 + (4 \times 10.7692) = 150 + 200 + 43.08 = \text{₹ } 393.08 \\[4pt]
\text{Product Q} &= 200 + 300 + (6 \times 10.7692) = 200 + 300 + 64.62 = \text{₹ } 564.62
\end{aligned}
$$

**Distortion Table:**

| Metric | Product P | Product Q |
|---|---|---|
| ABC Cost | ₹362.80 | ₹716.00 |
| Traditional Cost | ₹393.08 | ₹564.62 |
| Distortion | +₹30.28 (over-costed) | −₹151.38 (under-costed) |
| % Distortion | +8.35% | −21.14% |

**Comment:** Traditional costing **over-costs Product P** (high-volume, simple) and **substantially under-costs Product Q** (low-volume, complex). This can mislead management into under-pricing Q and over-pricing P, leading to loss of market share in P and hidden losses in Q. ABC provides a more accurate cost picture, enabling better pricing, product-mix, and cost-control decisions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Adaptation]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the limitations of traditional costing system. How does Activity-Based Costing overcome these limitations?*

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Limitations of Traditional Costing:**

1. **Single Allocation Base:** Traditional costing allocates all overheads using one base (DLH or MH), which is not appropriate for batch-level, product-level, and facility-level activities.
2. **Cost Distortion:** It leads to over-costing of high-volume products and under-costing of low-volume, complex products.
3. **Ignores Non-Value-Adding Activities:** Traditional systems do not distinguish between value-adding and non-value-adding activities, providing no insight for process improvement.
4. **Unsuitable for Modern Manufacturing:** In highly automated plants, direct labour is a small fraction of total cost, making DLH a poor cost driver.
5. **Provides Aggregate Cost Data:** It does not reveal the *cause* of overhead costs, limiting management's ability to control them.

**How ABC Overcomes These Limitations:**

1. **Multiple Cost Drivers:** ABC uses a different cost driver for each activity pool, ensuring a causal relationship between activity and cost.
2. **Accurate Cost Assignment:** It traces costs to activities and then to products based on actual consumption, eliminating distortion.
3. **Activity Analysis:** ABC separates value-adding from non-value-adding activities, enabling continuous improvement (Lean/Six Sigma).
4. **Suitable for Automation:** ABC does not depend on direct labour hours, making it appropriate for modern, automated environments.
5. **Management Insight:** By exposing the *drivers* of cost, ABC empowers managers to control costs at the activity level.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A manufacturing firm has three activity cost pools. The following data is given:*

| Activity Pool | Cost (₹) | Cost Driver | Total Driver Volume |
|---|---|---|---|
| Machining | 4,00,000 | Machine hours | 10,000 hrs |
| Setup | 1,50,000 | No. of setups | 150 |
| Inspection | 50,000 | Inspection hours | 500 hrs |

*Two products A and B consume:*

| Driver | Product A | Product B |
|---|---|---|
| Machine hours | 6,000 | 4,000 |
| No. of setups | 50 | 100 |
| Inspection hours | 200 | 300 |

*Compute the overhead cost per unit if A produces 2,000 units and B produces 1,000 units.*

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Cost Driver Rates**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Machining Rate} &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = \text{₹ } 40 \text{ per MH} \\[6pt]
\text{Setup Rate} &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{150} = \text{₹ } 1{,}000 \text{ per setup} \\[6pt]
\text{Inspection Rate} &= \frac{50{,}000}{500} = \text{₹ } 100 \text{ per inspection hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Overhead Allocation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Product A Total OH} &= (6{,}000 \times 40) + (50 \times 1{,}000) + (200 \times 100) \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}40{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 3{,}10{,}000 \\[8pt]
\text{Product B Total OH} &= (4{,}000 \times 40) + (100 \times 1{,}000) + (300 \times 100) \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}60{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 2{,}90{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $3{,}10{,}000 + 2{,}90{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000$ ✓

**Step 3: Overhead Per Unit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Product A OH per unit} &= \frac{3{,}10{,}000}{2{,}000} = \text{₹ } 155 \\[4pt]
\text{Product B OH per unit} &= \frac{2{,}90{,}000}{1{,}000} = \text{₹ } 290
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** Although Product A consumes **60%** of machine hours, it absorbs only **51.7%** of total overhead because Product B consumes more setup and inspection resources — a classic case where traditional MH-based allocation would have wrongly favoured Product A.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Activity-Based Costing**
>
> ✅ ABC is a **two-stage** causal costing method: Resources → Activities → Products
> ✅ **Cost driver** = root cause of activity cost (e.g., number of setups); **Cost object** = product being costed
> ✅ Four activity levels: **Unit, Batch, Product, Facility** — non-volume levels (B, P, F) are the main source of distortion under traditional methods
> ✅ Driver Rate = Total Cost Pool ÷ Total Driver Volume
> ✅ Overhead Assigned to Product = Driver Rate × Product's Driver Consumption
> ✅ **Always verify:** Sum of assigned overheads must equal total overhead pool
> ✅ ABC suits companies with **product diversity, high overheads, and complex processes** (e.g., electronics, automobiles, pharmaceuticals)
> ✅ Traditional costing **over-costs** high-volume simple products and **under-costs** low-volume complex products
> ✅ ABC provides insight for **pricing, make-or-buy, product mix, customer profitability, and process improvement** decisions
> ✅ KTU 2024 scheme weightage: ABC is a **Module 3 core topic** with 14-mark derivations and 3-mark theory questions guaranteed every exam cycle
> ✅ **Common board trap:** Confusing "cost driver rate" with "total cost assigned" — the rate is *per unit of driver*, the assignment is *rate × consumption*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Costing and Variance Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Standard Costing and Variance Analysis # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is Standard Costing? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Costing** is a technique of cost accounting which compares the *standard costs* (predetermined costs) of products or services with the *actual costs* incurred during a period, to determine the **variances**, analyze the *causes* of variances, and report these variances to management for taking corrective actions. In simple words, Standard Costing is like preparing a **"budgeted recipe card"** before cooking a dish. The recipe card lists the *exact* quantity of ingredients (materials), time (labor), and fuel (overheads) that should ideally be used. After cooking, you compare what was *actually* used against the recipe card. Any difference (more flour, extra time, additional fuel) is a **variance** that needs investigation. ## 1.2 What is Variance Analysis? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Variance Analysis** is the systematic process of breaking down total cost variances into their sub-components (such as material price variance, material quantity variance, labor rate variance, labor efficiency variance, etc.) to identify the **root causes** of deviations from standards and to facilitate managerial control. > [!NOTE] > **Variance = Actual Cost − Standard Cost** > - If **Positive (Adverse/Unfavorable, U)**: Actual > Standard → Cost overrun (bad) > - If **Negative (Favorable, F)**: Actual < Standard → Cost saving (good) ## 1.3 Types of Standards Standards are not "one-size-fits-all." Different standards serve different managerial purposes: | Standard Type | Definition | When Used | |---|---|---| | **Ideal Standard** | Represents *perfect* conditions with no wastage, no idle time, no breakdowns. | Long-term benchmarks, theoretical performance | | **Normal Standard** | Anticipated *average* conditions over a business cycle, including normal wastage. | Most common in practice | | **Basic Standard** | Standards kept *unchanged* over long periods; used as a base for index numbers. | Trend analysis | | **Current Standard** | Standards revised to reflect *current* operating conditions. | Short-term operational control | ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are a **KTU engineering student preparing for a Semester Exam**: - You **plan** to study **6 hours daily** for **60 days** (Standard: 360 hours total). - You *actually* studied for **5 hours daily** due to distractions (Quantity variance) and you used **extra reference books** beyond budget (Price variance). - Variance Analysis tells you: *"You studied 30 hours less than planned (efficiency variance) and overspent ₹500 on books (price variance)."* This is exactly what Standard Costing does in a factory — it measures *what should have happened* vs. *what actually happened*. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Objectives of Standard Costing 1. **Cost Control**: Identify areas of excessive cost. 2. **Performance Evaluation**: Measure efficiency of departments, foremen, workers. 3. **Decision Making**: Provide reliable cost data for pricing, bidding, budgeting. 4. **Inventory Valuation**: Price raw materials, WIP, and finished goods at standard costs. 5. **Management by Exception**: Focus managerial attention only on significant variances. ## 2.2 Preliminaries for Installing Standard Costing - **Establishment of Standards**: For materials, labor, and overheads by the standards committee (consisting of engineers, accountants, production managers). - **Type of Standards**: Decide ideal, normal, or basic. - **Standard Cost Card**: A document showing standard quantity and standard price for each element of cost for one unit of output. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Cost Card** for one unit of Product X: > - Material: 4 kg @ ₹50/kg = ₹200 > - Labor: 2 hours @ ₹30/hour = ₹60 > - Overhead: ₹40 > - **Total Standard Cost = ₹300 per unit** ## 2.3 Classification of Variances (Master Framework) ``` Total Cost Variance ├── Material Cost Variance (MCV) │ ├── Material Price Variance (MPV) │ └── Material Usage/Quantity Variance (MUV / MQV) ├── Labor Cost Variance (LCV) │ ├── Labor Rate Variance (LRV) │ └── Labor Efficiency Variance (LEV) └── Overhead Cost Variance (OCV) ├── Variable Overhead Variance │ ├── Variable OH Expenditure Variance │ └── Variable OH Efficiency Variance └── Fixed Overhead Variance ├── Fixed OH Expenditure Variance ├── Fixed OH Volume Variance │ ├── Fixed OH Capacity Variance │ └── Fixed OH Calendar Variance └── Fixed OH Efficiency Variance ``` ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **ALL FORMULAS BELOW ARE EXAM-CRITICAL. Memorize the SQ × SP and AQ × AP logic.** Let: - $AP$ = Actual Price, $AQ$ = Actual Quantity, $SP$ = Standard Price, $SQ$ = Standard Quantity (for actual output) - $AR$ = Actual Rate, $AH$ = Actual Hours, $SR$ = Standard Rate, $SH$ = Standard Hours (for actual output) ### 2.4.1 Material Variances $$MPV = AQ \times (AP - SP) = (AQ \times AP) - (AQ \times SP)$$ $$MUV = SP \times (AQ - SQ) = (AQ \times SP) - (SQ \times SP)$$ $$MCV = MPV + MUV = (AQ \times AP) - (SQ \times SP)$$ ### 2.4.2 Labor Variances $$LRV = AH \times (AR - SR) = (AH \times AR) - (AH \times SR)$$ $$LEV = SR \times (AH - SH) = (AH \times SR) - (SH \times SR)$$ $$LCV = LRV + LEV = (AH \times AR) - (SH \times SR)$$ ### 2.4.3 Variable Overhead Variances $$VOH\text{ Expenditure} = AH \times (AR_v - SR_v)$$ $$VOH\text{ Efficiency} = SR_v \times (AH - SH)$$ ### 2.4.4 Fixed Overhead Variances $$FOH\text{ Expenditure} = Actual\ FOH - Budgeted\ FOH$$ $$FOH\text{ Volume} = Budgeted\ FOH - Absorbed\ FOH = (SH - Budgeted\ Hours) \times SFOH\ Rate$$ $$FOH\text{ Capacity} = (Budgeted\ Hours - Actual\ Hours) \times SFOH\ Rate$$ $$FOH\text{ Calendar} = (Actual\ Working\ Days - Budgeted\ Working\ Days) \times Standard\ Hours\ per\ Day \times SFOH\ Rate$$ ### 2.4.5 Sales Variances $$Sales\ Price\ Variance = Actual\ Quantity \times (Actual\ Price - Standard\ Price)$$ $$Sales\ Volume\ Variance = Standard\ Profit \times (Actual\ Quantity - Budgeted\ Quantity)$$ ## 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Manufacturing Firms**: Used to monitor production efficiency daily. - **Engineering Projects**: Standard costs for steel, cement, and labor are tracked against actuals in EPC projects. - **IT/Software Industry**: Standard hours for coding/testing modules vs. actual hours consumed. - **Construction**: Standard brick consumption per square foot vs. actual. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Logical Derivation of Material Cost Variance We start with a simple premise: $$\text{Material Cost} = \text{Quantity} \times \text{Price}$$ So, total material cost has *two* components — **Price** and **Quantity**. Any deviation can occur due to either. **Step 1**: Define what *actually* happened. $$Actual\ Material\ Cost = AQ \times AP$$ **Step 2**: Define what *should have* happened for the actual output produced. $$Standard\ Material\ Cost\ (for\ actual\ output) = SQ \times SP$$ **Step 3**: The total material cost variance is the difference. $$MCV = (AQ \times AP) - (SQ \times SP)$$ **Step 4**: To isolate the *price effect*, we hold quantity constant at **AQ** and compare actual price vs. standard price. $$MPV = AQ \times (AP - SP) = (AQ \times AP) - (AQ \times SP)$$ **Step 5**: To isolate the *quantity (usage) effect*, we hold price constant at **SP** and compare actual quantity vs. standard quantity allowed. $$MUV = SP \times (AQ - SQ) = (AQ \times SP) - (SQ \times SP)$$ **Step 6**: Adding Step 4 and Step 5: $$\begin{aligned} MPV + MUV &= (AQ \times AP) - (AQ \times SP) + (AQ \times SP) - (SQ \times SP) \\ &= (AQ \times AP) - (SQ \times SP) \\ &= MCV \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1: Material Variances **Problem**: The standard material required to produce one unit of Product X is **10 kg** at a standard price of **₹5 per kg**. During a period, **1,100 units** were produced. Actual material used was **11,500 kg** costing **₹63,250**. **Given Data**: - Standard Quantity per unit = 10 kg - Standard Price (SP) = ₹5/kg - Actual Output = 1,100 units - Actual Quantity (AQ) = 11,500 kg - Actual Cost = ₹63,250 → Actual Price (AP) = 63,250 ÷ 11,500 = **₹5.50/kg** **Step 1: Compute Standard Quantity for Actual Output** $$SQ = 1{,}100 \times 10 = 11{,}000 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2: Material Price Variance** $$\begin{aligned} MPV &= AQ \times (AP - SP) \\ &= 11{,}500 \times (5.50 - 5.00) \\ &= 11{,}500 \times 0.50 \\ &= ₹5{,}750 \text{ (Adverse)} \quad \text{— Price rose} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Material Usage Variance** $$\begin{aligned} MUV &= SP \times (AQ - SQ) \\ &= 5 \times (11{,}500 - 11{,}000) \\ &= 5 \times 500 \\ &= ₹2{,}500 \text{ (Adverse)} \quad \text{— Excess material used} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Material Cost Variance** $$\begin{aligned} MCV &= MPV + MUV \\ &= ₹5{,}750 + ₹2{,}500 \\ &= ₹8{,}250 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **Verification (Direct Method)**: $$MCV = (AQ \times AP) - (SQ \times SP) = 63{,}250 - (11{,}000 \times 5) = 63{,}250 - 55{,}000 = ₹8{,}250 \text{ (A)} \checkmark$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2: Labor Variances **Problem**: Standard labor for one unit of Product Y: **4 hours at ₹20 per hour**. Actual production: **500 units**. Actual hours worked: **2,200 hours**, actual wages paid: **₹48,400**. **Given Data**: - SH per unit = 4 hrs, SR = ₹20/hr - Actual Output = 500 units, AH = 2,200 hrs - Actual Wages = ₹48,400 → AR = 48,400 ÷ 2,200 = **₹22/hr** **Step 1: Standard Hours for Actual Output** $$SH = 500 \times 4 = 2{,}000 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 2: Labor Rate Variance** $$\begin{aligned} LRV &= AH \times (AR - SR) \\ &= 2{,}200 \times (22 - 20) \\ &= 2{,}200 \times 2 \\ &= ₹4{,}400 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Labor Efficiency Variance** $$\begin{aligned} LEV &= SR \times (AH - SH) \\ &= 20 \times (2{,}200 - 2{,}000) \\ &= 20 \times 200 \\ &= ₹4{,}000 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Labor Cost Variance** $$\begin{aligned} LCV &= LRV + LEV = 4{,}400 + 4{,}000 = ₹8{,}400 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **Verification**: $$LCV = (AH \times AR) - (SH \times SR) = 48{,}400 - (2{,}000 \times 20) = 48{,}400 - 40{,}000 = ₹8{,}400 \text{ (A)} \checkmark$$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3: Fixed Overhead Variances **Problem**: Budgeted fixed overhead = **₹60,000** for **6,000 standard hours**. Actual fixed overhead incurred = **₹64,000**. Actual hours worked = **5,500 hours**. Standard hours for actual output = **5,000 hours**. Standard fixed overhead absorption rate = ₹60,000 ÷ 6,000 = **₹10/hour**. **Step 1: Absorbed Fixed Overhead** $$\begin{aligned} Absorbed\ FOH &= SH \times SFOH\ Rate \\ &= 5{,}000 \times 10 \\ &= ₹50{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Fixed Overhead Expenditure Variance** $$FOH\ Expenditure = 64{,}000 - 60{,}000 = ₹4{,}000 \text{ (Adverse)}$$ **Step 3: Fixed Overhead Volume Variance** $$FOH\ Volume = (SH - Budgeted\ Hours) \times Rate = (5{,}000 - 6{,}000) \times 10 = ₹10{,}000 \text{ (Adverse)}$$ **Step 4: Total Fixed Overhead Variance** $$Total\ FOH\ Variance = 64{,}000 - 50{,}000 = ₹14{,}000 \text{ (Adverse)} \checkmark$$ **Step 5: Sub-division of Volume Variance** > **Capacity Variance** = $(Budgeted\ Hours - Actual\ Hours) \times Rate = (6{,}000 - 5{,}500) \times 10 = ₹5{,}000 \text{ (A)}$ > > **Efficiency Variance** = $(Actual\ Hours - SH) \times Rate = (5{,}500 - 5{,}000) \times 10 = ₹5{,}000 \text{ (A)}$ **Check**: Capacity + Efficiency = 5,000 + 5,000 = ₹10,000 (A) = Volume Variance ✓ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 4: Sales Variances **Problem**: Budgeted sales: **1,000 units @ ₹100 each** at standard cost **₹70**. Actual sales: **1,200 units @ ₹105 each** at actual cost **₹75**. **Step 1: Sales Price Variance** $$\begin{aligned} Sales\ Price\ Variance &= AQ \times (AP - SP) \\ &= 1{,}200 \times (105 - 100) \\ &= ₹6{,}000 \text{ (Favorable)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Sales Volume Variance (on Standard Profit)** $$\begin{aligned} Standard\ Profit\ per\ unit &= 100 - 70 = ₹30 \\ Sales\ Volume\ Variance &= ₹30 \times (1{,}200 - 1{,}000) \\ &= ₹6{,}000 \text{ (Favorable)} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.6 Cause-and-Effect Matrix (Causes of Variances) | Variance | Possible Causes | |---|---| | **Material Price (Adverse)** | Purchase from new supplier, market price rise, urgent order, loss of bulk discount | | **Material Price (Favorable)** | Bulk discount, price fall, use of substitute cheaper material | | **Material Usage (Adverse)** | Poor quality material, untrained workers, machine breakdowns, theft | | **Labor Rate (Adverse)** | Wage hike, use of higher-grade workers, overtime | | **Labor Efficiency (Adverse)** | Poor training, faulty tools, bad working conditions, low morale | | **Overhead Expenditure** | Unexpected rise in rent, insurance, supervisory salaries | | **Capacity (Adverse)** | Strikes, power cuts, idle time, absenteeism | | **Efficiency (Adverse)** | Lower productivity due to motivation issues | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Variance Decomposition Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Cost Variance
Actual Cost - Standard Cost] --> B[Material Cost Variance
MCV] A --> C[Labor Cost Variance
LCV] A --> D[Overhead Cost Variance
OCV] B --> B1[Material Price Variance
MPV = AQ x AP minus AQ x SP] B --> B2[Material Usage Variance
MUV = AQ x SP minus SQ x SP] C --> C1[Labor Rate Variance
LRV = AH x AR minus AH x SR] C --> C2[Labor Efficiency Variance
LEV = AH x SR minus SH x SR] D --> D1[Variable Overhead Variance
VOHV] D --> D2[Fixed Overhead Variance
FOHV] D1 --> D1A[VOH Expenditure
AH x ARv minus AH x SRv] D1 --> D1B[VOH Efficiency
SRv x AH minus SRv x SH] D2 --> D2A[FOH Expenditure
Actual minus Budgeted] D2 --> D2B[FOH Volume
Budgeted minus Absorbed] D2B --> D2B1[FOH Capacity
Hours Gap x Rate] D2B --> D2B2[FOH Efficiency
AH minus SH x Rate] ``` ## 4.2 Standard Costing Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1: Set Standards
SQ SP SH SR SOHRate] --> P2[Step 2: Establish Standard Cost Card] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Record Actual Costs
AQ AP AH AR] P3 --> P4[Step 4: Compute Variances
Actual minus Standard] P4 --> P5[Step 5: Analyze Causes
Investigate Root Cause] P5 --> P6[Step 6: Report to Management
Exception Reporting] P6 --> P7[Step 7: Take Corrective Action
Continuous Improvement] P7 --> P1 ``` ## 4.3 Material Variance Sub-Decomposition (2x2 Matrix Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SQ1["SQ x SP
Standard for Actual Output"] A1[Standard Material Cost] end subgraph AQ_SP["AQ x SP
At Standard Price"] A2[Actual Quantity at Standard Price] end subgraph AQ_AP["AQ x AP
Actual Material Cost"] A3[Actual Material Cost] end A3 -->|minus| A2 A2 -->|minus| A1 A1 -->|minus| A3 ``` | Cell | Component | Computation | |---|---|---| | Top-Left Cell | **Material Price Variance** | $AQ \times AP - AQ \times SP$ | | Bottom Cell | **Material Usage Variance** | $AQ \times SP - SQ \times SP$ | | **Total** | **Material Cost Variance** | $AQ \times AP - SQ \times SP$ | ## 4.4 Sales Variance Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD SV[Total Sales Variance] --> SPV[Sales Price Variance
AQ x AP minus AQ x SP] SV --> SVV[Sales Volume Variance
SP x AQ minus SP x BQ] SVV --> SMV[Sales Mix Variance] SVV --> SQV[Sales Quantity Variance] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define Standard Costing. List any three objectives of Standard Costing.** **Model Answer**: Standard Costing is a cost accounting technique that compares predetermined standard costs with actual costs to determine variances, analyze causes, and report to management for corrective action. Three objectives: 1. **Cost Control** — Identifies areas of excessive expenditure. 2. **Performance Evaluation** — Measures efficiency of departments and workers. 3. **Management by Exception** — Focuses attention only on significant variances. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for any three objectives, or any two with brief explanations.)* ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between Ideal Standards and Normal Standards.** **Model Answer**: | Basis | Ideal Standard | Normal Standard | |---|---|---| | Basis of Setting | Perfect conditions assumed | Average conditions over a cycle | | Wastage | No wastage allowed | Normal wastage allowed | | Attainability | Difficult to achieve | Reasonably attainable | | Use | Long-term benchmarks | Day-to-day operational control | | Motivation | May demotivate workers | Realistic target setting | *(3 marks for tabulated comparison with at least 3 distinguishing points.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**: > 1. **Sign Convention Errors**: Students frequently forget to mark variances as (A) or (F). KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for missing this on each variance. > 2. **Formula Confusion**: Mixing MPV with MUV formulas is the #1 error. Remember: **MPV uses AQ × price-difference**, **MUV uses SP × quantity-difference**. > 3. **No Verification**: Always verify $MCV = MPV + MUV$. Examiners reward the verification step. > 4. **Calendar vs Capacity**: Don't confuse these — Calendar relates to *days*, Capacity relates to *hours within available days*. > 5. **SQ Computation**: Always compute $SQ = Standard\ per\ unit \times Actual\ Output$. Using budgeted output instead of actual is a common trap. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (Choice 1): **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** The standard material input for one unit of product is 20 kg at ₹15 per kg. During June, the actual output was 500 units. The actual material used was 10,500 kg costing ₹1,68,000. Calculate all material variances. **(b) [7 Marks]** Standard hours for one unit of output = 5 hours, standard rate = ₹40 per hour. During a month, actual output was 400 units, actual hours worked = 1,900 hours, and total wages paid = ₹83,600. Calculate Labor Cost Variance, Labor Rate Variance, and Labor Efficiency Variance. #### **Solution to (a):** **Given**: - Standard Quantity per unit = 20 kg, Standard Price = ₹15/kg - Actual Output = 500 units, AQ = 10,500 kg - Actual Cost = ₹1,68,000 **Step 1: Compute Standard Quantity for Actual Output** $$SQ = 500 \times 20 = 10{,}000 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2: Compute Actual Price** $$AP = \frac{1{,}68{,}000}{10{,}500} = ₹16 \text{ per kg}$$ **Step 3: Material Price Variance** $$\begin{aligned} MPV &= AQ \times (AP - SP) \\ &= 10{,}500 \times (16 - 15) \\ &= 10{,}500 \times 1 \\ &= ₹10{,}500 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Material Usage Variance** $$\begin{aligned} MUV &= SP \times (AQ - SQ) \\ &= 15 \times (10{,}500 - 10{,}000) \\ &= 15 \times 500 \\ &= ₹7{,}500 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Material Cost Variance** $$\begin{aligned} MCV &= MPV + MUV \\ &= 10{,}500 + 7{,}500 \\ &= ₹18{,}000 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **Verification**: $$MCV = (AQ \times AP) - (SQ \times SP) = 1{,}68{,}000 - (10{,}000 \times 15) = 1{,}68{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹18{,}000 \text{ (A)} \checkmark$$ #### **Solution to (b):** **Given**: - SH per unit = 5 hrs, SR = ₹40/hr - Actual Output = 400 units, AH = 1,900 hrs - Actual Wages = ₹83,600 **Step 1: Compute Standard Hours for Actual Output** $$SH = 400 \times 5 = 2{,}000 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 2: Compute Actual Rate** $$AR = \frac{83{,}600}{1{,}900} = ₹44 \text{ per hour}$$ **Step 3: Labor Rate Variance** $$\begin{aligned} LRV &= AH \times (AR - SR) \\ &= 1{,}900 \times (44 - 40) \\ &= 1{,}900 \times 4 \\ &= ₹7{,}600 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Labor Efficiency Variance** $$\begin{aligned} LEV &= SR \times (AH - SH) \\ &= 40 \times (1{,}900 - 2{,}000) \\ &= 40 \times (-100) \\ &= ₹4{,}000 \text{ (Favorable)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Labor Cost Variance** $$\begin{aligned} LCV &= LRV + LEV \\ &= 7{,}600 \text{ (A)} + 4{,}000 \text{ (F)} \\ &= ₹3{,}600 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **Verification**: $$LCV = (AH \times AR) - (SH \times SR) = 83{,}600 - (2{,}000 \times 40) = 83{,}600 - 80{,}000 = ₹3{,}600 \text{ (A)} \checkmark$$ --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (Choice 2): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** From the following data, calculate: (i) Fixed Overhead Expenditure Variance, (ii) Fixed Overhead Volume Variance, (iii) Fixed Overhead Capacity Variance, (iv) Fixed Overhead Efficiency Variance. | Particulars | Value | |---|---| | Budgeted Output | 4,000 units | | Standard Hours per unit | 3 hours | | Budgeted Fixed Overhead | ₹60,000 | | Actual Fixed Overhead incurred | ₹64,000 | | Actual Hours Worked | 11,000 hours | | Actual Output | 3,800 units | **(b) [7 Marks]** The standard sales price of a product is ₹200 per unit and standard cost is ₹150 per unit. Budgeted sales were 1,000 units. Actual sales: 1,200 units at ₹210 per unit, actual cost ₹155 per unit. Compute: (i) Sales Price Variance, (ii) Sales Volume Variance, (iii) Sales Profit Variance. #### **Solution to (a):** **Step 1: Compute Standard Fixed Overhead Rate** $$SFOH\ Rate = \frac{Budgeted\ FOH}{Budgeted\ Hours} = \frac{60{,}000}{4{,}000 \times 3} = \frac{60{,}000}{12{,}000} = ₹5 \text{ per hour}$$ **Step 2: Compute Standard Hours for Actual Output** $$SH = 3{,}800 \times 3 = 11{,}400 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 3: Absorbed Fixed Overhead** $$Absorbed\ FOH = SH \times Rate = 11{,}400 \times 5 = ₹57{,}000$$ **(i) Fixed Overhead Expenditure Variance**: $$FOH\ Expenditure = Actual\ FOH - Budgeted\ FOH = 64{,}000 - 60{,}000 = ₹4{,}000 \text{ (Adverse)}$$ **(ii) Fixed Overhead Volume Variance**: $$\begin{aligned} FOH\ Volume &= Budgeted\ FOH - Absorbed\ FOH \\ &= 60{,}000 - 57{,}000 \\ &= ₹3{,}000 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Fixed Overhead Capacity Variance**: $$\begin{aligned} FOH\ Capacity &= (Budgeted\ Hours - Actual\ Hours) \times Rate \\ &= (12{,}000 - 11{,}000) \times 5 \\ &= 1{,}000 \times 5 \\ &= ₹5{,}000 \text{ (Adverse)} \end{aligned}$$ **(iv) Fixed Overhead Efficiency Variance**: $$\begin{aligned} FOH\ Efficiency &= (Actual\ Hours - SH) \times Rate \\ &= (11{,}000 - 11{,}400) \times 5 \\ &= (-400) \times 5 \\ &= ₹2{,}000 \text{ (Favorable)} \end{aligned}$$ **Verification**: Capacity + Efficiency = 5,000 (A) + 2,000 (F) = ₹3,000 (A) = Volume Variance ✓ #### **Solution to (b):** **Given**: - Standard Price = ₹200, Standard Cost = ₹150, Standard Profit = ₹50/unit - Budgeted Quantity = 1,000 units, Actual Quantity = 1,200 units - Actual Price = ₹210, Actual Cost = ₹155 **(i) Sales Price Variance**: $$\begin{aligned} Sales\ Price\ Variance &= AQ \times (AP - SP) \\ &= 1{,}200 \times (210 - 200) \\ &= ₹12{,}000 \text{ (Favorable)} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Sales Volume Variance**: $$\begin{aligned} Sales\ Volume\ Variance &= Standard\ Profit \times (AQ - BQ) \\ &= 50 \times (1{,}200 - 1{,}000) \\ &= ₹10{,}000 \text{ (Favorable)} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Sales Profit Variance**: $$\begin{aligned} Sales\ Profit\ Variance &= Actual\ Profit - Budgeted\ Profit \\ Actual\ Profit &= (AP - AC) \times AQ = (210 - 155) \times 1{,}200 = 55 \times 1{,}200 = ₹66{,}000 \\ Budgeted\ Profit &= (SP - SC) \times BQ = (200 - 150) \times 1{,}000 = ₹50{,}000 \\ Sales\ Profit\ Variance &= 66{,}000 - 50{,}000 = ₹16{,}000 \text{ (Favorable)} \end{aligned}$$ **Verification**: SPV + SVV = 12,000 (F) + 10,000 (F) = ₹22,000 (F). Note: This differs from Sales Profit Variance because the *cost variance* (₹6,000 A from actual cost being higher) also affects it. Cost variance = AQ × (SC − AC) = 1,200 × (150 − 155) = ₹6,000 (A). So: 22,000 (F) − 6,000 (A) = ₹16,000 (F) ✓ --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST FOR KTU EXAMS** - **Standard Costing** = Predetermined cost vs. actual cost comparison. - **Variance** = Actual − Standard. (A) means cost overrun, (F) means savings. - **Material Variances**: - $MPV = AQ \times (AP - SP)$ - $MUV = SP \times (AQ - SQ)$ - $MCV = MPV + MUV = (AQ \times AP) - (SQ \times SP)$ - **Labor Variances**: - $LRV = AH \times (AR - SR)$ - $LEV = SR \times (AH - SH)$ - $LCV = LRV + LEV = (AH \times AR) - (SH \times SR)$ - **SQ and SH must be for ACTUAL OUTPUT**, not budgeted output. - **Fixed Overhead Variance Tree**: Expenditure + Volume; Volume = Capacity + Efficiency. - **Sales Variances**: Price (revenue side) + Volume (quantity × standard profit). - **Favorable vs Adverse**: A lower cost or higher revenue is **Favorable (F)**; higher cost or lower revenue is **Adverse (A)**. - **Verification Step**: Always re-derive total variance directly to confirm sub-variances sum correctly. - **Causes Analysis**: Each variance has identifiable operational causes — interviewers love asking this. - **Standard Cost Card**: Foundation document showing SQ × SP, SH × SR, and OH rates per unit. - **Examination Tip**: KTU 14-mark questions often ask *two* variances (e.g., material + labor, or overhead + sales) with sub-parts — practice both simultaneously. - **Common Trap**: When computing AP or AR, divide total cost by total quantity/hours **carefully**. - **Format**: Always present variance calculations in tabular form with (A)/(F) labels for full marks.

topics.title: Marginal Costing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Marginal Costing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marginal Costing** is a fundamental managerial accounting technique under the broader framework of Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis. It is a system of **ascribing costs** to cost units whereby the costs of production are segregated into two components: **Variable Costs (Marginal Costs)** and **Fixed Costs**, and only the **Variable Costs** are charged to the products, while Fixed Costs are written off in full against the contribution for the period.

According to the **Institute of Cost and Management Accountants (ICMA, London)**:
> "Marginal Costing is the ascertainment, by differentiating between fixed and variable costs, of marginal cost and of the effect on profit of changes in volume or type of output."

The **Marginal Cost** itself is defined as:
$$\text{Marginal Cost} = \frac{\Delta \text{Total Cost}}{\Delta \text{Output (Units)}}$$

Where **Δ (Delta)** represents the change in the respective variable. In economic theory, marginal cost is the cost of producing **one additional unit** of output.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The Taxi Analogy:** Imagine you are a taxi driver who owns the car outright (no loan). The **fuel** you consume depends on the kilometers you drive — drive more, burn more fuel. The **car insurance, road tax, and depreciation** stay the same whether you drive 1 km or 100 km in a day. Now, suppose a passenger asks, *"What is the cost of taking me on this 1 km ride?"* The honest answer is: only the marginal fuel cost. The insurance and depreciation are **sunk** — they happen regardless. **Marginal Costing applies this exact logic to manufacturing businesses.**

In simple words:
- **Variable Costs** behave like *fuel* — they rise and fall with production.
- **Fixed Costs** behave like *insurance* — they are paid whether you produce 1 unit or 10,000 units.
- Marginal costing only assigns the *fuel* (variable) to each unit, and treats *insurance* (fixed) as a period expense recovered from total **Contribution**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition | Nature |
|------|------------|--------|
| **Marginal Cost** | Cost of producing **one additional unit** of output | Variable in nature |
| **Variable Cost** | Cost that varies **directly** with output | Per-unit cost is constant; Total varies |
| **Fixed Cost** | Cost that remains **constant** in total irrespective of output | Per-unit cost varies; Total is constant |
| **Semi-Variable Cost** | Cost having both fixed and variable components | Partially fixed, partially variable |
| **Contribution** | Sales minus Variable Cost — what remains to cover Fixed Cost and generate Profit | Sales − Variable Cost |
| **Contribution Margin (per unit)** | Selling price per unit minus variable cost per unit | Measured in ₹/unit |
| **P/V Ratio** | Contribution as a percentage of Sales | Expressed as a % |
| **Break-Even Point (BEP)** | The sales volume at which **Total Revenue = Total Cost** (Profit = Zero) | Units or ₹ |
| **Margin of Safety (MoS)** | Excess of actual/projected sales over break-even sales | Units or ₹ or % |
| **Key Factor** | The scarce resource (limiting factor) that constrains production | Material/Labour/Machine hours |

## 1.4 Why Marginal Costing Matters in Engineering Management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **engineer-turned-manager**, marginal costing is the bridge between technical production decisions and financial outcomes. Decisions like *"Should I produce this component in-house or buy it from a vendor?"*, *"Should I accept this special export order at a discount?"*, *"What is the minimum production volume to keep the plant viable?"* — all of these are answered rigorously by marginal costing.

Engineers in **manufacturing, process industries, EPC (Engineering, Procurement, Construction), and production planning** roles use marginal costing daily for:
- Pricing of special/export orders
- Make-or-buy decisions
- Profit planning and target costing
- Optimal product mix selection
- Shut-down or continue decisions

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Cost Behaviour Patterns

Understanding **how costs behave** as output changes is the bedrock of marginal costing. There are three fundamental patterns:

### 2.1.1 Fixed Cost Behaviour
- **Total Fixed Cost** is a horizontal straight line parallel to the X-axis (output axis).
- **Fixed Cost per unit** is a hyperbola — it falls steeply as output rises.

```
Total Fixed Cost:    |---------  (Constant, horizontal line)
FC per unit:         \___  (Declining curve / hyperbola)
```

### 2.1.2 Variable Cost Behaviour
- **Total Variable Cost** is a straight line rising from the origin at a constant slope.
- **Variable Cost per unit** is constant (horizontal line).

```
Total Variable Cost: / (Straight line from origin)
VC per unit:         |-----  (Constant horizontal line)
```

### 2.1.3 Semi-Variable (Mixed) Cost Behaviour
- **Total Semi-Variable Cost** is a straight line that starts from a positive Y-intercept (the fixed component) and slopes upward.
- It must be **segregated** using methods like:
  - **High-Low Method**
  - **Least Squares Regression Method**
  - **Scatter Graph Method**

## 2.2 The Concept of Contribution

> [!NOTE]
> **Contribution is the lifeline of marginal costing.** It is the amount that *contributes* toward covering fixed costs and, after they are absorbed, becomes profit. Think of contribution as the **survival money** of the firm — every rupee of sales, after paying for the variable cost, contributes to keeping the firm alive.

$$\text{Contribution} = \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost}$$

$$\text{Contribution} = \text{Fixed Cost} + \text{Profit}$$

When Contribution = Fixed Cost → **Profit = 0** (Break-Even Point).

## 2.3 Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis

CVP analysis is the **practical application** of marginal costing. It studies the **inter-relationship** between:
- **C**ost
- **V**olume (output/activity level)
- **P**rofit

The core assumptions of CVP analysis are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions of CVP / Marginal Costing:**
> 1. All costs can be **accurately classified** into fixed and variable.
> 2. **Fixed costs remain constant** throughout the relevant range.
> 3. **Variable cost per unit** is constant (linear behaviour).
> 4. **Selling price per unit** is constant.
> 5. **Sales mix** is constant (in multi-product firms).
> 6. **Inventory levels** are constant (production = sales) OR only changes in profit are measured.
> 7. The firm operates within the **relevant range** of activity.
> 8. The firm is a **price-taker** in a competitive market.

## 2.4 The Break-Even Point (BEP) — The Heart of Marginal Costing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the volume of sales at which the firm incurs **no profit and no loss**. It is the **threshold of survival**. Below BEP = loss; above BEP = profit. The graphic representation is the **Break-Even Chart**, where the Total Revenue line and Total Cost line intersect.

There are **multiple ways** to compute BEP, and the examiner expects you to know all of them:

### Method 1: Contribution Margin Approach
$$\text{BEP (in units)} = \frac{\text{Total Fixed Cost}}{\text{Contribution per unit}}$$

### Method 2: Equation Method
At BEP: Sales = Variable Cost + Fixed Cost + Profit (= 0)
$$S = V + F \implies \text{Let } S = P \cdot x, \, V = v \cdot x$$
$$P \cdot x = v \cdot x + F \implies x = \frac{F}{P - v}$$

### Method 3: P/V Ratio Approach
$$\text{BEP (in ₹)} = \frac{\text{Total Fixed Cost}}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$$

### Method 4: Graphical Method
Plot Total Cost line (starts at Fixed Cost on Y-axis, slopes upward with variable cost) and Total Sales line (starts at origin, slopes upward with selling price). The intersection is BEP.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **The following table is your KTU board-exam lifesaver. Memorize every formula, and remember to use `₹` for monetary units and `units` for quantity.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Notation |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Marginal Cost (per unit) | $MC = \frac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q}$ | ₹/unit |
| 2 | Contribution (Total) | $C = S - V$ | ₹ |
| 3 | Contribution per unit | $c = p - v$ | ₹/unit |
| 4 | **P/V Ratio** | $\text{P/V} = \frac{C}{S} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | **P/V Ratio (alt)** | $\text{P/V} = \frac{\text{Change in Profit}}{\text{Change in Sales}} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | **BEP (units)** | $BEP_{units} = \frac{F}{c}$ | units |
| 7 | **BEP (₹ sales value)** | $BEP_{₹} = \frac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ | ₹ |
| 8 | **BEP (using MC & MR)** | $BEP = \text{Point where } MC = MR$ | units |
| 9 | **Margin of Safety (MoS)** | $MoS = \text{Actual Sales} - BEP_{Sales}$ | ₹ or units |
| 10 | **MoS (%)** | $MoS\% = \frac{\text{MoS}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | **Required Sales (units)** | $x = \frac{F + \text{Target Profit}}{c}$ | units |
| 12 | **Required Sales (₹)** | $S_{req} = \frac{F + \text{Target Profit}}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ | ₹ |
| 13 | **Sales to earn given MoS** | $S = \frac{F}{\text{P/V} \times \left(1 - \frac{MoS\%}{100}\right)}$ | ₹ |
| 14 | **Profit using MoS** | $\text{Profit} = MoS \times \text{P/V Ratio}$ | ₹ |
| 15 | **Break-Even Sales (Multi-product)** | $BEP_{Total} = \frac{F}{\text{Composite P/V}}$ | ₹ |
| 16 | **Composite P/V Ratio** | $\text{Composite P/V} = \sum (\text{Sales Mix\%} \times \text{P/V of each product})$ | % |
| 17 | **Desired Sales Mix Ratio** | $\text{Proportion of A} = \frac{F + \text{Profit}}{\text{Total Contribution}}$ | ratio |
| 18 | **Operating Leverage (DOL)** | $DOL = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Profit}} = \frac{C}{P}$ | times |
| 19 | **Key Factor Profit** | $\text{Profit} = \left(\frac{C}{LF}\right) \cdot (\text{Available LF}) - F$ | ₹ |
| 20 | **High-Low Variable Cost** | $VC/unit = \frac{C_{high} - C_{low}}{Q_{high} - Q_{low}}$ | ₹/unit |

**Where:** $S$ = Sales, $V$ = Variable Cost, $F$ = Fixed Cost, $p$ = Selling price/unit, $v$ = Variable cost/unit, $c$ = Contribution/unit, $Q$ = Quantity, $C$ = Total Contribution, $LF$ = Limiting Factor units.

## 2.6 P/V Ratio — The Engineer's Strategic Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **P/V Ratio (Profit/Volume Ratio)** measures the rate at which profit changes with a change in sales. A higher P/V ratio means **more profit per rupee of sales** and a **lower break-even point**. From a managerial engineering perspective, the P/V ratio helps compare the profitability efficiency of different products, divisions, or processes.

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$$

A P/V ratio of **40%** means that for every ₹100 of sales, ₹40 contributes to fixed costs and profit. It is a **measure of efficiency**, not absolute profit.

## 2.7 Margin of Safety — The Cushion Against Loss

> [!NOTE]
> **Margin of Safety (MoS)** is the **amount by which sales can drop** before the firm begins to incur losses. It is the **buffer zone** of profitability. A low MoS signals high business risk; a high MoS signals resilience.

$$\text{Margin of Safety} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{Break-Even Sales}$$
$$\text{Margin of Safety \%} = \frac{\text{Margin of Safety}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$$

A related powerful relationship:
$$\text{Profit} = \text{Margin of Safety} \times \text{P/V Ratio}$$

## 2.8 Multi-Product Break-Even Analysis

In real engineering firms, multiple products share common fixed costs. The **Composite P/V Ratio** is used:

$$\text{Composite P/V Ratio} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Sales Mix\%}_i \times \text{P/V Ratio}_i)$$

Then:
$$\text{Composite BEP (₹)} = \frac{\text{Total Fixed Cost}}{\text{Composite P/V Ratio}}$$

The BEP in units of each product is then found by multiplying the composite BEP by the sales mix ratio.

## 2.9 Key Factor (Limiting Factor) Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Key Factor (Limiting Factor)** is the scarce resource that restricts a company's ability to produce — for example, raw material availability, machine hours, skilled labour, or factory space. When a key factor exists, products should be ranked by **Contribution per unit of the key factor** (not by contribution per unit of product), and the most profitable product is produced first until the key factor is exhausted.

$$\text{Rank} \Rightarrow \text{Sort by} \quad \frac{\text{Contribution per unit}}{\text{Key Factor per unit}} \quad (\text{Descending order})$$

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | Marginal Costing Application |
|----------|------------------------------|
| **Automotive** | Make-or-buy of components; pricing of spare parts |
| **Cement / Steel** | Optimal product mix of cement grades / steel sections |
| **EPC / Construction** | Bid pricing for tenders; cost-plus contracts |
| **Electronics Manufacturing** | Outsourcing decisions for PCBs, semiconductors |
| **Power Plants** | Optimal generation mix; merit order dispatch |
| **Process Industries** | Joint product / by-product cost allocation |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Basic BEP Formula (Contribution Margin Approach)

We begin from the fundamental equation. At any level of activity:

$$\text{Sales} = \text{Variable Cost} + \text{Fixed Cost} + \text{Profit}$$

Let:
- $S$ = Total Sales Revenue (₹)
- $V$ = Total Variable Cost (₹)
- $F$ = Total Fixed Cost (₹)
- $P$ = Profit (₹)
- $x$ = Number of units sold
- $p$ = Selling price per unit (₹/unit)
- $v$ = Variable cost per unit (₹/unit)

We know that:
$$S = p \cdot x \quad \text{and} \quad V = v \cdot x$$

Substituting:
$$p \cdot x = v \cdot x + F + P$$

Rearranging:
$$p \cdot x - v \cdot x = F + P$$
$$(p - v) \cdot x = F + P$$
$$x = \frac{F + P}{p - v}$$

At the **Break-Even Point**, profit $P = 0$. Substituting $P = 0$:
$$x_{BEP} = \frac{F}{p - v} = \frac{F}{c}$$

Where $c = p - v$ is the **Contribution per unit**. This is the **fundamental BEP formula**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: P/V Ratio from First Principles

We start with the **Identity Equation**:
$$S = V + F + P$$

From this, the contribution $C$ is defined as:
$$C = S - V$$

Substituting $C = F + P$ into the expression for P/V ratio:
$$\text{P/V} = \frac{C}{S} = \frac{F + P}{S}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 1 (the unity identity for sales):
$$\text{P/V} = \frac{F + P}{S} \times 100\%$$

This derivation makes it clear that **P/V ratio encapsulates both fixed cost recovery and profit generation** in a single elegant metric.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Profit = Margin of Safety × P/V Ratio

Let:
- $A$ = Actual Sales (₹)
- $B$ = Break-Even Sales (₹)
- $MoS$ = Margin of Safety = $A - B$

We can express the profit at actual sales:
$$P = A \cdot \text{P/V} - F$$

Also, at BEP, $B \cdot \text{P/V} = F$ (since contribution equals fixed cost at BEP):
$$F = B \cdot \text{P/V}$$

Substituting:
$$P = A \cdot \text{P/V} - B \cdot \text{P/V}$$
$$P = (A - B) \cdot \text{P/V}$$
$$P = MoS \cdot \text{P/V}$$

This is a beautifully useful relationship — it allows us to compute profit **without separately calculating variable cost**.

## 3.4 Worked Example 1: Single Product BEP (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A company manufactures a single product with the following data:
- Selling price per unit: **₹ 50**
- Variable cost per unit: **₹ 30**
- Total Fixed Costs: **₹ 4,00,000**

**Calculate:** (a) BEP in units, (b) BEP in ₹, (c) P/V Ratio, (d) Sales required to earn a profit of ₹ 6,00,000, (e) Profit at sales of ₹ 15,00,000.

**Solution:**

**(a) BEP in units:**

Contribution per unit:
$$c = p - v = 50 - 30 = 20 \, \text{₹/unit}$$

BEP (units):
$$x_{BEP} = \frac{F}{c} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{20} = 20{,}000 \, \text{units}$$

**(b) BEP in ₹:**

Sales value at BEP:
$$S_{BEP} = x_{BEP} \times p = 20{,}000 \times 50 = \text{₹ } 10{,}00{,}000$$

**(c) P/V Ratio:**
$$\text{P/V} = \frac{c}{p} \times 100 = \frac{20}{50} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**(d) Sales to earn ₹ 6,00,000 profit:**

Required contribution = Fixed Cost + Target Profit:
$$C_{req} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000$$

Required sales in ₹:
$$S_{req} = \frac{C_{req}}{\text{P/V}} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = \text{₹ } 25{,}00{,}000$$

Required sales in units:
$$x_{req} = \frac{C_{req}}{c} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{20} = 50{,}000 \, \text{units}$$

**(e) Profit at sales of ₹ 15,00,000:**

Total contribution at this sales level:
$$C = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = \text{₹ } 6{,}00{,}000$$

Profit:
$$P = C - F = 6{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 2{,}00{,}000$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 2: Multi-Product BEP (Composite Method)

**Problem:** A company makes two products, A and B, with the following data:

| Particulars | Product A | Product B |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| Selling price per unit | ₹ 100 | ₹ 150 |
| Variable cost per unit | ₹ 60 | ₹ 90 |
| Sales mix ratio | 2 | 3 |

Total Fixed Costs: **₹ 5,40,000**

**Calculate:** (a) Composite P/V Ratio, (b) Composite BEP in ₹, (c) BEP units of A and B.

**Solution:**

**(a) Per-unit P/V Ratios:**

For Product A:
$$\text{P/V}_A = \frac{100 - 60}{100} \times 100 = 40\%$$

For Product B:
$$\text{P/V}_B = \frac{150 - 90}{150} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Sales Mix Percentages:**

Total mix parts = 2 + 3 = 5 parts
$$\text{Mix}_A = \frac{2}{5} = 40\%, \quad \text{Mix}_B = \frac{3}{5} = 60\%$$

**Composite P/V Ratio:**
$$\text{Composite P/V} = (0.40 \times 0.40) + (0.60 \times 0.40) = 0.16 + 0.24 = 0.40 = 40\%$$

**(b) Composite BEP in ₹:**
$$BEP_{Total} = \frac{F}{\text{Composite P/V}} = \frac{5{,}40{,}000}{0.40} = \text{₹ } 13{,}50{,}000$$

**(c) BEP units of A and B:**

BEP of Product A:
$$\text{Sales of A at BEP} = 13{,}50{,}000 \times 0.40 = \text{₹ } 5{,}40{,}000$$
$$\text{Units of A} = \frac{5{,}40{,}000}{100} = 5{,}400 \, \text{units}$$

BEP of Product B:
$$\text{Sales of B at BEP} = 13{,}50{,}000 \times 0.60 = \text{₹ } 8{,}10{,}000$$
$$\text{Units of B} = \frac{8{,}10{,}000}{150} = 5{,}400 \, \text{units}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Multi-Product BEP:** Total units of A + B at BEP = 5,400 + 5,400 = 10,800 units. Weighted contribution per unit = (0.4 × 40) + (0.6 × 60) = 16 + 36 = 52. Total contribution = 10,800 × 52 / 5 (mix factor) = 5,40,000 (scaling)... Or simply check: Total contribution = 5,400 × 40 + 5,400 × 60 = 2,16,000 + 3,24,000 = 5,40,000 = Fixed Cost. ✓ **Break-even verified.**

## 3.6 Worked Example 3: Margin of Safety, P/V Ratio, and Profit Link

**Problem:** A firm has the following data:
- Total Fixed Cost: **₹ 1,80,000**
- BEP Sales: **₹ 6,00,000**
- Actual Sales: **₹ 9,00,000**

**Calculate:** (a) P/V Ratio, (b) Margin of Safety, (c) Profit.

**Solution:**

**(a) P/V Ratio:**
$$\text{P/V} = \frac{F}{BEP} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 0.30 = 30\%$$

**(b) Margin of Safety:**
$$MoS = \text{Actual Sales} - BEP = 9{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 3{,}00{,}000$$

MoS %:
$$MoS\% = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 33.33\%$$

**(c) Profit (using MoS × P/V formula):**
$$P = MoS \times \text{P/V} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = \text{₹ } 90{,}000$$

**Cross-verification:**
$$C = 9{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = 2{,}70{,}000$$
$$P = C - F = 2{,}70{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000 = 90{,}000 \, \text{✓}$$

## 3.7 Worked Example 4: Key Factor / Limiting Factor Analysis

**Problem:** A company produces two products, X and Y, with the following information:

| Particulars | Product X | Product Y |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| Selling price per unit | ₹ 40 | ₹ 50 |
| Variable cost per unit | ₹ 24 | ₹ 30 |
| Machine hours required per unit | 2 hrs | 4 hrs |

Total Machine hours available: **1,000 hours**
Total Fixed Costs: **₹ 5,000**

**Calculate:** (a) Which product is more profitable per machine hour? (b) Total profit if the optimal mix is followed.

**Solution:**

**(a) Contribution and Contribution per machine hour:**

Contribution per unit:
$$c_X = 40 - 24 = 16 \, \text{₹}, \quad c_Y = 50 - 30 = 20 \, \text{₹}$$

Contribution per machine hour (the key factor ranking metric):
$$\frac{c_X}{hr_X} = \frac{16}{2} = 8 \, \text{₹/hr}$$
$$\frac{c_Y}{hr_Y} = \frac{20}{4} = 5 \, \text{₹/hr}$$

**Ranking:** Product X ranks **first** (₹8/hr > ₹5/hr).

**(b) Optimal mix and profit:**

Produce **all 1,000 hours on Product X** (since it has higher contribution per scarce resource):
$$\text{Units of X} = \frac{1000}{2} = 500 \, \text{units}$$

Total contribution:
$$C_{total} = 500 \times 16 = \text{₹ } 8{,}000$$

Profit:
$$P = C_{total} - F = 8{,}000 - 5{,}000 = \text{₹ } 3{,}000$$

If the firm had **not** used key-factor analysis and had produced, say, 250 units of each:
$$C = 250 \times 16 + 250 \times 20 = 4{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 9{,}000$$
$$P = 9{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 4{,}000 \text{ (only by lucky coincidence; not optimal in general)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Insight:** Producing 500 of X and 0 of Y **maximizes contribution** from the scarce machine hours. The key-factor rule is: **rank products by contribution per unit of limiting factor**, then allocate the scarce resource in descending order of rank.

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix: Decision-Making with Marginal Costing

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix maps common management decisions to the marginal-costing formula. Engineers frequently face these in plant operations, supply chain, and project management roles.

| Decision | Marginal Costing Test | Action |
|----------|----------------------|--------|
| **Make or Buy** | Compare **Marginal Cost of Making** vs **Purchase Price** | If MC < Price → **Make** |
| **Accept Special Order** | Compare **Special Order Price** vs **Variable Cost** of additional production | If Price > VC → **Accept** (if no opportunity cost) |
| **Continue or Shut-Down** | Check if **Contribution** is positive after closure | If Positive → **Continue** in short run |
| **Drop a Product Line** | Compute the **Lost Contribution** vs **Saved Fixed Cost** | Keep if Lost Contribution > Saved FC |
| **Sales Mix Change** | Re-compute **Composite P/V Ratio** | Adopt if new Composite P/V > old |
| **Replace a Machine** | Compare **Variable Cost saved** vs **Incremental Fixed Cost** of new machine | Replace if savings > incremental FC |
| **Pricing Decision** | Use $P = \frac{F + \text{Profit}}{x} + v$ | Mark-up over marginal cost |

## 3.9 Differential Cost Analysis (Extension)

**Differential Cost** is the change in cost resulting from a decision alternative. It is closely linked to marginal costing.

$$\text{Differential Cost} = \text{Cost of Alternative A} - \text{Cost of Alternative B}$$

**Differential Revenue:**
$$\text{Differential Revenue} = \text{Revenue of Alternative A} - \text{Revenue of Alternative B}$$

A decision is favourable if **Differential Revenue > Differential Cost**, i.e., **Differential Profit > 0**.
