# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost Classification Flow (The Foundation of Marginal Costing)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Total Cost] --> nodeB{Fixed or Variable?}
    nodeB -->|Fixed| nodeC[Fixed Cost]
    nodeB -->|Variable| nodeD[Variable Cost]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Examples: Rent, Salary, Insurance, Depreciation]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Examples: Raw Material, Direct Labour, Direct Expenses]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Period Cost - written off in full]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Product Cost - charged per unit]
    nodeG --> nodeI[In Marginal Costing: Treated as Period Cost]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[In Marginal Costing: Charged to Product]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Total Contribution = Sales - Total Variable Cost]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Contribution covers Fixed Cost, balance = Profit]
```

## 4.2 Break-Even Chart (Cost-Volume-Profit Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Origin 0] -->|X-axis: Units| nodeB[Output Volume]
    nodeA -->|Y-axis: Rupees| nodeC[Sales/Cost]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Fixed Cost Line: Horizontal]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Variable Cost Line: From origin, slope v]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Total Cost Line: Starts at F, slope v]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Total Sales Line: From origin, slope p]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Intersection Point = BEP]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Above BEP: Profit Zone]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Below BEP: Loss Zone]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Vertical drop from BEP to FC line = Fixed Cost]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Vertical drop from BEP to X-axis = BEP Units]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Marginal Costing Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Managerial Decision Required] --> startB{Is there a Key Limiting Factor?}
    startB -->|No| startC[Single Product/Multi-Product with constant mix]
    startB -->|Yes| startD[Key Factor Analysis Required]
    startC --> startE[Compute Contribution per unit]
    startD --> startF[Compute Contribution per unit of Key Factor]
    startF --> startG[Rank products descending by C per unit of Key Factor]
    startG --> startH[Allocate scarce resource to highest rank first]
    startH --> startI[Compute total Contribution and Profit]
    startE --> startJ[Compute P/V Ratio and BEP]
    startJ --> startK[Apply Decision Test: MC vs Price, etc.]
    startI --> startK
    startK --> startL[Decide: Make/Buy, Accept/Reject, Continue/Close]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: CVP Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Sales Data] --> procA[Step 1: Classify Costs]
    inputB[Cost Records] --> procA
    inputC[Production Volume] --> procA
    procA --> procB[Step 2: Segregate Fixed and Variable]
    procB --> procC[Step 3: Compute Contribution C equals S minus V]
    procC --> procD[Step 4: Compute P over V Ratio]
    procD --> procE[Step 5: Compute BEP equals F divided by PV]
    procE --> procF[Step 6: Compute Margin of Safety]
    procF --> procG[Step 7: Compute Profit equals MoS times PV]
    procG --> outputA[Decision Support: Pricing, Mix, Make or Buy]
    procG --> outputB[Risk Assessment: Margin of Safety percent]
    procG --> outputC[Target Setting: Required Sales for Target Profit]
    procG --> outputD[Performance Evaluation: Actual vs BEP]
```

## 4.5 Marginal Costing vs. Absorption Costing — A Comparative View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    compA[Marginal Costing] --> compB[Only Variable Cost charged to product]
    compA --> compC[Fixed Cost treated as period cost]
    comp1[Absorption Costing] --> comp2[Both Variable and Fixed Cost charged to product]
    comp1 --> comp3[Fixed Cost absorbed via overhead rates]
    compB --> compD[Inventory valued at variable cost only]
    comp2 --> compE[Inventory valued at full cost]
    compC --> compF[Profit depends only on sales volume]
    comp3 --> compG[Profit depends on production and sales]
    compD --> compH[Used for internal decisions: KTU focus]
    compE --> compI[Used for statutory financial reporting]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Marginal Costing. State any two assumptions of CVP analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Marginal Costing is a technique of cost ascertainment that segregates total costs into **Fixed Costs** and **Variable Costs (Marginal Costs)**, and charges only the variable costs to products, while fixed costs are written off entirely against the contribution of the period.

**Two Assumptions of CVP Analysis:**
1. **Cost Behaviour:** All costs can be accurately classified into fixed and variable categories, with variable cost per unit remaining constant and fixed cost remaining constant in total within the relevant range.
2. **Selling Price Stability:** The selling price per unit remains constant irrespective of changes in volume, and the sales mix remains unaltered in multi-product situations.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the concept of 'Contribution' in marginal costing. How is it different from profit?**

**Model Answer:**

**Contribution** is the difference between **Sales Revenue** and **Variable Cost** of sales. It represents the amount that *contributes* toward the recovery of fixed costs and, subsequently, the generation of profit.

$$\text{Contribution} = \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost}$$

**Difference from Profit:**

| Basis | Contribution | Profit |
|-------|--------------|--------|
| Formula | Sales − Variable Cost | Contribution − Fixed Cost |
| Nature | Gross surplus | Net surplus |
| Fixed Cost | Not yet deducted | Already deducted |
| Purpose | Decision-making, planning | Performance reporting |

Contribution is a **decision-making concept**; profit is the **final accounting result**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1: Comprehensive BEP & CVP Problem

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**Question:** A manufacturing company provides the following information for the year ending 31st March 2024:

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|-------------|------------|
| Sales (20,000 units) | 20,00,000 |
| Direct Material | 6,00,000 |
| Direct Labour | 4,00,000 |
| Direct Expenses | 2,00,000 |
| Factory Overhead (60% fixed) | 3,00,000 |
| Selling Overhead (75% variable) | 1,00,000 |
| Administrative Overhead (Fully Fixed) | 2,00,000 |

**Required:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** Prepare a marginal cost statement and calculate the **BEP in units** and **BEP in ₹**.
- **(b) [7 Marks)** Compute the **P/V Ratio**, **Margin of Safety**, and the **sales required to earn a profit of ₹ 5,00,000**.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Classify Costs into Fixed and Variable**

| Cost Element | Total ₹ | Variable ₹ | Fixed ₹ |
|--------------|---------|------------|---------|
| Direct Material | 6,00,000 | 6,00,000 | 0 |
| Direct Labour | 4,00,000 | 4,00,000 | 0 |
| Direct Expenses | 2,00,000 | 2,00,000 | 0 |
| Factory Overhead (60% fixed) | 3,00,000 | 1,20,000 | 1,80,000 |
| Selling Overhead (75% variable) | 1,00,000 | 75,000 | 25,000 |
| Administrative Overhead | 2,00,000 | 0 | 2,00,000 |
| **Total** | **18,00,000** | **13,95,000** | **4,05,000** |

**Step 2: Marginal Cost Statement**

| Particulars | ₹ | ₹ |
|-------------|---|---|
| Sales (20,000 units) | | 20,00,000 |
| **Less: Variable Cost** | | |
| Direct Material | 6,00,000 | |
| Direct Labour | 4,00,000 | |
| Direct Expenses | 2,00,000 | |
| Variable Factory OH (40% of 3,00,000) | 1,20,000 | |
| Variable Selling OH (75% of 1,00,000) | 75,000 | 13,95,000 |
| **Contribution** | | **6,05,000** |
| **Less: Fixed Cost** | | |
| Fixed Factory OH (60% of 3,00,000) | 1,80,000 | |
| Fixed Selling OH (25% of 1,00,000) | 25,000 | |
| Administrative Overhead | 2,00,000 | 4,05,000 |
| **Profit** | | **2,00,000** |

**Step 3: Per-Unit Calculations**

Selling price per unit:
$$p = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{20{,}000} = \text{₹ 100 per unit}$$

Variable cost per unit:
$$v = \frac{13{,}95{,}000}{20{,}000} = \text{₹ 69.75 per unit}$$

Contribution per unit:
$$c = p - v = 100 - 69.75 = \text{₹ 30.25 per unit}$$

**(a) BEP Calculations**

BEP in units:
$$BEP_{units} = \frac{F}{c} = \frac{4{,}05{,}000}{30.25} = 13{,}388.43 \approx 13{,}389 \text{ units}$$

BEP in ₹:
$$BEP_{₹} = 13{,}388.43 \times 100 = \text{₹ 13,38,843}$$

> **Alternative verification using formula:**
> $$BEP_{₹} = \frac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}} \implies \text{P/V} = \frac{6{,}05{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 30.25\%$$
> $$BEP_{₹} = \frac{4{,}05{,}000}{0.3025} = \text{₹ 13,38,843} \text{ ✓}$$

**(b) P/V Ratio, MoS, and Required Sales**

P/V Ratio:
$$\text{P/V} = \frac{6{,}05{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 30.25\%$$

Margin of Safety (₹):
$$MoS = 20{,}00{,}000 - 13{,}38{,}843 = \text{₹ 6,61,157}$$

Margin of Safety (%):
$$MoS\% = \frac{6{,}61{,}157}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 33.06\%$$

Sales required for ₹ 5,00,000 profit:
$$S_{req} = \frac{F + P_{target}}{\text{P/V}} = \frac{4{,}05{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000}{0.3025} = \frac{9{,}05{,}000}{0.3025} = \text{₹ 29,91,736}$$

> **Verification via Profit formula:**
> $$P = MoS \times \text{P/V} = 6{,}61{,}157 \times 0.3025 = \text{₹ 2,00,000} \text{ ✓ (matches the statement)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2: Make or Buy Decision + Key Factor Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**Question:** XYZ Engineering Ltd. manufactures two products, P and Q, with the following data:

| Particulars | Product P | Product Q |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| Selling price per unit | ₹ 200 | ₹ 250 |
| Variable cost per unit | ₹ 140 | ₹ 150 |
| Machine hours per unit | 4 hours | 5 hours |
| Direct labour hours per unit | 2 hours | 3 hours |

Total available: **Machine hours: 8,000 hrs; Labour hours: 6,000 hrs**
**Total Fixed Costs:** ₹ 1,60,000

**Required:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the **Composite P/V Ratio** and the **BEP in ₹** assuming equal sales mix.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** Identify the **Key Factor**, rank the products by **contribution per unit of key factor**, and compute the **maximum profit**.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Per-Unit Contribution and P/V Ratio**

Contribution per unit:
$$c_P = 200 - 140 = \text{₹ 60}$$
$$c_Q = 250 - 150 = \text{₹ 100}$$

P/V Ratio per product:
$$\text{P/V}_P = \frac{60}{200} \times 100 = 30\%$$
$$\text{P/V}_Q = \frac{100}{250} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Step 2: Composite P/V Ratio (with equal mix)**

Since sales mix is equal (1:1), each product represents 50% of total sales:
$$\text{Composite P/V} = (0.50 \times 0.30) + (0.50 \times 0.40) = 0.15 + 0.20 = 0.35 = 35\%$$

**Step 3: Composite BEP in ₹**
$$BEP_{Total} = \frac{F}{\text{Composite P/V}} = \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{0.35} = \text{₹ 4,57,143}$$

**(b) Key Factor Identification**

**Compute contribution per unit of each potential limiting factor:**

For **Machine hours**:
$$\frac{c_P}{MH_P} = \frac{60}{4} = \text{₹ 15 per machine hour}$$
$$\frac{c_Q}{MH_Q} = \frac{100}{5} = \text{₹ 20 per machine hour}$$

For **Direct labour hours**:
$$\frac{c_P}{DLH_P} = \frac{60}{2} = \text{₹ 30 per labour hour}$$
$$\frac{c_Q}{DLH_Q} = \frac{100}{3} = \text{₹ 33.33 per labour hour}$$

**Determine Key Factor:**

For each factor, compute the **maximum possible output** if all hours were used on that product:
- If all machine hours on P: 8,000/4 = 2,000 units of P
- If all machine hours on Q: 8,000/5 = 1,600 units of Q
- If all labour hours on P: 6,000/2 = 3,000 units of P
- If all labour hours on Q: 6,000/3 = 2,000 units of Q

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test:** To make **1 unit of Q**, we need 5 machine hours and 3 labour hours. Available hours: 8,000 MH and 6,000 DLH. Suppose we make $x$ units of Q: Need $5x$ MH and $3x$ DLH. Machine hours constraint: $5x \leq 8{,}000 \implies x \leq 1{,}600$. Labour hours constraint: $3x \leq 6{,}000 \implies x \leq 2{,}000$. The **tighter constraint is machine hours** — so **Machine Hours is the Key Factor.**

**Ranking by contribution per machine hour:**
1. **Product Q**: ₹ 20 per machine hour ← **First priority**
2. **Product P**: ₹ 15 per machine hour ← **Second priority**

**Step 4: Maximum Profit Computation**

Produce all 8,000 machine hours on Product Q:
$$\text{Units of Q} = \frac{8{,}000}{5} = 1{,}600 \text{ units of Q, 0 units of P}$$

Total contribution:
$$C_{total} = 1{,}600 \times 100 = \text{₹ 1,60,000}$$

Maximum profit:
$$P_{max} = C_{total} - F = 1{,}60{,}000 - 1{,}60{,}000 = \text{₹ 0 (Break-even)}$$

> **Interpretation:** When the key factor is fully utilized on the most profitable product, contribution exactly equals fixed cost — the firm breaks even. Producing **any** Product P would actually **reduce** total profit (since ₹15/MH < ₹20/MH), because machine hours would be reallocated inefficiently.

> **If the firm had unlimited machine hours**, producing 2,000 units of Q and 0 units of P would yield:
> $$C = 2{,}000 \times 100 = 2{,}00{,}000$$
> $$P = 2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}60{,}000 = \text{₹ 40,000 profit}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Cost Classification Error (2-3 marks lost):** Mistaking **semi-variable costs** as fully fixed or fully variable. Always re-read the question: *"Factory Overhead is 60% fixed"* means **40% is variable**. Examiners explicitly test this in 7-mark problems.
>
> 2. **Wrong BEP Formula Application (3-4 marks lost):** Using `BEP = F / (p - v)` with **TOTAL** sales and **TOTAL** variable cost (instead of per-unit values). This gives a **meaningless result**. Always use **per-unit** values for `BEP (units)` and **P/V ratio** for `BEP (₹)`.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Show Both BEP Outputs (1-2 marks lost):** The question asks for "BEP" — examiners expect **both** units and ₹ value. If the question asks for *P/V Ratio*, you must show the formula substitution.
>
> 4. **Confusing Contribution with Profit (2 marks lost):** Contribution = Sales − Variable Cost. Profit = Contribution − Fixed Cost. They are **not interchangeable**.
>
> 5. **Key Factor Misidentification (4 marks lost):** In multi-product key factor problems, the **only** way to identify the key factor is to check which resource constraint **binds first**. Do not blindly pick the resource that "sounds" scarce.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Marginal Cost Statement Format (2 marks lost):** For 7-mark problems, the KTU examiner expects a **properly formatted marginal cost statement** with three sections: Sales, Less Variable Cost, Contribution, Less Fixed Cost, Profit. Avoid one-line calculations.
>
> 7. **Rounding Errors (1 mark lost):** When BEP comes out to 13,388.43 units, **never round up to 13,389** for the purpose of computing BEP in ₹. Multiply the exact value by selling price, then round. Or be consistent throughout.
>
> 8. **Forgetting the Formula for Sales to Earn Target Profit (3 marks lost):** Many students write `S = F / P/V` (this is BEP, not target profit sales). The correct formula is `S = (F + Target Profit) / P/V`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Marginal Costing (Module 3)**

- **Marginal Cost** = Change in Total Cost / Change in Output (per additional unit).
- **Marginal Costing** charges **only variable cost** to products; **fixed costs are period costs**.
- **Contribution** = Sales − Variable Cost = Fixed Cost + Profit.
- **P/V Ratio** = Contribution / Sales × 100 — measures **profitability efficiency**.
- **BEP (units)** = Fixed Cost / Contribution per unit.
- **BEP (₹)** = Fixed Cost / P/V Ratio.
- **Profit** = Margin of Safety × P/V Ratio (a powerful shortcut).
- **Margin of Safety** = Actual Sales − BEP Sales; in % = MoS / Actual Sales × 100.
- **Composite P/V Ratio** = Σ (Sales Mix % × Individual P/V) — used in **multi-product firms**.
- **Key Factor** = the resource that limits production; rank products by **Contribution per unit of Key Factor**.
- **Operating Leverage (DOL)** = Contribution / Profit — measures business risk; higher DOL = higher risk.
- **Assumptions to memorize:** Fixed cost constant; Variable cost per unit constant; Sales price constant; Linear cost behaviour; Constant sales mix; Constant inventory OR only change in profit measured.
- **Make or Buy:** Make if Marginal Cost of Making < Purchase Price (provided fixed cost is not affected or saved FC is considered).
- **Special Order Decision:** Accept if Special Price > Variable Cost (and idle capacity exists, no opportunity cost).
- **Shut-down Decision:** Continue in the short run if positive contribution is earned (i.e., revenue covers variable cost).
- **Profit = (Sales × P/V) − Fixed Cost** — use this master formula for quick recomputation.
- **Required Sales (₹)** for Target Profit = (Fixed Cost + Target Profit) / P/V Ratio.
- **In every problem, FIRST classify costs, SECOND prepare the marginal cost statement, THIRD compute P/V and BEP, FOURTH answer the sub-questions.**
- **Always cross-verify:** Total Contribution at BEP must equal Total Fixed Cost.
- **Diagrams to draw in exam:** Cost classification tree, Break-Even Chart (sketch axes, FC line, VC line, TC line, Sales line, mark BEP).
- **Numerical accuracy tip:** Use ₹ for monetary values, "units" for quantities, "%" for ratios — keep units explicit in every line of the answer.

> **Final Word:** Marginal costing is **not just a chapter** — it is the **decision-making backbone** of every engineering manager. In the KTU 2024 scheme exam, this topic is **consistently high-weightage** in Part B and is also tested conceptually in Part A. Master the formulas, practice at least 5-7 numerical problems (especially multi-product and key factor types), and you are guaranteed full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost - Volume-Profit Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cost - Volume-Profit Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis** is a managerial accounting technique that systematically examines the inter-relationship between **Cost**, **Volume** (activity/output level), and **Profit**, in order to determine how changes in these variables affect a firm's operating profit and to assist in short-term decision-making.

In the precise KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CVP analysis is also referred to as **Break-Even Analysis** or **Profit-Planning Analysis**. It is built on the **marginal costing** philosophy, where costs are segregated into **Fixed Costs (FC)** and **Variable Costs (VC)**, and the focus is on **Contribution Margin** rather than profit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The module mandates study of CVP analysis including assumptions, P/V ratio, break-even point (BEP) — single and multi-product, margin of safety, and the effect of changes in selling price, cost, and mix on profit.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tub" Intuition

Imagine a **large water tub** representing your **Total Contribution** (the pool of money left after paying variable costs from sales).

- The **bottom of the tub is fixed** at the level of **Fixed Costs** (rent, salaries, depreciation). The tub *must* fill up to this level before any profit appears.
- **Water pouring in** represents the **Contribution per unit** sold.
- **Once the tub overflows past the fixed cost line**, every additional drop is pure **Profit**.
- The **height at which water starts overflowing** is the **Break-Even Point (BEP)**.
- The **distance between the current water level and the overflow rim** is the **Margin of Safety** — how much sales can fall before losses begin.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic:** *“Cushion-First, Profit-Later”* — In CVP, contribution must first **cushion** the fixed costs; only the surplus becomes profit.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Introduced

| Term | Plain-English Meaning | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Sales Revenue | Total money received from customers | $S$ |
| Variable Cost | Costs that change **directly** with output | $VC$ |
| Fixed Cost | Costs that remain **constant** regardless of output | $FC$ |
| Contribution | Sales minus Variable Cost (the "earning power") | $C$ |
| Contribution per unit | Contribution earned on each extra unit sold | $c$ |
| P/V Ratio | Contribution as a percentage of Sales | $\text{P/V}$ |
| Break-Even Point | Sales volume where Total Cost = Total Sales (zero profit) | $\text{BEP}$ |
| Margin of Safety | The "cushion" of sales above BEP | $\text{MoS}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Behavioural Classification of Costs

CVP analysis is founded on a strict **cost-behaviour model**:

**1. Fixed Costs (FC)**
- Remain **constant in total** but vary **per unit** inversely with output.
- Examples: Factory rent, supervisor salary, straight-line depreciation, insurance premium.

**2. Variable Costs (VC)**
- Vary **in total** in direct proportion to output, but remain **constant per unit**.
- Examples: Direct materials, direct labour (piece-rate), sales commission, packing cost.

**3. Semi-Variable Costs**
- Contain both fixed and variable components (e.g., electricity bill — fixed service charge + variable usage charge).
- For CVP purposes, these must be **split** into fixed and variable elements using the **High-Low Method** or **Scattergraph Method**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to split semi-variable costs before applying CVP formulas. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this oversight.

---

## 2.2 Fundamental Relationships

The core CVP equation is built as follows:

$$\text{Profit} = \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost} - \text{Fixed Cost}$$

$$\text{Profit} = \text{Contribution} - \text{Fixed Cost}$$

Since **Contribution** is the pivotal concept:

$$\text{Contribution} = \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Expression | Units / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Contribution (Total) | $C = S - VC$ | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 2 | Contribution per unit | $c = p - v$ | ₹ per unit |
| 3 | P/V Ratio | $\text{P/V} = \dfrac{C}{S} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 4 | P/V Ratio (alternate) | $\text{P/V} = \dfrac{c}{p} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 5 | Break-Even Point (units) | $\text{BEP}_{Q} = \dfrac{FC}{c}$ | Units |
| 6 | Break-Even Point (₹) | $\text{BEP}_{S} = \dfrac{FC}{\text{P/V ratio}} \times 100$ | ₹ |
| 7 | Desired Sales Volume (units) | $Q^{*} = \dfrac{FC + P_{t}}{c}$ | Units |
| 8 | Desired Sales Value (₹) | $S^{*} = \dfrac{FC + P_{t}}{\text{P/V ratio}} \times 100$ | ₹ |
| 9 | Margin of Safety (absolute) | $\text{MoS} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$ | ₹ |
| 10 | Margin of Safety Ratio | $\text{MoS\%} = \dfrac{\text{MoS}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 11 | Profit using MoS | $\text{Profit} = \text{MoS} \times \text{P/V ratio}$ | ₹ |
| 12 | Multi-product BEP — Weighted Avg P/V | $\text{WAPV} = \sum (w_i \times \text{P/V}_i)$ | Percentage (%) |
| 13 | Overall BEP (multi-product) | $\text{BEP}_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{FC}{\text{WAPV}} \times 100$ | ₹ |
| 14 | Cash Break-Even Point | $\text{Cash BEP} = \dfrac{FC - \text{Depreciation}}{c}$ | Units |
| 15 | Operating Leverage (DOL) | $\text{DOL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Profit}}$ | Times |

Where:
- $p$ = Selling price per unit, $v$ = Variable cost per unit, $c$ = Contribution per unit
- $S$ = Sales revenue, $P_t$ = Target profit
- $w_i$ = Sales mix proportion of product $i$
- $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes magnitude when evaluating negative profit scenarios.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

CVP analysis is not merely an academic exercise. In the **engineering industry**, it underpins:

- **Make-or-Buy Decisions** — Should we manufacture a component in-house (incurring fixed setup costs) or buy from a vendor?
- **Product Mix Selection** — In a multi-product factory, which product line maximizes contribution per machine-hour?
- **Plant Shutdown Decisions** — Will temporarily shutting down a loss-making unit reduce overall losses?
- **Pricing Tenders** — At what minimum volume does a quoted engineering contract become profitable?
- **Capital Budgeting (preliminary)** — What sales volume must a new production line achieve to justify its installation cost?

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Real-World Connect:** Many Kerala-based MSMEs in the electronics and rubber-products sector use CVP analysis instinctively to set monthly production targets before their break-even date.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Point

We start from the fundamental profit identity:

$$\text{Profit} = \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost} - \text{Fixed Cost}$$

**Step 1:** Express Sales and Variable Cost in terms of units sold ($Q$), unit selling price ($p$), and unit variable cost ($v$):

$$S = p \cdot Q, \quad VC = v \cdot Q$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the profit identity:

$$\text{Profit} = pQ - vQ - FC = Q(p - v) - FC$$

**Step 3:** At the Break-Even Point, profit = 0. Setting Profit = 0:

$$0 = Q_{\text{BEP}}(p - v) - FC$$

**Step 4:** Note that $(p - v)$ is the **contribution per unit**, denoted $c$. So:

$$Q_{\text{BEP}}(p - v) = FC$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $Q_{\text{BEP}}$:

$$Q_{\text{BEP}} = \frac{FC}{p - v} = \frac{FC}{c}$$

**Step 6:** To get BEP in rupees (sales value), multiply by price:

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} = Q_{\text{BEP}} \times p = \frac{FC \cdot p}{p - v} = \frac{FC}{\text{P/V ratio}}$$

This is the rigorous derivation of the two most-tested CVP formulas.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Single Product

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> The Apex Engineering Co. manufactures a precision gear. The data per unit is as follows:
> - Selling Price (p) = **₹200 per unit**
> - Variable Cost (v) = **₹120 per unit**
> - Total Fixed Costs (FC) = **₹4,00,000 per annum**
>
> **Required:**
> 1. Calculate the **P/V Ratio**.
> 2. Calculate the **Break-Even Point** in units and in rupees.
> 3. Calculate the **sales required to earn a profit of ₹1,20,000**.
> 4. If actual sales in a year were **5,000 units**, calculate the **Margin of Safety** and **Profit**.
> 5. What is the **Operating Leverage** at the actual sales level?

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step (a): Compute Contribution per unit**

$$c = p - v = 200 - 120 = ₹80 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step (b): P/V Ratio**

$$\text{P/V} = \frac{c}{p} \times 100 = \frac{80}{200} \times 100 = 40\%$$

> Hence every ₹100 of sales generates ₹40 of contribution.

**Step (c): Break-Even Point in Units**

$$Q_{\text{BEP}} = \frac{FC}{c} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{80} = 5{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step (d): Break-Even Point in Rupees**

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} = \frac{FC}{\text{P/V ratio}} \times 100 = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{40} \times 100 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$$

> Cross-check: $5{,}000 \text{ units} \times ₹200 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$ ✓

**Step (e): Sales Volume for Target Profit of ₹1,20,000**

$$Q^{*} = \frac{FC + P_t}{c} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000}{80} = \frac{5{,}20{,}000}{80} = 6{,}500 \text{ units}$$

**Step (f): Margin of Safety (MoS)**

Actual Sales Value = $5{,}000 \times 200 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$

$$\text{MoS} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales} = 10{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = 0$$

$$\text{MoS\%} = \frac{0}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 0\%$$

> This implies Apex is operating **exactly at break-even**. The margin of safety is zero, indicating **high business risk**.

**Step (g): Profit at actual sales**

$$\text{Profit} = \text{Contribution} - FC = (5{,}000 \times 80) - 4{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹0$$

**Step (h): Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)**

$$\text{DOL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Profit}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{0} = \text{Undefined (}\infty\text{)}$$

> When actual sales equal BEP sales, DOL is theoretically infinite — a 1% change in sales causes an infinitely large percentage change in profit. This is the **maximum-risk zone**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Effect of Multiple Changes

**Problem:** Continuing the Apex example, suppose the company undertakes the following **simultaneous** changes next year:

- Selling price is **reduced from ₹200 to ₹190** (to be competitive)
- Variable cost per unit **rises from ₹120 to ₹128** (due to raw-material inflation)
- Fixed costs **increase from ₹4,00,000 to ₹4,80,000**
- Target sales volume is **6,000 units**

**Find the new profit.**

**Step (a): New contribution per unit**

$$c_{\text{new}} = 190 - 128 = ₹62 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step (b): New total contribution**

$$C_{\text{new}} = 6{,}000 \times 62 = ₹3{,}72{,}000$$

**Step (c): New Profit**

$$\text{Profit} = C_{\text{new}} - FC_{\text{new}} = 3{,}72{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = -₹1{,}08{,}000 \text{ (Loss)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often compute each change in isolation and add/subtract them. **This is wrong.** The correct method is to **rebuild the entire CVP structure from scratch** with the new values. The "sequential change" approach introduces compounding errors.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of Results

| Scenario | MoS% | DOL | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| MoS very high (>40%) | High | Low (~1.5×) | **Safe zone** — cushion against sales decline |
| MoS around 20%–40% | Moderate | Moderate | **Healthy zone** — typical of stable firms |
| MoS around 10%–20% | Low | High (>3×) | **Risk zone** — vulnerable to recession |
| MoS < 10% | Very low | Very high (>5×) | **Danger zone** — one bad quarter can cause loss |
| MoS = 0 | Zero | Infinite | **BEP exactly** — any decline causes loss |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CVP System Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following block diagram captures how CVP analysis is operationalised within a firm's management accounting information system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[INPUT DATA LAYER] --> nodeB[COST BEHAVIOUR CLASSIFICATION]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Fixed Cost Pool FC]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Variable Cost Pool VC]
    nodeA --> nodeE[REVENUE LAYER: Selling Price p, Volume Q]
    nodeC --> nodeF[CONTRIBUTION ENGINE]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Contribution C = S minus VC]
    nodeG --> nodeH[P/V RATIO CALCULATOR]
    nodeH --> nodeI[BREAK EVEN POINT SOLVER]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[BEP Units]
    nodeI --> nodeK[BEP Rupees]
    nodeG --> nodeL[PROFIT ENGINE]
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Profit equals C minus FC]
    nodeI --> nodeN[MARGIN OF SAFETY MODULE]
    nodeE --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO[MoS equals Actual Sales minus BEP Sales]
    nodeL --> nodeP[OPERATING LEVERAGE METER]
    nodeG --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Decision Output to Management]
    nodeH --> nodeQ
    nodeO --> nodeQ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows represent **flow of financial data**. The central engine is the **Contribution Engine** — every key metric (BEP, MoS, DOL) ultimately derives from it.

---

## 4.2 Multi-Product BEP Decision Flow

When a firm produces more than one product, a weighted-average approach is mandatory. The decision-flow is:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Multi Product BEP Request]) --> collect[Gather Data per Product]
    collect --> price[Identify Selling Price pi]
    collect --> vcost[Identify Variable Cost vi]
    collect --> mix[Identify Sales Mix Ratio wi]
    price --> contrib[Compute Contribution ci equals pi minus vi]
    vcost --> contrib
    contrib --> pvr[Compute Individual P/V Ratio]
    pvr --> weighted[Compute Weighted Average P/V Ratio]
    mix --> weighted
    weighted --> bepval[Compute Overall BEP in Rupees]
    bepval --> alloc[Allocate BEP Value per Product by Mix]
    alloc --> result([Print Break Even Sales per Product])
```

---

## 4.3 Profit Volume Graph (Conceptual Block Layout)

A conventional **Profit-Volume (P/V) graph** plots Profit (Y-axis) against Volume (X-axis). The schematic architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    origin[Origin 0,0] --> xaxis[Volume Axis horizontal]
    origin --> yaxis[Profit Axis vertical]
    yaxis --> fcLine[Fixed Cost Loss Line at minus FC]
    xaxis --> bepPoint[BEP Marked at Q BEP on X axis]
    fcLine --> profitLine[Profit Line Slope equals c passing through minus FC at zero volume]
    profitLine --> bepPoint
    bepPoint --> profitZone[Profit Zone to the right of BEP]
    bepPoint --> lossZone[Loss Zone to the left of BEP]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hint:** When asked to draw a P/V graph in the KTU answer sheet, always label: the **slope** as $c$, the **Y-intercept** as $-FC$, the **X-intercept** as BEP units, and shade the **profit zone** distinctly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis. State any four assumptions of CVP analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** CVP analysis is a managerial accounting technique that studies the relationship between cost, volume, and profit to determine the break-even point and to assess the impact of changes in selling price, costs, and volume on profit.

**Four Key Assumptions:**

1. **Behaviour of costs:** All costs can be accurately classified into fixed and variable categories; semi-variable costs are split.
2. **Linearity:** Both total cost and total revenue are linear functions of output within the relevant range.
3. **Constant selling price:** The selling price per unit remains unchanged across all volume levels.
4. **Constant sales mix:** In multi-product firms, the relative proportion of products sold remains stable.
5. **No inventory changes:** Sales volume equals production volume (opening and closing stock are constant).
6. **Single product or constant mix:** The analysis is conducted for either a single product or a stable product mix.

*(Any four carry ½ mark each; definition carries 1 mark. Total: 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between **Contribution** and **Profit**. How is P/V ratio calculated?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Contribution** is the difference between Sales and Variable Cost. It represents the amount available to cover fixed costs and generate profit.
> $$\text{Contribution} = \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost}$$
>
> **Profit** is the residual amount left after deducting *both* variable costs and fixed costs from sales.
> $$\text{Profit} = \text{Contribution} - \text{Fixed Cost}$$

**P/V Ratio Calculation:**

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 = \frac{c}{p} \times 100$$

It measures the **rate at which sales contribute** toward fixed costs and profit. A higher P/V ratio indicates greater efficiency in covering fixed costs.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Exam Pattern Reminder (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice**. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A. [14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**A single-product engineering company provides the following data:**

| Particulars | Amount |
|---|---|
| Selling price per unit | ₹150 |
| Variable cost per unit | ₹90 |
| Fixed costs (total) | ₹3,00,000 |

**Compute:**
1. The **Break-Even Point (BEP)** in units and in sales value.
2. The **sales volume** required to earn a target profit of ₹90,000.
3. The **Margin of Safety** and its percentage, given that the company actually sold **8,000 units** in the year.
4. The **Profit** if selling price is reduced by ₹10 per unit (with all other parameters unchanged) and the company continues to sell 8,000 units.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Contribution per unit:**

$$c = p - v = 150 - 90 = ₹60$$

**Step 2 — P/V Ratio:**

$$\text{P/V} = \frac{60}{150} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Step 3 — BEP in units:**

$$Q_{\text{BEP}} = \frac{FC}{c} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{60} = 5{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — BEP in ₹:**

$$\text{BEP}_{₹} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = ₹7{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Sales for target profit of ₹90,000:**

$$Q^{*} = \frac{FC + P_t}{c} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}000}{60} = \frac{3{,}90{,}000}{60} = 6{,}500 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6 — Margin of Safety:**

Actual Sales = $8{,}000 \times 150 = ₹12{,}00{,}000$

$$\text{MoS} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}50{,}000 = ₹4{,}50{,}000$$

$$\text{MoS\%} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 37.5\%$$

**Step 7 — Profit at reduced price (₹140):**

New contribution per unit = $140 - 90 = ₹50$

$$\text{Profit} = (8{,}000 \times 50) - 3{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$$

*(Marks split: Step 1–2 → 1 mark; Step 3–4 → 2 marks; Step 5 → 1 mark; Step 6 → 1 mark; Step 7 → 2 marks.)*

---

#### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Explain the significance and limitations of Margin of Safety. How does operating leverage influence managerial decisions based on CVP?**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Significance of Margin of Safety:**

1. **Risk Indicator:** A larger MoS indicates that the firm can absorb a significant drop in sales before incurring losses. A MoS above 25–30% is generally considered safe.
2. **Profit Forecasting:** $\text{Expected Profit} = \text{MoS} \times \text{P/V ratio}$, providing a direct link between sales uncertainty and profit.
3. **Performance Benchmark:** Management often sets MoS targets (e.g., minimum 20%) as a key result area.

**Limitations of Margin of Safety:**

1. Based on **historical or projected data**, which may not reflect future realities.
2. Assumes a **constant cost-volume-profit relationship**, ignoring step-fixed costs.
3. **Multi-product firms** with varying sales mixes make MoS less meaningful on an aggregate basis.
4. Ignores **external factors** like competitor action, regulatory changes, and macroeconomic shocks.

**Operating Leverage and Decision-Making:**

The **Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)** is defined as:

$$\text{DOL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Profit}}$$

- A **high DOL** (say, >3) implies that a small change in sales causes a magnified change in profit — characteristic of firms with **high fixed costs** (capital-intensive industries).
- A **low DOL** (<1.5) implies a more stable profit profile — typical of firms with **high variable costs**.
- Managers of high-DOL firms must prioritise **sales volume stability**, **market diversification**, and **cost control over fixed-cost expansion**.

---

### **Question B. [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**A company manufactures three products — X, Y, and Z. The following data is provided:**

| Particulars | Product X | Product Y | Product Z |
|---|---|---|---|
| Selling price per unit (₹) | 100 | 150 | 200 |
| Variable cost per unit (₹) | 70 | 90 | 120 |
| Sales mix (units) | 3 | 2 | 1 |

**Total fixed costs = ₹5,40,000.**

**Required:**
1. Calculate the **contribution per unit** for each product.
2. Calculate the **individual P/V ratios** and the **weighted average P/V ratio**.
3. Compute the **overall Break-Even Sales Value** and **BEP units for each product**.
4. Compute the **total profit** if the company sells a total of **10,000 units** (in the same mix).

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Contribution per unit:**

$$c_X = 100 - 70 = ₹30$$

$$c_Y = 150 - 90 = ₹60$$

$$c_Z = 200 - 120 = ₹80$$

**Step 2 — Individual P/V Ratios:**

$$\text{P/V}_X = \frac{30}{100} \times 100 = 30\%$$

$$\text{P/V}_Y = \frac{60}{150} \times 100 = 40\%$$

$$\text{P/V}_Z = \frac{80}{200} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Step 3 — Weighted Average P/V Ratio:**

Total mix ratio = $3 + 2 + 1 = 6$

$$w_X = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5, \quad w_Y = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}, \quad w_Z = \frac{1}{6}$$

$$\text{WAPV} = (0.5 \times 30) + \left(\frac{1}{3} \times 40\right) + \left(\frac{1}{6} \times 40\right)$$

$$\text{WAPV} = 15 + 13.333 + 6.667 = 35\%$$

**Step 4 — Overall BEP in Rupees:**

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{FC}{\text{WAPV}} = \frac{5{,}40{,}000}{0.35} = ₹15{,}42{,}857.14$$

**Step 5 — Allocate BEP by Sales Mix:**

For each "bundle" of 6 units (3X + 2Y + 1Z), the sales value = $(3 \times 100) + (2 \times 150) + (1 \times 200) = 300 + 300 + 200 = ₹800$.

Number of bundles at BEP = $\frac{15{,}42{,}857.14}{800} = 1{,}928.57$ bundles.

**BEP Units per Product:**

$$Q_X = 1{,}928.57 \times 3 = 5{,}785.71 \approx 5{,}786 \text{ units}$$

$$Q_Y = 1{,}928.57 \times 2 = 3{,}857.14 \approx 3{,}857 \text{ units}$$

$$Q_Z = 1{,}928.57 \times 1 = 1{,}928.57 \approx 1{,}929 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6 — Profit at 10,000 units total (mix preserved):**

Total bundles = $\frac{10{,}000}{6} = 1{,}666.67$ bundles.

Total contribution = $1{,}666.67 \times [(3 \times 30) + (2 \times 60) + (1 \times 80)]$

Total contribution per bundle = $90 + 120 + 80 = ₹290$

$$\text{Total Contribution} = 1{,}666.67 \times 290 = ₹4{,}83{,}333$$

$$\text{Profit} = 4{,}83{,}333 - 5{,}40{,}000 = -₹56{,}667 \text{ (Loss)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Always compute the sales mix ratio** before weighted P/V ratio. Forgetting this is the most common error, costing **2 marks**.
> 2. **Bundle method** is preferred over mix-percentage method for allocating BEP units — examiners expect clear "bundle" logic.
> 3. **Round off BEP units carefully** — KTU expects whole units for sales, but the bundle method naturally yields decimals.

---

#### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Discuss the practical applications of CVP analysis in modern engineering firms. How is Cash Break-Even Point different from Conventional Break-Even Point?**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Practical Applications of CVP in Engineering Firms:**

1. **Pricing of Tenders and Contracts:** Engineering firms bidding for projects can compute the minimum contract volume required to cover fixed overheads.
2. **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** Compare the variable cost of in-house production (including additional fixed set-up cost) with the purchase price from a vendor.
3. **Sales Mix Optimisation:** Firms producing multiple SKUs use weighted P/V ratios to identify the most profitable product mix.
4. **Plant Utilisation Studies:** Determine the volume at which a new production line becomes profitable.
5. **Profit Planning & Budgeting:** CVP serves as the foundation for flexible budgeting and break-even sales forecasting.
6. **Decision During Recession:** Identify loss-minimising volumes and decide whether to operate below BEP (if contribution covers some fixed costs) or shut down.

**Difference between Cash BEP and Conventional BEP:**

| Basis | Conventional BEP | Cash BEP |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\frac{FC}{c}$ | $\frac{FC - \text{Depreciation (non-cash)}}{c}$ |
| Assumption | Fixed costs include depreciation | Only **cash** fixed costs are considered |
| BEP Level | **Higher** | **Lower** |
| Use | Long-term profitability analysis | Short-term liquidity / cash-flow planning |
| Rationale | Treats depreciation as a real cost | Treats depreciation as a book entry, not an outflow |

> **Insight:** Cash BEP is reached earlier than conventional BEP because non-cash depreciation is excluded. Many firms therefore operate *below* conventional BEP yet remain *cash-positive* in the short run.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section before every KTU exam.**

- **CVP Analysis** studies the simultaneous effect of Cost, Volume, and Profit on operating income.
- The **fundamental equation**: $\text{Profit} = \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost} - \text{Fixed Cost}$.
- **Contribution** = Sales − Variable Cost; it is the engine of CVP.
- **P/V Ratio** = (Contribution / Sales) × 100 — measures contribution efficiency.
- **BEP (units)** = $\dfrac{FC}{c}$ ; **BEP (₹)** = $\dfrac{FC}{\text{P/V ratio}} \times 100$.
- **Target Sales** = $\dfrac{FC + \text{Target Profit}}{c}$ (units) or $\dfrac{FC + \text{Target Profit}}{\text{P/V ratio}}$ (₹).
- **Margin of Safety** = Actual Sales − BEP Sales; **Profit** = MoS × P/V ratio.
- **Operating Leverage (DOL)** = Contribution / Profit — high DOL means high business risk.
- **Multi-product BEP** uses **Weighted Average P/V Ratio** based on sales mix.
- **Cash BEP** excludes non-cash depreciation from fixed costs and is always lower than conventional BEP.
- **Assumptions to memorise:** linear cost-revenue behaviour, constant selling price, constant sales mix, no inventory change, fixed/variable classification possible.
- **Common Pitfalls:**
  - Forgetting to split semi-variable costs.
  - Computing multi-product BEP using *arithmetic average* instead of *weighted* average.
  - Mixing up contribution and profit terminologies.
  - Confusing BEP with target profit volume.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
