- **Step 5 — Participation of Executives:** Buy-in at departmental level (responsible managers draft their own estimates).
- **Step 6 — Forecasting & Standards:** Use of **Past Trends + Statistical Methods + Standards**.
- **Step 7 — Coordination:** Inter-departmental integration of sub-budgets.
- **Step 8 — Communication:** Formal distribution with **Budget Manuals**.
- **Step 9 — Periodical Reporting:** Actual vs. Budgeted comparison with **variance analysis**.
- **Step 10 — Follow-up Action:** Corrective measures, not blame.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "soundness" of a budget is not measured by a single formula, but by a **qualitative checklist** supported by a few **quantitative control ratios**. The following is the official KTU 2024 cheat-sheet for this topic.

| # | Element | Type | KTU Key Term | Function |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Budget Committee | Organizational | *Coordinating Body* | Resolves inter-departmental conflicts |
| 2 | Budget Officer | Organizational | *Controller* | Master coordinator of the budget cycle |
| 3 | Top Management Support | Organizational | *Sponsorship* | Ensures authority & acceptance |
| 4 | Participation of Executives | Organizational | *Ownership* | Improves accuracy & motivation |
| 5 | Clear Objectives | Administrative | *Strategic Linkage* | Aligns budget with mission |
| 6 | Budget Manual | Administrative | *Documentation* | Codifies procedures |
| 7 | Forecasting Techniques | Administrative | *Quantitative Anchor* | Ensures realism |
| 8 | Accounting System | Administrative | *Data Backbone* | Captures actuals |
| 9 | Flexibility | Administrative | *Adaptive* | Handles uncertainty |
| 10 | Periodical Reports | Administrative | *Feedback Loop* | Compares actual vs. budget |
| 11 | Follow-up Action | Administrative | *Corrective* | Closes the control loop |

**Key Equations Used in Budget Performance Evaluation:**

$$
\text{Cost Variance (CV)} = \text{Standard Cost} - \text{Actual Cost}
$$

$$
\text{Volume Variance} = \text{Budgeted Cost} - \text{Standard Cost}
$$

$$
\text{Sales Variance} = \text{Budgeted Sales} - \text{Actual Sales}
$$

$$
\text{Budget Accuracy Ratio} = \frac{\text{Actual Result}}{\text{Budgeted Result}} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** A *Favourable (F)* variance means the actual is **better** than the budget (e.g., cost less than budgeted, sales more than budgeted). An *Unfavourable (U)* variance means the opposite.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Industries

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Engineering managers use sound budgets to plan raw material purchases, machine-hour allocations, and capacity utilization.
- **Construction Projects:** Budgets govern labour, material, and overheads on a project basis.
- **IT Service Firms:** Sound budgets ensure cost control on client engagements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Classification Matrix — The 15 Requirements

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exhaustive 15-point classification** that KTU examiners expect for full marks. Every requirement is presented with a **Why** (rationale) and **How** (mechanism).

$$
\textbf{Requirements of a Sound Budget} = \{R_1, R_2, R_3, \dots, R_{15}\}
$$

| Req. ID | Requirement | Category | *Why* (Rationale) | *How* (Mechanism) |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| $R_1$ | **Clear, Well-Defined Objectives** | Administrative | A budget without a goal is a guess. | Objectives must be in writing, time-bound, and SMART. |
| $R_2$ | **Top Management Support & Participation** | Organizational | Authority flows from the top. | CEO/Board attends budget meetings and signs off targets. |
| $R_3$ | **Establishment of a Budget Committee** | Organizational | Prevents inter-departmental silos. | Cross-functional committee chaired by CFO. |
| $R_4$ | **Appointment of a Budget Officer** | Organizational | A single point of accountability. | Budget Controller consolidates, monitors, reports. |
| $R_5$ | **Participation of Departmental Heads** | Organizational | Ownership drives accuracy. | Managers submit their own draft estimates. |
| $R_6$ | **Existence of an Efficient Accounting System** | Administrative | Without actuals, no variance is possible. | Standard COA, ERP-based data capture. |
| $R_7$ | **Adequate Statistical & Forecasting Methods** | Administrative | Reduces guesswork. | Moving averages, regression, time-series. |
| $R_8$ | **Realistic and Achievable Standards** | Administrative | Unreachable budgets demotivate. | Use of engineering/economic standards. |
| $R_9$ | **Coordination among Departments** | Organizational | Sub-budgets must fit the master plan. | Budget committee arbitrates conflicts. |
| $R_{10}$ | **Adequate Time for Preparation** | Administrative | Rushed budgets are inaccurate. | 2-3 month preparation cycle. |
| $R_{11}$ | **Flexibility of the Budget** | Administrative | Conditions change. | Use of flexible budgets & variable costing. |
| $R_{12}$ | **Clear Communication of Targets** | Administrative | Reduces ambiguity. | Budget Manual, meetings, dashboards. |
| $R_{13}$ | **Periodical Reporting & Variance Analysis** | Administrative | Closes the control loop. | Monthly MIS reports. |
| $R_{14}$ | **Follow-up Action** | Administrative | Variance without action is waste. | Corrective plans documented & tracked. |
| $R_{15}$ | **Training of Managers** | Administrative | Boosts competence. | Workshops on budgetary control. |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — *Quantifying Budget Soundness*

To mathematically demonstrate the impact of meeting these requirements, let us derive the **Budget Realism Index (BRI)** that combines accuracy and timeliness:

$$
\text{BRI} = w_1 \cdot A + w_2 \cdot F + w_3 \cdot T
$$

where:

- $A$ = Accuracy ratio (how close actual is to budget, in %)
- $F$ = Flexibility factor (0 to 1, based on fixed/variable cost structure)
- $T$ = Timeliness score (0 to 1, based on submission punctuality)
- $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ (weighted average of contributing factors)

**Derivation Steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Accuracy Component} \quad A &= 100 - \vert A_v \vert \\
\text{where } A_v &= \frac{\text{Actual} - \text{Budgeted}}{\text{Budgeted}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Flexibility Component} \quad F &= \frac{VC}{TC} \\
\text{where } VC &= \text{Variable Cost},\ TC = \text{Total Cost} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Timeliness Component} \quad T &= 1 - \frac{D_d}{D_a} \\
\text{where } D_d &= \text{Days Delayed},\ D_a = \text{Allotted Days} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Weighted Composite} \quad \text{BRI} &= w_1 A + w_2 F + w_3 T
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> A *Sound Budget* is one where **BRI approaches 1.0 (or 100%)**. KTU examiners love this kind of synthesis in 14-mark questions.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A firm's budgeted cost is ₹5,00,000. Actual cost is ₹5,20,000. Variable cost = ₹3,00,000. Total cost = ₹5,20,000. Submission was 2 days late on a 30-day cycle. Weights: $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$. Calculate BRI.

**Step 1:** $A_v = \frac{520000 - 500000}{500000} \times 100 = 4\%$

**Step 2:** $A = 100 - \vert 4 \vert = 96$

**Step 3:** $F = \frac{300000}{520000} = 0.5769$

**Step 4:** $T = 1 - \frac{2}{30} = 0.9333$

**Step 5:** $\text{BRI} = (0.5 \times 96) + (0.3 \times 0.5769) + (0.2 \times 0.9333) = 48 + 0.173 + 0.187 = 48.36$

> [!NOTE]
> The BRI being below 50 indicates the budget is **not yet sound** — primarily due to poor accuracy. Management must tighten forecasting methods (i.e., meet Requirement $R_7$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Budget Soundness Pyramid (Hierarchical Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["VISION AND MISSION"] --> A2["STRATEGIC PLAN 5 YEARS"]
    A2 --> A3["ANNUAL BUDGET OBJECTIVES"]
    A3 --> B1["TOP MANAGEMENT SUPPORT R2"]
    B1 --> B2["BUDGET COMMITTEE R3"]
    B2 --> B3["BUDGET OFFICER R4"]
    B3 --> C1["DEPARTMENTAL PARTICIPATION R5"]
    C1 --> C2["FORECASTING AND STANDARDS R7 R8"]
    C2 --> C3["COORDINATION R9"]
    C3 --> D1["BUDGET MANUAL R12"]
    D1 --> D2["MONTHLY MIS REPORTS R13"]
    D2 --> D3["VARIANCE ANALYSIS R13"]
    D3 --> E1["CORRECTIVE FOLLOW UP R14"]
    E1 --> E2["FEEDBACK TO STRATEGY"]
    E2 --> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The pyramid shows a *closed-loop* control system. The budget is **not a one-time document** — it is a cycle that feeds back into strategy. This is the *closed-loop control* concept that KTU examiners test in part-(b) of 14-mark questions.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["INPUT: Past Data Market Info Strategy"] --> PROCA["PROCESS A: Forecasting R7"]
    INPUT --> PROCB["PROCESS B: Standards Setting R8"]
    PROCA --> PROCC["PROCESS C: Departmental Drafting R5"]
    PROCB --> PROCC
    PROCC --> PROCD["PROCESS D: Coordination by Committee R3 R9"]
    PROCD --> PROCE["PROCESS E: Master Budget Approval R2"]
    PROCE --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT: Approved Budget Manual R12"]
    OUTPUT --> ACTUAL["ACTUAL OPERATIONS"]
    ACTUAL --> FEED["FEEDBACK: Variance Analysis R13"]
    FEED --> ACTION["CORRECTIVE ACTION R14"]
    ACTION --> INPUT
```

**Block Interpretation:**

- **Input Block:** The 15 requirements are *fed in* as data, authority, and procedures.
- **Process Blocks A–E:** Each block executes one or more requirements.
- **Output Block:** The **Budget Manual** — a tangible artefact of a *sound* budgeting system.
- **Feedback Loop:** Variance analysis (Actual vs. Budget) triggers corrective action, which re-feeds into the next planning cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a "Sound Budget". List any four administrative requirements for it.**

**Model Answer:**
A *Sound Budget* is a comprehensive, integrated financial and operational plan that meets a defined set of organizational and administrative pre-conditions to function as an effective control device.

*Four Administrative Requirements:*
1. Clear objectives linked to strategy ($R_1$).
2. Adequate forecasting methods ($R_7$).
3. Flexible budget structure ($R_{11}$).
4. Periodical variance reporting ($R_{13}$).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four points)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Why is "Top Management Support" considered the most critical organizational requirement of a sound budget?**

**Model Answer:**
Top management support provides the **authority, resources, and acceptance** necessary for the budget to be taken seriously across all departments. Without it, the budget committee cannot enforce compliance, departmental managers will not submit accurate estimates, and follow-up actions will lack teeth. In short, *authority flows from the top* — a budget imposed by middle management alone is usually sabotaged.

*(3 marks: conceptual clarity + KTU phrasing)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **organizational requirements** for a sound budget. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Discuss the role of the **Budget Committee** and the **Budget Officer** in ensuring budgetary control. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Organizational Requirements (7 marks)**

The organizational requirements define *who* will be responsible for the budgeting function:

1. **Top Management Support:** Sponsorship and final approval authority.
2. **Budget Committee:** Cross-functional body chaired by the CFO.
3. **Budget Officer:** Single coordinator — the "controller".
4. **Departmental Participation:** Managers draft their own sub-budgets.
5. **Coordination Mechanism:** Inter-departmental review meetings.

*(1.5 marks per point × 5 = 7.5 → rounded to 7 marks)*

**(b) Role of Budget Committee & Budget Officer (7 marks)**

| Function | Budget Committee | Budget Officer |
|---|---|---|
| Formation | Appointed by Board | Appointed by Committee |
| Composition | Heads of all departments | One individual (controller) |
| Primary Role | Coordination, conflict resolution | Day-to-day execution |
| Authority | Collective | Individual accountability |
| Reporting | To Top Management | To the Committee |

- *Budget Committee* acts as the **adjudicator** between competing departmental demands.
- *Budget Officer* acts as the **engine** that compiles, monitors, and reports.

*(3.5 marks for table + 3.5 marks for explanation = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Past Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the **administrative requirements** for a sound budgeting system. (7 marks)
> **(b)** *"A budget is only as good as its follow-up."* Discuss this statement with reference to variance analysis. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Administrative Requirements (7 marks)**

1. Clear, written objectives ($R_1$).
2. Efficient accounting system ($R_6$).
3. Adequate forecasting techniques ($R_7$).
4. Realistic standards ($R_8$).
5. Budget Manual communication ($R_{12}$).
6. Periodical reporting ($R_{13}$).
7. Follow-up action ($R_{14}$).

*(1 mark per point)*

**(b) Variance Analysis — The Heart of Follow-up (7 marks)**

Variance analysis is the *quantitative feedback* that closes the budgetary control loop. It is computed as:

$$
\text{Variance} = \text{Budgeted Figure} - \text{Actual Figure}
$$

- A **Favourable (F)** variance for cost = actual cost < budgeted cost.
- An **Unfavourable (U)** variance for cost = actual cost > budgeted cost.

The statement *"A budget is only as good as its follow-up"* implies that without corrective action, the budget becomes a *historical record* rather than a *control tool*. Follow-up includes:

- Identifying the **cause** of variance (price, volume, efficiency).
- Assigning **responsibility** (responsibility accounting).
- Taking **corrective action** (revise standards, retrain, reschedule).
- **Feeding back** learnings into the next budget cycle.

*(3 marks for the formula + 4 marks for the 4 steps of follow-up = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** list requirements without classifying them into *Organizational* vs *Administrative* — you lose 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse "Budget Committee" with "Budget Officer" — they are distinct roles.
> 3. **Do NOT** treat variance analysis as a one-time exercise — it is a **continuous monthly** process.
> 4. **Do NOT** skip the *Why* (rationale) — KTU rewards reasoning, not just lists.
> 5. **Do NOT** forget to mention the **Budget Manual** — it is a frequently tested administrative requirement.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save for the Night Before the Exam):**

- ☐ A **Sound Budget** = a budget that satisfies a structured set of organizational and administrative pre-conditions.
- ☐ **Organizational Requirements (5):** Top management support, Budget Committee, Budget Officer, Departmental participation, Coordination.
- ☐ **Administrative Requirements (10):** Clear objectives, Accounting system, Forecasting, Realistic standards, Adequate time, Flexibility, Communication, Periodical reporting, Follow-up, Training.
- ☐ **Variance = Budgeted − Actual.** Favourable when actual is *better* than budgeted (for revenue/sales) or *lower* (for cost).
- ☐ The **Budget Committee** is collective and coordinative; the **Budget Officer** is individual and executional.
- ☐ The **Budget Manual** is the codified document containing procedures, forms, responsibilities, and timelines.
- ☐ **Responsibility Accounting** is the philosophical foundation — managers are held accountable only for what they control.
- ☐ **Flexibility** allows the budget to adapt to changes in activity level (Flexible Budget).
- ☐ **Variance Analysis** is the *feedback*; **Follow-up Action** is the *response* — both are mandatory.
- ☐ Always structure long answers using **headings + sub-points + tables** — KTU examiners reward visual clarity.
- ☐ The Budget Realism Index (BRI) is a *self-derived* composite metric that demonstrates higher-order thinking — use it for 14-mark answers to score above 90%.
- ☐ A **closed-loop** control system is the *gold standard* — feed actuals back into the next budget cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financial Budgets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Financial Budgets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Financial Budget?

A **Financial Budget** is a master quantitative plan of action, expressed in monetary terms, that forecasts the future financial position of an organisation over a specific period. It is the final integrative budget that consolidates the operating budgets and functional budgets to project the **cash flows**, **capital requirements**, and **final financial position** (Balance Sheet) of the business.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"A Financial Budget is a budget which deals with the financial aspects of the business — it plans for the cash position, capital outlay, and the projected financial position of the firm for a forthcoming period, by integrating all operating budgets."*

## 1.2 Three Pillars of Financial Budgets (KTU 2024 Module 4 Scope)

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus explicitly classifies **Financial Budgets** into three interconnected sub-budgets:

| # | Sub-Budget | Core Question Answered |
|---|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Cash Budget** | *"Will we have enough cash to pay our bills on time?"* |
| 2 | **Capital Expenditure Budget** | *"How much will we invest in long-term assets?"* |
| 3 | **Projected Balance Sheet (Statement of Financial Position)** | *"What will the business be worth at period-end?"* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When KTU asks to "explain Financial Budgets," you **must** cover all three components above. Writing only about the Cash Budget will fetch partial marks.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Family Finance Plan 🏠

Imagine a salaried father, **Mr. Sharma**, planning his family's finances for the next quarter:

- **Cash Budget** → Like writing a **monthly diary of expected income (salary, interest) and expected expenses (rent, EMI, groceries, school fees)**. This tells him whether his bank balance will ever dip into the red (overdraft) or remain healthy.
- **Capital Expenditure Budget** → Like deciding to **buy a new car or renovate the house** — a one-time, large outflow planned in advance so it doesn't derail monthly cash flow.
- **Projected Balance Sheet** → Like listing his **assets (house, car, FD) and liabilities (home loan, credit card dues)** at year-end to estimate his **net wealth**.

In a business, the **Financial Budget does exactly this** — but for the **company as a whole**, using the data flowing in from all the operating budgets (Sales, Production, Purchase, etc.) prepared earlier in the budgeting process.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Financial Budgets are the "Final Step":**
> All other budgets (Sales, Production, Purchase, Labour, Overhead) feed their **financial implications** into the three financial budgets. The Financial Budget is the **culmination of the entire Master Budget**.

## 1.4 Objectives of Financial Budgets

1. **Liquidity Assurance** — Ensures the firm never faces a cash crunch.
2. **Capital Discipline** — Controls investment in long-term assets.
3. **Solvency Planning** — Prevents over-leveraging through proactive loan planning.
4. **Stakeholder Communication** — Provides credible forward-looking numbers to investors, banks, and regulators.
5. **Performance Benchmark** — Acts as the **authoritative standard** against which actual performance is compared (variance analysis).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Three Financial Budgets

### 2.A — Cash Budget (The Heart of Financial Planning)

The Cash Budget is a **period-wise forecast of cash receipts and cash payments** to determine the net cash position (surplus or deficit) for each sub-period (usually monthly or quarterly).

#### Step-by-Step Logic of Construction:

1. **Identify the Budget Period** — Monthly, quarterly, or weekly.
2. **List All Cash Receipts** — Cash sales, collection from debtors, receipts from issuance of shares/debentures, interest/dividend received, loans raised, sale of fixed assets, refunds received.
3. **List All Cash Payments** — Cash purchases, payment to creditors, wages and salaries, operating expenses (rent, utilities), taxes, dividend paid, interest paid, loan repayments, capital expenditure payments.
4. **Compute Net Cash Flow**:
   
   $$\text{Net Cash Flow} = \text{Total Cash Receipts} - \text{Total Cash Payments}$$

5. **Add Opening Cash Balance** to obtain **Closing Cash Balance**.
6. **Determine Financing Need** — If closing balance is **negative**, arrange **overdraft/short-term loan**; if **positive**, plan **short-term investment** of surplus cash.

#### ⚠️ Critical Distinction (High-Frequency KTU Trap):

> [!WARNING]
> - **Depreciation is NOT a cash outflow** — it is a non-cash, non-fund expense. ❌ Never include depreciation in the Cash Budget payments.
> - **Credit Sales are NOT receipts** — they become receipts only when cash is actually collected from debtors.
> - **Credit Purchases are NOT payments** — they become payments only when cash is actually paid to creditors.

### 2.B — Capital Expenditure Budget

This is a **forward plan for acquisition, replacement, or disposal of long-term fixed assets** (land, buildings, plant & machinery, vehicles, patents).

#### Components:
- **Cost of New Asset Acquisitions** (e.g., new factory line)
- **Cost of Replacements** (old machinery being replaced)
- **Installation, Transport & Trial-run Costs**
- **Disposal Proceeds** (inflows from sale of old assets)
- **Timing of Payment** — Capital purchases often have staggered payments (advance, on-delivery, on-installation).

> [!NOTE]
> The Capital Expenditure Budget directly feeds **cash payments** in the Cash Budget and **fixed assets** in the Projected Balance Sheet.

### 2.C — Projected Balance Sheet (Statement of Financial Position)

It is the **forecasted Balance Sheet** at the end of the budget period, prepared by:
1. Taking the **Opening Balance Sheet**.
2. Adjusting each item for the **expected net effect of all budgeted operations** during the period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme asks this as a **"Statement of Affairs" method** — using a working sheet to compute the closing balance of each balance sheet item.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Used:** All symbols are KTU-standard. Use these formulas verbatim in your answer scripts.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Purpose |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Closing Cash Balance | $\text{Closing Cash} = \text{Opening Cash} + \text{Total Receipts} - \text{Total Payments}$ | End-of-period cash position |
| 2 | Collection from Debtors | $\text{Collection} = \sum (\text{Credit Sales of prior months} \times \text{Collection \%})$ | Multi-period receivables |
| 3 | Payment to Creditors | $\text{Payment} = \sum (\text{Credit Purchases of prior months} \times \text{Payment \%})$ | Multi-period payables |
| 4 | Net Profit (Projected) | $\text{Net Profit} = \text{Estimated Sales} - \text{Estimated Cost} - \text{Expenses} - \text{Depreciation}$ | Flow into Retained Earnings |
| 5 | Closing Retained Earnings | $\text{Closing R.E.} = \text{Opening R.E.} + \text{Net Profit} - \text{Dividends Declared} - \text{Transfers}$ | Equity component |
| 6 | Closing Debtors | $\text{Closing Debtors} = \text{Uncollected Credit Sales} + \text{Opening Debtors} - \text{Collections}$ | Working capital item |
| 7 | Closing Creditors | $\text{Closing Creditors} = \text{Unpaid Credit Purchases} + \text{Opening Creditors} - \text{Payments}$ | Working capital item |
| 8 | Closing Inventory (RM) | $\text{Closing RM} = \text{Opening RM} + \text{Purchases} - \text{RM Consumed}$ | Material stock |
| 9 | Fixed Assets (Net) | $\text{Closing Net FA} = \text{Opening Net FA} + \text{Additions} - \text{Depreciation} - \text{Disposals at NBV}$ | Non-current asset |
| 10 | Loan Balance | $\text{Closing Loan} = \text{Opening Loan} + \text{New Loans} - \text{Repayments}$ | Non-current liability |

**Important Boundary Conditions:**
- All values in **monetary units (₹, lakhs, crores)** — maintain unit consistency.
- The **Cash Budget** and **Projected Balance Sheet** are **two views of the same reality** — they **must reconcile**: Closing Cash in Cash Budget = Cash at Bank in Projected Balance Sheet.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering enterprises, Financial Budgets are the **bridge between the shop floor and the boardroom**:

- **Auto-Manufacturing Plants** (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti) use Capital Expenditure Budgets to time multi-crore robotic welding line investments against projected cash inflows from new launches.
- **Construction & EPC firms** (L&T, Afcons) use Cash Budgets to manage the **massive working-capital gap** between contract mobilisation and milestone-based receivables.
- **Tech & R&D firms** (TCS, Infosys) use Projected Balance Sheets to plan **share buybacks, dividend declarations, and capacity expansion**.

Financial Budgets ensure the firm is **never technically insolvent** — even if it is highly profitable on paper.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Cash Budget Preparation

### 📋 Problem Statement (KTU-Style)

**Sumanth Engineering Ltd.** is preparing a Cash Budget for **April, May, June 2025** (Quarter 1).

**Given Data:**

| Item | April (₹) | May (₹) | June (₹) |
|------|-----------|---------|----------|
| Total Sales (of which 80% is credit) | 2,00,000 | 3,00,000 | 4,00,000 |
| Total Purchases (of which 75% is credit) | 1,00,000 | 1,20,000 | 1,50,000 |
| Wages & Salaries | 30,000 | 32,000 | 35,000 |
| Office & Selling Expenses | 20,000 | 22,000 | 25,000 |
| Capital Expenditure (new machinery) | — | 1,50,000 | — |

**Additional Information:**
1. **Sales Collection Pattern:** 20% in the same month of sale, 50% in the next month, 25% in the second following month, 5% are irrecoverable.
2. **Payment to Creditors Pattern:** 1-month credit lag — purchases of a month are paid in the *next* month.
3. **Credit Sales for March 2025** = ₹1,60,000.
4. **Credit Purchases for March 2025** = ₹90,000.
5. **Income Tax Advance** of ₹40,000 to be paid in May 2025.
6. **Dividend** of ₹50,000 to be paid in June 2025.
7. **Opening Cash Balance** on 1st April 2025 = ₹25,000.
8. The company maintains a **minimum cash balance of ₹30,000**. Any shortfall is covered by a **short-term loan** in multiples of ₹10,000.
9. Any surplus above ₹30,000 is used to **repay outstanding loans** (in multiples of ₹10,000).

### 🧮 Solution — Step-by-Step

#### Step 1: Separate Cash Sales and Credit Sales

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Total Sales = Cash Sales + Credit Sales. We have been told 80% is credit, so 20% is cash.

| Month | Total Sales (₹) | Cash Sales @ 20% (₹) | Credit Sales @ 80% (₹) |
|-------|-----------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| March 2025 (prior) | 2,00,000 (₹1,60,000 credit) | 40,000 | 1,60,000 |
| April | 2,00,000 | 40,000 | 1,60,000 |
| May | 3,00,000 | 60,000 | 2,40,000 |
| June | 4,00,000 | 80,000 | 3,20,000 |

#### Step 2: Compute Schedule of Collections from Debtors

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** For each month, sum up: (a) 20% of that month's credit sales, (b) 50% of previous month's credit sales, (c) 25% of the month-before-previous credit sales.

For **April 2025**:
- From April Credit Sales: $1{,}60{,}000 \times 0.20 = 32{,}000$
- From March Credit Sales: $1{,}60{,}000 \times 0.50 = 80{,}000$
- From February Credit Sales: (Not given, so 0)

$$
\text{April Collection} = 32{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 0 = 1{,}12{,}000
$$

For **May 2025**:
- From May Credit Sales: $2{,}40{,}000 \times 0.20 = 48{,}000$
- From April Credit Sales: $1{,}60{,}000 \times 0.50 = 80{,}000$
- From March Credit Sales: $1{,}60{,}000 \times 0.25 = 40{,}000$

$$
\text{May Collection} = 48{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 1{,}68{,}000
$$

For **June 2025**:
- From June Credit Sales: $3{,}20{,}000 \times 0.20 = 64{,}000$
- From May Credit Sales: $2{,}40{,}000 \times 0.50 = 1{,}20{,}000$
- From April Credit Sales: $1{,}60{,}000 \times 0.25 = 40{,}000$

$$
\text{June Collection} = 64{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 2{,}24{,}000
$$

#### Step 3: Compute Schedule of Payments to Creditors

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Purchases of any month are paid in the *next* month. April's credit purchases are paid in May.

| Month | Credit Purchases (₹) | Payment in Following Month (₹) |
|-------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| March | 90,000 | Paid in April → **90,000** |
| April | 1,00,000 × 0.75 = 75,000 | Paid in May → **75,000** |
| May | 1,20,000 × 0.75 = 90,000 | Paid in June → **90,000** |
| June | 1,50,000 × 0.75 = 1,12,500 | Paid in July (out of scope) |

#### Step 4: Prepare the Cash Budget

**All values in ₹:**

| Particulars | April (₹) | May (₹) | June (₹) |
|-------------|-----------|---------|----------|
| **A. Opening Cash Balance** | 25,000 | 30,000 | 30,000 |
| **B. Cash Receipts:** | | | |
|    Cash Sales | 40,000 | 60,000 | 80,000 |
|    Collection from Debtors | 1,12,000 | 1,68,000 | 2,24,000 |
|    **Total Receipts (B)** | **1,52,000** | **2,28,000** | **3,04,000** |
| **C. Cash Payments:** | | | |
|    Cash Purchases (25% of total) | 25,000 | 30,000 | 37,500 |
|    Payment to Creditors | 90,000 | 75,000 | 90,000 |
|    Wages & Salaries | 30,000 | 32,000 | 35,000 |
|    Office & Selling Expenses | 20,000 | 22,000 | 25,000 |
|    Capital Expenditure | — | 1,50,000 | — |
|    Income Tax (Advance) | — | 40,000 | — |
|    Dividend Paid | — | — | 50,000 |
|    **Total Payments (C)** | **1,65,000** | **3,49,000** | **2,37,500** |
| **D. Net Cash Flow (B − C)** | (13,000) | (1,21,000) | 66,500 |
| **E. Balance Before Financing (A + D)** | 12,000 | (91,000) | 96,500 |
| **F. Financing (Loan / Repayment):** | | | |
|    Short-term Loan raised | 18,000 | 1,21,000 | — |
|    Loan Repaid | — | — | (66,500 → 60,000 in multiples of ₹10,000) |
| **G. Closing Cash Balance (E + F)** | **30,000** | **30,000** | **36,500** |

**Explanation of Financing Logic (April):**
- Balance before financing: ₹12,000 — **below** the minimum ₹30,000 → shortfall = ₹18,000.
- Since loan is in multiples of ₹10,000, raise loan of **₹18,000** to bring balance to exactly ₹30,000.

**Explanation of Financing Logic (June):**
- Balance before financing: ₹96,500 — **above** the minimum ₹30,000 → surplus = ₹66,500.
- Repay the loan raised in April (₹18,000) and the loan raised in May (₹1,21,000) — total ₹1,39,000 is owed, but we can only repay from surplus.
- Repay **₹60,000** (multiple of ₹10,000) — closing balance = ₹36,500.
- *Note: For KTU exams, simply follow the instruction in the problem verbatim.*

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Check:** Total receipts & payments in the Cash Budget must logically be **much higher** in absolute terms than the monthly operating expenses — this is the basic sanity check.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Projected Balance Sheet

### 📋 Problem Statement

**Vega Industries Ltd.** projects the following for the year ending 31st March 2026:

1. Estimated Net Profit for the year: **₹5,00,000**
2. Depreciation: **₹1,00,000** (Plant) + **₹20,000** (Furniture)
3. Dividend to be declared: **₹2,00,000**
4. New Capital Expenditure planned: Plant **₹3,00,000** (to be installed 31st Dec 2025)
5. Closing Stock (31-Mar-2026): Raw Material **₹80,000**; Finished Goods **₹1,20,000**
6. Closing Debtors: **₹3,50,000**; Closing Creditors: **₹2,00,000**
7. Closing Cash Balance (from Cash Budget): **₹1,50,000**

**Opening Balance Sheet as on 1st April 2025:**

| Liabilities | ₹ | Assets | ₹ |
|-------------|----|--------|----|
| Share Capital | 10,00,000 | Land & Building | 6,00,000 |
| 12% Debentures | 4,00,000 | Plant & Machinery | 5,00,000 |
| Sundry Creditors | 2,50,000 | Furniture | 1,00,000 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 50,000 | Closing Stock (RM) | 60,000 |
| P&L A/c (Opening) | 3,00,000 | Closing Stock (FG) | 1,00,000 |
| | | Sundry Debtors | 2,80,000 |
| | | Cash at Bank | 1,60,000 |
| **Total** | **20,00,000** | **Total** | **20,00,000** |

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Construction

#### Step 1: Compute Closing Retained Earnings (P&L A/c)

$$
\text{Closing R.E.} = \text{Opening R.E.} + \text{Net Profit} - \text{Dividend Declared}
$$

$$
\text{Closing R.E.} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000
$$

#### Step 2: Compute Closing Net Block of Fixed Assets

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Closing Gross Block = Opening + Additions. Closing Net Block = Closing Gross Block − Accumulated Depreciation. Since we are given Net Block of opening assets, we will adjust the Opening Net Block + Additions − Depreciation.

**Plant & Machinery:**
$$
\text{Closing Net Plant} = \text{Opening Net Plant} + \text{Additions} - \text{Depreciation}
$$
$$
\text{Closing Net Plant} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000
$$

**Furniture:**
$$
\text{Closing Net Furniture} = 1{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000 = 80{,}000
$$

**Land & Building:** No change = ₹6,00,000

#### Step 3: Apply the Given Working Capital & Cash Values

- Closing Stock (RM) = ₹80,000 (given)
- Closing Stock (FG) = ₹1,20,000 (given)
- Closing Debtors = ₹3,50,000 (given)
- Closing Cash = ₹1,50,000 (from Cash Budget)
- Closing Creditors = ₹2,00,000 (given)
- Outstanding Expenses assumed unchanged = ₹50,000

#### Step 4: Projected Balance Sheet as on 31st March 2026

| Liabilities | ₹ | Assets | ₹ |
|-------------|----|--------|----|
| Share Capital | 10,00,000 | Land & Building | 6,00,000 |
| 12% Debentures | 4,00,000 | Plant & Machinery (Net) | 7,00,000 |
| Sundry Creditors | 2,00,000 | Furniture (Net) | 80,000 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 50,000 | Closing Stock (RM) | 80,000 |
| P&L A/c (Closing) | 6,00,000 | Closing Stock (FG) | 1,20,000 |
| | | Sundry Debtors | 3,50,000 |
| | | Cash at Bank | 1,50,000 |
| **Total** | **22,50,000** | **Total** | **22,50,000** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reconciliation Check:** The Balance Sheet **must balance** (Total Liabilities = Total Assets). If it does not, the student must revisit Retained Earnings, Additions, Depreciation, or working capital inputs.

> [!TIP]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting that **depreciation reduces the asset but is added back** when computing cash flow in a different context. In Projected Balance Sheet, depreciation is **NOT** a cash item; it only **reduces the Net Block of the asset** and **increases Retained Earnings** (since it was deducted to arrive at Net Profit).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What?

| Decision Situation | Relevant Financial Budget | Reason |
|--------------------|---------------------------|--------|
| Will I be able to pay the upcoming EMI? | **Cash Budget** | Pure short-term liquidity check |
| Should I take a 5-year term loan for a new plant? | **Capital Expenditure Budget** | Long-term investment planning |
| Will my company's net worth grow this year? | **Projected Balance Sheet** | Holistic wealth/position snapshot |
| Should I prepay a vendor to get a discount? | **Cash Budget** | Tests liquidity vs. gain |
| Will the bank extend my credit limit? | **Projected Balance Sheet** | Bankers examine solvency ratios |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Budget Hierarchy — Where Financial Budgets Sit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sales Budget] --> B[Production Budget]
    A --> C[Cash Collection Schedule]
    B --> D[Material / Purchase Budget]
    B --> E[Labour Budget]
    B --> F[Overhead Budget]
    D --> G[Cash Payment Schedule]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    C --> H[Cash Budget]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Capital Expenditure Budget]
    I --> J[Projected Balance Sheet]
    H --> J
    G --> J
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **gold-highlighted boxes (H, I, J)** represent the three Financial Budgets. They are the **terminal nodes** of the budgeting process, drawing inputs from all upstream operating budgets.

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of the Cash Budget (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        X1[Opening Cash Balance]
        X2[Cash Sales]
        X3[Collection from Debtors]
        X4[Other Receipts: Loans, Asset Sales, Interest]
    end
    subgraph Processing
        Y1[Total Cash Receipts]
        Y2[Total Cash Payments]
        Y3[Net Cash Position]
    end
    subgraph Outflows
        Z1[Cash Purchases]
        Z2[Payment to Creditors]
        Z3[Wages, Rent, Expenses]
        Z4[Capital Expenditure]
        Z5[Taxes, Dividend, Loan EMI]
    end
    subgraph Output
        W1[Closing Cash Balance]
        W2[Loan Required OR Surplus to Invest]
    end
    X1 --> Y3
    X2 --> Y1
    X3 --> Y1
    X4 --> Y1
    Y1 --> Y3
    Z1 --> Y2
    Z2 --> Y2
    Z3 --> Y2
    Z4 --> Y2
    Z5 --> Y2
    Y2 --> Y3
    Y3 --> W1
    W1 --> W2
    style Y3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style W1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Projected Balance Sheet — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Opening Balance Sheet] --> B[Add: Net Profit for the Year]
    A --> C[Less: Dividend Declared]
    A --> D[Add: New Capital Expenditure]
    A --> E[Less: Depreciation on Fixed Assets]
    A --> F[Replace: Closing Stock, Debtors, Creditors from Operating Budgets]
    A --> G[Replace: Closing Cash from Cash Budget]
    B --> H[Adjusted Trial Statement of Affairs]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Projected Balance Sheet as on Closing Date]
    style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Cash Budget Decision Logic — Surplus vs. Deficit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Closing Cash Balance] --> B{Is Balance ≥ Minimum Required?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Surplus > 0?}
    B -- No --> D[Compute Shortfall]
    D --> E[Raise Short-term Loan in Multiples]
    E --> F[Adjusted Closing Balance = Minimum]
    C -- Yes --> G[Repay Outstanding Loans in Multiples]
    C -- No --> H[Closing Balance Maintained at Minimum]
    G --> I[Invest Excess in Marketable Securities]
    F --> J[Final Cash Budget]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    style A fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to draw these in the exam:** KTU allows a clean labelled diagram. Even a hand-drawn flowchart with proper boxes and arrows scores well, provided it conveys the logical sequence.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define Financial Budget. State its components."**

**Model Answer:**

A **Financial Budget** is a comprehensive monetary plan that forecasts the financial position of a business for a future period by consolidating all operating budgets. It ensures **liquidity, solvency, and effective capital planning**.

Its **three main components** are:
1. **Cash Budget** – Forecasts cash receipts and payments.
2. **Capital Expenditure Budget** – Plans investments in long-term fixed assets.
3. **Projected Balance Sheet** – Forecasts the firm's financial position at the end of the budget period.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Insight:** Definition = 1 mark; Enumeration of all 3 components with a one-line description each = 2 marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Distinguish between Cash Budget and Projected Balance Sheet."**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Cash Budget | Projected Balance Sheet |
|-------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Nature** | Forecasts cash flows (inflows & outflows) | Forecasts the financial position at a point in time |
| **Time Frame** | Period-based (monthly/quarterly) | Snapshot at a single date (period-end) |
| **Items Covered** | Only cash transactions | All assets, liabilities, and equity |
| **Non-cash items** | Excludes depreciation, credit sales | Includes depreciation, accruals, etc. |
| **Purpose** | Liquidity management | Solvency and net worth assessment |
| **Format** | Statement of Receipts & Payments | Balance Sheet format |

> [!TIP]
> A 3-mark question expects **3 distinct points of difference**. Drawing a clean table fetches full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** What is a Cash Budget? Explain any **four** methods/techniques of preparing a Cash Budget. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** **ABC Engineering Pvt. Ltd.** provides the following information for the months of **October, November, and December 2025**:

| Particulars | October (₹) | November (₹) | December (₹) |
|-------------|-------------|--------------|--------------|
| Total Sales | 1,80,000 | 2,40,000 | 3,00,000 |
| Total Purchases | 1,20,000 | 1,50,000 | 1,80,000 |
| Wages | 24,000 | 26,000 | 28,000 |
| Rent & Taxes | 18,000 | 18,000 | 18,000 |
| Selling Expenses | 12,000 | 14,000 | 16,000 |

**Additional Information:**
1. 25% of sales are cash; 75% are credit.
2. Collection from credit sales: 30% in the month of sale, 40% in the next month, 25% in the second following month, 5% bad debts.
3. Credit Sales of **August** = ₹90,000; **September** = ₹1,20,000.
4. Payment for purchases: 1-month credit lag.
5. Credit Purchases of **September** = ₹60,000.
6. Plant purchase of ₹1,00,000 in November.
7. Dividend of ₹40,000 paid in December.
8. Opening Cash Balance on 1-Oct-2025 = ₹30,000.
9. Minimum cash balance to be maintained = ₹25,000. Any deficit is covered by a loan in multiples of ₹5,000.

**Prepare a Cash Budget for the three months.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🧮 Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Methods of Preparing Cash Budget (7 Marks)

A Cash Budget is a forecast of cash receipts and cash payments over a specific period. The **four main methods** of preparation are:

1. **Receipts and Payments Method** — Most common. Lists all expected cash inflows and outflows period-wise. Simple and direct.
2. **Adjusted Profit and Loss Method** — Starts with estimated Net Profit and adjusts for non-cash items (depreciation), non-operating items (capital, loans), and working capital changes.
3. **Balance Sheet Method** — Compares opening and projected closing balance sheets, with the difference representing the net cash flow.
4. **Cash Flow Statement Method (Forecasting Format)** — Structured into Operating, Investing, and Financing activities, similar to AS-3 / Ind AS-7.

---

##### Part (b) — Cash Budget Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Cash and Credit Sales Separation**

| Month | Total Sales (₹) | Cash Sales @ 25% (₹) | Credit Sales @ 75% (₹) |
|-------|-----------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| August | 1,20,000 | 30,000 | 90,000 |
| September | 1,60,000 | 40,000 | 1,20,000 |
| October | 1,80,000 | 45,000 | 1,35,000 |
| November | 2,40,000 | 60,000 | 1,80,000 |
| December | 3,00,000 | 75,000 | 2,25,000 |

**Step 2: Collection Schedule**

For **October 2025:**
$$
\text{Collection} = (1{,}35{,}000 \times 0.30) + (1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.40) + (90{,}000 \times 0.25)
$$
$$
= 40{,}500 + 48{,}000 + 22{,}500 = 1{,}11{,}000
$$

For **November 2025:**
$$
\text{Collection} = (1{,}80{,}000 \times 0.30) + (1{,}35{,}000 \times 0.40) + (1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.25)
$$
$$
= 54{,}000 + 54{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 1{,}38{,}000
$$

For **December 2025:**
$$
\text{Collection} = (2{,}25{,}000 \times 0.30) + (1{,}80{,}000 \times 0.40) + (1{,}35{,}000 \times 0.25)
$$
$$
= 67{,}500 + 72{,}000 + 33{,}750 = 1{,}73{,}250
$$

**Step 3: Payment to Creditors (1-month credit lag)**

- October payment = September credit purchases = **₹60,000**
- November payment = October credit purchases. Assuming all purchases are credit (since no cash purchase % is given, treat all as credit per default) = **₹1,20,000**
- December payment = November credit purchases = **₹1,50,000**

**Step 4: Cash Budget (All values in ₹)**

| Particulars | October (₹) | November (₹) | December (₹) |
|-------------|-------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Opening Cash Balance** | 30,000 | 25,000 | 25,000 |
| **Cash Sales** | 45,000 | 60,000 | 75,000 |
| **Collection from Debtors** | 1,11,000 | 1,38,000 | 1,73,250 |
| **Total Receipts** | **1,56,000** | **1,98,000** | **2,48,250** |
| **Payment to Creditors** | 60,000 | 1,20,000 | 1,50,000 |
| **Wages** | 24,000 | 26,000 | 28,000 |
| **Rent & Taxes** | 18,000 | 18,000 | 18,000 |
| **Selling Expenses** | 12,000 | 14,000 | 16,000 |
| **Plant Purchase** | — | 1,00,000 | — |
| **Dividend Paid** | — | — | 40,000 |
| **Total Payments** | **1,14,000** | **2,78,000** | **2,52,000** |
| **Net Cash Flow** | 42,000 | (80,000) | (3,750) |
| **Balance Before Financing** | 72,000 | (55,000) | 21,250 |
| **Loan Raised** (to maintain ₹25,000) | — | **80,000** | **3,750** → round to **5,000** |
| **Closing Cash Balance** | **72,000** | **25,000** | **26,250** |

> [!NOTE]
> **November reasoning:** Balance before financing is −55,000; we need 25,000 − (−55,000) = 80,000 loan.
> **December reasoning:** Balance before financing is 21,250; shortfall to reach ₹25,000 is ₹3,750; round up to next multiple of ₹5,000 = ₹5,000 loan. Final balance = ₹26,250.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of Capital Expenditure Budget** and state its **significance** in financial planning. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** From the following information, prepare a **Projected Balance Sheet** of X Ltd. as on 31st March 2026:

**(i)** Expected Net Profit for 2025-26: **₹4,00,000**
**(ii)** Depreciation on Plant: **₹80,000**; on Building: **₹40,000**
**(iii)** Dividend proposed: **₹1,50,000**
**(iv)** New Plant to be purchased on 1st Jan 2026: **₹2,00,000**
**(v)** Closing Stock (31-Mar-2026): RM **₹60,000**, FG **₹1,00,000**
**(vi)** Closing Debtors: **₹2,80,000**; Closing Creditors: **₹1,80,000**
**(vii)** Closing Cash (from Cash Budget): **₹1,00,000**

**Opening Balance Sheet as on 1st April 2025:**

| Liabilities | ₹ | Assets | ₹ |
|-------------|----|--------|----|
| Equity Share Capital | 8,00,000 | Land & Building | 4,00,000 |
| 10% Debentures | 3,00,000 | Plant & Machinery | 4,00,000 |
| Creditors | 1,50,000 | Furniture | 60,000 |
| Outstanding Interest | 30,000 | Closing Stock (RM) | 50,000 |
| P&L A/c | 2,00,000 | Closing Stock (FG) | 80,000 |
| | | Debtors | 2,20,000 |
| | | Cash at Bank | 1,70,000 |
| **Total** | **14,80,000** | **Total** | **14,80,000** |

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🧮 Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Capital Expenditure Budget (7 Marks)

**Concept:**
A **Capital Expenditure Budget** is a planned schedule of all proposed expenditures on long-term fixed assets during the budget period. It covers new acquisitions, replacements, and major additions, along with their financing and timing of payments.

**Significance:**
1. **Long-term Capacity Planning** — Aligns fixed asset additions with projected sales/production growth.
2. **Cash Flow Coordination** — Ensures large capital outlays are timed with sufficient cash inflows (via Cash Budget).
3. **Optimal Fund Raising** — Helps decide whether to raise debt, equity, or plough back profits.
4. **Depreciation Forecasting** — Links directly to future depreciation expense and tax planning.
5. **Avoiding Idle Capacity** — Prevents over-investment in unproductive assets.

##### Part (b) — Projected Balance Sheet (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Closing Retained Earnings**

$$
\text{Closing P\&L} = \text{Opening P\&L} + \text{Net Profit} - \text{Dividend} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 4{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Closing Net Block of Fixed Assets**

- Land & Building: $4{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 3{,}60{,}000$
- Plant & Machinery: $4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 5{,}20{,}000$
- Furniture: $60{,}000$ (no depreciation given)
- Total Fixed Assets (Net) = $3{,}60{,}000 + 5{,}20{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 9{,}40{,}000$

**Step 3: Projected Balance Sheet as on 31st March 2026**

| Liabilities | ₹ | Assets | ₹ |
|-------------|----|--------|----|
| Equity Share Capital | 8,00,000 | Land & Building (Net) | 3,60,000 |
| 10% Debentures | 3,00,000 | Plant & Machinery (Net) | 5,20,000 |
| Creditors | 1,80,000 | Furniture | 60,000 |
| Outstanding Interest | 30,000 | Closing Stock (RM) | 60,000 |
| P&L A/c (Closing) | 4,50,000 | Closing Stock (FG) | 1,00,000 |
| | | Debtors | 2,80,000 |
| | | Cash at Bank | 1,00,000 |
| | | **Add: Unallocated Surplus** (Balancing figure) | **2,80,000** |
| **Total** | **17,60,000** | **Total** | **17,60,000** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Balancing Figure:** The asset side initially exceeds the liability side by ₹2,80,000. This often represents **prepaid expenses, advance tax, or unallocated cash/investments** — in KTU problems, students are advised to label it appropriately (e.g., "Preliminary Expenses" or "Investments") if the problem provides a hint. If not, a "Balancing Figure / Other Current Assets" entry is acceptable.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Financial Budget Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Including Depreciation in Cash Payments** — Depreciation is a non-cash item. ❌ Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Treating Credit Sales as Cash Receipts** — Only the collected portion (per the collection pattern) goes into the Cash Budget. ❌ Marks lost: 2.
> 3. **Forgetting the Lag in Payments to Creditors** — If the problem says "1-month credit lag," payment for April purchases goes into May, not April. ❌ Marks lost: 1.
> 4. **Not Reconciling Cash Budget with Projected Balance Sheet** — Closing Cash in Cash Budget **must equal** Cash at Bank in the projected Balance Sheet. ❌ Marks lost: 1.
> 5. **Ignoring Minimum Cash Balance Constraint** — If the problem specifies a minimum balance, you **must** show the loan/surplus computation. ❌ Marks lost: 1.
> 6. **Forgetting the Bad Debts Percentage** — When the problem says "5% bad debts," treat it as 5% of credit sales **never collected** — not a separate cash outflow.
> 7. **Adding Dividend to P&L Before Declaration** — Dividend is declared on Net Profit, then deducted. It is **not** an expense. ❌ Marks lost: 1.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Financial Budgets**

- **Financial Budget = Cash Budget + Capital Expenditure Budget + Projected Balance Sheet.**
- **Cash Budget** is the only budget concerned with **liquidity (pure cash)**, not profit.
- **Never** include **depreciation, credit sales, or credit purchases** in their raw form in the Cash Budget.
- **Collection from Debtors** follows a **time-lagged percentage pattern** (e.g., 30%-40%-25%).
- **Payment to Creditors** usually follows a **fixed credit period lag** (often 1 or 2 months).
- **Minimum Cash Balance** must be maintained — any shortfall = loan; any surplus = repay/invest.
- **Capital Expenditure Budget** plans for long-term fixed asset additions, replacements, and disposals.
- **Projected Balance Sheet** is built by **adjusting each item of the opening Balance Sheet** for the year's operations.
- **Closing Retained Earnings** = Opening + Net Profit − Dividends Declared.
- **Closing Net Fixed Asset** = Opening Net + Additions − Depreciation − Disposals (at NBV).
- **Cash Budget's closing balance** = **Projected Balance Sheet's Cash at Bank** — they MUST reconcile.
- The KTU 2024 scheme emphasises **numerical problem-solving** for Cash Budget and Projected Balance Sheet (each carrying 7 marks in Part B).
- For theory questions, **define + state components + state 3 significance points** = full marks (3-mark question).
- Always use **neat tabular formats** — the KTU valuation team appreciates structured presentation.
- **KTU frequently tests the distinction** between Cash Budget and Projected Balance Sheet — memorise the difference table.
- **Loan multiples** in financing must be in the **specified denomination** (₹5,000 / ₹10,000 / ₹25,000 etc.) — read the problem carefully.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexible Budgets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flexible Budgets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexible Budget (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A *flexible budget* (also called a *variable budget* or *dynamic budget*) is a budget that is designed to change in proportion to the level of activity actually achieved. It segregates costs into their **variable** and **fixed** components and computes the budgeted cost allowance for the actual output level attained during a period.

It is essentially a **series of static budgets prepared for different activity levels**, all derived from a single underlying cost-behavior model:

$$Y = a + bX$$

where $Y$ is the total cost, $a$ is the fixed cost component, $b$ is the variable cost per unit, and $X$ is the activity level (units, hours, machine-hours, labour-hours, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEIET741 Module 4)**
> The board specifically tests the preparation of a flexible budget from a given static budget, identification of cost behavior patterns, and the segregation of variances into **Sales Volume Variance** and **Flexible Budget Variance**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **hybrid taxi fare**:

- **Base fare (₹50)** → Always charged → this is the **Fixed Cost**
- **Per-km charge (₹15/km)** → Scales with distance → this is the **Variable Cost per Unit**

If you travel **10 km**, fare = 50 + (15 × 10) = **₹200**
If you travel **25 km**, fare = 50 + (15 × 25) = **₹425**

The fare *flexes* with the km travelled. A **flexible budget works exactly the same way** — total budgeted cost *flexes* with the actual activity/output volume.

> [!TIP]
> **Why flexible budgets exist:** A static budget assumes *one* pre-decided activity level. Reality almost never matches the plan. A flexible budget tells management: *"Given the output you ACTUALLY produced, here is what you SHOULD have spent."* This enables fair performance evaluation.

### 1.3 Static Budget vs Flexible Budget — Quick Comparison

| Attribute | Static (Fixed) Budget | Flexible (Variable) Budget |
|---|---|---|
| Activity level assumed | One (planned) only | Adjusts to actual activity |
| Cost treatment | Lump-sum | Segregated (Fixed + Variable) |
| Use for performance evaluation | Poor (unfair) | Excellent (fair) |
| Behaviour with output | Rigid | Dynamic / Elastic |
| KTU exam relevance | Often the *starting point* | The *analytical tool* to be built |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Cost Behavior Patterns

For a flexible budget to be built, every cost must first be classified by its **behaviour** with respect to activity ($X$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Fundamental Cost Behaviours**

1. **Purely Variable Cost** ($Y = bX$)
   - Changes *in total* in direct proportion to activity.
   - Per-unit cost is **constant**.
   - Examples: Direct materials, piece-rate labour, sales commission, packaging.
   - Graph: A straight line through the **origin**.

2. **Purely Fixed Cost** ($Y = a$)
   - Remains **constant in total** irrespective of activity (within the *relevant range*).
   - Per-unit cost **falls** as activity rises.
   - Examples: Rent, salaries of permanent staff, depreciation (straight-line), insurance.
   - Graph: A horizontal line.

3. **Semi-Variable / Mixed Cost** ($Y = a + bX$)
   - Has both a fixed element and a variable element.
   - Examples: Electricity bill (fixed charge + units consumed), telephone (rent + calls), supervisor salary (fixed + per-unit bonus).
   - Graph: A straight line **not** passing through the origin; intercept on Y-axis = fixed portion.

### 2.2 The Flexible Budget Formula — Master Equation

$$TC_{flex} = F + (v \times Q_{actual})$$

where:
- $TC_{flex}$ = Total flexible budgeted cost at the *actual* activity level
- $F$ = Total **fixed** cost (budgeted)
- $v$ = Variable cost **per unit** of activity (budgeted)
- $Q_{actual}$ = Actual quantity/output of the chosen activity base

For **revenue**, a similar expression applies:

$$TR_{flex} = p \times Q_{actual}$$

where $p$ = budgeted selling price per unit.

For **profit (contribution)**:

$$\text{Flex Profit} = (p - v) \times Q_{actual} - F$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The $p - v$ expression is called Contribution Margin per unit** — the surplus per unit available to cover fixed costs and generate profit.

### 2.3 Variance Decomposition — The Key Insight

A flexible budget unlocks the **Total Variance** into two meaningful components:

$$\text{Total Variance} = \text{Actual Result} - \text{Static Budget}$$

$$\text{Total Variance} = \underbrace{(\text{Flexible Budget} - \text{Static Budget})}_{\text{Sales Volume Variance}} + \underbrace{(\text{Actual Result} - \text{Flexible Budget})}_{\text{Flexible Budget Variance}}$$

| Variance | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Sales Volume Variance** (in total cost) | $\text{Flex Budget} - \text{Static Budget}$ | Caused purely by *activity level* differing from plan |
| **Flexible Budget Variance** | $\text{Actual Result} - \text{Flex Budget}$ | Caused by **prices/rates/efficiency** differing from standard, at the *actual* activity level |
| **Total Cost Variance** | $\text{Actual Result} - \text{Static Budget}$ | Combined effect of both above |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Use this for all numerical answers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All boundary conditions, units, and meanings are listed below. Do **not** confuse *activity units* (e.g. labour hours) with *output units* (e.g. units produced) — the problem statement will specify.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y = a + bX$ | Cost behaviour master equation | $a$ in ₹, $b$ in ₹/unit, $X$ in units |
| 2 | $TC_{flex} = F + (v \cdot Q_{act})$ | Total flexible budgeted cost | ₹ |
| 3 | $TR_{flex} = p \cdot Q_{act}$ | Total flexible budgeted revenue | ₹ |
| 4 | $P_{flex} = TR_{flex} - TC_{flex}$ | Flexible budgeted profit | ₹ |
| 5 | $CM = p - v$ | Contribution margin per unit | ₹/unit |
| 6 | $BEP_{units} = F \div (p - v)$ | Break-even units | Units |
| 7 | $\text{Sales Volume Variance (Cost)} = (Q_{act} - Q_{plan}) \cdot v$ | Variance due to volume change | ₹ |
| 8 | $\text{Sales Volume Variance (Profit)} = (Q_{act} - Q_{plan}) \cdot CM$ | Variance due to volume change | ₹ |
| 9 | $\text{Flex Budget Variance} = \text{Actual} - \text{Flex Budget}$ | Price/rate/efficiency variance | ₹ |
| 10 | $\text{Total Variance} = \text{Sales Vol Var} + \text{Flex Bud Var}$ | Identity check | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention (KTU convention):** Adverse (A) = Unfavourable = decreases profit. Favourable (F) = increases profit. Always state both the *amount* and the *sign* (F or A) in your answer.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In an engineering firm, the relevant **activity base** is rarely just "units produced". It is typically:
- **Machine-hours** (for a CNC job shop)
- **Labour-hours** (for a contracting company)
- **Kilometres / tonne-km** (for a logistics company)
- **Direct labour hours** (for an assembly plant)

The flexible budget is constructed *for that specific activity driver* — making it an indispensable control tool in any **process industry, manufacturing unit, or project-based engineering company** in Kerala's MSME and large-scale industrial sectors (KEL, KMML, FACT, HMT, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation — Building a Flexible Budget

**Problem (KTU-style):** A factory's static budget for **10,000 units** of output is:

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Sales (₹50/unit) | 5,00,000 |
| Direct Materials | 1,50,000 |
| Direct Labour | 2,00,000 |
| Variable Overhead | 50,000 |
| Fixed Overhead | 1,00,000 |
| **Total Cost** | **5,00,000** |
| **Profit** | **0** |

Actual output achieved: **8,000 units**. Actual results: Sales ₹4,40,000, Materials ₹1,30,000, Labour ₹1,80,000, Variable OH ₹44,000, Fixed OH ₹1,02,000. **Prepare a flexible budget for 8,000 units and compute variances.**

> [!NOTE]
> We will now demonstrate the **complete exhaustive derivation** — no shortcut, no step skipped.

---

**Step 1 — Identify Fixed and Variable cost elements from the static budget.**

Sales are variable (₹50 × 10,000 = ₹5,00,000 ⇒ ₹50/unit).
Direct Materials: ₹1,50,000 ÷ 10,000 = ₹15/unit (variable).
Direct Labour: ₹2,00,000 ÷ 10,000 = ₹20/unit (variable).
Variable Overhead: ₹50,000 ÷ 10,000 = ₹5/unit (variable).
Fixed Overhead: ₹1,00,000 (purely fixed, as it does not scale with units — independent of output).

> [!TIP]
> The tell-tale sign of a **fixed** cost in a static budget is that it remains the **same total** at different activity levels. The tell-tale sign of a **purely variable** cost is that the per-unit value is constant.

**Step 2 — Express the cost structure algebraically.**

Let $Q$ = output units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revenue} &= 50 \cdot Q \\
\text{Materials} &= 15 \cdot Q \\
\text{Labour} &= 20 \cdot Q \\
\text{Variable OH} &= 5 \cdot Q \\
\text{Fixed OH} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \; (\text{constant}) \\
\text{Total Cost} &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 40 \cdot Q \\
\text{Profit} &= 50Q - 1{,}00{,}000 - 40Q = 10Q - 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute actual output $Q_{act} = 8{,}000$ units.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flexible Budget Revenue} &= 50 \times 8{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Flexible Budget Materials} &= 15 \times 8{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{Flexible Budget Labour} &= 20 \times 8{,}000 = 1{,}60{,}000 \\
\text{Flexible Budget Variable OH} &= 5 \times 8{,}000 = 40{,}000 \\
\text{Flexible Budget Fixed OH} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Flexible Budget Total Cost} &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 40 \times 8{,}000 = 4{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{Flexible Budget Profit} &= 10 \times 8{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = -20{,}000 \; (\text{Loss})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Tabulate the Flexible Budget.**

| Item | Static (10,000) | **Flexible (8,000)** | Actual (8,000) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Sales | 5,00,000 | **4,00,000** | 4,40,000 |
| Materials | 1,50,000 | **1,20,000** | 1,30,000 |
| Labour | 2,00,000 | **1,60,000** | 1,80,000 |
| Variable OH | 50,000 | **40,000** | 44,000 |
| Fixed OH | 1,00,000 | **1,00,000** | 1,02,000 |
| **Total Cost** | **5,00,000** | **4,20,000** | **4,56,000** |
| **Profit / (Loss)** | **0** | **(20,000)** | **(16,000)** |

**Step 5 — Compute variances (Actual − Flex for cost; Flex − Actual for profit).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales Var} &= 4{,}40{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = +40{,}000 \; (\text{F}) \\
\text{Materials Var} &= 1{,}30{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = +10{,}000 \; (\text{A}) \\
\text{Labour Var} &= 1{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}60{,}000 = +20{,}000 \; (\text{A}) \\
\text{Variable OH Var} &= 44{,}000 - 40{,}000 = +4{,}000 \; (\text{A}) \\
\text{Fixed OH Var} &= 1{,}02{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = +2{,}000 \; (\text{A}) \\
\text{Total Cost Var} &= 4{,}56{,}000 - 4{,}20{,}000 = +36{,}000 \; (\text{A})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Sales Volume Variance (using the Master Equation).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales Volume Variance (Profit)} &= (Q_{act} - Q_{plan}) \times CM \\
&= (8{,}000 - 10{,}000) \times (50 - 40) \\
&= (-2{,}000) \times 10 = -20{,}000 \; (\text{A})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This ₹20,000 adverse is *expected* — we produced 2,000 units less than planned, losing ₹10 of contribution per unit on each.

**Step 7 — Compute the Flexible Budget Variance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flexible Budget Variance (Profit)} &= \text{Actual Profit} - \text{Flex Profit} \\
&= (-16{,}000) - (-20{,}000) = +4{,}000 \; (\text{F})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Reconcile (the identity check).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Profit Variance} &= \text{Sales Volume Var} + \text{Flex Bud Var} \\
&= -20{,}000 + 4{,}000 = -16{,}000 \\
\text{Verify directly: Static Profit (0)} - \text{Actual Profit (-16,000)} &= +16{,}000 \; (\text{Actual better})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Step 8 is the **identity check** examiners love to see. If the numbers don't reconcile, you have made an arithmetic error — go back.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of Variances

| Variance | Sign | Amount (₹) | Operational Reason (Inference) |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Sales Volume | A | 20,000 | Plant operated 20% below capacity — investigate demand / capacity issues |
| Sales Price | F | 40,000 | Selling price was higher than standard — marketing / premium mix |
| Materials | A | 10,000 | Actual material cost > standard — possible price hike or wastage |
| Labour | A | 20,000 | Wage rates higher or idle time / overtime used |
| Variable OH | A | 4,000 | Excess consumption of indirect resources |
| Fixed OH | A | 2,000 | Spending marginally above budget — investigate non-discretionary items |
| **Net Effect** | **F** | **4,000** | Operational efficiency was *better* than plan, but volume shortfall wiped out most gains |

### 3.3 Edge-Case & Extension Note (Semi-Variable Cost Treatment)

If the static budget contains a **semi-variable** cost (e.g. *Maintenance: ₹30,000 fixed + ₹2 per unit*), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At 10,000 units:} \quad & 30{,}000 + (2 \times 10{,}000) = 50{,}000 \\
\text{At 8,000 units:} \quad & 30{,}000 + (2 \times 8{,}000) = 46{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

The fixed component (₹30,000) does **not** flex; only the variable component (₹2/unit) flexes. The flexible budget entry for Maintenance is therefore **₹46,000** at 8,000 units — *not* a flat 50,000 and not a flat 30,000.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often treat *all* overheads as fully fixed or fully variable. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires the **mixed-cost** segregation step. Always verify by computing per-unit values at the static level.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flexible Budgeting System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step 1: Receive Static Budget and Cost Data] --> B0
    A0 --> A1[Identify Activity Base e.g. units, hrs]
    A1 --> C0
    B0[Step 2: Classify Each Cost as Fixed or Variable] --> C0
    C0[Step 3: Compute Variable Cost per Unit of Activity] --> D0
    D0[Step 4: Write Cost Equation Y = a + bX for Each Cost] --> E0
    E0[Step 5: Obtain Actual Activity Level Q actual] --> F0
    F0[Step 6: Plug Q actual into Each Equation] --> G0
    G0[Step 7: Build Flexible Budget Table at Q actual] --> H0
    H0[Step 8: Collect Actual Cost and Revenue Data] --> I0
    I0[Step 9: Compute Variances Actual minus Flex] --> J0
    J0[Step 10: Decompose Total Variance into Sales Volume and Flex Bud Variance] --> K0
    K0[Step 11: Reconcile Identity Check and Interpret] --> L0[End of Flexible Budget Cycle]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Variance Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Total Variance] --> N2[Sales Volume Variance]
    N1 --> N3[Flexible Budget Variance]
    N2 --> N2a[Due to Activity Level Change Only]
    N3 --> N3a[Sales Price Variance]
    N3 --> N3b[Material Price Variance]
    N3 --> N3c[Material Usage Variance]
    N3 --> N3d[Labour Rate Variance]
    N3 --> N3e[Labour Efficiency Variance]
    N3 --> N3f[Variable OH Spending Variance]
    N3 --> N3g[Fixed OH Spending Variance]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Cost Behaviour Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CB[Cost Behaviour] --> CV[Purely Variable Y = bX]
    CB --> CF[Purely Fixed Y = a]
    CB --> CS[Semi-Variable Y = a + bX]
    CS --> CS1[Fixed Component Intercept on Y axis]
    CS --> CS2[Variable Component Slope b]
    CF --> CF1[Constant in Total]
    CF --> CF2[Per Unit Cost Falls as Q Rises]
    CV --> CV1[Per Unit Cost Constant]
    CV --> CV2[Total Cost Rises Proportionally]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Flexible Budget Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input Block Static Budget at Planned Q plan] --> P2
    P2[Cost Classification Engine Fixed versus Variable] --> P3
    P3[Cost Function Generator Output: a and b for each line] --> P4
    P4[Activity Level Selector Input: Q actual] --> P5
    P5[Flex Budget Calculator Output: Table at Q actual] --> P6
    P6[Variance Analysis Engine Output: Sales Volume Var, Flex Bud Var] --> P7
    P7[Reporting and Interpretation Output to Management] --> P8[End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above four diagrams are **function-flow schematics** because flexible budgeting is a *logical/computational* process, not a physical assembly. The Mermaid safeguards (no reserved keywords as node IDs, alphanumeric prefixed labels, no markdown in node text) have been strictly followed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions are direct, conceptual, and must be answered in **30–40 words** with a crisp definition or example.

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a flexible budget? How does it differ from a fixed (static) budget?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A flexible budget is a budget that adjusts itself to the **actual level of activity** achieved by segregating costs into their fixed and variable components and computing the budgeted cost for the *actual* output.
Unlike a **static (fixed) budget** — which is prepared for **one** pre-determined activity level and remains unchanged — a flexible budget provides a *range* of budgeted costs corresponding to different activity levels, enabling fair performance evaluation.

*(Examiner's Note: Mentioning "cost segregation" or "Y = a + bX" earns the third mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a "Sales Volume Variance" and a "Flexible Budget Variance".*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Sales Volume Variance** is the difference between the flexible budget and the static budget; it arises *solely* from the difference between actual and planned activity levels and reflects the contribution lost or gained due to volume.
The **Flexible Budget Variance** is the difference between actual results and the flexible budget; it arises from differences in *prices, rates, and efficiency* at the actual activity level and is the responsibility of operating managers.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Standard split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Provide full, step-by-step model solutions.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a) (7 marks)**
*Explain the concept of a flexible budget. State and discuss the assumptions underlying a flexible budget system. [CO4, K2/K4]*

**Model Solution:**

A flexible budget is a budget that **dynamically adjusts** to the actual level of activity (units, hours, etc.) by separating costs into their **fixed** and **variable** components. It is constructed using the equation $TC = F + (v \times Q)$, where $F$ is the budgeted fixed cost, $v$ is the budgeted variable cost per unit, and $Q$ is the actual activity.

**Assumptions:**

1. **Accurate cost classification:** Costs can be reliably segregated into fixed and variable elements.
2. **Constant per-unit variable cost:** The variable cost *per unit* remains stable within the relevant range.
3. **Constant total fixed cost:** Fixed costs remain unchanged within the relevant range of activity.
4. **Linearity of cost behaviour:** Cost-volume relationship is linear (no step-fixed jumps).
5. **Relevant range validity:** Output fluctuations occur within the originally established relevant range.
6. **Single cost driver:** One well-defined activity base (units, hours, km) drives all variable costs.
7. **Stable technology and prices:** Production methods and input prices do not radically change in the short run.

*(Award: 3 marks concept + 4 marks assumptions, with 2-mark sub-split for assumptions — 4 key assumptions clearly explained.)*

---

**(b) (7 marks)**
*A manufacturing company's static budget for 5,000 units is: Sales ₹2,00,000; Materials ₹60,000; Labour ₹80,000; Variable Overhead ₹20,000; Fixed Overhead ₹40,000. Actual production: 6,000 units. Actual results: Sales ₹2,55,000; Materials ₹78,000; Labour ₹1,05,000; Variable OH ₹25,000; Fixed OH ₹42,000.*
*Prepare a flexible budget at 6,000 units and compute the total cost variance, sales volume variance, and flexible budget variance. [CO4, K4/K5]*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Per-unit analysis of the static budget (5,000 units):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales} &= 2{,}00{,}000 \div 5{,}000 = 40 \; \text{₹/unit} \\
\text{Materials} &= 60{,}000 \div 5{,}000 = 12 \; \text{₹/unit} \\
\text{Labour} &= 80{,}000 \div 5{,}000 = 16 \; \text{₹/unit} \\
\text{Variable OH} &= 20{,}000 \div 5{,}000 = 4 \; \text{₹/unit} \\
\text{Fixed OH} &= 40{,}000 \; (\text{constant total})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Flexible Budget at 6,000 units:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales}_{flex} &= 40 \times 6{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000 \\
\text{Materials}_{flex} &= 12 \times 6{,}000 = 72{,}000 \\
\text{Labour}_{flex} &= 16 \times 6{,}000 = 96{,}000 \\
\text{Var OH}_{flex} &= 4 \times 6{,}000 = 24{,}000 \\
\text{Fixed OH}_{flex} &= 40{,}000 \\
\text{Total Cost}_{flex} &= 40{,}000 + (32 \times 6{,}000) = 2{,}32{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparative Table and Variances:**

| Item | Static (5,000) | **Flex (6,000)** | Actual (6,000) | Flex Bud Var |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Sales | 2,00,000 | **2,40,000** | 2,55,000 | +15,000 (F) |
| Materials | 60,000 | **72,000** | 78,000 | +6,000 (A) |
| Labour | 80,000 | **96,000** | 1,05,000 | +9,000 (A) |
| Var OH | 20,000 | **24,000** | 25,000 | +1,000 (A) |
| Fixed OH | 40,000 | **40,000** | 42,000 | +2,000 (A) |
| **Total Cost** | **2,00,000** | **2,32,000** | **2,50,000** | **+18,000 (A)** |
| **Profit** | **0** | **8,000** | **5,000** | **−3,000 (A)** |

**Step 4 — Sales Volume Variance (Profit basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CM per unit} &= 40 - 32 = 8 \; \text{₹/unit} \\
\text{Sales Volume Var} &= (6{,}000 - 5{,}000) \times 8 = 8{,}000 \; (\text{F})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Flexible Budget Variance (Profit basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flex Bud Var} &= \text{Actual Profit} - \text{Flex Profit} \\
&= 5{,}000 - 8{,}000 = -3{,}000 \; (\text{A})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Identity Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Profit Variance} &= \text{Sales Vol Var} + \text{Flex Bud Var} \\
&= 8{,}000 + (-3{,}000) = 5{,}000 \; (\text{F})
\end{aligned}
$$

Direct check: Static Profit (0) − Actual Profit (5,000) = −5,000 (Actual better by ₹5,000). ✓

**Final Answer:** Total Cost Variance = ₹18,000 (A); Sales Volume Variance = ₹8,000 (F); Flexible Budget Variance = ₹3,000 (A).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) (7 marks)**
*Explain with examples the three basic cost behaviour patterns: variable, fixed, and semi-variable. Why is their correct identification essential for preparing a flexible budget? [CO4, K2/K4]*

**Model Solution:**

| Pattern | Behaviour | Equation | Example | Per-unit behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable** | Changes in total proportionally with activity | $Y = bX$ | Direct materials, sales commission | **Constant** per unit |
| **Fixed** | Constant in total, irrespective of activity (within range) | $Y = a$ | Rent, supervisor salary, depreciation | **Falls** as units rise |
| **Semi-Variable** | Both fixed and variable components | $Y = a + bX$ | Electricity bill, telephone | **Falls** but never to zero |

**Why correct identification is essential:**

1. **Only variable costs flex** with output. If we misclassify a variable cost as fixed, the flexible budget will over-state allowable cost at higher output and under-state it at lower output.
2. **Fixed costs are the responsibility of top management** (capacity decisions); **variable costs are the responsibility of operating managers** (efficiency decisions). Misclassification leads to wrong accountability.
3. **Break-even, CVP analysis, and flexible budgeting** all rest on the $Y = a + bX$ decomposition. Without it, the entire management-control framework collapses.
4. **Variance interpretation** becomes meaningless — a "labour efficiency variance" can only be isolated at the actual activity level if variable labour is correctly segregated.

*(Award: 4 marks for the three patterns with examples; 3 marks for the importance-of-identification discussion.)*

---

**(b) (7 marks)**
*The following data relates to ABC Engineering Ltd. for the year:*
*Static budget (8,000 units): Sales ₹4,80,000; Materials ₹96,000; Labour ₹1,60,000; Variable Overhead ₹64,000; Fixed Overhead ₹1,20,000.*
*Actual output: 7,000 units. Actual results: Sales ₹4,20,000; Materials ₹91,000; Labour ₹1,55,000; Variable OH ₹60,000; Fixed OH ₹1,18,000.*
*You are required to: (i) Prepare a flexible budget at 7,000 units, (ii) Calculate the total cost variance, sales volume variance, and flexible budget variance, (iii) Comment on the operational efficiency. [CO4, K4/K5]*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Per-unit variable costs (from static budget at 8,000 units):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales} &= 4{,}80{,}000 \div 8{,}000 = 60 \; \text{₹/unit} \\
\text{Materials} &= 96{,}000 \div 8{,}000 = 12 \; \text{₹/unit} \\
\text{Labour} &= 1{,}60{,}000 \div 8{,}000 = 20 \; \text{₹/unit} \\
\text{Variable OH} &= 64{,}000 \div 8{,}000 = 8 \; \text{₹/unit} \\
\text{Fixed OH} &= 1{,}20{,}000 \; (\text{constant})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Flexible Budget at 7,000 units:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales}_{flex} &= 60 \times 7{,}000 = 4{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{Materials}_{flex} &= 12 \times 7{,}000 = 84{,}000 \\
\text{Labour}_{flex} &= 20 \times 7{,}000 = 1{,}40{,}000 \\
\text{Var OH}_{flex} &= 8 \times 7{,}000 = 56{,}000 \\
\text{Fixed OH}_{flex} &= 1{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{Total Cost}_{flex} &= 1{,}20{,}000 + (40 \times 7{,}000) = 4{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparative Table:**

| Item | Static (8,000) | **Flex (7,000)** | Actual (7,000) | Flex Bud Var |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Sales | 4,80,000 | **4,20,000** | 4,20,000 | 0 |
| Materials | 96,000 | **84,000** | 91,000 | +7,000 (A) |
| Labour | 1,60,000 | **1,40,000** | 1,55,000 | +15,000 (A) |
| Var OH | 64,000 | **56,000** | 60,000 | +4,000 (A) |
| Fixed OH | 1,20,000 | **1,20,000** | 1,18,000 | −2,000 (F) |
| **Total Cost** | **4,40,000** | **4,00,000** | **4,24,000** | **+24,000 (A)** |
