| **Profit** | **40,000** | **20,000** | **−4,000 (Loss)** | **−24,000 (A)** |

**Step 4 — Sales Volume Variance (Profit basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CM per unit} &= 60 - 40 = 20 \; \text{₹/unit} \\
\text{Sales Volume Var} &= (7{,}000 - 8{,}000) \times 20 = -20{,}000 \; (\text{A})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Flexible Budget Variance (Profit basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flex Bud Var} &= \text{Actual Profit} - \text{Flex Profit} \\
&= (-4{,}000) - 20{,}000 = -24{,}000 \; (\text{A})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Identity Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Profit Variance} &= \text{Sales Vol Var} + \text{Flex Bud Var} \\
&= (-20{,}000) + (-24{,}000) = -44{,}000 \; (\text{A})
\end{aligned}
$$

Direct check: Actual Profit (−4,000) − Static Profit (40,000) = −44,000. ✓

**Step 7 — Comments on Operational Efficiency:**

- The plant produced **12.5% less** than planned (7,000 vs 8,000 units). This alone caused a **₹20,000 adverse** volume variance.
- Even at the reduced output, **all variable costs except fixed overhead** were higher than the flexed standard. This indicates **poor cost control** — likely excess material usage, idle time, or wage overruns.
- The only favourable variance was a **₹2,000 saving on fixed overhead**, suggesting modest discipline on discretionary spending.
- **Overall verdict:** The company has a **dual problem** — capacity utilisation *and* operational efficiency. Management should investigate the **root causes** of the volume shortfall (demand, machine breakdown, supply chain) and the cost overruns (supplier pricing, labour productivity).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Flexible Budget questions (KTU 2024 pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Identity Check (2 marks).** The reconciliation *Total = Sales Volume + Flex Budget* is a **separate sub-question** in most KTU valuation keys. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Treating all overheads as fully fixed.** KTU 2024 explicitly tests the **semi-variable** case. A cost like "Maintenance: ₹30,000 + ₹2/unit" must be split. Misclassifying = full marks lost.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention.** A "Favourable" cost variance means actual cost is *less* than flex. A "Favourable" revenue variance means actual revenue is *more* than flex. Mixing up = deduction.
> 4. **No per-unit working shown.** Examiners award partial credit for the *segregation step* (₹/unit extraction). Always show: "₹96,000 ÷ 8,000 units = ₹12/unit" explicitly.
> 5. **Using the static budget values directly without re-deriving per-unit figures.** If actual output ≠ planned output, the static numbers are *useless* — re-compute everything from the per-unit rate.
> 6. **Confusing activity base with output units.** "Per machine-hour" is *not* the same as "per unit produced" if the problem specifies machine-hours as the driver.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ☐ **Flexible Budget** = Budget that flexes with actual activity level. Built from $Y = a + bX$.
- ☐ **Three cost behaviours:** Variable ($Y = bX$), Fixed ($Y = a$), Semi-Variable ($Y = a + bX$).
- ☐ **The Master Equation:** $TC_{flex} = F + (v \times Q_{actual})$.
- ☐ **Variance Identity:** $\text{Total Variance} = \text{Sales Volume Var} + \text{Flex Bud Var}$.
- ☐ **Sales Volume Variance** is caused ONLY by the activity-volume difference; it is *not* a manager's responsibility for cost control.
- ☐ **Flex Budget Variance** measures **price/rate/efficiency** performance at the *actual* output — this is what operating managers are accountable for.
- ☐ **Contribution Margin** = Selling Price per unit − Variable Cost per unit. Used in CM × (ΔQ) for sales-volume variance.
- ☐ **Sign Convention (F / A):** Favourable increases profit; Adverse decreases profit. Always state both.
- ☐ **Always show per-unit extraction** as a separate sub-step (₹/unit working).
- ☐ **Always end with the reconciliation check** (Step 8 / Identity) — it is a guaranteed 1–2 mark reward.
- ☐ **Mixed/semi-variable costs** must be split into fixed and variable components before flexing.
- ☐ **Fixed costs remain constant in total**; they do NOT change with output within the relevant range.
- ☐ **Per-unit fixed cost FALLS** as output rises; per-unit variable cost stays CONSTANT.
- ☐ **Application Areas:** Manufacturing, job shops, engineering projects, process industries — any firm where cost behaviour is well-defined.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zero Based Budgets and Budgetary Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Zero Based Budgets and Budgetary Control # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB)** is a budgeting method in which every line item of expenditure in the budget must be **justified from a "zero base"** at the beginning of each budgeting period, as if the activities for which the budget is being prepared were being undertaken for the **first time**. Previous period's budget figures are NOT used as a starting reference. ZBB was originally developed by **Peter A. Pyhrr** in **1970** at the Texas Instruments company, and was popularized when he implemented it as a corporate budgeting manager at **Chrysler Corporation** in the early 1970s. The philosophy demands that **all expenditures — old as well as new — must compete for available scarce resources** on an equal footing, based purely on merit and current needs. ## 1.2 Budgetary Control — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Budgetary Control** is the continuous process of (a) **preparing budgets** (financial, quantitative, and time-based plans), (b) **comparing actual results with budgeted figures**, and (c) **taking corrective managerial action** wherever deviations occur, so that organizational objectives are achieved in the most efficient and economical manner. The Chartered Institute of Management Accountants (CIMA), London defines it as: *"The establishment of budgets relating the responsibilities of executives to the requirements of a policy, and the continuous comparison of actual with budgeted results, either to secure by individual action the objectives of that policy, or to provide a basis for its revision."* ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Annual Family Subscription Review" Imagine a **Kerala household** reviewing all its monthly subscription services every January. Instead of **auto-renewing** everything (Netflix, gym, Amazon Prime, newspaper, broadband, music app), the head of the family writes each subscription on a **blank sheet** and asks: | Question Asked | Traditional Budgeting | Zero-Based Budgeting | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | "Do we need this at all?" | Assumed **YES** (kept) | Must be re-justified from **zero** | | "How much was it last year?" | Used as **base** | **Irrelevant** starting point | | "Can we afford a better plan?" | Compared to last year | Compared to **ranked needs** | > [!TIP] > In **Traditional Budgeting**, the manager begins with "Last year I spent ₹10 lakhs; this year I need ₹11 lakhs" (an *incremental* approach). In **ZBB**, the manager begins with "I have ₹0. Tell me why I deserve any money at all this year." That starkness is the **essence of Zero-Based Budgeting**. ## 1.4 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU - **Decision Package** — A document identifying and describing a specific activity/function in a manner that allows management to evaluate and rank it against other competing activities. - **Decision Unit** — The lowest organizational level for which a decision package is prepared (e.g., a department, a cost center, a project). - **Ranking** — Arranging decision packages in descending order of priority for resource allocation. - **Managerial Audit** — The evaluation of decision packages by middle and senior management to determine their worth. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of Zero-Based Budgeting The ZBB cycle is executed through a **5-stage structured process**: 1. **Identification of Decision Units** — The organization is divided into meaningful sub-units (departments, projects, cost centers). Every unit becomes a *decision unit*. 2. **Preparation of Decision Packages** — For each decision unit, one or more *decision packages* are prepared. Each package specifies: - The **objective/goal** of the activity - The **alternative ways** of achieving it (e.g., "in-house" vs "outsource") - The **cost** of each alternative (at different service levels — minimum, current, improved) - The **benefits and performance measures** - The **consequences of NOT approving** the package 3. **Evaluation and Ranking** — Managers rank their decision packages from highest to lowest priority. Senior management re-ranks across departments, since a low-priority item in one unit may be more critical than a high-priority item in another. 4. **Allocation of Resources** — Top management allocates available funds to the highest-ranked packages first, working downward until the resource pool is **exhausted (cut-off line)**. The boundary package — the last one funded — is called the **"marginal or cut-off package."** 5. **Monitoring and Control** — Approved packages are monitored during the period. Unfunded packages are documented and may be re-submitted later. ## 2.2 Distinctive Features of ZBB > [!IMPORTANT] > The "Three Zeros" of ZBB — every activity starts from **Zero**; every period starts from **Zero**; and every rupee must be **Justified**. - **Top-Down AND Bottom-Up** — Combines participative preparation with executive-level review. - **Implies evaluation of all activities** — old, new, alternative. - **Eliminates budgetary slack** — Because nothing is taken for granted. - **Demands information** — Substantial documentation; cost-intensive to operate. - **Oriented toward objectives** — Each package links directly to a goal. ## 2.3 Advantages of ZBB - **Rational allocation** of scarce financial resources. - **Eliminates unproductive/obsolete activities** that survive only by historical inertia. - **Improves cost consciousness** throughout the organization. - **Encourages innovation** — alternative methods must be considered. - **Better communication** between levels of management through decision packages. - **Facilitates performance auditing** at the activity level. ## 2.4 Limitations of ZBB - **Time-consuming and costly** to operate — heavy paperwork. - **Difficult to apply in large, complex, multi-activity organizations**. - **Subjectivity in ranking** — Managers may inflate benefits of their own packages. - **Short-run oriented** — May cut R&D, training, and maintenance — costs that pay back over the long run. - **Discourages incrementalism** — useful, small improvements may be dropped if not in a "package." ## 2.5 Theory of Budgetary Control Budgetary control operates on the **Plan → Execute → Compare → Act** loop (commonly called the **"Variance Analysis Cycle"**): | Step | Function | KTU Term | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1. Establish budgets | Translate plans into quantified targets | *Budget Setting* | | 2. Record actuals | Capture real performance data | *Performance Reporting* | | 3. Compare actual vs budget | Compute differences | *Variance Computation* | | 4. Analyze variances | Identify causes (favorable / adverse) | *Variance Analysis* | | 5. Take corrective action | Adjust operations, revise targets, or accept | *Management by Exception* | ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Budgetary Control Variances > [!NOTE] > Although ZBB itself involves no formulas, Budgetary Control requires computing **Variances**. These are the most-tested numerical tools in KTU Module 4. $$ \text{Variance} = \text{Actual Result} - \text{Budgeted Result} $$ | Variance Type | Formula | Interpretation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Total Cost Variance (TCV)** | $\Delta C \;=\; C_{\text{actual}} - C_{\text{budget}}$ | Positive = Adverse ; Negative = Favourable (for costs) | | **Total Sales Variance (TSV)** | $\Delta S \;=\; S_{\text{actual}} - S_{\text{budget}}$ | Positive = Favourable ; Negative = Adverse (for revenue) | | **Material Price Variance (MPV)** | $\text{MPV} \;=\; (P_{\text{actual}} - P_{\text{std}}) \times Q_{\text{actual}}$ | Sign follows cost nature | | **Material Usage Variance (MUV)** | $\text{MUV} \;=\; (Q_{\text{actual}} - Q_{\text{std}}) \times P_{\text{std}}$ | — | | **Labour Rate Variance (LRV)** | $\text{LRV} \;=\; (R_{\text{actual}} - R_{\text{std}}) \times H_{\text{actual}}$ | — | | **Labour Efficiency Variance (LEV)** | $\text{LEV} \;=\; (H_{\text{actual}} - H_{\text{std}}) \times R_{\text{std}}$ | — | | **Sales Volume Variance** | $(Q_{\text{actual}} - Q_{\text{budget}}) \times P_{\text{budget}}$ | — | | **Sales Price Variance** | $(P_{\text{actual}} - P_{\text{budget}}) \times Q_{\text{actual}}$ | — | | **Fixed Overhead Volume Variance** | $(\text{Std Hrs} - \text{Bud Hrs}) \times \text{Fixed OH Rate}$ | — | | **Absorption Rate** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Overhead Cost}}{\text{Total Standard Hours}}$ | ₹ per hour | > ⚠️ **Sign Convention Rule (KTU Board Expectation):** Always state whether the variance is **Favourable (F)** or **Adverse (A)** explicitly in your answer, even if the numerical value looks correct. ## 2.7 Engineering Industry Application In an **engineering firm**, Budgetary Control is used to monitor project costs against sanctioned estimates. ZBB is increasingly used in **public-sector engineering departments** (e.g., Kerala PWD, Indian Railways, ISRO) where every new scheme must be justified afresh before parliamentary approval — replacing the older "incremental" approach that perpetuated obsolete schemes. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — ZBB Ranking & Cut-Off (Full Solution) > **[KTU University Exam Style Numerical — 14 Mark Pattern]** **Problem (Q.21(b) format):** The Production Manager of **Kerala Engineering Works Ltd.** has identified four decision packages (P-1, P-2, P-3, P-4) and four decision units (Maintenance, Quality, R&D, Tool Room). The total cost of each package and the manager's priority ranking (1 = highest) are given below. **Total funds available = ₹ 18,00,000.** Allocate the funds and identify the **cut-off (marginal) package**. | Package | Decision Unit | Cost (₹) | Manager's Rank | | :---: | :--- | ---: | :---: | | P-1 | Maintenance (Essential) | 5,00,000 | 1 | | P-2 | Quality Control (Routine) | 4,00,000 | 2 | | P-3 | R&D (Process Improvement) | 6,00,000 | 3 | | P-4 | Tool Room (Replacements) | 5,00,000 | 4 | **Solution — Step-by-Step Allocation:** **Step 1:** Arrange packages in **descending order of priority** (Rank 1 first). | Order | Package | Cost (₹) | Cumulative Cost (₹) | Funds Available (₹) | Status | | :---: | :---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | :--- | | 1 | P-1 | 5,00,000 | 5,00,000 | 18,00,000 | **Fully Funded** | | 2 | P-2 | 4,00,000 | 9,00,000 | 13,00,000 | **Fully Funded** | | 3 | P-3 | 6,00,000 | 15,00,000 | 9,00,000 | **Fully Funded** | | 4 | P-4 | 5,00,000 | 20,00,000 | 3,00,000 | **REJECTED** | **Step 2:** Verify cumulative total. $$ \text{Total Cost of Funded Packages} \;=\; 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 15{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate unutilized balance. $$ \text{Unutilized Funds} \;=\; 18{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 3{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹} $$ **Step 4:** The amount needed for P-4 is ₹ 5,00,000 but only ₹ 3,00,000 remains. Since P-4 is the **last (lowest ranked) package**, it is **rejected** in full. > **Cut-off / Marginal Package = P-3** (the last package to be **fully funded**). **Step 5 (if a *partial* funding is permitted by the KTU examiner):** If partial funding of the marginal package is allowed, P-4 could receive ₹ 3,00,000 (i.e., 60% of its requirement) and ₹ 2,00,000 would remain as the **marginally unfunded** amount. $$ \text{Marginally Unfunded Amount} \;=\; 5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 2{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Budgetary Control Variance Computation > **[Typical KTU Numerical — Direct application of formula sheet]** **Problem:** The standard material cost for manufacturing **1 unit of an engineering product** is: **10 kg @ ₹ 50/kg**. During May 2024, actual production was **1,200 units**, consuming **12,500 kg @ ₹ 52/kg**. Compute **MPV** and **MUV**. **Given Data:** $$ Q_{\text{std}} = 10 \times 1200 = 12{,}000 \; \text{kg}, \quad Q_{\text{act}} = 12{,}500 \; \text{kg} $$ $$ P_{\text{std}} = ₹\,50 / \text{kg}, \quad P_{\text{act}} = ₹\,52 / \text{kg} $$ **Step 1 — Material Price Variance (MPV):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MPV} &= (P_{\text{act}} - P_{\text{std}}) \times Q_{\text{act}} \\ &= (52 - 50) \times 12{,}500 \\ &= 2 \times 12{,}500 \\ &= ₹\,25{,}000 \; (\text{Adverse — A}) \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Because actual price (₹ 52) is **higher** than standard (₹ 50), the variance is **Adverse** (unfavourable). The firm paid ₹ 25,000 more than the standard cost allows. **Step 2 — Material Usage Variance (MUV):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MUV} &= (Q_{\text{act}} - Q_{\text{std}}) \times P_{\text{std}} \\ &= (12{,}500 - 12{,}000) \times 50 \\ &= 500 \times 50 \\ &= ₹\,25{,}000 \; (\text{Adverse — A}) \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Actual usage exceeded standard usage by 500 kg — an **Adverse** usage variance, indicating possible wastage, inferior material, or untrained operators. **Step 3 — Total Material Cost Variance (MCV):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MCV} &= \text{MPV} + \text{MUV} \\ &= 25{,}000 \,(\text{A}) + 25{,}000 \,(\text{A}) \\ &= ₹\,50{,}000 \; (\text{Adverse}) \end{aligned} $$ **Verification (Direct Method):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Actual Cost} &= 12{,}500 \times 52 = ₹\,6{,}50{,}000 \\ \text{Standard Cost} &= 12{,}000 \times 50 = ₹\,6{,}00{,}000 \\ \text{MCV} &= 6{,}50{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,50{,}000 \; (\text{A}) \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ZBB vs Traditional Budgeting | Dimension | Traditional Budgeting | Zero-Based Budgeting | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Starting Point | Last year's actual/budget | **Zero** — every period | | Treatment of Old Activities | Auto-carried forward | Must be re-justified | | Approach | **Incremental** | **Comprehensive / Sweeping** | | Cost of Operation | Low (mechanical) | High (intensive) | | Managerial Effort | Moderate | **Very High** | | Resource Allocation Rationality | Moderate | **High** (ranked competition) | | Risk to Long-Term Activities | Low | **Higher** (may be cut) | | Suitability | Stable, mature organizations | Cost-conscious, restructured firms | | Time Horizon Focus | Short-to-medium | Short (tactical) | | Innovation Encouragement | Low | **High** | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid — ZBB Process Flow (The Complete 5-Stage Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Stage 1: Identify Decision Units
Departments, Projects, Cost Centers] --> B[Stage 2: Prepare Decision Packages
Costs, Benefits, Alternatives, Consequences of Rejection] B --> C[Stage 3: Evaluate and Rank Packages
Departmental and then Cross-Departmental] C --> D[Stage 4: Allocate Resources
Fund from Top-Ranked Package Downward] D --> E{Is the Resource
Pool Exhausted?} E -- No --> F[Approve Next Package] F --> C E -- Yes --> G[Identify Cut-Off / Marginal Package] G --> H[Stage 5: Monitor Execution
Variance Analysis, Take Corrective Action] H --> I[Period End: Build Performance Audit Report] I --> J[Next Budget Cycle Starts at ZERO] J --> A style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style G fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style H fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,color:#000 style J fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5c3566,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid — Structure of a Decision Package (KTU Standard) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Decision Package Header
Unit Name, Period, Manager] --> B[Objective Statement
What goal is served?] A --> C[Activity Description
What work is performed?] A --> D[Alternatives Considered
In-house, Outsource, Automation, Do Nothing] A --> E[Cost at Service Levels
Minimum, Current, Improved, Enhanced] A --> F[Benefits and Performance Measures
Output, Quality, Savings, Strategic Value] A --> G[Consequences of Non-Approval
Operational, Legal, Reputational Risks] style A fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,color:#000 style C fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,color:#000 style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style F fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style G fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid — Budgetary Control Loop (Variance Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Strategic Plan] --> B[Prepare Functional Budgets
Sales, Production, Cost, Cash, Capital] B --> C[Sanction Budgets by Top Management] C --> D[Execute Operations During the Period] D --> E[Record Actual Performance Data] E --> F[Compute Variances
Actual minus Budget] F --> G{Variance
Significant?} G -- No --> H[Continue Operations] H --> D G -- Yes --> I[Investigate Causes
Favourable or Adverse] I --> J[Take Corrective Action
or Revise Budget] J --> K[Feedback to Top Management] K --> A style A fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5c3566,color:#000 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style F fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style G fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style I fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,color:#000 style K fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5c3566,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid — Ranking Matrix Visualization (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Rank 1: P-1 Maintenance
Cost 5,00,000 - FUNDED]:::funded P2[Rank 2: P-2 Quality Control
Cost 4,00,000 - FUNDED]:::funded P3[Rank 3: P-3 R and D
Cost 6,00,000 - FUNDED]:::funded P4[Rank 4: P-4 Tool Room
Cost 5,00,000 - REJECTED]:::rejected POOL[Available Funds
18,00,000]:::pool POOL --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 -.Unutilized 3,00,000.-> P4 classDef funded fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,color:#000 classDef rejected fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 classDef pool fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366,color:#000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q.A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q&A Bank]` **"Define Zero-Based Budgeting. Who introduced it?"** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB)** is a method of budgeting in which every line item of expenditure must be **justified afresh from a zero base** in each budget period, irrespective of what was spent in the previous period. Activities are analyzed in *decision packages* and ranked in order of priority, with funds allocated only to those packages whose benefits justify the cost. The technique was **introduced by Peter A. Pyhrr in 1970 at Texas Instruments**, and was later implemented at Chrysler Corporation in the early 1970s, gaining widespread recognition thereafter. *(Keywords / phrases for 3 marks: "ZBB definition", "from zero", "Pyhrr", "Texas Instruments / Chrysler")* --- ### Q.A2 — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q&A Bank]` **"Distinguish between a Decision Unit and a Decision Package in ZBB."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > A **Decision Unit** is the *lowest organizational sub-unit* — such as a department, cost center, project, or function — for which a ZBB analysis is conducted independently. A **Decision Package**, on the other hand, is the *document prepared for each decision unit* that identifies a specific activity, lists alternative methods of performing it, the costs at various service levels, the expected benefits, and the consequences of rejection. In short, the **Decision Unit is the entity**, and the **Decision Package is the proposal** that supports or opposes funding of an activity in that entity. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 4 — ZBB)** > Students **commonly lose 3-4 marks** in the following ways: > 1. **Forgetting to state "Favourable / Adverse"** beside every variance value. (KTU explicitly deducts for missing sign-convention.) > 2. **Confusing Decision Unit and Decision Package** — these are *not* the same concept. > 3. **In ZBB numericals, not showing the cumulative-cost column** — examiners want the *step-by-step exhaustion of the resource pool*. > 4. **Omitting the "Consequences of Non-Approval"** clause from a decision package — it is mandatory in the KTU 2024 Scheme answer key. > 5. **Confusing MPV sign convention** — when $P_{\text{act}} > P_{\text{std}}$, the variance is Adverse, *not* the formula's positive sign. Always *interpret* the sign. --- ### 📘 QUESTION A — (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]`** **(a)** Explain the **concept of Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB)**. Discuss its **features and limitations**. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** The following are the decision packages of **Trivandrum Engineering Pvt. Ltd.** The total funds available are **₹ 20,00,000**. Rank the packages and identify the **cut-off package**. *(7 Marks)* | Package | Activity | Cost (₹) | Rank | | :---: | :--- | ---: | :---: | | DP-A | Preventive Maintenance | 4,00,000 | 1 | | DP-B | Quality Audit | 5,00,000 | 2 | | DP-C | R&D (New Product) | 8,00,000 | 3 | | DP-D | Worker Training | 3,00,000 | 4 | | DP-E | Market Expansion | 6,00,000 | 5 | #### ✅ Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — ZBB: Concept, Features & Limitations (7 Marks)** **Concept (3 Marks):** ZBB, developed by **Peter Pyhrr (1970)**, is a budgeting technique where every expenditure, irrespective of its nature or history, must be **justified from a "zero base"** at the start of each budget period. Each activity is documented as a **decision package**, evaluated, and ranked against all other activities for available funds. The technique rejects the incremental approach of traditional budgeting. **Features (2 Marks):** - Starts from **zero** every period. - Requires **decision packages** for each activity. - **Ranking** of packages based on benefit-cost merit. - Top-down **resource cut-off** until funds are exhausted. - Encourages **alternative methods** of performing work. **Limitations (2 Marks):** - **Costly and time-consuming** due to voluminous documentation. - **Subjective ranking** by managers can lead to bias. - **Inappropriate for long-term investments** (e.g., R&D, training) that may be wrongly cut. - **Difficult to implement** in large multi-product organizations. --- **Part (b) — Ranking & Cut-Off Computation (7 Marks)** **Step 1:** Sort the packages by rank. | Order | Package | Cost (₹) | Cumulative Cost (₹) | Status | | :---: | :---: | ---: | ---: | :--- | | 1 | DP-A | 4,00,000 | 4,00,000 | **Funded** | | 2 | DP-B | 5,00,000 | 9,00,000 | **Funded** | | 3 | DP-C | 8,00,000 | 17,00,000 | **Funded** | | 4 | DP-D | 3,00,000 | 20,00,000 | **Funded (exactly exhausts pool)** | | 5 | DP-E | 6,00,000 | 26,00,000 | **REJECTED** | **Step 2:** Verify exhaustion. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Funds Available} &= ₹\,20{,}00{,}000 \\ \text{Sum of Funded Packages} &= 4{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 \\ &= ₹\,20{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Identify the cut-off. > **Cut-off (Marginal) Package = DP-D (Worker Training)**, since it is the *last package fully funded* before the resource pool is exhausted. **DP-E (Market Expansion)** is the *first rejected* package. --- ### 📗 QUESTION B (ALTERNATIVE) — (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern — Internal Choice]`** **(a)** Explain the **meaning, objectives, and essential features of Budgetary Control**. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** From the following data of **Kochi Precision Tools Ltd.**, compute the **Material Price Variance (MPV)**, **Material Usage Variance (MUV)**, and **Total Material Cost Variance (MCV)**. *(7 Marks)* **Data:** - Standard material for 1 unit: **8 kg @ ₹ 40/kg** - Actual production in July 2024: **500 units** - Actual material consumed: **4,200 kg @ ₹ 42/kg** #### ✅ Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Budgetary Control: Meaning, Objectives, Features (7 Marks)** **Meaning (2 Marks):** Budgetary Control is the establishment of budgets relating the responsibilities of executives to organizational policy, and the **continuous comparison of actual with budgeted results** to secure, by corrective action, the objectives of the policy — or to provide a basis for its revision (CIMA definition). **Objectives (3 Marks):** - **Planning** all business activities in advance in numerical/quantitative terms. - **Coordination** among departments by aligning their targets. - **Performance evaluation** through variance analysis. - **Implementation of management by exception** — focus on significant deviations only. - **Corrective action** to bring operations back on track. **Essential Features (2 Marks):** - Establishment of budgets for every function. - Continuous comparison of actual vs. budget. - Analysis of variances (F = favourable; A = adverse). - Corrective managerial response. - Periodic reporting to top management. --- **Part (b) — Variance Computation (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Standard Quantity and Standard Cost:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{std}} &= 8 \text{ kg/unit} \times 500 \text{ units} = 4{,}000 \text{ kg} \\ \text{Standard Cost} &= 4{,}000 \times 40 = ₹\,1{,}60{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Material Price Variance (MPV):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MPV} &= (P_{\text{act}} - P_{\text{std}}) \times Q_{\text{act}} \\ &= (42 - 40) \times 4{,}200 \\ &= 2 \times 4{,}200 \\ &= ₹\,8{,}400 \; (\text{Adverse — A}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Material Usage Variance (MUV):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MUV} &= (Q_{\text{act}} - Q_{\text{std}}) \times P_{\text{std}} \\ &= (4{,}200 - 4{,}000) \times 40 \\ &= 200 \times 40 \\ &= ₹\,8{,}000 \; (\text{Adverse — A}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Total Material Cost Variance (MCV):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MCV} &= \text{MPV} + \text{MUV} \\ &= 8{,}400\,(\text{A}) + 8{,}000\,(\text{A}) \\ &= ₹\,16{,}400 \; (\text{Adverse}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Verification (Direct Method):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Actual Material Cost} &= 4{,}200 \times 42 = ₹\,1{,}76{,}400 \\ \text{Standard Material Cost} &= 4{,}000 \times 40 = ₹\,1{,}60{,}000 \\ \text{MCV} &= 1{,}76{,}400 - 1{,}60{,}000 = ₹\,16{,}400 \; (\text{A}) \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 4 — Quick Recall Box** - ✅ **ZBB founder:** Peter A. Pyhrr, **1970**, Texas Instruments → popularized at **Chrysler**. - ✅ **Decision Unit = the entity**; **Decision Package = the proposal/document**. - ✅ **Cut-off (Marginal) Package = the last package FULLY funded** before the resource pool is exhausted. - ✅ **Unfunded Package = the first package to be REJECTED** (not partially, by KTU convention). - ✅ ZBB is **costly, time-consuming, and demands documentation** — list this in any "limitations" answer. - ✅ ZBB **opposes incrementalism**; traditional budgeting is **incremental**. - ✅ Budgetary Control = **planning + comparison + variance analysis + corrective action**. - ✅ CIMA Definition is the **official KTU expected answer** for Budgetary Control. - ✅ **Variance = Actual − Budgeted**; always annotate **F (Favourable)** or **A (Adverse)**. - ✅ For **cost variances**: Positive = Adverse; For **revenue variances**: Positive = Favourable. - ✅ **MPV + MUV = MCV** ; the formula must be shown as a *verification check*. - ✅ Apply the **"sign-convention statement"** in every numerical — examiners look for it. - ✅ In ZBB numericals, always include a **cumulative-cost column** to demonstrate the resource-pool exhaustion. - ✅ In a decision package, the **"Consequences of Non-Approval"** clause is **mandatory** under the KTU 2024 Scheme. - ✅ **Public-sector engineering units** (Kerala PWD, Railways, ISRO) are real-world ZBB examples. --- **End of Module 4 — Zero Based Budgets and Budgetary Control Notes** *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Construction of Various Budgets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET741 - MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction of Various Budgets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Budget** is a quantitative financial plan, prepared and approved prior to a defined accounting period, containing planned revenues, expenses, assets, liabilities, and cash flows. In the context of **Management Accounting**, the **construction of various budgets** refers to the systematic preparation of interdependent functional budgets (Sales, Production, Material, Labor, Overhead, Cash, Flexible, etc.) and their consolidation into a coordinated **Master Budget**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> *"A budget is a financial and/or quantitative statement, prepared and approved prior to a defined period of time, of the policy to be pursued during that period for the purpose of attaining a given objective."* — Institute of Cost and Management Accountants (ICMA), London.

### 1.2 Classification of Budgets (KTU Framework)

| Category | Type of Budget | Nature |
|----------|----------------|--------|
| **Based on Function** | Sales, Production, Material, Labor, Overhead, Cash | Functional |
| **Based on Flexibility** | Fixed (Static) Budget, Flexible (Variable) Budget | Operational |
| **Based on Time** | Long-term, Short-term, Rolling Budget | Time-bound |
| **Based on Coverage** | Master Budget, Functional Budgets | Comprehensive |
| **Special Types** | Zero-Based Budget, Capital Budget, Performance Budget | Strategic |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Financial Planner"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Why do we need budgets?**  
> Imagine a family of four planning a vacation to Munnar for **₹50,000**. The father estimates *income from salary* (₹1,00,000), the mother plans *expenses* (food, travel, stay). The children cannot decide how much they can spend until income and savings are known.  
> Similarly, in a manufacturing firm, the *Purchase Manager* cannot order raw materials until the *Production Manager* fixes output, who in turn cannot fix output until the *Sales Manager* forecasts demand.  
> **A budget is this interlinked financial roadmap** — every manager knows the goal, the resources, and the timing.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Glossary)

- **Budget Period:** The time span (monthly, quarterly, annually) for which the budget is prepared.
- **Budgetary Control:** Comparing actual results with budgeted figures to identify variances.
- **Budget Manual:** Document containing rules, procedures, and responsibilities for budget preparation.
- **Master Budget:** Consolidated summary of all functional budgets, including budgeted P&L, balance sheet, and cash flow.
- **Flexible Budget:** Budget adjusted to actual activity level, useful for performance evaluation.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Budget — The Complete Architecture

The **Master Budget** is the culmination of all functional budgets. It contains:
1. **Operational Budgets** (Sales, Production, Material, Labor, Overhead, Selling, Administration)
2. **Financial Budgets** (Cash Budget, Budgeted Income Statement, Budgeted Balance Sheet)

The flow follows a top-down logic:

$$\text{Sales Forecast} \rightarrow \text{Sales Budget} \rightarrow \text{Production Budget} \rightarrow \text{Material, Labor, Overhead Budgets} \rightarrow \text{Cash Budget} \rightarrow \text{Master Budget}$$

### 2.2 Construction of Specific Budgets — Stepwise Logic

#### (A) Sales Budget
- **Logic Step 1:** Forecast sales volume × Selling price = Sales value.
- **Logic Step 2:** Adjust for credit sales (collection schedule).
- **Logic Step 3:** Segment by product, region, and time period.

#### (B) Production Budget
- **Logic Step 1:** Compute required production = Sales + Desired Closing Stock − Opening Stock.
- **Logic Step 2:** Coordinate with capacity constraints.

#### (C) Material (Purchase) Budget
- **Logic Step 1:** Required Material = Production × Standard Material per unit.
- **Logic Step 2:** Purchase Quantity = Required Material + Closing Stock − Opening Stock.

#### (D) Labor Budget
- **Logic Step 1:** Total Labor Hours = Production units × Standard hours per unit.
- **Logic Step 2:** Total Labor Cost = Total Labor Hours × Wage rate per hour.

#### (E) Overhead Budget
- **Logic Step 1:** Variable overheads = Activity level × Variable cost per unit.
- **Logic Step 2:** Fixed overheads remain constant.
- **Logic Step 3:** Total = Variable + Fixed overheads.

#### (F) Cash Budget
- **Logic Step 1:** Opening Cash Balance.
- **Logic Step 2:** Add: Budgeted Cash Receipts (Cash Sales, Collections from Debtors, Loans, Asset Sales).
- **Logic Step 3:** Less: Budgeted Cash Payments (Material purchases, Wages, Overheads, Capital expenditure, Loan repayments, Taxes, Dividend).
- **Logic Step 4:** Closing Cash Balance = Opening + Receipts − Payments.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table must be memorized for KTU ESE. Critical equations appear in 80% of past papers.**

| S.No | Budget Type | Key Formula | Variables / Notes |
|:----:|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Production Budget | $P = S + C_{end} - O_{start}$ | $P$=Production, $S$=Sales, $C$=Closing stock |
| 2 | Material Purchase | $MP = (P \times U) + C_{end} - O_{start}$ | $U$=Material per unit |
| 3 | Material Cost | $MC = MP \times R$ | $R$=Rate per unit material |
| 4 | Labor Cost | $LC = P \times H \times W$ | $H$=Hours/unit, $W$=Wage/hour |
| 5 | Variable Overhead | $VO = P \times V$ | $V$=Variable OH per unit |
| 6 | Cash Closing Bal. | $C_{end} = O_{start} + R - P$ | $R$=Receipts, $P$=Payments |
| 7 | Flexible Budget Cost | $FB = FC + (VC/u \times A)$ | $FC$=Fixed, $VC$=Var cost, $A$=Actual level |
| 8 | Budgeted P&L | $P/L = Sales - (MC+LC+OH+Exp)$ | Standard format |
| 9 | ZBB Decision | $Y = \sum B_i - \sum C_i$ | $B_i$=Benefit, $C_i$=Cost of each package |
| 10 | Variance | $V = (A - B) \times S$ | $A$=Actual, $B$=Budgeted, $S$=Standard |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Firms
- **Cash Budgets** prevent liquidity crises during long project cycles (e.g., construction firms, shipyards).
- **Flexible Budgets** are critical in seasonal manufacturing (e.g., textile, FMCG) where output varies.
- **Zero-Based Budgets** are used by PSUs and Government engineering departments to cut wasteful spending.
- **Capital Budgets** evaluate machinery purchase decisions using NPV, IRR, and Payback Period.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Construction of a Cash Budget (14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem:** XYZ Engineering Ltd. has provided the following information for the quarter ending 31st March 2025:
> - Opening Cash Balance (1st Jan): **₹40,000**
> - Credit Sales: Jan ₹2,00,000; Feb ₹3,00,000; Mar ₹4,00,000
> - Cash Sales: Jan ₹60,000; Feb ₹80,000; Mar ₹1,00,000
> - Collection pattern: 30% in month of sale, 60% next month, 10% in 2nd next month
> - Material Purchases (all credit): Jan ₹1,50,000; Feb ₹1,80,000; Mar ₹2,00,000
> - Payment pattern: 50% in same month, 50% in next month
> - Wages: Jan ₹30,000; Feb ₹35,000; Mar ₹40,000
> - Other Expenses: Jan ₹25,000; Feb ₹20,000; Mar ₹30,000
> - Plant Purchase in Feb: ₹1,00,000

#### Step 1 — Compute Total Cash Receipts

For January:
- Cash Sales = ₹60,000
- Collection from Dec credit sales (assumed ₹1,50,000 @ 10%) = **₹15,000**
- Collection from Jan credit sales (₹2,00,000 × 30%) = ₹60,000
- Total Receipts (Jan) = 60,000 + 15,000 + 60,000 = **₹1,35,000**

For February:
- Cash Sales = ₹80,000
- From Jan credit (₹2,00,000 × 60%) = ₹1,20,000
- From Feb credit (₹3,00,000 × 30%) = ₹90,000
- Total = 80,000 + 1,20,000 + 90,000 = **₹2,90,000**

For March:
- Cash Sales = ₹1,00,000
- From Feb credit (₹3,00,000 × 60%) = ₹1,80,000
- From Mar credit (₹4,00,000 × 30%) = ₹1,20,000
- From Jan credit (₹2,00,000 × 10%) = ₹20,000
- Total = 1,00,000 + 1,80,000 + 1,20,000 + 20,000 = **₹4,20,000**

#### Step 2 — Compute Total Cash Payments

For January:
- Material: ₹1,50,000 × 50% = ₹75,000
- Wages = ₹30,000
- Other Expenses = ₹25,000
- Total = 75,000 + 30,000 + 25,000 = **₹1,30,000**

For February:
- Material: Feb (₹1,80,000 × 50%) = ₹90,000
- Material: Jan (₹1,50,000 × 50%) = ₹75,000
- Wages = ₹35,000
- Other Expenses = ₹20,000
- Plant = ₹1,00,000
- Total = 90,000 + 75,000 + 35,000 + 20,000 + 1,00,000 = **₹3,20,000**

For March:
- Material: Mar (₹2,00,000 × 50%) = ₹1,00,000
- Material: Feb (₹1,00,000 remaining 50% paid in Mar... already accounted: ₹90,000) = ₹90,000
- Wages = ₹40,000
- Other = ₹30,000
- Total = 1,00,000 + 90,000 + 40,000 + 30,000 = **₹2,60,000**

#### Step 3 — Prepare the Cash Budget Table

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Month} \quad & \textbf{Jan} \quad \textbf{Feb} \quad \textbf{Mar} \\
\text{Opening Balance} \quad & 40{,}000 \quad 45{,}000 \quad 15{,}000 \\
+ \text{Receipts} \quad & 1{,}35{,}000 \quad 2{,}90{,}000 \quad 4{,}20{,}000 \\
\hline
\text{Total Available Cash} \quad & 1{,}75{,}000 \quad 3{,}35{,}000 \quad 4{,}35{,}000 \\
- \text{Payments} \quad & 1{,}30{,}000 \quad 3{,}20{,}000 \quad 2{,}60{,}000 \\
\hline
\textbf{Closing Balance} \quad & \mathbf{45{,}000} \quad \mathbf{15{,}000} \quad \mathbf{1{,}75{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4 — Analytical Verification
- Check: Closing of Jan = Opening of Feb ✓
- Check: Closing of Feb = Opening of Mar ✓
- Trend: Cash dips in Feb due to Plant purchase — management may arrange a short-term loan.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Flexible Budget Construction (7-Mark)

> **Problem:** A factory has fixed overheads ₹60,000/month. Variable cost is ₹20/unit. Selling price ₹40/unit. Compute Flexible Budget at activity levels 2,000, 3,000, 4,000 units.

#### Step 1 — Identify Cost Behaviour
- Fixed Cost (FC) = ₹60,000 (constant)
- Variable Cost per unit (VC) = ₹20
- Selling Price (SP) = ₹40

#### Step 2 — Apply the Flexible Budget Equation

$$FB_{level} = (SP \times A) - \left[ FC + (VC \times A) \right]$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate for Each Level

For 2,000 units:
$$\begin{aligned}
FB_{2000} &= (40 \times 2000) - [60000 + (20 \times 2000)] \\
&= 80{,}000 - [60{,}000 + 40{,}000] \\
&= 80{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = \mathbf{-20{,}000 \text{ (Loss)}}
\end{aligned}$$

For 3,000 units:
$$\begin{aligned}
FB_{3000} &= (40 \times 3000) - [60000 + (20 \times 3000)] \\
&= 1{,}20{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = \mathbf{0 \text{ (Break-even)}}
\end{aligned}$$

For 4,000 units:
$$\begin{aligned}
FB_{4000} &= (40 \times 4000) - [60000 + (20 \times 4000)] \\
&= 1{,}60{,}000 - 1{,}40{,}000 = \mathbf{20{,}000 \text{ (Profit)}}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4 — Analytical Inference
- The Break-Even Point (BEP) is at **3,000 units** (as expected, since FC/SP-VC ratio = 60,000/20 = 3,000).
- This flexible budget is used for **variance analysis** when actual output differs from budgeted.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Production & Material Budget (7-Mark)

> **Problem:** Forecasted sales for Q1: Jan 1,000; Feb 1,200; Mar 1,400 units. Desired closing stock = 25% of next month's sales. Opening stock (Jan 1) = 250 units. Material required = 4 kg/unit. Opening material stock = 500 kg. Desired closing material stock = 30% of next month's usage. Material rate = ₹50/kg.

#### Step 1 — Production Budget

For January:
$$P_{Jan} = S_{Jan} + C_{end,Jan} - O_{start,Jan}$$
$$P_{Jan} = 1000 + (25\% \times 1200) - 250 = 1000 + 300 - 250 = \mathbf{1050 \text{ units}}$$

For February:
$$P_{Feb} = 1200 + (25\% \times 1400) - 300 = 1200 + 350 - 300 = \mathbf{1250 \text{ units}}$$

For March:
$$P_{Mar} = 1400 + (25\% \times \text{Apr sales, assumed 1500}) - 350 = 1500 + 375 - 350 = \mathbf{1525 \text{ units}}$$

#### Step 2 — Material Purchase Budget

Usage in Feb = 1250 × 4 = 5,000 kg → Closing material = 30% × 5,000 = 1,500 kg

For January:
$$MP_{Jan} = (1050 \times 4) + 1500 - 500 = 4200 + 1500 - 500 = \mathbf{5200 \text{ kg}}$$

Total Cost (Jan) = 5200 × ₹50 = **₹2,60,000**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Budget

| Situation | Recommended Budget | Reason |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| Single product, one activity level | Fixed (Static) Budget | Simplicity |
| Seasonal demand variations | Flexible Budget | Adjusts to actual level |
| Cash flow planning | Cash Budget | Tracks liquidity |
| Strategic capex decisions | Capital Budget | Long-term investment |
| Cost control in PSUs | Zero-Based Budget | Justifies every expense |
| Daily operational control | Rolling Budget | Continuously updated |
| Total firm planning | Master Budget | Holistic integration |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
*"What is a Master Budget? List its four essential components."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**  
A Master Budget is a comprehensive financial plan consolidating all functional and financial budgets of an organization for a specific period. It is a blueprint for total business operations, comprising both operating and financial plans. Its four essential components are: (1) **Sales Budget** (forecast of revenues), (2) **Production and Cost Budgets** (material, labor, overhead), (3) **Cash Budget** (liquidity planning), and (4) **Budgeted Financial Statements** (Projected Income Statement and Balance Sheet). It coordinates interdepartmental activities, providing benchmarks for performance evaluation and ensuring strategic objectives are operationally translated. *(3 marks — definition 1 + components 1 + utility 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
*"Differentiate between Fixed Budget and Flexible Budget."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Fixed Budget | Flexible Budget |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|
| Basis of Preparation | Single activity level | Multiple activity levels |
| Cost Behaviour | Does not change with output | Adjusts with actual output |
| Usefulness | Planning only | Planning + Performance evaluation |
| Variance Analysis | Not effective | Highly effective |
| Suitability | Stable industries | Volatile / seasonal industries |

A Fixed Budget is static and prepared for one level of activity; a Flexible Budget is dynamic and built using cost-volume relationships to reflect actual output. *(3 marks — concept 1 + 4 distinctions 1 + implication 1)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**  
**Question A (14 Marks):**  
*(a)* From the following data, prepare a **Cash Budget** for the months of April, May, and June 2024. (7 Marks)

- Opening Cash Balance (1st April): ₹50,000  
- Credit Sales: April ₹3,00,000; May ₹4,50,000; June ₹5,00,000  
- Cash Sales: April ₹1,00,000; May ₹1,50,000; June ₹1,25,000  
- Collections: 40% in month of sale, 50% next month, 10% in 2nd month (assume March credit sales were ₹2,00,000)  
- Wages: April ₹60,000; May ₹75,000; June ₹80,000  
- Material Purchases: April ₹2,00,000; May ₹2,50,000; June ₹3,00,000 (paid 1 month lag)  
- Office Expenses: ₹25,000 per month  
- Income Tax Payment in May: ₹40,000

*(b)* Explain the **concept of Zero-Based Budgeting** and outline the four steps in its preparation. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — Cash Budget (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Cash Receipts**

For April:
- Cash Sales = ₹1,00,000
- From March credit (₹2,00,000 × 10%) = ₹20,000
- From April credit (₹3,00,000 × 40%) = ₹1,20,000
- **Total Receipts (April) = ₹2,40,000**

For May:
- Cash Sales = ₹1,50,000
- From April credit (₹3,00,000 × 50%) = ₹1,50,000
- From May credit (₹4,50,000 × 40%) = ₹1,80,000
- **Total Receipts (May) = ₹4,80,000**

For June:
- Cash Sales = ₹1,25,000
- From May credit (₹4,50,000 × 50%) = ₹2,25,000
- From June credit (₹5,00,000 × 40%) = ₹2,00,000
- From April credit (₹3,00,000 × 10%) = ₹30,000
- **Total Receipts (June) = ₹5,80,000**

**Step 2 — Total Cash Payments**

For April:
- Material (March purchase paid in April, assumed ₹1,80,000) = ₹1,80,000
- Wages = ₹60,000
- Office = ₹25,000
- **Total = ₹2,65,000**

For May:
- Material (April) = ₹2,00,000
- Wages = ₹75,000
- Office = ₹25,000
- Tax = ₹40,000
- **Total = ₹3,40,000**

For June:
- Material (May) = ₹2,50,000
- Wages = ₹80,000
- Office = ₹25,000
- **Total = ₹3,55,000**

**Step 3 — Final Cash Budget**

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Month} \quad & \textbf{April} \quad \textbf{May} \quad \textbf{June} \\
\text{Opening Cash} \quad & 50{,}000 \quad 25{,}000 \quad 1{,}65{,}000 \\
+ \text{Receipts} \quad & 2{,}40{,}000 \quad 4{,}80{,}000 \quad 5{,}80{,}000 \\
\hline
\text{Total Cash} \quad & 2{,}90{,}000 \quad 5{,}05{,}000 \quad 7{,}45{,}000 \\
- \text{Payments} \quad & 2{,}65{,}000 \quad 3{,}40{,}000 \quad 3{,}55{,}000 \\
\hline
\textbf{Closing Cash} \quad & \mathbf{25{,}000} \quad \mathbf{1{,}65{,}000} \quad \mathbf{3{,}90{,}000}
\end{aligned}$$

*(Mark split: Receipts computation 3 marks, Payments computation 2 marks, Final table 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — Zero-Based Budgeting (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**  
Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB) is a budgeting approach where every expense in every line item must be **justified from scratch ("zero base")** for each new period, rather than relying on prior period's budget. It is suitable for cost control in PSUs, service organizations, and government departments.

**Four Steps in ZBB Preparation (5 Marks):**

1. **Identification of Decision Units:** Divide the organization's activities into logical packages called "decision units" (e.g., a department, a project, a function).  
2. **Preparation of Decision Packages:** For each unit, prepare packages describing the activities, objectives, alternative levels of effort, costs, benefits, and consequences.  
3. **Evaluation and Ranking:** Management evaluates and ranks packages based on cost-benefit analysis, prioritizing those with maximum benefit-cost ratio.  
4. **Allocation of Resources:** Resources are allocated to approved packages, starting from the highest-ranked package until the budget ceiling is exhausted.

**Advantages:** Promotes efficient resource use, eliminates wasteful spending, encourages participative management.  
**Limitations:** Time-consuming, requires skilled staff, may create conflicts in prioritization.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**  
*(a)* Construct a **Production Budget, Material Purchase Budget, and Labor Budget** from the data below. (7 Marks)

- Sales forecast: July 5,000 units; Aug 7,000 units; Sep 8,000 units; Oct 6,000 units.
- Closing stock: 20% of next month's sales (Oct stock = 1,200 units).
- Opening stock (1st July): 1,000 units.
- Material per unit: 3 kg.
- Material rate: ₹40/kg.
- Labor hours per unit: 4 hours.
- Wage rate: ₹25/hour.

*(b)* The following data relates to a manufacturing firm for the year ending 31st March 2025. Prepare a **Flexible Budget** for 80% and 120% activity levels and find the break-even point. (7 Marks)

- Total Fixed Cost = ₹2,40,000; Variable Cost per unit = ₹60; Selling Price = ₹100; Normal capacity = 6,000 units.

---

**Model Solution (a) — Production, Material, and Labor Budgets (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Production Budget**

| Month | Sales (units) | Closing Stock (20% of next) | Opening Stock | Production (units) |
|:-----:|:-------------:|:---------------------------:|:-------------:|:-----------------:|
| July  | 5,000 | 1,400 (20% of 7,000) | 1,000 | **5,400** |
| Aug   | 7,000 | 1,600 (20% of 8,000) | 1,400 | **7,200** |
| Sep   | 8,000 | 1,200 | 1,600 | **7,600** |

*Formula used:* $P = S + C_{end} - O_{start}$

**Step 2 — Material Purchase Budget (July example):**
$$MP_{Jul} = (5400 \times 3) + \text{Closing Material} - \text{Opening Material}$$
Assuming opening material = 1,000 kg, closing = 1,200 kg:
$$MP_{Jul} = 16{,}200 + 1{,}200 - 1{,}000 = 16{,}400 \text{ kg}$$
$$\text{Material Cost} = 16{,}400 \times 40 = \mathbf{₹6{,}56{,}000}$$

**Step 3 — Labor Budget (July):**
$$LC_{Jul} = 5400 \times 4 \times 25 = \mathbf{₹5{,}40{,}000}$$

*(Mark split: Production 3, Material 2, Labor 2 = 7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — Flexible Budget (7 Marks):**

**At 80% activity (4,800 units):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales} &= 4800 \times 100 = 4{,}80{,}000 \\
\text{Variable Cost} &= 4800 \times 60 = 2{,}88{,}000 \\
\text{Contribution} &= 4{,}80{,}000 - 2{,}88{,}000 = 1{,}92{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= 1{,}92{,}000 - 2{,}40{,}000 = \mathbf{-48{,}000 \text{ (Loss)}}
\end{aligned}$$

**At 120% activity (7,200 units):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales} &= 7200 \times 100 = 7{,}20{,}000 \\
\text{Variable Cost} &= 7200 \times 60 = 4{,}32{,}000 \\
\text{Contribution} &= 7{,}20{,}000 - 4{,}32{,}000 = 2{,}88{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= 2{,}88{,}000 - 2{,}40{,}000 = \mathbf{48{,}000 \text{ (Profit)}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Break-Even Point (Units):**
$$BEP = \frac{Fixed\ Cost}{SP - VC} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{100 - 60} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{40} = \mathbf{6{,}000 \text{ units}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing Cash & Credit:** Students often include credit sales in cash receipts directly. **Always apply the collection pattern first.**
> 2. **Forgetting Lag Period:** Material purchases paid in the *next* month means a one-month shift in the payment schedule.
> 3. **Closing Stock Error:** Closing stock of one period = Opening stock of next period. Do not recompute opening.
> 4. **Flexible Budget confusion:** In ZBB, every expense starts from "zero," not from last year's figure.
> 5. **Sign Convention:** BEP formula uses *Selling Price − Variable Cost per unit* (Contribution per unit), not the reverse.
> 6. **Unit Mismatch:** Production in units but material in kg — convert carefully using standard quantity.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Budget** is a *quantitative*, *forward-looking*, *approved financial plan* — distinguishing it from forecasts or estimates.
- ✅ The **Master Budget** integrates operating + financial budgets into a single coordinated plan.
- ✅ **Cash Budget** follows the format: Opening + Receipts − Payments = Closing.
- ✅ **Production Budget** = Sales + Closing Stock − Opening Stock.
- ✅ **Flexible Budget** is constructed using cost-behavior equations: $FB = FC + (VC/u \times A)$.
- ✅ **Zero-Based Budgeting** requires *every* expense to be freshly justified, not just incremental changes.
- ✅ In **variances**, $V = (A - B) \times S$ for price, $V = (A_q - S_q) \times S_p$ for usage.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, structure: **(a)** numerical problem = data + step-wise computation + final table; **(b)** theory = definition + steps + advantages/limitations.
- ✅ Always show **2-mark answer as table format** wherever comparison is asked (differences, types).
- ✅ Master the **BEP formula**: $BEP_{units} = \dfrac{FC}{SP - VC}$ and $BEP_{value} = \dfrac{FC}{P/V\ Ratio}$.
- ✅ **Capital Budget** evaluations use NPV, IRR, and Payback — but at KTU 2024 UG level, NPV and ARR are the most frequently tested.
